author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: This was nice and bright.  My only change is I cooked the shrimp first, seasoned with salt, pepper, garlic powder and a pop of smoked paprika, then set aside.  I also seared the zucchini for some color but only partially cooked, then set aside.  Proceeded with the recipe, added zucchini back in to finish cooking, added shrimp on top before serving.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @MHCF I like your version!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @MHCF yes! we almost always cook shrimp first and separate so it doesn’t get overcooked

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Clam juice... Is this a "must?"

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @Jane clam juice always adds great flavor and it’s pretty easy to find in most grocery stores! don’t knock til you try it

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @Jane There wasn’t any at the store I was at, so I used water and a splash of fish sauce. I’d say it was a great substitution

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @Nadia thank you! As someone who is allergic to clams, I always just skip that ingredient. This is an excellent substitute idea.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody:
I added pepper flakes with garlic to allow them to release their oils, then beans and clam juice. I let the beans cook for about 5 minutes before adding shrimp to cook them down a little and release the starch. Then shrimp and lemon juice, turn down the heat. Don’t «cook» shrimp, just let it sit in hot pan until pink and cooked through. 

Last add butter to make a sauce. It was delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Tasty! Be sure to follow the instructions and stick to just the white and light green parts of the leeks. Felt like it could use a hint more acid, so I may try adding a small summer tomato with the beans and shrimp.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Double the lemon! You’ll thank me later!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I had leftover without schrmp - made a lovely breakfast topped with poached egg.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: The recipe is delicious on its own, but I added thinly sliced fresh fennel to the leeks which added a perfect pop to this.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Similar to Lemony Shrimp and Bean Stew but I like this more. The zucchini and clam juice work well and give it a very nice flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @Carol I thought the same thing! I make that dish quite a lot. I will try this new variation.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Use two cans of Beans

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have clam juice, but I had some dashi packets and used that. Came out great!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Perfect!  Our garden is exploding with zucchini, yellow and pattypan squash, best picked when they are only a couple of inches in size, some with flowers still attached.  I'll add fresh shrimp and revel in a fantastic summer meal.  Leftovers are even better the next day.   Thank you for this one, NYT!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: This dish has lovely, fresh flavor and is perfect for a light spring/summer meal. Would work well as a first plate with a heartier meal too.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: It would also work for a light lunch. Or a heavier mid-day meal as well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Great easy, healthy dinner.  Made as written except I cooked my own Rancho Gordo beans instead of using canned.  The clam juice really adds a good flavor to the broth.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Instead of clam juice you could make a quick stock with shrimp shells

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @Jean That’s what I was thinking! We’ll be shelling the shrimp anyway, why throw them away and buy something else?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I'm not sure the shrimp shells add anything. It would be much better to use shrimp heads

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @Pepe Luis I found it surprising what I got out of simmering shrimp shells.   To All:  For best flavor, simmer shrimp shells ONLY 5 MINUTES.  Google for Fish Stock from Shrimp shells. There’s a few sites that show the research on this shorter stewing time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Could I replace the clam juice with white wine?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I'm planning half chicken stock and half white wine. Clam juice is hard to find around here.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I order clam juice from Amazon.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @Carol clamazon was right there

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: A new riff on one of our favorites, the lemony shrimp and white bean stew! Excellent way to refresh the recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I used my own fish stock instead of clam juice, turned out great.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Large shrimp (16-18 per pound) need only 3 minutes cooking max (the 4-5 min called for here will have the shrimp overcooked).  Smaller shrimp need even less time. I like my zucchini crisp-tender so put them in at same time as shrimp (cooked 3 min).  Very delicious and fresh dish!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: This is not a post fromThis Tessa... I am the OG. But she sounds correct, so I will follow her advice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: To make this a more Thai-adjacent recipe, substitute lime juice for the lemon.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @JD”Thai-adjacent”- terrific phrase!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @JD And sub fish sauce for the clam juice. Have fun w a Thai pepper, Thai basil and/or cilantro.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Like others have said, boiling  the shells for a few minutes makes a flavorful broth.   While clam juice adds lots of flavor, I find it a bit too salty

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody:
This was an awesome dish, even better if I added the red pepper flakes earlier!

Though this was a poor way to discover my fiancée was allergic to either shrimp or clam juice (both of which she’s had plenty of before)!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I live in Europe....clam juice is impossible to find.  What can I use instead?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: If you can get fish broth, that would work, otherwise buy shrimp with shell and make a broth with the shells.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: This was a delightful, simple but tasty meal. Crusty garlic bread a must. The only addition I made was to add lemon zest with the lemon juice. A little white wine in the sauce would be good, too.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody:
This is like a shrimp scampi dish over beans and veggies instead of spaghetti. In fact it would be good over spaghetti if you want more carbs. I cooked my beans from scratch and added some white wine; otherwise followed the recipe. Next time I’ll follow a reader’s suggestion to pre-cook the shrimp in garlic and spices/herbs for more taste, then add to the top of the stew to reheat. 
Comes together very quickly!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: What could you substitute for the clam juice?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @Don P. Fish broth or cook the shrimp shells to make a shrimp broth

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @Theater Goer, good idea—flavor with less sodium.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Can I use frozen shrimp?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Could you sub chicken stock for clam juice? Not a fan of clam juice....

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I was wondering the same.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: This sounds yummy but my sis is allergic to shellfish.  What about subbing chicken for the shrimp and using white wine instead of the clam juice?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: What could we use besides clam juice? We’re on a lower sodium diet for health reasons.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: With what can the clam juice be replaced? In my country this is not available.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @Suzanne if your shrimp has shells don’t throw away. Sauté them a little then add water or a mix of water and

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Sauté/simmer the shrimp shells for 5 minutes only. The flavor compounds are highly volatile, so cooking longer will yield a very bland stock. You’re better off planning ahead and making your own clam juice if you can get your hands on some clams or mussels: shrimp stock doesn’t compare.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @Sarah F , We live in southern Portugal and am going to try this cooking local clams in garlic, white wine and butter - use this liquid as clam broth and add cooked clams in final step

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Just made this tonight! SO good!! I did cook the shrimp separately and briefly, as suggested in the comments. Also cooked the zucchini separately with the leeks, garlic and red pepper and set aside. Then added the beans to the clam broth and simmered for a bit. Added back the zucchini and leeks when the liquid had reduced and finally the shrimp and butter and chives for just a warming period. This all helped the zucchini stay somewhat crunchy and the shrimp moist and not overcooked. Definitely serve with good crusty bread for sopping up the juices.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I loved this recipe! Instead of using clam juice, I did a half cup of chicken broth mixed with a half cup of Sauvignon Blanc. Perfect! At the end, drizzled a little bit of Calabrian chili oil over the top. 😋

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I used vegetable broth instead of the clam juice and it worked nicely.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Delicious.  Made exactly per recipe and my wife and I really enjoyed it.  Maybe add a little extra lemon to brighten up the flavors.  Did need a few pinches of salt to improve overall flavor.   The red pepper flakes are a must, IMO!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious, especially considering the little work involved. My daughter took one bite and said "this tastes like summer". I followed the recipe exactly, other than adding more squash than called for. Served it with a rosemary sourdough bread for dipping, and the flavors complimented each other quite nicely. Today, I took the leftovers and turned them into a pasta salad as mentioned in the caption, and I think that's almost better than dinner last night!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: This was a delicious recipe. I increased most of the ingredient measures by a bit to support a 1.25 lbs of shrimp and instead of upping the clam juice added some white wine to the poaching liquid. Delish.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: 5 Stars! I cooked this EXACTLY as recipe dictated, even timing the sautéing exactly as indicated, and using 5 garlic cloves, even though mine were huge (we love garlic) and this was AMAZING! I used half zucchini and half yellow squash, maybe more red pepper flakes than indicated and fresh baked hot crusty bread. This was so good my husband commented “We broke our Friday night Hot Wings ritual with something healthy and just as tasty!” Score!!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: As long as you are using clam juice,why not try recipe with clams versus shrimp.  Think will try as with other ingredients, may be very tasty.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I don’t like clam juice and used vegetable broth.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent but I couldn’t resist cooking the leeks and zucchini longer to deepen the flavor. Seven minutes for the leeks, another seven-ish for the zucchini. Otherwise made as instructed and it was a delight.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I caramelized the heck out of the leeks and got some good char on the zucchini to add flavor, and then end result was ok, but I wouldn't use clam juice again - it gave it a little bit of a fishy flavor. Next time I might try white wine and chicken broth. Overall it's a good concept and it was easy to prepare.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: This was perfect for a quick after work dinner.  Very tasty, very easy, filling. Perfect with a side salad.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I really liked this but made a few additions with some trepidation including some fresh ginger, coconut milk, and a handful of chard for a little color. Served with rice, came out great.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone used white wine instead of clam juice? Asking for a friend.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I just made this and just happened to have clam juice. But as I poured it in, I thought I could have used white wine -(head slap) which I always have on hand. Go for it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Fantatic as written. Topped with grated parm, chopped crispy bacon and sourdough croutons. Oh, my. Keeper!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Protein, fiber, quick, easy, and delicious! This one will be a dinner staple as long as squash is in season. Only change I made was a dollop of Calabrian chili paste instead of red pepper flakes for a bigger kick that carried throughout the dish.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: This such a lovely, bright summer dish, and really can be prepped in 30 mins if you start with shelled and deveined shrimp. We substitute butter beans and use two cans instead of one. And 5 large garlic cloves (probably equal to 10 medium). Perfect!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: We couldn't find clam juice at our grocery so we subbed chopped clams in their juices (6oz) which worked out well since we were also short on shrimp. We also didn't rinse the beans. Came out so so good, definitely added to our rotation!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Can this be made ahead of time?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Very similar to the OG Hershey cocoa  recipe I’ve used for years. It calls for buttermilk.  This batter won’t look like any cake batter you ever made, but trust the process. It is wonderful

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @Jo KUKUKA oh oh wrong recipe!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Replacing zucchini with yellow summer squash because an influential partner doesn’t like bitterness that zucchini skins sometimes have. This is quite similar to the bean and shrimp stew elsewhere in Cooking.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight & took the advice of some others by adding a 2nd can of beans & more lemon juice.  It was really good!  I had some frozen, cooked shrimp, so used that.  Thawed 1st then added 4 minutes after adding the beans, just to warm through.  Will definitely make this again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night for my husband and we both loved it! I cut the amount of oil and butter by 1/3. I substituted chicken broth with a splash of fish sauce and white wine. Doubled the lemon juice.  Paired with my homemade sourdough bread and white wine. A nice, light summery dish.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Clam juice is so flavorful! Treat yourself to any kind of bivalve mollusk (except scallops and oysters - too expensive and their broth is too subtle), steamed with some wine/water until the mollusks open and release their juices. freeze the liquid from the bottom of the pot for the next time you need clam juice. Anyplace near the sea will have some kind edible mollusk, Cockles, mussels, razor clams, quahogs, soft shell clams (called steamers in New England), and will be better than bottled.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I’m allergic to zucchini. Would yellow squash (which I can eat) work?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Lovely, delicately flavored country stew. The cooking times were off throughout; leek and squash needed more time. If no shrimp on hand, any stable white fish (hake, haddock, cod, tilapia) would suffice. If no "crusty" bread available, croutons would be good. Final comment, this is not a full meal - something else needed to complete/complement the stew.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Oh what to say here … this is an overrated recipe for flavor vs effort and the estimated  time of prep plus cook is laughable.   The recipe is extremely mid.   I put all effort into it.   I doubled the recipe — made a reduced shrimp stock from the shells - (+ paprika, garlic)  for 50% and purchased a high quality clam juice for the other 8oz.   I followed the most helpful hints for when to add and remove the zucchini and leeks etc so to preserve crunch — and 2 1/2 hours later in this total 30” prep …we ate an extremely mediocre recipe.     This is not my usual experience with NYT recipes —& I will never make this again.     My family agrees.   It’s okay.  Not great.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: How could this possibly have taken you 2.5 hours to make? I can't fathom what you did.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I found it a little bland, even with the crushed red pepper. I added some Herb de Provence and this made it much more flavorful.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Cooked pretty much as written except juiced whole lemon, maybe 3 T. Love the shrimp/white bean combination. We alway sub aleppo pepper for pepper flakes.  Quick — complète mise en place is crucial.  Might try some saffron some time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Clam broth is often found near the canned tuna and anchovies. Not in the aisle with chicken, beef and soups.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I doubled the recipe and used a teaspoon of crushed red pepper. Only had 2 bottles of clam juice but had seafood stock. So I added 2 cups of seafood stock. This recipe is a keeper.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody:
Added a 4 zucchini
Double leek
2lb shrimp
More red pepper 
All else same

A more dense soup, which made it amazing.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Being Keto, I omitted the beans. Terrific.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Was skeptical because it looked bland but so good! I marinated the shrimp briefly in olive oil, chipotle powder and Old Bay. Old Bay is salty so will cut down on salt in recipe next time. Added more lemon juice than recipe calls. Next time will herb it up: mint, basil, etc…Delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: This was fantastic! We made it last night but increased it to 1 and 1/2. We stretched the clam juice with about a half cup of white table wine. Perfect dinner for a perfect night outside!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody:
Doubled the recipe. It worked out great! Followed the recipe to a tee. 
Delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody:
I doubled the garlic and added finely chopped chorizo 
Delicious!!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: My Safeway claimed they had clam juice, and I spent twenty minutes searching. Took one of the butchers to find it hidden on a top shelf above the canned tuna fish. I'm using two bottles of it and added more beans and lemon juice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody:
Very tasty. I may have slightly overcooked the shrimp. I will have to investigate how to prevent it from continuing to cook once ready while still in the Dutch oven. 

First time deveining shrimp too. Next time I’ll have the fish store do it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Why would one add the crushed red pepper at the end rather than bloom it in the oil at the beginning?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Agree with the comment that more lemon juice would make this even better, but great and easy dish.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Such a lovely dish and very easy to make! I doubled the zucchini, lemon and crushed red pepper. Otherwise followed the recipe and instructions. Next time I might add even more lemon. Don’t be afraid of clam juice…it adds great flavor. I never thought zucchini and beans would go with shrimp but this dish is perfect. Highly recommend!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Like MHCF, I quickly and separately seared the shrimp (16-20 Argentine Reds) and the squash (mix of zuke and summer) to part-way, added them at the finish. Also added fresh corn, steamed, stripped from the cob (threw the cob in w/the juice add, also a large lemon peel strip). A good glug of Thai anchovy sauce right after the leek/garlic sauté. S&P in small doses throughout. Some garlic toasts to accompany. Bright flavors, but still savory; worth it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: This tastes like Spring! Made as described. I was worried I didn't cook the zucchini enough, but it was perfect with a little crunch. Used my own "Big Pot of Beans" with a little of the broth which made it divine.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I was excited to try this. Followed the instructions exactly but I found it to be a little meh. Too soupy and not enough zing.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Love, love, love this recipe.  I didn’t have zucchini so I added spinach and shiitake mushrooms and it was delightful.  I think the recipe is a good base that can be customized to what’s in your pantry.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: This was the worst NYT recipe I've made. I made the recipe exactly as written, and did not skimp on the salt and pepper; it was very bland and lackluster. My husband said it was almost inedible. I was disappointed because it sounded really good, but the flavors just weren't there. Will not make again. Sue Li's Lemony Shrimp and Bean Stew is much better and yields a delicious dinner!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: This was SO easy & delicious. Used half zucchini and yellow squash for color and served on a bed of fresh spinach with quartered grape tomatoes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Easy and fabulous!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have leeks, so I used an entire bunch of scallions. I chopped them but not very finely, and added after cooking the zucchini and garlic. Turned out delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: The only change I made was to cut the shrimp in thirds before adding them, so they were closer to the size of the other ingredients.  Delicious and easy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Cooking for 2 so did 3/4 of a lb of shrimp but then accidentally added the whole thing of clam juice so had too much broth. Took the shrimp out then cooked down the clam juice for a while. Unfortunately this made the beans dissolve a bit but we added a ton of parm and it was DELICIOUS. I tried to lick the spoon. I really think this dish needs parm.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: This was good, not great. There was something missing and I couldn't put my finger on it. Very quick and easy to put together.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I thought the same -- something missing but not sure exactly what. White wine? Extra lemon juice? Another herb (dill, parsley, fennel)?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Thank you for such precise measurements. Although, like most cooks, I  don’t measure ingredients when I’m just making a meal without a recipe. But when I do follow a recipe I like to make it as the writer did, which involves more than bunches and handfuls!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Kinda meh.  A bit bland and muddy. Served with white rice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: @hilary- yes, you can. Or white Vermouth. . Vermouth is a handy fortified wine to have in the pantry. It stores well, even when it has been opened. It is infused with herb so it is very food friendly.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: We grilled the shrimp & zucchini then placed them in top of the finished beans. Served with crusty sourdough bread. Triple the recipe if you want leftovers. I recommend being extra generous with both garlic & lemon quantities. My kids licked their plates clean.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I made the recipe pretty much as written. I actually doubled it, added the red pepper flakes at the beginning and forgot to sauté the garlic so I threw it in with the shrimp and beans. As others suggested, I added extra lemon juice. This is a keeper. So easy and so delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Loved it! Made it twice in a week!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Simple, delicious and elevated mid week meal.  I add a splash of white wine,  extra red pepper and lemon. I like the idea of seasoning and searing  the shrimp first. Will try next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: This is really delicious. I omitted the zucchini because I forgot to purchase but it turned out great and very easy. This is a great use of pantry/freezer supplies. I used frozen shrimp with no issues just watched them closely so as not to overcook. I keep sauted leeks in the freezer too and always have beans and lemon on hand. Definitely going into rotation.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: There are very few New York Times recipes that I won’t make again. This is one of them.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I subbed a couple of tablespoons fish sauce for the clam juice with water to make 8 oz of liquid. Too salty. Next time, 1 tablespoon of fish sauce. Or I'll just buy clam juice. I also doubled the lemon juice. A delicious and simple meal. Note: don't cook smaller shrimp more than 3 minutes, medium shrimp 4 minutes tops!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: I doubled the clam juice 16 oz. This dish tasted special. Making it again this weekend.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious, and so simple to make! I took the advice of other commenters and added a teaspoon or so of lemon zest and a few tablespoons of grated pecorino romano. I add white wine to nearly everything, so in went 1/4 c of chardonnay. Cooked zucchini and leeks a little longer than stated to soften them up a bit more. Great summer meal!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: Definitely need to cook the shrimp first and set aside.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
commentBody: cook shrimp first , remove and return with lemon juice and chives.  add 1/2 cup white wine before clam broth

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I made this dish according to the recipe and it was very tasty.  I will definitely make this again.  My only substitution was avocado oil for safflower oil because that's what I had.  I love that it stretches ground beef, especially with current prices.  It is a perfect recipe to use the abundance of asparagus in the spring.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Excellent recipe, but I had to double to feed 4.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I used broccolini in Australian winter and it worked great!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous recipe! For crunchier tofu, slice into 1/2 inch cubes. Toss with a little oil and cornstarch. Bake in 425-F oven for 10-15 minutes. I also recommend doubling it if you're feeding 4 heathy appetites.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
Isn't the point of eating tofu, to dispense with meat? 
Tofu should replace the meat, shouldn't it?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @S Woo It can, but it doesn’t always.  Recipes like this can help reduce dependency on animal protein for those not ready to give it up.  And they can just be tasty recipes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Tofu is delicious and also good for you, (and the planet.) Also, super economical. It is not a punishment, or a replacement, or a substitute, per se. I plan on making this with Impossible Burger or Beyond Burger, as I cook for someone who does not eat meat; an easy recipe substitute for anyone who does not want to consume beef.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
@Someone Who Cooks lol the carbon footprint of the production of beyond beef and the impossible burger is worse than actual beef. Look it up.
Plus, those things are filled with chemicals

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Made as written, this is tasty comfort food. The tofu soaks up the sauce and pairs well with the texture of the ground beef. My only issue was that I couldn’t get the tofu crumbles to crisp, despite pressing and draining  the tofu. I could try baking them next time, but the lack of crispiness didn’t seem to affect the overall dish. I doubled the recipe to serve four and have some leftovers for lunch the next day. Served with quick pickles and lots of basil, mint, and cilantro.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This is easily a 2 hour dinner, I’m not sure where the 30 minutes comes from. I even used microwaved rice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Great flavors!  Could it be made with skirt steak?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Me I think so, just adjust cooking time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This is dinner tonight, but I think I'll make it with half ground beef, half ground pork.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Delicious recipe!  Thank you!  Quick and seasonal with the asparagus. I used a pound of ground beef, but stretching with tofu is a good idea. I plan to share with my WIC clients who can choose tofu. Mushrooms were a good addition.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Reasonably good recipe. Appreciated the opportunity to use tofu & seasonal asparagus. Once the mise en place is done, which took time, it does come together quickly. While it’s an international audience here, I wish the recipe was scaled for US sized packages of tofu & beef.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @S Woo like mapo tofu, it includes both. I use plant-based ground in recipes like this.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Tried this recipe this evening and it was a big hit. I thought I had tofu, but didn't, so added some sliced mushrooms instead. Threw in a few halved cherry tomatoes towards the end to add a bit of color. This serves two or possibly three, but not four. Moving it to my "Make Again" folder.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Impossible meat works great with this recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Great base to explore. Used groind turkey and loved it.  Will add mushrooms, Thai basil and some green peppers next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Beef!? In this economy?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I'm not a big fan of tofu... I want to double the gr beef... thoughts?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Any thoughts on what happens if you add more tofu and skip the ground beef to make vegetarian?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: We don’t eat meat so I’m considering using a mixture of mushrooms (processed into crumbles), cooked on high heat to get rid of the moisture/add flavor & tj’s extra firm tofu, crumbled. I think I’d add a bit of fish sauce (or vegan equivalent) to the tofu for additional umami (we’re pescatarians).  Then proceed with the recipe. I like using a mix of mushroom and tofu to mimic ground beef.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Too many ingredients for a busy working mom.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Why do they have to keep finding new “neutral” oils with names I’ve never heard of and definitely don’t keep in my pantry?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: "Low" sodium broth, soy sauce plus oyster sauce = lots of sodium.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I made this with ground chicken only (no tofu) and it’s delicious. The sauce gives it plenty of flavoring. I will make a bit extra sauce next time since I like to have more to top the rice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious and will become a regular for us. We substituted ground turkey and it worked perfectly. The combination of ground meat and crumbled tofu is so light and delicious. We think it would work equally well with well chopped broccoli or snow peas as well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
This was a great meal however I substituted beef for ground bison. So I needed more oil in the pan.
I also wanted it more veggie heavy and added one chopped zucchini and served it with soba noodles. 
Delicious. I would increase the volume of sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I’ve never met an asparagus that gets “crisp-tender” (?) in two minutes…needed to add some water to speed up the cooking. But nice flavors.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I was so disappointed in this recipe. All of the flavors should have been great, but it tasted like nothing and I found myself frowning trying to eat it. Wish I hadn’t bothered, do not make this recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @anne is wrong. This was fine.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
@anne

Totally agree, this was one of the most bland recipe I have tried from NYT.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Easy and delicious. My tofu didn’t caramelize even with heat and time, but I’m attributing that to the brand.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This is a lovely recipe, easy and tasty.  One hour from entering the kitchen to plating.  Made precisely as written.  The only downside is using part of a package of tofu, my household is not great at finishing off odds and ends.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I made this pretty much as written. It was quite good. Next time I would replace the ground beef with ground pork. I would also add shallots to the meat as I cook it. For me, this recipe needed shallot in addition to the green onion. The asparagus was perfect in this.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Four servings???? I doubled the recipe.  My son and I ate the whole thing, with rice, and could have eaten more. Absolutely delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and surprisingly easy to make. After reading the comments, I microwaved the tofu on high for 2 minutes before adding it to the pan, which helped it crisp up nicely. I also cut the asparagus into small ½-inch pieces, as the recipe suggested, and they cooked perfectly. I made with 90% ground beef ( vs 80%) and it still tastes great!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Loved this recipe for its overall simplicity and deliciousness. I decided to double the recipe because I didn't want to end us with a half package of tofu. Doubling this recipe works well and serves 4 to 5, or makes a great leftover option for a two.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I replaced the asparagus for broccolini I had on hand. delicious, with a clean and bright finish. My five year old and husband both enjoyed, which is a rarity. paired with steamed cabbage and trumpet mushrooms.  Will definitely make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Great use of asparagus in a dinner recipe! Timing guidance was great! And pretty quick for after-work cooking. We doubled the recipe mainly because we had a pound of (Beyond) beef and a pound of tofu. Used vegan versions of sauce ingredients and didn't double the sugar, though. Served over brown rice. Nice that the base recipe is flexible enough to welcome hot sauce for those that like it and flavorful for those that enjoy it as-is. I think it would be great with green beans later in the season.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I am new to the tofu world. This recipe has convinced me that I’ve been missing out. I made this tonight and wow, it was a hit. Very healthy, very filling and now on my list of “make again” recipes!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Doubled this and made it with green beans because I couldn't find asparagus - it was fantastic as meal prep for a few dinners.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Took closer to an hour to prepare, not 30 minutes. Not a fan of ginger, I skipped it.  Absolutely delicious and will make again, doubling the recipe and freezing part for later.  Enjoy!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: followed the recipe with some tweaks: used a pound of ground beef (while keeping the tofu 8oz). I also tripled the amount of sauce, because in my family we love sauce. also deglazed the pan with more chicken broth after browning the tofu. we loved it and found it very tasty and umami forward. to make it even better, i feel like it could have had a touch of brightness at the end. maybe vinegar or citrus. and/or try another commenters suggestions of fresh herbs on top. will make it again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions for a substitute for oyster sauce ? I have an allergy to crustaceans….

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Tchr I wonder if fish sauce would work?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Tchr if you have only a crustacean allergy oyster should be ok for you as it is a mollusk. But if you have a full blown shellfish allergy you can use items like hoisin sauce, some fish sauce, or Worcestershire sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Tchr oyster sauce is made from mollusks and not crustaceans. But if you are allergic to all shellfish you can try hoisin sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Will this work using all ground beef?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I’d like to make a veg version. Anybody try this with Abbots faux ground beef sub for beef?  How best to  sub oyster sauce?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @MonicaM You can buy vegetarian “oyster” sauce made with mushrooms

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: think this world work with ground poultry?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Do you think it would work with ground chicken? I have some in the freezer that I would like to use up.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I made it tonight with ground chicken and it was great. I had a 16 oz package of chicken and a 14-oz package of tofu, so I doubled everything else. Next time I'll make even more sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Could you use ground chicken or turkey? Would that work?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions for a tofu substitute?  I really dislike tofu

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: My husband does not like tofu.  Could I use boneless, skinless chicken thigh pieces as well?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Would it freeze well?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I would like to make this, but will sub seitan for beef. No fish sauce because of allergies.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: What’s a good substitution for oyster sauce? One of my crew has a shellfish allergy

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Miriam You can find vegan oyster sauce!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: What can I use in place of oyster sauce?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: They make vegan/mushroom “oyster” sauce. I know Lee Kim kee makes one. I buy mine from Whole Foods: not sure of the brand it’s the organic vegan one.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: My family very much enjoyed this.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Rather than making the sauce (step1), is there a bottled substitute?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Laughable how 45 'reviews' warrant a 5 star, it'll be 3 stars in a week.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I'm planning to make this. Can I use vegan ground meat?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: seems like a super recipe, but as I'm not allowed to eat tofu I'm just ging to leave it out

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Hi: I don't think the tofu was dry enough, as it never turned golden after 10 mins.  I too substituted avocado oil.  I could have double the asparagus. I think it would be great with toasted sesame seeds. And I used regular sesame oil, but toasted would have been better even though it's a drop. It turned out well and tasted even better!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I used homemade bone broth instead of the chicken broth and mixed green beans with aspargus. It was a real success!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I loved the spice/sauce combination but my husband doesn’t like tofu and I don’t like to eat beef so we compromised and made this with a pound of ground turkey and no tofu or ground beef!  I added mushrooms and it was delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is definitely a keeper! I used ground turkey as that's all I had and it was good but I will use beef next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This was just perfect! I used a pound of lean ground beef along with the tofu so we'd have leftovers. Didn't have oyster sauce so used hoisin and a bit of extra chicken broth. All the prep added significantly to the time, but it was so worth it!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I made this according to the recipe in every way, but I used vegan ground "beef" and reduced the scallions to half (my daughter doesn't like them). Although our daughter liked the final dish, my husband and I were not that thrilled with it. It really wasn't that flavorful to us. Sorry.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I actually don't have any neutral oil at the moment, so I used my "sizzle" olive oil, and it came out great. Cutting the asparagus into short pieces (I cut at an angle) meant that the fibrous bits were quite edible. A warning: the tofu stuck to my cast iron pan worse than eggs. I guess I need to  work on the pan's seasoning.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I quadrupled sauce ingredients and added extra broth, but this was still fairly dry. Even with low sodium ingredients, this recipe was way too salty. Prep and cook time was at least an hour.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Very nice quick weeknight dinner.  Followed the recipe as is, it was perfect.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This was so yummy and so easy! I added more tofu and some broccoli but otherwise I followed the recipe word for word.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: We're pretty adventurous eaters and we thought this was terrible. I absolutely love tofu and asparagus but this didn't work for us.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Delicious recipe. Great flavors in the sauce. I had trouble with my tofu sticking to the pan and not turning golden. Any suggestions? Otherwise I really enjoyed the dish.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Excellent recipe!  Used Canola oil.  Don't add more tofu or beef in order to use up a package.  8 oz of each is perfect for the sauce amount.  My husband wanted double the sauce (he's a super salt guy).  I try to be healthy and was happy with the amount in this recipe.  Serve with white rice.  Delicious!  Going on rotation!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty and easy to make for a quick meal. Made as directed but doubled it for leftovers. Will keep in the rotation

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly according to the recipe except for using Beyond meat instead of ground beef.  It was SO good. Will keep it in the rotation! But I agree, it is NOT a 30min prep. Delicious meal!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was OK. I added sirracha for a little more punch. It was fairly quick and easy, for sure, but I'm not sure I would make it again. I did like the sauce. I think, for me, it might be better with chicken chunks. I admit I'm not a huge fan of ground beef. I was happy for the addition of tofu.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Pretty good, but I usually use ground beef with less fat. It’s. It about not eating meat; it’s about adding more tofu to your eating habits.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I used frozen then thawed, squeezed and crumbled tofu; texturally pleasing and easy to deal with. My tofu-adverse husband enjoyed this dish, and the leftover protein portion was delicious incorporated into some ramen. Threw on some chopped peanuts for crunch

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! Served over ramen. My only complaint is now I have thawed half a pound of beef and drained half a block of tofu so it would probably have been better to have just doubled the entire meal. Also the tofu stuck to my stainless steel pan so I’ll have to figure that out.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recommendation in the Most Helpful comments of pre-baking the tofu (cut into 1/2" size pieces) with some oil and cornstarch, adding it at the end of the stirfry process.   This was a delicious meal--the prep of all the items does take longer than 1/2 hour.  More like an hour for prepping the items and then 15-30 minutes for the actual stir-fry.  Will make this again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: Could use a hit of acid, maybe some lemon juice or preserved lemon.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: @Val Lemon juice elevated this dish a lot! Delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: This is very nice, but not vegetarian, unless you substitute for the chicken stock.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: @David Indeed, and parmesan

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: @David It calls for vegetable broth not chicken stock. Perhaps the recipe has been modified since you commented?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: Took some fun turns with this and it was incredible. Used bigger pasta noodles, I always double the garlic in recipes, threw in some frozen corn, leftover pesto that was gonna go bad and added in zest & juice of a lemon. Served with bread and pork tenderloin. Phenomenal meal!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: This is really good. I used tepary beans because I'm not a huge fan of blackeyed peas, only had 5 oz of baby spinach so I supplemented with some pea shoots. Next time I'd consider chopping the spinach roughly, there were just a lot of stems. Might increase the parm. Definitely will make this again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: @MJ I was thinking cannelini beans.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: I used cannelini beans - it was good.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: What’s the best substitute for those who are not a fan of black-eyed peas? Chickpeas? White beans?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: @MJ I like the idea of garbanzo beans, because then you’ve got a springtime pasta e ceci!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious and very filling. I used chard instead of spinach and orzo because that's what I had. A squeeze of lemon at the end was nice. I doubled this so we'd have leftovers and I'm glad I did. This is my new go-to beans and greens stew.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was really good - light comfort food!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: I added extra peas, carrots and black-eyed peas than the recipe calls for. I also added extra broth, garlic and Herbs de Provence creating a more savory flavor. I served this with some toasted sourdough, extra cheese and it is a new family favorite.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t have any dill, so I added a tablespoon of prepared pesto. I’m sure it would have been delicious with the dill, but the pesto was a more than adequate substitute.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: I forgot — I skipped the dill! It was more of chicken noodle soup without the chicken. So good!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody:
Perhaps the dish was too hot.  I recently did a deep
dive into Cacio e Pepe and learned the cheese becomes a clumpy mess if you add it to the pasta when it’s too hot.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: @kmorrow I am preparing it right now and have decided I’m going to hold off on adding the Parmesan since I plan to enjoy it over a few days. Thanks for substantiating my approach.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: We added some red pepper flakes with the garlic, it added a little punch to the broth.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: can't eat dried beans/peas so used black lentils, and had Tuscan kale so used instead of spinach. also I always have parmesan broth cubes in my freezer made from all those rinds and added that. Great recipe and as others have said good for variations of convenience, too.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: This is great! Highly recommend. I wanted a little kick to balance the sweet (a little sweet heat if you will), so I added some crushed red pepper and I must say I recommend it if you like spice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: I was impressed by how much flavor this had for something so simple. The cheese is essential for bringing it together. I used the better than bouillon roasted vegetable base and followed other commenters' advice to finish with a squeeze of lemon.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed.  Added a squeeze of lemon at the end.  I might add more spinach next time.  Loved it!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is perfect as written (the measurements don’t have to be exact, it’s not a cake). For what appears to be a simple dish reveals much complexity. I don’t agree that it needs vinegar or lemon, as many have suggested, because without the acid we are left to enjoy all these more subtle notes. This is comfort in a bowl and such an easy way to add veggies to your diet. Adding it to my regular rotation.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: This is a really innovative recipe. I usually snort at the "If you do substitute every other ingredient, this recipe is FANTASTIC!" Anyway, I used my husband's good homemade poultry stock and sprayed in probably half a cup of boxed white wine, and I also used twice as much butter as the recipe calls for. That's not going to kill you. One additional note: don't skip the crushed red pepper or parmigiano. And I think it's a fantastic recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: Made this for an easy dinner and it turned out so good. My only changes were to add cremini mushrooms and some fresh shrimp that I had on hand and didn’t want to go to waste. Delicious and hearty meal. I think I will add some level of citrus next time, otherwise…definitely a win!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: How would one use dried black-eyed peas instead of canned?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: Was hoping to find a go-to veggie one pot meal. Unfortunately this was not a hit at my house. Not enough flavor. I used black eyed peas and black beans and it needed substantially more seasoning and broth.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: Mmmmm…this is SO good! I followed my own advice I gave to another Comment Writer and subbed in garbanzo beans for the black-eyed peas, mostly because I didn’t want to run out to the store, but also because then this is a delightful springtime pasta e ceci. I used 3/4 cup diced yellow onion, a mix of chicken stock and vegetable Better than Bouillon, and Pecorino Romano in place of the Parmesan. My two tablespoons of butter were salted and wow, is that luxurious! I also stirred in the juice of half a lemon with the dill and the cheese. Most of my broth was soaked up by the ditalini, and my spinach sort of disappeared, so I’ll add probably 1 1/2 cups water, the rest of my 12 oz bag baby spinach, and the juice from the other half of the lemon when I heat the leftovers up on the stove.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: This was an easy tasty dish! Added a parmesan rind when adding the broth. Added extra carrots and used spinach from our garden. Also served with homemade croutons!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is so yummy!! I swapped out broth for homemade chicken stock and cannellini beans for the black eyes peas and now obsessed with this dish.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious! I used about 1.25 - 1.5 tsp Diamond Crystal salt in total. I wish recipes included a range of salt when they say salt to taste. 
I followed the quantities in the recipe exactly, though a few ingredients were substituted: 2/3 cup white wine in place of some broth, a medley of cooked beans and lentils that were in my freezer instead of black eyed peas, and basil (from the indoor garden) instead of dill. I would definitely include the white wine again. The basil was overpowered by other flavors, so I will make a point to get some dill next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: We enjoyed this very much, and it's not much work. As recommended by others, I stirred about 1/2 t. red pepper flakes in with the garlic. Used cannellini beans and added them along with the ditalini and carrots to build their flavor. I recommend stirring in the spinach after turning off the heat. That way it wilts but doesn't overcook. Stirred in juice of half a lemon along with the dill and served the cheese at the table.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: Thanks everyone who added comments- it was helpful. My family really enjoyed this recipe- the dill made it interesting. Like other commenters, I also added a dash of lemon, upped the butter and cheese a wee bit.  I had some extra mushrooms around so just threw those in as well and it was great. I love how this recipe can be adjusted to what you have around.The made enough for 3 and a good bit of left overs.  Next time, I’ll

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: I haven’t had any luck introducing Parmesan to a liquid. It always separates from the liquid. What am I doing wrong?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: @April  I get the same result. In this case, don't mind because I will put it on for serving

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody:
@April
I used a microplane to grate a chunk of parm and that seemed to work

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: first of all-- use Reggiano-Parmigiano. Not kraft off the shelf or any domestic. Sorry I know that sounds snobby-- but you gotta use the REAL thing.  Introducing Parmesan or Pecorino to a liquid and making it glossy or creamy  just takes heat and a little patience. Seriously-- practice with cacio e pepe or alfredo-- which use no butter or cream.  Use your hot pasta liquid and keep stirring.  Believe me--- you WILL get it!! You WILL get the hang of it...

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: Is this better made a day ahead or is it good to eat right away?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody:
@Joanne

The pasta may get mushy if you make it ahead

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: What would be a good substitute for spinach?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody:
Extra peas
Add corn. Can sub kale or a mixture
Add pesto and add lemon juice at end or in bowl

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe! After I tasted it with the Parmesan cheese I added nutritional yeast as well. Definitely improved the taste and consistency.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody:
Wow, I’m amazed that I’m such an early Commenter!!! I made this because I had everything home for it (so I thought!), it seemed very fast—which it is!—and it’s relatively healthy. Two things happened that I think may have improved the dish:
1. I only had 2 bundles of soba noodles, so I made 6.5 oz of noodles instead of 8 oz, and in NYT Cooking app noodle math, this is the correct amount! 
2. My only smooth nut butter was almond butter. The only other comment on here is questioning the potentially bizarre combination of peanut butter and salmon, and my mistake eliminates this. Honestly, since I can’t speak to the experience the combination, I think I could go either way, but for now, Trader Joe’s creamy raw almond butter is working me!

I used nearly all the broccoli stems in addition to the florets. I had a half lime sitting in my fridge, so I juiced it and added it to the sauce. I also added waaaayyy more cilantro than 2 T, as well as 1/2 cup thinly sliced scallions. I then drizzled chili crisp and sprinkled toasted sesame seeds over everything. 

I’ll be making this again, for sure, but incorporate more lime juice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: I love to make this for meal prep - you can make such large portions and it keeps really well! Added mild + hot italian sausage and deglazed with some white wine. Used cannelini beans instead of black eyed peas and omitted the dill! Would probably 1.5x the spinach to make it greener

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: CZ - when you say you deglaze with some white wine, are you cooking the sausage in a pan and then de-glazing the little bits from the sausage, or are you using the wine during step 1 before the broth? In addition, when you meal prep, do you wait to add the pasta, or is it still good days later?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: I added chopped fresh asparagus and English peas. No black eyed peas. Delicious, healthy and pretty.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious as written. I made this for my 10-year old vegetarian daughter and it came together very quickly, has lots of fantastic and healthy ingredients, and tastes savory and nourishing, while also feeling light enough to be a spring/summer soup. I made some french bread to go alongside and sop up all that delicious broth. A total winner.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: For a veggie and pasta lover like me, this one really hit a home rin. I amped up some of the veggies.  Divine.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: this is akin to a minestrone with more cheese and a bit of dill, which i skipped because it's the kind of herb that overpowers everything. also if you're not finishing the entire portion, the pasta loses that great al-dente texture from absorbing the broth. nice to try, but doesn't earn a spot in my rotation

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: I made it exactly as written and it was delicious. It doesn't take long for the small pasta to cook so make sure you have everything else ready to go into the pot. A "few minutes shy of al dente" might only take a couple of minutes, depending on your pasta.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
commentBody: Wow that came out way better than i would have expected! No notes, 5 stars,

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: I can’t believe I get to comment first. This was good, and I assume similar to the addictive cabbage recipe. I rinsed the cabbage beforehand and the water took a while to evaporate. Also, I added quite a bit of lemon. It worked well with the fish I served, but I’d tone it down a bit next time to let the earthiness of the miso shine through.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: @MHO did it hold up as a leftover?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent. I wanted to make it right away so I used a bag of shredded cabbage I already had on hand - cook time was a little shorter as a result and it did not need to go in the oven. Paired it with simple salmon grilled with mayo/sriracha marinade. Will definitely make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious.  Didn't have dill and didn't substitute.  Easy on the lemon.  Will make many more times.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: One of the best things I’ve made in a long time, truly shows the beauty in simplicity! I used a big purple cabbage and made it for meal prep. I’ve been taking bites every time I’ve opened the fridge. It’s so addicting. Love that it tastes great hot or cold. New recipe obsession.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: @Ami Check out Yasmin Fahr’s Miso-Roasted Broccoli if you enjoyed this cabbage. I haven’t tried this cabbage yet, but I certainly will soon. The miso broccoli immediately went into my regular rotation.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: Very nice and easy as is. I stirred in tofu at the end (1 packed sliced and torn into dime sized pieces, tossed with corn starch, salt, pepper, sprayed with oil and roasted in the oven for 20-30 min). Will make a bit of additional sauce to better cover the tofu next time. Devoured it very warm, seems like it’ll be great chilled, too. Crispy chow mein noodles or peanuts could be nice to try on top.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: I loved this! Used up a chunk of leftover cabbage and ate with a dollop of kimchi for lunch. Flexible, simple recipe. The butter miso combo is salty and creamy and delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: kimchi with this cabbage sounds bombbb, good rec will add it next time

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: great utilitarian side! made it alongside the “sheet-pan chicken and potatoes with feta, lemon and dill” and both turned out fantastic. would benefit from using a high quality miso so that that flavor really sings.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and super easy. Used ghee in place of butter. I should’ve waited till I added the miso to salt because it was a bit too salty.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: I agree with the other cooks.  This is an easy fabulous recipe.  What a great way to make cabbage.  It's such an overlooked vegetable, but it's so cheap and plentiful all year round that I'm going to make it more often.  Once again, thank you NYT Cooking!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: Really liked this. Made exactly as written. Easy prep and a unique flavor. Served with roasted salmon. Would make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: This was divine and so easy. I could have eaten the whole pan myself and maybe next time I will!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: This was good, but I wish I hadn't stirred up the brown bits on the bottom of the pan. The miso that stuck to the pan and was scooped up tasted burned and overpowered the cabbage, which was good overall.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone veganized this? Would olive or coconut or avocado oil work instead of the butter?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
commentBody: It never mentions when the cabbage should to into the oven, neither at what temperature. I guess it’s between steps 2 and 3.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Wouldn't steaming the broccoli allow more control over the cooking as well as avoiding the potential problem of excess water?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: @carmencatt - I normally blanch my broccoli (and most green vegetables for that matter). I find it leaves them brighter and crisper. I find steaming inconsistently cooks things, and it’s harder to control how cooked things get.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: If you are going to boil ( I’d say blanch) the broccoli, shock it by putting the cooked broccoli in an ice water bath. This stops the cooking and keeps the green vibrant.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Helen unfortunately shocking the broccoli won’t let you dress it while warm, which should help the dressing absorb better.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Why not just steam the broccoli?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: this is blanching the broccoli so it stays green after cooking.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Jennifer I dream broccoli all the time, and it stays green. So I think either method can work, based on your preference.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Name Terry M, I dream of broccoli all the time too ;-)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was great. Simple with great flavor. I added a can of white beans to make it more of a meal (instead of a side).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Amii what a great idea!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Can’t think of broccoli and ginger without adding garlic, even if just used to wipe the serving bowl all over with a halved clove or two. It’s the same way i imbue Caesar salad, just by swiping the bowl with garlic. Depending on freshness of garlic, may use 1 or 3.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Anut. How about adding minced garlic to the dressing? Raw or sautéed in the oil? Or … garlic powder or garlic salt? (I’m a garlic lover, but I have to be careful because my husband is only so so.)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I love this! I made this salad using broccoli that was left behind in the veg drawer. It is key to dress this while the broccoli is warm, which is mentioned in the prelude to the recipe. I understand why folks may want to shock the broccoli in ice water, but that will cause it to absorb less flavor. I kept in the fridge for a few days & added it as a side to almost every meal. Really good.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Rachel S, Thanks for sharing. I plan on keeping this for leftovers, too. Did you find that it lost its bright color after time, as Jane reported in her note?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Even better, microwave it. It preserves more of the nutrients.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Tough Shopper totally agree with this comment and also you can control how much you cook it much more easily

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Tough Shopper how long do you microwave it for?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I agree that this is delicious, but having dressed my salad well in advance of guests, I discovered that the lemon has turned the broccoli an unattractive shade of green. If I make this again, I will combine closer in time to when I eat…

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Jane the recipe, and several comments, mention letting the warm (blanched) broccoli absorb the dressing so it flavors up optimally. If you wait until the last minute, it seems the absorption won’t have time. Unless you’re adding the lemon juice independently of the dressing? I haven’t made it yet so I’m just guessing.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I felt this needed a little maple syrup to balance the salt and acid. Otherwise a great recipe. Didn’t find that boiling created any issues wirh excess water (I did drain in a paper towel lined mixing bowl first), and didn’t bother shocking in ice water. Two minutes of boiling and a few minutes drain yielded bright, crisp-tender florets.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Why not roast the broccoli so that the dressing doesn’t get diluted?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: This dressing is greater than the sum of its parts.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
