commentBody: The description calls for "blanching" the broccoli.  That means it should be shocked in cold water after boiling to stop the cooking process.  I think the recipe missed that step.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: What a fresh tasting, tasty, easy salad!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I've made this with organic broccoli florets from Whole Foods, and it works very well. They are already blanched and cut bite-sized, so it makes it very easy.  Just thaw the frozen broccoli on a clean kitchen/bar towel and then blot to get rid of all excess water.  Then toss with the dressing.  I liked it as is, but I added some chili crisp and it was great. I'm thinking of making it again replacing some of the olive oil with sesame oil and sprinkling with toasted sesame seeds.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I put the broccoli in just till the water started reboiling, came out al dente perfect. Definitely a keeper.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: This recipe caught my eye because I have scallions and about a pound of broccoli stalks left over from using the crowns.  I see no reason why I could not shred them and use them instead of crowns.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: We slice the stems and cook them along with the florets - same amount of time - and I prefer them to the florets at this point.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Great tip for shredding fresh ginger from à produce manager at a Safeway on Oahu — freeze the ginger (you can keep a large piece this way) then easy to peel and grate on a Microplane as needed.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I prepare my broccoli this way also for warm salads. It is so hot that most of the water evaporates and it is evenly cooked.  Great recipe!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I loved the al dente broccoli with the miso sauce. It was surprisingly good. I will make this regularly.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Shocking the broccoli in cold water cnacels the effect stated in the recipe: "Blanched broccoli florets are tossed in the vibrant dressing while warm, which helps the vegetable soak up all of the dressing’s tangy flavor. "

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I microwaved the broccoli in the same Pyrex measuring cup I mixed in and stored. Two minutes. Easy as pie. No water accumulated in the bottom. Love the mix of flavors. Next time, I might add garlic and/or soy sauce instead of salt. It might also benefit from finely grated zest.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I would steam the broccoli too and add the salt to the dressing or even leave it out?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: What can I use instead of miso?  allergies...

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody:
@Grit
Coconut aminos or anchovies are my go-tos.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I steamed it too.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: best to steam rather than boil broccoli.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: @rubi … or dream Broccoli…😂

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: turned out great! I subbed thinly chopped red onion for scallions as that's what I had. Nice little touch of color too

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Added just a bit of agave

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I made this but added a char in the bottom of a dutch oven to the broccoli after steaming. This helped drive off the excess water and added a great flavour while keeping the broccoli warm before going into the dressing.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone used this dressing with roasted broccoli instead?  I'd be curious to hear the results, because I prefer roasted florets.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Steve N. Go for it! It will be different than this but delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: A friend showed me how she cooks broccoli ahd I do it this way always now.  Cut into flowerets,  stems also, roll out 3 or 4 pieces of  paper toweling, as many as you need, wet them, wrap broccoli in them, put in the microwave for as many minutes as you want.  2 minutes for me in a recipe like this is plenty.  Of course choose paper towels wisely.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody:
@Diana
I use a silicon microwave dish with a cover and a rack.  Put a little water 💦 in the bottom and vegs on the rack. Cover. Choose time according to vegs. I live at 6000 feet and it works well to brighten broccoli

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Wow this is so good. It’s good when it’s warm, it’s good at room temperature, and it’s good cold. I did add a hefty pinch of chili flake to the dressing as well as a clove of garlic.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Why not just microwave the broccoli?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Used sesame oil instead of olive oil.  Added a yummy Asian flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: So easy, so quick, so delicious.  I steamed the broccoli, and also some chopped up garlic scape (because I could!).  Yum.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Best to serve soon after preparing as the broccoli color turns yellow in a couple hours.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: If you make it ahead of time - 4-6 hours - you can skip cooking the broccoli. The oil and salt will tenderize it, leaving it bright green with a tender snap.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody:
DOUBLE THE SAUCE! Oh this sauce is SO good! So simple but so addictive. I love the way the broccoli soaked it in. I did not, however, rest it on paper towels as directed. So I found there was quite a bit of water in the bottom of my bowl at the end of stirring. I will do this step next time.

But getting back to the SAUCE!! I made a side of seared fish to go with this salad for a quick dinner and used the extra sauce as a marinade and topping for the fish and it was outstanding! (Just an extra squeeze or two of lemon for the fish once it was on the plate, next to a huge portion of broccoli). The sauce brought everything together so well! My boyfriend and I ate the entire salad along with the fish in one sitting!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: A stunningly good recipe, deceptive in its complexity. I ended up with a small bit of excess liquid in the bowl hours later - so I added a drained can of chickpeas to the broccoli and juice, which was just perfect to make for a full meal.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I can never manage to whisk miso "until well combined" -- there are always little beads of miso remaining. Solution? Use a stick blender! Perfectly smooth dressing in five seconds. As others suggested, I microwaved the broccoli and froze the ginger before microplaning. Excellent ideas.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: We love this recipe. I have been sharing with friends and family.  Super easy and delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Yes, as someone mentioned, the finished dish will fade in color over not a very long time period.  Also, I was heavy handed on the lemon juice and don’t recommend.  Of course, quality broccoli goes a long way.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: To uncomplicate things, grate ginger with a coarse grater. Fine graters take too long.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: When this calls for miso, is it miso powder, paste, liquid?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Pixi The directions state to whisk the dressing mashing the miso until the miso dissolves, so I would use miso paste.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody:
@Pixi
Google “miso”.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Perhaps roast the broccoli in the oven?  my favorite way to cook it, actually, and you could even use frozen broccoli in that case.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: How would roasted broccoli be with this recipe. TIA

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: What can you substitute for the miso?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Do you have a glutenfree substitute for miso?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Christina Hikari Organic White Miso is certified gluten-free. It’s fermentation base is made with rice, not wheat or barley. It also happens to be a brand that is highly recommended and used by chefs, so you can use it with confidence. It’s available at most grocery stores where you would find miso paste and also on Amazon. Now you can enjoy a great quality white miso paste that is also gluten-free!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Could there be a substitute for white miso? Don't ever use it, don't want to buy it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Anne Wallace I use white miso all the time— there are a lot on this site that use it. See if any appeal to you. Trader Joes sells it in a pouch so it’s not that much (compared to Asian supermarkets) to give it a go.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: This is an Asian inspired dish, and no Asian worth their salt uses olive oil to cook with. It’s expensive and will flavor the oil unnaturally as compared to non-flavored oils.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I can’t eat ginger. Any ideas for a substitute?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Dale garlic

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Can this be made with frozen broccoli florets?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Susan, yes, I used frozen broccoli florets which were already conveniently cut smaller. I steamed them then took off the lid for a few minutes while I finished the dressing, which allowed extra moisture to evaporate, no need to mess with a towel.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Soooo good!! Delicious exactly as written. I didn’t feel the need to add any salt or pepper.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Really delicious especially with farm-fresh broccoli. I'll make this again and again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: what about just making it with uncooked broccoli?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Didn't have any miso, so used a tablespoon of tahini and soy sauce to taste -- yummy!  That said, will buy one of those miso packets at Trader Joe's and give it another go. I have high hopes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I made this and it is delicious. To speed up prep, I whirred the ginger, miso, lemon juice and olive oil in my mini food processor. I chilled it for 5-6 hours and had it for dinner with some carmel pork chops. I was out of scallions and it was great anyway - it will be even better with them next time. Chilled its perfect for a hot summer night.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody:
I roast broccoli just for a few moments with a little olive oil prior to using it in salads and it works 
wonderfully.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Doron Narkiss brilliant! I've never been sure when you're supposed to start the timer..

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious. And so fast made as written
I will try the roasted broccoli suggestion in the winter

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: For those of you who prefer the boiling water method, I have 2 “tricks” I use after boiling and draining. The first is to place the broccoli in a large, clean kitchen towel and whirl it about.  A surprising amount of water is released into the towel.  The second is to use a decent salad spinner, which gives the same centrifugal force.  I find the towel works best, but hold onto to it or risk flying broccoli.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: Our family LOVED this. We had it on the side with stake and potatoes and it was the absolute perfect summer meal! Will be an ongoing staple. Side note--- I added radishes and carrots and let those marinate overnight then added the broccoli next day with the steaks!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I do a lot of wok cooking and rely on Woks of Life website. They consistently recommend cooking broccoli briefly in boiling water before stir frying. The method works to keep the broccoli crisp tender.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: It was good I thought but not great. I did add corn fresh off the cob to add a bit more variety to it which worked out well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was so tasty! I added white beans and a fried egg on top and some chile flakes and it was the perfect light dinner. Will definitely make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe almost exactly. Except we used what we had which was "Hacho miso" in the fridge which is more of a dark color but it was absolutely delicious. I steamed the broccoli. Super easy with a great flavor profile. I eat broccoli a lot and can see why this recipe lends itself to a steamed broccoli vs. roasted. But thats just me. I liked that the author gave suggestions on how to serve next day. I will make this again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: How about frozen and thawed broccoli florets in a pinch?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody:
Outstanding! 
I used 3 TB EVOO/1 TB roasted sesame oil & tossed w/ 1/2 C toasted slivered almonds alongside salt & pepper shrimp. Reserved 2 scoops broccoli & tossed w/ toasted walnuts & snow peas for next-day lunch. Also fab.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious! Nice and green and fresh. Added some chopped cucumbers since I didn’t have enough broccoli on hand. I thought afterwards that fresh English peas might be a nice textural addition as well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I do not understand all of the positive comments. I was really looking forward to this salad and followed the recipe to a T. The result was a mushy, wet, gray slop that I could not eat. Yes, I drained the cooked broccoli well on paper towels for even longer than the recommended time. It’s a good thing my partner likes broccoli in any form because he has committed to eating it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
commentBody: I added asparagus tips and broccolini that I had as well as some chopped parsley.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I had all the filling ingredients so I thought why not? I didn't have wonton wrappers and I was too lazy to make empanada dough. I confess I used premade piecrust rolled out extra thin and cut circles. Then I brushed them with egg yolk, and baked them. Turned out delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
@lyn
!!! I doubt I would have had the nerve to try pie dough (unless I was under the influence of an edible), but RESPECT! Taste is everything, yes?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I love the sound of this recipe: it fuses Asian, Eastern European, Irish influences to deliver something divine. I made pierogies once, with leek and goat cheese (in lieu of onion and low fat cottage cheese) and it turned out blah. I now know what I needed: lovely sweet cabbage and crunchy sauerkraut, and dill. I am braving a freezing rain to get me some wanton wrappers. Happy New Year everybody. May it be better than the last!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone done a veganized version?  I’m thinking of nutritional yeast, tahini and a little of the sauerkraut juice to replace the cheese. Maybe a touch of white miso and grainy mustard.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Rachel I think that could work. I think I would consider hummus or thinning the tahini a bit.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I don't think you need to replace the cheese. It will still be good without it, and introducing a new flavor like tahini or mustard will make it whole other thing.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Christine I agree. I tasted as I went along and the filling was amazing.. Maybe some vegan butter to hold it together but I think tahini or hummus would detract from the leek/dill/potato/kraut deliciousness

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I'm not into making dumplings but the filling ingredients sounded so amazing so I just diced and boiled the potatoes without mashing, and mixed them with the cabbage ingredients. Fantastic side dish!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Long time listener, first time caller. These were delicious. I used green cabbage because the store was out of Napa. I roughly chopped the leeks and cabbage and pulsed each in the food processor. I did the same with the potatoes once they were cooled. I added a teaspoon of red pepper flakes to the dumpling mixture for a little kick. Finally, I was too lazy to make the dumplings so I sprayed the wontons with cooking spray and put them on mini muffin tins before adding the dumpling mixture. I baked them at 400 for ten minutes and served them with a dip made with Greek yogurt, fresh chopped dill, and a pinch of salt. 😋

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Micki B this was great!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Any ideas about a gluten free way to make these or any other dumplings?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: What about using spring roll wrappers? They are gluten free.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: There are a lot of gluten-free recipes for dumpling dough floating around. You could make these with a more "perogy" style dough and shape and they'd be excellent!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Joanie spring roll wrappers are not gluten free by default. You may be able to find some that at are GF though.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: A big hit. In case this is helpful for others planning ahead, we made these, boiled them all and cooled them on sheet pans. Then, right before dinner, we fried them crisp in butter in two large sauté pans, holding them in a warm oven until all were done. Sprinkled generously with dill and they were delish. I think a little Greek yogurt or sour cream would have gilded the lily in a nice way.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Never, ever mix the potatoes and cheese mix with the cabbage and sauerkraut mix. And don't use napa cabbage, it's way too tough, use regular green cabbage.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Stephen G this is a bizarre comment, you give no reason not to mix. My mixture turned out beautiful and tasty as per instructions

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Stephen G please share why you recommend not to mix ! Thank you!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: there is no potato and cheese mixture in this recipe, just mashed potatoes to which you add cabbages and cheeses at the end. even if it were, I'd mix them without a second thought, as I have many a time before in different recipes, to no ill effect.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: i hate sauerkraut and dill and I may still make these!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @mll I also don’t like those ingredients.  Can you think of any substitutions?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Susan these would be delicious with kimchi instead of saurkraut. Preserved lemons could also be an unusual and punchy replacement!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I love that recipe but I’ve never eaten butter and never use it I find that it’s not necessary in this recipe and they are wonderful dumplings I use avacado oil thank you for sharing this recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I'm wondering about the technique for cooking the potatoes.  Peeling them raw, dicing them, boiling them, and then draining them is never a technique I would use to cook potatoes.  Peeling potatoes raw removes their most nutritious part, which is the layer of flesh directly under the skin.  And then, boiling them in water pre-diced and draining them removes flavor and even more of the nutrition.  Is it done this way to achieve a certain texture?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Exciting to see a polish-influenced vegetarian dumpling! Filling was great, but next time I would reduce the dill and add a little more sauerkraut. Turns out wonton wrappers have a lot of options at the store - I went with "thin", but in the video it looks like she might have used a medium thickness, which I would consider using next time to give them a bit more sturdiness. Couldn't help but add a dollop of sour cream for serving. Served as a main with roasted carrots.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone used simple green cabbage?  I find myself with a huge supply of it and need some ideas for using it.  Too lazy to make sauerkraut or kimchee!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Greta I used savoy cabbage and they went down a treat. I imagine it will be the same for green cabbage!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
This has to be one of my favourite NYT recipes so far. My Polish friend dared to say they were like a superior version of pierogi.
We formed the dumplings all together before a group dinner which was a fun activity for all ages

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I liked these, went super well with a pot of borscht. But I had problems with many of the dumplings falling apart. I got wonton wrappers and they looked good, but as they boiled they started leaking and some outright fell apart. Anyone else have any hot tips? I’ll try keeping them at a simmer next time I guess?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Agree that these were good. I was eating the filling out of the bowl as I wrapped. I used extra sharp cheddar in place of the Swiss. Honestly I don't think the cheese is really necessary, though sure it's good. But don't be afraid to omit it if you avoid cheese (but I'd hate to not top these with butter and sour cream!)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I’m so fortunate that we have a pierogi store with both savory and sweet,  but I might try making these on a weekend.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I think I willl make this as a side dish with no wrapper.  Any thoughts?  I dont have so much time to wrap.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: this was absolutely delicious! it definitely took pre-planning and I had to watch a youtube video to understand the wonton fold, but totally totally worth the effort.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Loved this recipe and the dumplings are even better the next day cold.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: These were relatively easy to make and had a really nice mix of flavors. Froze about 15 and have had them both boiled and pan fried. Both were excellent.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: so bland, not interesting at all. and way too much work.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Excellent directions and I was very grateful for the video.  Dumpling have great flavor!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Made the filling as written and thought it was delicious. It seemed perfectly suited to pelmeni, and since I couldn't find wonton wrappers without various unpronounceable ingredients, I made pelmeni dough instead. Using this recipe: https://www.pantsdownapronson.com/pelmeni-recipe/#recipe and 10g of filling (about 2 tsp.; I have a #100 cookie scoop) I got about 100 pelmeni, using setting #5 on my pasta machine, and re-rolling the dough. Planning to serve with butter and sour cream...classic!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
commentBody: These dumplings are absolutely fabulous. I will be making again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fiery Vindaloo Chili Recipe
commentBody: If I were to use powdered Kashmiri chili, how much should I sub for the whole chilis?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fiery Vindaloo Chili Recipe
commentBody: That cinnamon stick is going to blendable? I feel like there will be splinters in the mix. Tell me I’m wrong

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fiery Vindaloo Chili Recipe
commentBody: @Betsy Marks you don’t need a lot of it even a tiny piece is fine and skip the cardamom. The main flavor to accentuate is garlic as Vindalho originated from Portugal where it’s called Carne de vinha d'alhos ("meat in wine and garlic"). It’s not an aromatic dish like many North Indian dishes. It was brought to Goa by Portuguese sailors and modified to make it spicier to preserve it better. We rarely use cardamon in our food  except in desserts where it’s quite predominant.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fiery Vindaloo Chili Recipe
commentBody: @Helga do Rosario Gomes the British added cardamom in their version during the British Raj. After the British took over Goa from the Portuguese, they also took over all the cooks and used (stole) the recipes as well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fiery Vindaloo Chili Recipe
commentBody:
@Betsy Marks
Most recipes will remind us to remove the cinnamon stick. It’s already imparted its flavor during cooking.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fiery Vindaloo Chili Recipe
commentBody: You could serve on a bed of lettuces for a salad or on tortilla chips for nachos. A change from the usual rice/roti/naan.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fiery Vindaloo Chili Recipe
commentBody: This sauce goes well with plant based meat substitutes. I liked beans and tempeh combo for texture, and added a beef bouillon cube in case.  Don’t judge please: i am heart healthy, not ethical vegan. Sauce is medium hot not fiery. Worth the depth of flavor of whole spices but powder or paste is still a nice variant on southwest standard chili, a staple with us.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fiery Vindaloo Chili Recipe
commentBody: I wonder if this could be made in a slow cooker?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fiery Vindaloo Chili Recipe
commentBody: Seeing "fiery" in the title, I went with caution, emptied the seeds from the Kashmiri chili pods and skipped the Thai chilis. The result was a tasty dish with essentially no heat.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fiery Vindaloo Chili Recipe
commentBody: I used guajillo chiles as they were easy for me to get - is the recipe incorrect?  I tend to like spicy food, and I read 'fiery vindaloo' as something like a 4 or 5 spice level on a scale of 1-5.  Using 3 guajilo chiles, I ended up around a 2 on that same relative scale.  Should it be '8' guajillo chiles?  Or should it really be Kashmiri or nothing?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fiery Vindaloo Chili Recipe
commentBody: CJ, the recipe does specify a bird's eye chili, which is there for the heat (shouldn't be "optional" in a "fiery" dish!). Kashmiri are quite mild, and Guajillo not not much hotter, but both will contribute colour and flavor, which the bird's eye won't. Or use a finely minced habanero instead of the bird's eye.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fiery Vindaloo Chili Recipe
commentBody: Can a couple of tablespoons of premixed vindaloo spice powder be substituted for step 1?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fiery Vindaloo Chili Recipe
commentBody: I had a box of MDH Kashmiri Mirch that I used about 3 tsp (left out the birds eye chili since I didn't have it) and it was a good solid but not overpowering heat.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fiery Vindaloo Chili Recipe
commentBody: I just noticed that 1 small tomato chopped should be a cup? That sounds….impossible.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty! Made as written, except I only used 2 salmon fillets (as I was cooking for two people). There was more sauce than needed for 2 fillets, but the amount of cabbage was just right. If making for 4 people, I’d double the cabbage and add maybe just 1 more tablespoon of butter.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Used 2lb salmon and just cut into filets rather than cubes. Used reg green cabbage instead of Napa. Doubled sauce. Quick and delicious. And one garlic clove? Come on now. That’s not a real measurement. 1/2 to whole bulb.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: @APyou had me at 1/2 to whole bulb.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: @AP agreed. 1 clove is a rounding error.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: @AP agreed and added a couple of tbsp of cream to the sauce. Perfection!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Legit, this is delicious! While carmelizing cabbage (admittedly plus a rough-chopped onion, and minced garlic, which I add to basically everything, I chose to add sauce ingredients to THAT pan; I used my Air Fryer to make perfect salmon (legit, I'm a novice with an Air Fryer but it is THE fish method! No turning, no checking, perfect and juicy every. single. time. Just adjust for weight!!! #perfect). Okay, now that I'm off my pedestal (lol), It's really so, so good!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: The air fryer is the absolute best way to cook salmon! I don't eat salmon at restaurants any longer because it just doesn't compare to air-fried. I'm always on the lookout for great sauces to go with it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: @~Chrissie O.  We always grill salmon but at -2 here in Minneapolis it's a suboptimal method. I'm willing to become an air-fried salmon convert. Can you give me a quick

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: @Jan M brown sugar and Dijon mustard.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Can anyone comment why the salmon appears seared in photo but is steamed in recipe? Thanks!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: @KMR unfortunately, food magazines use stylists to cook dishes for visual appeal rather than what the dish looks like. It’s why videos with recipes are such a boon, as you can see what it’s supposed to look like.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This was super! I used regular cabbage but otherwise made as written. I was surprised but the salmon really did only take seven minutes of steaming. It didn't look done to me, but when I put a finger on it, it sprang back. Also, instead of S&P, I used Penzey's Outrage of Love which is kind of a spicy lemon pepper salt blend. It was delicious on this! The cabbage was cooked perfectly; carmelized, tender, but still a bit toothsome. It's in the monthly rotation for sure!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Finish cooking the salmon, then another 5-6 minutes to prepare the piccata sauce.....doesn't the fish cook significantly in this time?  (My husband is a fiend about having food served hot!)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: @Deborah You could partially cook salmon and then add at end to warm it up. That's what I did.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Really enjoyed this! I added some chicken stock w/ the water to add more flavor. Would probably be good with a little bit of a white wine reduction too.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: We cooked this just as described (using a coleslaw bag though) and it was delicious. Cabbage gives this recipe a great flavor. This is a wonderful re.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: The whole family loved this. I took note of comments and cooked my salmon in a skillet as the cabbage cooked, then chopped it and added it at the end. I used Dijon, and I forgot the chives, but this was great anyway. For those having trouble with the cabbage boiling, please note that on my stove, I had to go up to 8 to get a good golden char on the cabbage. At this temperature, I think the liquid must have cooked off immediately because all of the cabbage ended up golden and caramelized.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Any idea why this specifies a skillet with a lid? I don't see anywhere in the recipe where you're instructed to cover the dish, perhaps I am overlooking?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: In step 2, second sentence,  it says to cover.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: @Jenn Hi! In step 2, it says “ Arrange fish on top of cabbage in a single layer, cover and reduce heat to low.”

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Instructions to cover the skillet are under step 2, after you add the salmon atop the par-cooked cabbage.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This was really good, and easy too. I didn't have cabbage so I used kale and it worked well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Excellent.  Served with Trader Joe’s cauliflower gnocchi.  I’ll probably double the sauce to out on the gnocchi next time.  I did use chicken broth instead of water and a splash of white wine.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: delicious all the way

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I have been stuck in the house in NC for 4 days and came across this recipe yesterday. I just so happened to have all these ingredients on hand. I added frozen peas because I am in my 'frozen peas for protein' era, and made the recipe exactly as written.  Delicious! Thank you for a special dinner on day 4 of being stuck at home.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: How does the salmon look browned in the picture if you are steaming it?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Tastes great! Followed the recipe as written. I didn't have the hot mustard so, Dijon it was and I served it with steamed rice. It's so easy to eat it all up... like comfort food. Another appealing thing about this recipe is that it was actually ready in the 30 minutes it's supposed to take.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: It’s just the two of us & frequently cook with leftovers in mind. I used 1.5 lbs salmon & half a head of Napa cabbage, served with quinoa. The leftovers reheated beautifully in the InstantPot (18 mins, steam setting with leftover quinoa in an open aluminum foil packet, nestled on top). Absolutely delicious recipe that will be in regular rotation. The only change I’d recommend is to use the entire head of cabbage

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
After reading a lot of reviews, i decided to follow the directions as written, with one exception. I used whine wine in the sauce vs water. Warning, alcohol evaporates very quickly and the sauce gets really dry Fast. I loved the flavor vs water, but either use more wine or have a cup of water handy to thin the sauce. (Don't thin with more wine as the alcohol will not burn off in time). 
Overall, delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This was just OK. The recipe doesn't mention how much liquid comes out of the napa cabbage when it cooks - I had to drain a bunch off before I made the sauce (or maybe I should have used it in lieu of the 1/2 cup water?).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this with regular green cabbage and Napa cabbage. The Napa cabbage came out soggy and didn’t brown up as well. I much preferred the green cabbage.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: OMG, so delicious! I love reading through the notes from my fellow home chefs and their suggestions. Wine and some chicken stock for the sauce, it was divine! I added a chopped onion and minced a handful of garlic cloves with the cabbage. I always have capers in my pantry but alas I had used the last I had in the tuna fish I made for lunch, so I substituted delicious small green olives my husband has bought at our Italian market. I am looking forward to leftovers for lunch!! Wil make this often!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
I eat olives out of the jar. My boyfriend refuses to eat them. I loved this recipe. My boyfriend did not. If you’re cooking for someone who can’t deal with brine, this may not be the recipe for them!

That said, loved how simple and easy this was. I used green cabbage and it worked well. And I did a little less butter. (I also only did 2 salmon filets).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I love the components of this dish but thought that steaming the cabbage and the salmon was not quite right. I will make again broken apart and then add all together.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Any way to make this with less butter?  This is a Tablespoon per person.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Substitute olive oil in step 1 for the cabbage cooking. Keep the butter for the sauce step (step 3)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: The role of the butter here is to help thicken the sauce and temper the piquancy of the mustard and lemon. So if you're like me and don't enjoy fatty sauces (or if you're trying to reduce fat/calories), you need to replace the butter with something else that satisfies the same functions--a bit of cornstarch slurry, a spoonful of mild yogurt, a tablespoon of apricot jam, etc.--or just omit the butter for a thinner, tangier sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: @Vivek I was thinking of doing that!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Could you swap the cabbage for eg bok choy?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: @Jenn Step 2 has you cover the pan after adding the salmon to the cabbage.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I have baby bok choy- can I use that instead of cabbage?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: @evie made it today with bok choy! Excellent!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
Took way longer than 30 minutes to prep and cook.  After steaming fish and cabbage there was a fair amount of liquid in pan and there were no brown bits to stir up.  I used rosé instead of water, Dijon (since we always have Dijon) instead of Colman's, and bottled lemon juice.  Served with wild rice because that's what my wife asked for.  

Next time, a regular (not non-stick) pan, and less mustard.  And skinless salmon because taking the skin off those filets was a real pain).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: So delish! I actually used a bag of frozen surimi that my husband picked up in error, instead of crab legs. When I saw this recipe, I thought, why not? The sweetness of the surimi paired perfectly with the tangy sauce. Served on a bed of 50-50 jasmine rice-riced cauliflower. Didn’t have Napa cabbage on hand but a big ole head of green cabbage was a good sub. Adding to our rotation of comfort dishes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Meh!  Nothing special.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Addition of a shallot and a lot of extra garlic to the cabbage, as others have suggested, is good. I also found the addition of about 2 tsp of honey to the sauce to be tasty, just a hint of sweetness for balance.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Great weeknight elegant main course plus veg!  Easy prep.  All ingredients on hand except for fresh fish to buy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Meh. There are so many better things you can do with your salmon. Flavors neither came together nor played in nice counterpoint.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious! Not quick. 45 minutes to prep and cook.

Made dinner for two, so based on other comments, I cooked half the amount of cabbage and salmon, but full amount of sauce. I can't imagine serving less sauce! 

Had only large skin-on salmon fillet, so cut large serving size slices. Cooked the salmon atop cabbage skin side up. It was easier to peel the skin off after salmon was cooked. Used standard white cabbage. 4 medium garlic cloves. Served with white rice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I had to look up Napa Cabbage. Never tried it before. How is the flavor different from other cabbage? Where else would I use it?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: @Joan Pachner. It’s more tender than regular green cabbage and the flavor is more delicate. You can use it in soup or as a substitute for green cabbage in most recipes. It is often found in Chinese cuisine.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t read closely enough and used 2T Dijon mustard. Way too much and we like mustard. Be careful out there.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Had to use regular (probably not the right word!) cabbage and I couldn’t get any color on it. No worries. It’s easy and very, very good. Definitely going in my cook again folder

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Yummy!  Used regular green cabbage. Would have enjoyed a bit more sauce - but it was delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely loved this, and so easy. I used green cabbage as I had a quarter head I needed to get rid of, so I let it sear for a bit longer. Served with orecchiette and peas - yum!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: My husband doesn’t like salmon but said this is delicious. I used a tiny bit of butter in the sauce. And I forgot to make orzo but it was fine with just the cabbage. Will make again. Super easy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I used regular cabbage.  I cooked the salmon filet, whole, on the cabbage and chunked it after cooking. Easy. Delicious recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Easy and excellent. I used vermouth instead of water, but white wine would also be great.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: The sauce is good.  But steamed fish is jut not.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This is a solid weeknight recipe that comes together quickly.  I will definitely make this again.  I also adjusted the recipe for two pieces of salmon easily.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
Meh. 

Love salmon. Live in the PNW, so get it fresh and always wild. Love cabbage and all other ingredients too. Followed the recipe to a T. It was OK, but nothing to repeat.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Delicious weeknight dinner. Funny how the sauce changes everything so I suggest at least doubling the sauce, probably more if you want it to flavor the orzo (what I used) or rice. Used green cabbage and Dijon mustard.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I disliked the sauce, it just did not pair well. The salmon and cabbage were fabulous though

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Added a full bunch of asparagus chopped into 2 inch pieces to the cabbage. Salmon took maybe two extra minutes than state cook time. Served with garlicky orzo. Sauce is delicious. My husband and kids gobbled up the meal and it will stay in the repertoire.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: I'm astounded - 1280 gms of sodium per serving???  I looked at the ingredients and am stumped at how that can be.  Is most of that in the canned lentils? Or are they using salt as seasoning with a very heavy hand?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: This also caught my attention. I really wish the NYT would include specific information about how the sodium is calculated in the nutrition information. I end up doing my own calculations if the recipe looks like something I would enjoy, and the stated sodium content seems high.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Jane Milligrams*

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody:
Yes, very strange sodium amount. I've never used canned lentils since cooking puy or black lentils and then freezing in freezer ziplocks for future use is so easy.
Also, 2 Tablespoons of oil for small cubes of salmon cooked 5 minutes over Medium heat, then steamed for two minutes, seems unnecessary. I'll go for half that.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was wonderful. Cooked my own lentils and only had spinach-arugula mix but otherwise followed recipe pretty closely. Tasty and healthy — a definite keeper.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: The zucchini is not doing much, I’d skip it next time. Would add a mustard vinaigrette for more flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: @W1ndowMaker Agree — the grated zucchini was completely lost in this recipe. I’ll include it next time because it’s a veg, but I’ll cut it up into chunks.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: @W1ndowMaker totally agree that this recipe does not need the zucchini!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Will try to make even easier with tinned sardines. Use oil from tin to cook garlic, then warm the spinach, lemon, maybe a bit of chopped fruit, then off stove, add canned beans, veg, seasoning, herbs. Seems like a very versatile formula.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Appreciated the suggestion of adding a honey mustard vinaigrette as it really added a great flavor. Also,  I just cut the zucchini in quarter inch half moons. Grating it too much work and mushy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Canned lentils are very high in sodium. You can get low, or no, sodium versions and washing them helps a lot but the best option is too cook your own. Lentils are naturally low in sodium

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: I made this as written. It’s not bad but it’s underwhelming. It desperately needs some kind of dressing since the lemon juice and olive oil here just isn’t enough. The next time I will add a mustard vinaigrette perhaps, as mustard goes well with lentils.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Really good. I used baby bok choi instead of spinach and added a little ginger with the garlic. Like others I used dried lentils instead of canned.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody:
"Cook, turning occasionally, until fish is lightly golden in spots, 5 minutes. " 
This is a small thing, but I do think it can make a difference for us home cooks. 
In restaurant kitchens, when we cooked proteins like salmon, chicken, steaks, etc. - we only turned the item being cooked once. We tend to poke and prod food too much. Put it down in the pan, "let it cook" and turn it once at the end. Especially, with salmon. No need to flip it multiple times.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent - healthy and very flavourful. Do  prepare all your ingredients at the start. The recipe comes together very quickly.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: @JM Totally agree !

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: I read the other comments first, so I used only one can of lentils to cut down on the portions. I cooked the filet as one piece with skin on, high heat and lots of olive oil, salt, and pepper. Skin was easy to take off once it was 80% cooked through. I sautéed the zucchini and cut into thin slices only. Added everything else in the instructed order. The zucchini was soft but not mushy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t have dill so I used mint and it was lovely! This recipe is easy and healthy. I will be making this again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Amanda yes! I used mint and cilantro

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: The flavors in this work far better together than one would expect. Followed the recipe in terms of ingredients. Made my own green lentils. My salmon came with skin on so I baked it (another pan) but using the guidance of step 1. Then cut the cooked salmon. Far easier to wash a pan than skinning and cutting into cubes while raw.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Jeff I loved having the salmon skin releasing those fish oils into the whole dish .

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Hi I made this and agree that the zucchini added nothing and had a mushy texture despite squeezing out the liquid. Also before adding the lentils, etc, I removed the salmon and tossed the lentils, folding it all in. After that I divided into bowls and divided the salmon in the top.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Mimi I feel that adding the salmon to the main dish and incorporating the flavors together gets that terrific fish oil onto all of the components especially the spinach .

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: I bought canned lentils but the texture was so mushy I ditched them in favor of cooking dried. They cook easily, quickly, and you can control the texture.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I cooked my own lentils. It’s so easy, why not? This will serve more than 4. I made a half recipe, and my husband and I will get another meal from it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Not 'salad' for us; salmon in air fryer (!), dried lentils in broth. Prepped rest while lentils were cooking. Quartered zucchini in 1/4" rounds. Baby spinach mix wilted. Rather than waste the whole thing, blistered tomatoes, added ginger/jalapeno/cilantro, then a can of coconut milk plus curry spices. Turned it into a lentil-salmon curry with zucchini, tomato, spinach. Over Basmati rice? Delish! 4 stars tho DRASTIC changes cuz base ing were stupid-good together!! Will repeat! Yum!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: The flavor combination was very tasty, and this was a satisfying dinner. However, next time I'll cook the salmon, remove it, continue with recipe, and then add salmon back in. Also think it would be great with uncut salmon - cook whole portions per recipe and use the rest of the recipe as a base for it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is good, but as others have noted, the addition of some dijon mustard (maybe 1 1/2 Tb) really adds some punch.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody:
This was a filling and delicious meal!
My recommendations:
- Use dried lentils and soak them the night before to reduce sodium content
- Cut the zucchini into thin slices instead of grating for more crunch

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Agree with all the comments about the zucchini not adding much and the fact that it needs more acid/bite. Next time I will add either a mustard or a balsamic vinaigrette as the last step and I think then it will be a really good dish instead of just an ok one. PS my husband sprinkled some balsamic vinaigrette on his second helping and it was night and day!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Never heard of canned lentils so I bought it in a bag. I couldn't find salmon without skin so bought it and cut the skin off. I had this really nice lemon dressing so used that instead of squeezed lemons.  It was so tasty.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: It was expensive to make due to the salmon, I also had to skin the salmon and debone it (tedious.)  It makes a lot, so I'm going to try freezing the remainder, but I won't make it again.  Bland in flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Cassandra Costco bagged salmon . And I think the skin adds to the flavor so I don’t  take it out . Between the fish oil and ginger you can feel this finished with a little personal soy sauce at the table !

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: This recipe looks right up my alley, but when I checked the nutritional information, it said it was 723 cal per serving. How can that be? It’s basically vegetables, lentils and salmon. Appreciate any thoughts

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: I made this on a whim as I had dried lentils in the cabinet and a big freezer bag of salmon from Costco and it was a weeknight winner ! Easy Easy and proportions and seasoning are flexible . Now this is an easy weeknight one..or two depending on if you cook your beans from dry …pot dinner .

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody:
Okay, I am a luddite in the kitchen and only try easy recipes.

This was a great one for me, easy as pie. Easier than pie, actually.

My only comment was that just using the "times allotted" in the recipe, the salmon didn't really get done through and through. I had to keep the salmon -- and the spinach -- on a super low heat to make sure the salmon was fully cooked but the spinach didn't start to get cooked down/reduced. 

In the future I'll take more time with the salmon.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made this yesterday, Saturday. More of a one pot for me than a salad but a good recipe. I struggled getting the skin off w/o wasting meat. Wanted to pass along a tip if you only have skin-on salmon. Just saw it this morning on YouTube. Put the salmon in a strainer skin up. Slowly pour boiling water over it. The skin will tighten and pull up and then pull off easily!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was just ... meh. It doesn't know what it wants to be. A salad? A lentil bowl? Hot? Cold? It just ended up somewhere in between. Thank goodness I followed the suggestion to add a mustard vinaigrette, which helped. On a positive note, it packs a protein punch for people like me who don't eat any meat except fish. But I won't make this one again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: does anyone really cook with canned lentils? they're so easy to prepare.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: cooked my own lentils (1 cup dry) also added asparagus to the lentils about 20 mins in to about 30 mins cook in salted water.  was kind of plain overall, though very fresh - will add some Dijon when I eat the leftovers as other have said.  question: I couldn't taste the scallions at all - anyone else have that issue??

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: With a little tweaking of seasoning and lentils (i.e., Trader Joe’s), this dish was simply superb!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Loved this.  After I turned over the salmon I added all the scallions and shredded zucchini and let it steam. Had it as a warm dish and it was delicious.  BTW they make a shredding tool that looks like a potato peeler but shreds.  Best tool ever for shredding carrots, zucchini etc...

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe does not rinse the canned lentils after draining.  I would never buy beans or lentils canned with so much sodium.  
I generally make my own lentils with much less salt and more aromatics, herbs and seasonings other than salt.   Make a big pot of lentils, peas or beans over the weekend and use in various ways for quick weeknight meals.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Recommendations - 1 1/2 pounds of fish in the Dutch oven to cook all at once is too much. Ends up steaming, not browning. Cook in batches. Cook your own dry lentils - takes about 20 minutes - to cut down on the high sodium in canned lentils. I wanted more than 4 oz. of tomato. A splash of red wine vinegar at the end along with the lemon doesn't hurt. Add a bit of nonfat Greek yogurt with lemon juice and dill on top for serving. Actually better chilled the next day.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Agreed!  I used one pound of fish and it was plenty!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Could not find canned lentils and made them separately from dried - kind of changed the texture from what was expected I think.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Am I missing something? So does half the lemon zest go into the vinaigrette? It says add the other half

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: there is no lemon zest. 1 tbs is drizzled on salmon (step 2) and the remaining lemon juice is added in step 3

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: I’m thinking you could instantly upgrade this recipe (avoiding soggy canned lentils, chopping salmon, etc) by adapting these ingredients to the simple technique described in the “Warm Rice Salad with Salmon and Arugula”.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: I cannot find canned lentils at any grocery, only lentil soup. Is that what the recipe is referring to?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Today I learned lentils have to be cooked if you don’t get canned ones! Tried to eat this and almost broke a tooth on a lentil. I had never cooked with lentils before and checked the recipe multiple times to see if any prep was needed for them.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: If the recipe calls for canned lentils, but it's "totally fine if the salmon flakes apart" then why not use canned product for both?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Grating the zucchini is overkill, makes it watery and mush. It was an interesting luke warm dinner

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: I used Trader Joe's refrigerated lentils. I would not recommend because it turned out mushy and the lentils overpower the other flavors.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody:
This was ok — but will definitely do this with dried lentils rather than canned next time. I had a checkup the day after eating this and my blood pressure was up! Waaaay too much sodium. I drained and rinsed the canned lentils too. I would really like it if NYT recipes paid more attention to the nutritional value of food and recommended lower sodium/lower fat alternatives within each recipe so people without a ton of experience adapting recipes could make their versions healthier if they wanted to.

Also, I don’t like dill, so I left it out and it was very bland. I’ll probably add basil next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody:
Ron
The nutritional data stated 1280 milligrams per portion, so only 1.28 grams.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody:
@Jane,
I was surprised at the 1280 mg sodium per serving as well. I think it will largely depend on the particular can of lentils. Some are much lower in sodium than others. The cans are labeled as having 3.5 servings per can, and this recipe uses 2 cans, for 4 servings, so you'd have to multiply the label sodium amount by about 2. So i would buy reduced sodium cans of lentils, and would also rinse the lentils to minimize the sodium from the liquid.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Nice presentation, but somewhat bland.  Could have used a lemon vinaigrette.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful whole meal salad. Handy for Lent!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was very good. The next day I added the leftovers to some Chicken Broth to make it into a soup. I added some dill, garlic powder, and pepper and it turned out fantastic with a slight of toasted bread!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: This recipe gets points for being quick and one pot, but we found it seriously lacking in flavor. I think, if a proper dressing was made (instead of a sprinkling of lemon with salt and pepper), this could’ve been much more tasty. Perhaps a vinaigrette with some honey- it needed sweetness

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: It was great. Better to make the lentils yourself.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Yes to Dijon honey vinaigrette!  Didn’t have zuke so used some chopped green beans which added a nice crunch. Oh, and I forgot the bread but didn’t miss it!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: I really enjoyed this! I agree with the comments that cooking dry lentils will yield less sodium.  I used French lentils which also provided great texture.  Also agree with the comment on slicing the zucchini instead of shredding it.  I quarted it lengthwise and sliced it into bite-sized chunks.  The fresh dill added so much flavor, and I didn't feel the need to add mustard as some suggested.  I always add more garlic called for! My wife and I got two dinners and a lunch out of this. So good!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Thanks to the comments I made sure to rinse my canned lentils, and I only used one 19 oz can. With 1.5 lbs of salmon, that was plenty of lentils! Zucchini, tomatoes, dill made this feel very fresh and wholesome. I did add some maple and dijon per recommendations here but they didn't really cohere into a sauce so not sure how much they helped.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: This is so easy and delicious. I think you could double the spinach and skip the zucchini. I have read some of the comments about it being not flavorful enough. I’ve never found that but I am heavy handed on the lemon juice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Finally made it, so glad, it's very good, simple and fast. Cooked puy lentils earlier in the day. Used my U shaped tool to make zucchini matchsticks rather than grate it. Followed other commenters to make dressing w/dijon.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
commentBody: Loved this fish- so healthy and came together quickly. Found it to be a bit bland, so I mixed in some Thai Chile vinegar (thank you, Good Things by Samin Nosrat), and finished it with a dollop of Greek yogurt and olive oil. Will def make it again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Salmon needs way longer to cook - no instruction on raising the temp back up from low.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: @CW yep this. Did 10 min on low and thinking now it’s going to need another 10 on medium but don’t want the rice to scald

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: @CW just lifted the lid to see raw salmon haha came to the comments to say the same thing!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: @CW I rarely cook the fish or shrimp on top of rice, lentils, etc.  I bake it separately in the oven to make sure it is done to my liking.  Also, that way it is easy to remove the skin if there is skin.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Yes. Not “one pot” material! Jasmin Rice in rice cooker. Salmon seared w olive oil, coriander, muscat, cumin salt pepper and lemon juice in pan. Peas cooked separately too.  Zatziki needed lemon juice too. Yum!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Have to agree with the other comments. The fish was still raw and the rice was still hard after the stated cooking time. The directions need some work.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: This would be a skip for me. Made it tonight-was not impressed.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Excellent! Used 1 pound of salmon and half the rice and liquid. All of the coriander! Added a bit of lemon juice to the sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: I made this for dinner tonight. I'm a widow but I made the 4 servings and am freezing 2, one for a couple of days from now.  I think it's quite good. I added more salt and some salt to the cucumber sauce. I would make again, even for company.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody:
the fastest and tastiest preparation ever. Of
course I made changes to suit the family’s preferences : salmon rub for coriander; broccoli for peas; jasmine rice for badmati, and a drizzle of Korean bbq sauce.  Will definitely use prep method for future rice bowls!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: The salmon was barely cooked after 10 minutes over the rice. What did I do wrong?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: @Marjorie same for me!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: @Marjorie You likely did nothing wrong.  Next time, prepare the salmon separately, then add it to the rice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Not my family's favorite. The rice was still crunchy when the salmon was done, and it stuck to the fish making it hard to cook longer. Timing seemed off and the flavors weren't there for us!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: This recipe really does not work. The salmon was still completely raw after 10 minutes. This one was a swing and a miss.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: A tasty and easy recipe, with quick clean-up. The frozen peas stopped the simmer so we temporarily increased three heat. We also had to add a few tablespoons more water to keep the rice from drying out while the salmon finished cooking.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. Incredibly easy to make. Used only 1lb of skinless salmon fillets (salmon is so expensive now-a-days), followed all other directions exactly, all cooking times were spot on. This recipe is definitely going into the rotation. Thank you Kay Chun!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: The salmon was not cooked at all and because it needed to cook longer, the rice was crunchy. Will not make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Bland. Disgusting. Takes much longer than the suggested cooking time. Entire family rejected it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Since many complained that this recipe was not working out, I am responding that it does work, but I needed to leave the salmon in the pot about 5 minutes longer (total cook time 15 minutes). This is a nice mild dish — a novelty! I find that many of the recipes are heavy on spices (spicy hot) and having GERD, I cannot do spicy anymore (sadly). This dish is also gluten free. My only other adaptation was to add chopped scallions on top at the end. Will make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: i don’t know. 1 1/2 lb of salmon is 4 portions, minimum. or 6 portions  using standard dietary guidelines of 1/4 lb or size of deck of cards, per person. but only 1/2 cup frozen peas ? for 4 people ? just reading recipe tells me what other comments state: salmon will be half raw on very low rice-cooking temp. if you raise temp, rice will scorch. i cook salmon and rice, mostly brown, all the time. always separately.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight. Pretty flavorless. Hard pass.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Very disappointing. Love the concept but it was bland. A one-time experiment for me.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Assuming those who said the fish was still raw have never had sushi with salmon.  Salmon, like tuna, can be eaten raw.  Ahi tuna is often served seared with the middle portions just barely cooked.  Same goes for salmon.  Steaming the one-inch cubes for 10 minutes should be sufficient to cook them.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: yeah. but sushi is not just half-raw salmon cooked in pot with rice and yogurt.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Could this be made with canned salmon and a pressure cooker?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: I wouldn't think so.  Canned salmon is already cooked.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: I liked this. Rice and salmon well cooked. Will add more peas and place dill over salmon at end of cooking. Too hard to lift off rice. Didn't like the yogurt sauce. Will not include next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this just in the rice cooker instead of on the stove?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: New way to cook salmon for me. Overall I like this new technique - easy, fast, salmon is tender. Plus one pot! But I wish the recipe gave more guidance about how much salt to use. At first I thought it was pretty bland but then I added more salt and it was much, much better.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Thank for this recipe Kay Chun. I find it a dish with delicate balanced flavors. The second time I prepared it I cooked the rice in fish broth. This gave the dish a bit more umami. Looking forward to your next recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Add a dash of soy sauce instead of salt if you feel it isn’t salty enough. If the salmon is cut in cubes 10 min is plenty to cook it to medium. We liked this a lot and found the prep simple and perfect.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Love this preparation technique, as per my prior salmon review.  Tonight i used halibut (with sesame oil and furikake-like seasoning), and snap peas.  Drizzle of soy sauce for me, Japanese bbq sauce for hubby. 10/10!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody:
Loved it! Salmon was, however, completely raw after 10 minutes, so I put the entire pot in the oven (with the lid on) for 10 more minutes on 180C but just with ventilator and top grill. 
The rice was fluffy, peas were perfect and so was salmon. The yoghurt sauce is a must.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
commentBody: Didn’t have and doggone coriander seed, but subbed some cumin. Loved it. Easy and tasty and light. Great for a weeknight!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
