Personally, I find it easier to cook the meatballs in the oven rather than on the stove. I didn't find the flavor suffered. 15ish minutes at 400 convection setting worked nicely. 
Turning the sauce into a gravy by starting with a roux is also a yummy way to switch things up. 
Tasty, easy, and flexible. Recipe hat trick!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Piccata Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: So delicious these taste like fancy chicken nuggets! We halved the milk and used a whole head of garlic (duh). Served it with broccolli and rice and subbed the sauce for a homemade lemon garlic aoli. Def going to make this again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Piccata Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I have been cooking for 34 years and have excellent cooking equipment and this recipe was an absolute train wreck. Even upping the panko it was unflippable it looked like burnt meatloaf all clumps.  ZERO stars

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Piccata Meatballs Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe was FANTASTIC! 

I made it dairy free by subbing: 
- coconut milk (full fat) for milk
- homemade vegan parmesan for Parmesan 
- high quality olive oil and cornstarch for butter in the sauce to make it all come together. Bone broth makes for a richer sauce too.

The meatballs definitely benefited from staying in the fridge over night since the mixture is quite soft.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Piccata Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: this was SO DELICIOUS! I opted for orzo, which was delightful. I didn't make all 12 meatballs so I had leftover meatballs to prepare for lunch a couple days later... For a keto friendly option, I put the meatballs on top of quinoa this time around, added a heaping scoop of cottage cheese and some sliced cherry tomatoes, with a drizzle of olive oil. It was DELICIOUS. (I'm not sure if tomatoes are keto friendly? Anyway, tried to eliminate extra carbs). So, so, so yummy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Piccata Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This was a bit bland of a dish.  Did some things to jazz it up.  The lemon juice wasn’t enough, so I grated a whole frozen lemon and keep the shavings in the freezer.  Added some of that, much more salt, pepper and a dash of cayenne.  Also cooked noodles to al dente and doubled the sauce.    Added the noodles directly to the sauce and cooked on low for a short time so noodles soaked up sauce a bit.  Added grated Parmesan and stirred quickly to incorporate it into the sauce. Lastly added about a tablespoon of sour cream.  Family loved it after all that.  Good base recipe for me to play with.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Piccata Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Made as written, delicious. I would double the sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Piccata Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I doubled everything except the meatballs and finished off the meatballs back in the pan with the sauce instead of in the oven. Per other comments, I did not use milk and included the zest of the lemon in the meatball mixture, as well as some Dijon mustard. I didn’t measure out the lemon juice for the sauce - just included juice from the half of the lemon I didn’t slice for serving. Chopped extra parsley for serving as well and served alongside roasted broccoli.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Piccata Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Was phenomenal. Read the comments and used half the milk, also mixed everything together for the meatballs at the same time rather than making a panade with the milk and panko. Meatballs ended up holding together beautifully.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: The tahini made the noodles pretty rich, so I put probably a teaspoon of chili oil in my bowl and a ton of cucumbers to cut the creaminess/heaviness and it really helped. The contrast of the cold, crunchy radish and cucumber was necessary for the meal to feel complete for me.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe! Definitely add (I used TJ’s) fried shallots if possible for extra crunch and flavor. I used a large daikon radish and it was delicious but using other radishes makes sense depending on what you like. Very quick and easy to make and fed four well, and could sit on the stove for people eating at different times.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight. Hubs and I were surprised at how tasty it was because the ingredients seemed a bit random upon reading the recipe. We added edamame and bok choy micro greens on top. Delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: Don't discard the ramen seasoning packets! Save them to season some roasted veggies - we especially like with broccoli or cauliflower.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: This was a winner. I really liked the creaminess conferred by the tahini, which in some recipes I find to be too bitter. The bonus with this recipe is that here’s plenty of sauce to throw in extra veggies that you have on hand. Then it becomes a complete one dish meal in no time.  I added julienned carrots, red bell peppers, and cabbage, plus basil and lime juice. I subbed coconut aminos for the sugar, which I do in all Asian recipes that call for sugar.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: @sjaustin did you add those extra veggies before cooking soup?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: Tasty and easy! Suggest NOT adding the noodles to the pan and assembling each individual bowl with noodles. This will allow for a better leftover experience with noodles that aren’t soggy. Store the leftover meat mixture, noodles and veggies separately in the fridge.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: Good call on the separate storage!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: We had Mazemen Monday yesterday, because Kim Severson said it was. It was a hit. My husband doesn’t prefer noodles usually but he loved the flavors of this dish. My teens said it was a win, and the 9 year old ate his ramen plain (a win).  I loved having a dish with garlic and ginger, and I love tahini, which was surprising but excellent. The radish and sprouts added a crunch and per another reader I also added cukes. Next time I will double the meat/spices and serve with lettuce for my non noodle fans.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: This was sooo good. The noodles came out amazing and so flavorful. The tahini was a delicious addition. The toppings are customizable but I wouldn’t leave to out the scallions or grated radish! Served with lime and Laoganma chili crisp — sooo good!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: Used udon noodles instead of ramen. Used yuzu/mirin instead of ponzu.  Used a little less soy and did not add salt as suggested in final step. I did use normal chicken stock (not low sodium). Added chopped bok choy and Thai basil at the end. VERY good and easy to add on veggies, toppings, etc. Versatile base or good as is if you want to keep it simple!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: I made this and the aftertaste on the noodles was really strange. Could have been the tahini I used which had lemon juice and the ponzu sauce was lemony so maybe too much lemon? The kids did not like it at all and I tried to like it but ended up throwing the rest away because it was so unpleasant. I followed the directions exactly. Specific brands of ingredients could be helpful.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: I found a squeeze of fresh lime really helped to bring together

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: Really easy and great.  We were didn't have miso so I substituted an equal amount of fish sauce.  I cut down the tahini to just about 1 to 1.5 tablespoons, and omitted the sugar.  I also threw in some edamame with the noodles.  To serve, I squeezed some lime juice and drizzled over Trader Joe's chilli oil crunch.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: I don’t love Ramen noodles. What’s a noodle with flavor and a bit of bite to it? Like the kind in a crispy noodle dish? Egg noodles?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: @Easter Definitely not egg noodles. They are VERY soft. Maybe you could try it with udon noodles? They are kind of toothsome!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: @Easter are you thinking of yakisoba noodles? Like for stir fry? I’d give that a try. I’d also consider this with room temp soba noodles!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight with Brown Rice Udon noodles and ground chicken breast … all the ingredients came together to make a delicious sauce… I didn’t feel the need to add the sugar, but I didn’t add some green beans and  appreciate d the crunch from those and the the radishes and bean sprouts. A complete healthy one- bowl meal!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: My whole family loved this! Made it last night. The store didn't have any sprouts, so I added oyster mushrooms instead. So good. I agree that the radish (with scallion greens) were the perfect garnish to add freshness to the umami. Thank you!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: Loved this and so did the rest of the family, want to make it again ASAP!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: This was bland, and a lot of prep work.  The tahini should be in the sauce where it can dissolve.  I would not make it again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: @Faith yeah too much lemon zest does have a floral/medicinal aftertaste. Too much lemon juice is just tangy only

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! The sauce is rich and complex. Prepared as directed except I used Liviva Dried Shirataki noodles - they were sturdy and tasty. Garnished with bean sprouts, radish, cucumber, pickled red onions, avocado, Okazu chili crisp, and toasted sesame seeds. Yum yum!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: The tahini makes it heartier and adds protein. Not sure if I made it wrong but my dish was much more soupy. Still tasty though, will probably make again (it makes a lot)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: Yummy as written

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: Used a tamari ponzu combo sauce that I had.  That was the only alteration.  Family loved it.  A hit.  Will repeat.  Thank you!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: Wasn't sure about this... but LOVED it!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: everyday pantry ingredients

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: Simple, easy and delicious! Used daikon and broccoli sprouts. Will definitely make again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: Definitely worth a try- I didn’t have everything on the sauce list but I did have the miso and tahini- definitely riffable.  I also did the cucumbers and scallions, radishes and I added sliced celery because I wanted more vegetables.  It was sooooo good! I had to use dried rams but found decent quality.  Can imagine what fresh ramen would have been like!  Try this recipe!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: The recipe says to "season with salt and pepper" three different times. It is salty enough between the miso, soy sauce and ponzu sauce. No need to add any extra salt and pepper, let alone three times.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
commentBody: Made it for dinner tonight as a way to use up our last packets of dried instant ramen, a scant handful of dried mushrooms, some fresh broccoli florets, fresh carrot (which I substituted for the radish). A hit! I’ll be making this again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Skirt Steak With Smoky Cucumber Chimichurri Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I was worried the meat would need to be marinated, but the olive oil, salt, pepper and oregano were perfect. And the sauce was divine. Nice crunch and grilled flavor from the cucumbers, tang from the lemon, and salty umami from the capers. The one change I made was to add cilantro. Because I love cilantro. Definitely going in the rotation!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Skirt Steak With Smoky Cucumber Chimichurri Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! I replaced the parsley with cilantro, because I don’t like parsley. After tasting the sauce, I ended up adding more garlic, lemon juice, and crushed red peppers. The sauce mixed with yogurt was even better!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Skirt Steak With Smoky Cucumber Chimichurri Recipe
commentBody: Excellent meal. Paired with coleslaw (such as Pickleback) and thin frites.  I did add more lemon juice to the Chimichurri. Now on heavy rotation!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Skirt Steak With Smoky Cucumber Chimichurri Recipe
commentBody: Excellent as described!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Skirt Steak With Smoky Cucumber Chimichurri Recipe
commentBody: Recipe was fantastic as written!  Next time I may try chili crisp instead of just red pepper flakes, and sub out some of the olive oil with rice vinegar.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Skirt Steak With Smoky Cucumber Chimichurri Recipe
commentBody: Question: do I peel the cucumbers?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Skirt Steak With Smoky Cucumber Chimichurri Recipe
commentBody: @AMM no- Persian cukes don’t need to be peeled

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Skirt Steak With Smoky Cucumber Chimichurri Recipe
commentBody: Can this be done in an air fryer?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Skirt Steak With Smoky Cucumber Chimichurri Recipe
commentBody: I grill everything, or so I thought! Grilled Cucumber? Who knew!? Perfect as is–loved everything about it! Thank you Kay!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Skirt Steak With Smoky Cucumber Chimichurri Recipe
commentBody: I don't see how you can get the smoky flavor necessary to the cucumbers in an air fryer @DebDoo

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Skirt Steak With Smoky Cucumber Chimichurri Recipe
commentBody: Does the steak need to sit in the rub for a certain about of time? Or put it straight on the grill?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Skirt Steak With Smoky Cucumber Chimichurri Recipe
commentBody: @Meg no- I put it on right after I seasoned- was delicious

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Skirt Steak With Smoky Cucumber Chimichurri Recipe
commentBody: I made the recipe exactly as written and found it delicious! It came together quickly. NYT never accounts for all the chopping but in this case it wasn’t terrible—quartering the cucumbers, then a quick mince of parsley, garlic, and capers while the grill got hot, and the final chopping of the cucumbers once they came off the grill. This made a nice summer evening meal with a simple green salad.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Skirt Steak With Smoky Cucumber Chimichurri Recipe
commentBody:
@AMM 
Shouldn't be necessary. Just wash well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Spicy Gazpacho Recipe
commentBody: Don't discard the pulp after you strain. Add a little hot sauce and it makes a great salsa!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Spicy Gazpacho Recipe
commentBody: Removing the seeds in chilis does nothing to reduce the heat. The capsaicin is in the pith

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Spicy Gazpacho Recipe
commentBody: This is basically my recipe, but I prefer roasting the red peppers, for a silkier texture, something I got from an old Bon Appetit recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Spicy Gazpacho Recipe
commentBody: This is very close to Diana Kennedy's gazpacho recipe in her "Nothing Fancy" cookbook. We have used that recipe for years and give it a Texas touch by adding a small Serrano pepper. Kennedy's recipe does not add cucumber to the initial blending and opts for a green bell pepper, plus red wine vinegar. She also uses a food processor and adds the olive oil via the drip pusher very slowly as the last step which helps keep the oil blended.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Spicy Gazpacho Recipe
commentBody: So you don't have to cook this over heat??

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Spicy Gazpacho Recipe
commentBody: No, you eat it cold.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Spicy Gazpacho Recipe
commentBody: This was a bit too oily for me.  Also, really small servings after going through the sieve.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: I made it exactly as written, except served with rice. I made it for 2, but kept the eggplant chickpeas mixture for 4. This makes me think it could do with more of that when actually cooking for 4, because we ate it all 😉

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: This was good! Added lemon to the sauce and finished with hot pepper sesame oil. Might try with different type of noodle.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made this using tahini instead of Cashew butter?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody:
@skyandstars
Yes, I used tahini instead of the cashew butter.  It worked well as a substitution.  I used a little more salt than I normally use to bring out the flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody:
This is a great recipe base! After reading the comments, I did the following to spruce it up a bit: 

-added green beans (roasted) 
-roasted the chick peas, eggplant, and green beans on separate pans to let them crisp. I also salted the eggplant and let it sit in a colander for a while to help with the bitterness. 
-added fresh ginger, raw garlic, lime, and Serrano pepper to the sauce  
-subbed almond butter (Justin’s) since I didn’t have cashew butter or the energy to make fresh cashew butter 
-topped with scallions, Serrano pepper, and chili crisp. I wish I had cilantro because I think that would have added even more! 

This will be going into rotation. If I were to make this for guests I’d probably do two cans of chickpeas. Tofu would also be delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: @Philadelphia union worker - this was extremely helpful  especially the tweaks to the salad dressing

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: I made it in an oven skillet and added limejuice etc for the pan sauce to the pan - delicious. I didn’t have half pickles in my supermarket; went for ‘normal’ pickles, which kind of tastes like the pickled veg the Indonesian kitchen often uses in their dishes, so fine, but next time I’ll make the cucumbers in rice vinegar like suggested here.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody:
@Vera what are half pickles please? And how do they fit in this recipe? 
Thank you.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: Why half pickles?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: @Vera I don’t see anything in this recipe that says anything about pickles, half or otherwise. Or is this another case of the NYT Cooking Notes getting wires crossed with a different recipe?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: Very yum my family says. We never tried ponzu before and everyone likes it. Had to go to 3 stores before I found it. We followed most of the recipe except basmati rice sustituted for the noodles, and tofu, roasted with tamari, evoo and sesame oil on another sheet pan instead of garbanzo beans. 2 thumbs up, not difficult to make. Thanks!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: I made this and it was great! I added a lot more oil to the eggplant when roasting, and to my surprise, the pieces turned soft and sweet.   I doubled the sauce portion because a couple of the reviews said that it was dry, the sauce is delicious. I also added lime, which elevated the flavors.   I was out of green onions, so I saw online a suitable substitute was shallots, so I thinly sliced shallots and added them instead of the green onion and it turned out well. Thank you for a wonderful dish. I’m going to add it to my rotation.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: Looks colorful, but lacks distinctive flavor. Highly recommend  lots of hot sauce. This and the cheesy bean bake recipe are two of the most disappointing recipes I have made from this website

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: It was excellent but as with almost all nyt dishes I modified quite a bit - recipe was jumping off point. Peanut butter was fine instead of cashew because less than 1/2 the price. Tossed veggies in a bowl with dressing first for even coat. Didn’t use ponzu because couldn’t find any without sugar so just added tamari and rice vinegar for sauce. Added a tray of diced chicken thigh. Coated veggies and chicken in olive oil, tamari, balsamic and garlic powder. Served with left over brown rice and kale.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: This was a little bland.  The eggplant did not caramelize well even after extra roasting time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: There is a good idea in here somewhere but this recipe ain't it. 1.5 oz noodles per serving is not enough for a meal but if you up the noodles, the sauce is not enough. Even with the given amount, the sauce isn't punchy enough to carry the dish. Next eggplant will go back into the eggplant adobo.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: I can’t find roasted cashew butter in my local stores either. I used dark roasted peanut butter as a substitute. Didn’t peel eggplant. Otherwise made as directed. Nice flavors but rather dry.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night following the recipe exactly. It was good and my family said they liked it, but I agree with another commenter who thought it was dry. I can’t decide if the veggies needed more oil or a good squeeze of citrus or what, but they needed something. If I could get it to a juicier place, I’d probably also serve it over rice rather than noodles - I felt like I was fighting to get all of the flavors/textures in one bite with the noodles. Rice would also absorb the (delicious) sauce more easily.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: @Tara:  Made this with double sauce over long grain brown rice and it was great. One issue:  needs to bake long enough to really soften the eggplant but not so long that the chickpeas harden. The doubled sauce helps remoisten those chickpeas if they do dry out a little. (And it’s a very tasty sauce.)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: Skipped the noodles and the chickpeas, but this was a great way to make eggplant. I did not have ponzu so I used vegan soy sauce plus lemon juice. Also used raw cashew butter and it was still delicious. Added a spec of chili crisp. Great with cilantro if you like cilantro.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: Prepped as written but discovered no rice noodles in pantry. Used  fideo noodles instead. Still good but will try rice or even make sure rice noodles on hand. Also, I think a pinch or more of sumac in sauce and/or on top will give this a tangy kick.   Will definitely make this easy recipe again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: I enjoyed this dish! I used Japanese eggplants cut into rounds and subbed tahini for the cashew butter. I also added a fair bit of red pepper flakes to the sauce bc i can't help myself when it comes to revving up the spice level on anything and everything.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: @Name nolabelle, sorry for the late reply; that was a comment for another recipe that accidentally wound up here XD Sorry for the confusion!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: added ginger and garlic to the sauce and mixed the noodles and sauce before serving. delicious!!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: Flavor combination was good. But it was very dry. I most likely won’t try it again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: Dang. Yet another NYT recipe that sounds good but with unavailable ingredients. "Roasted Cashew Butter", be sure to not use raw as it won't work.  Not available in any grocery near me, not even on Amazon. Does anyone know where to get it?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: @Sniggins in my area cashew butter rarely says roasted on the front but you can suss it out by reading the ingredient list. Most of what supermarkets carry will have “roasted cashew” or “dry roasted cashews” as the first ingredient. Usually it is only the high end “natural” brands that have just cashews that aren’t roasted. Hope that helps!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: if you have a blender or food processor, you can dump a cup of roasted cashews in there and whizz till creamy. throw in a little water or oil to loosen up.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: @Sniggins:  I did find Wilderness Poets brand Organic Roasted Cashew Butter on Amazon.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody:
don't see half pickles, thinking new pickles?  do see salt and pepper twice.
very allergic to fish (iodine), so no ponzu. suggestions? might try w zucchini instead of eggplant.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody:
@Sharon

Check online for something to your taste: they basically say to mix soy sauce with some citrus and mirin and maybe a little sugar

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: What is Pozo?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: @Dan: This recipe calls for ponzu sauce (not "pozo"). To make this recipe, I quickly found and ordered a bottle of ponzu sauce online. Later I also found various recipes online for homemade ponzu sauce, most calling for a blend of citrus juices (lemon, orange & grapefruit), soy sauce, mirin, lemon zest, dried bonito flakes (or, for vegan, dried shiitake mushrooms), and kombu. I found it to be delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: Are we peeling the eggplant?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: The recipe says 1 1/2 lbs eggplant - about 8 cups. Is that correct? Maybe I’m wildly wrong about the weight and mass of eggplants, but I also feel like 8 cups would be overwhelming in this dish.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody:
We enjoyed this.  Roasted green beans also, a nice addition.  Didn't have ponzu so mixed
soy, lemon and mirin.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: Awful!  Not much taste and eggplant skin was tough.  Should be peeled but not making this again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: I think it means half-sour pickles. I wouldn't trust any brand other than these folks. https://batamptepickle.com/products/pickles/

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: I made this with toasted Chinese grocery store sesame paste, pickled nasturtium berries, hot pink Middle Eastern pickled turnips, and a quick side of cucumber black vinegar salad. The pickles cut the blandness and are a salty pantry accompaniment.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: I made it with sunflower butter and added basil but otherwise as written, it was so good and everyone loved it. If you use sunflower butter use less water.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: I am wondering if toasted almond butter could be a sub??

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: For people coming here from The Veggie newsletter, note that this recipe is not strictly vegetarian because of ponzu sauce, but can easily be modified to be.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: Underwhelmed by this. Cold noodles, hot veggies and room temperature dressing made an odd combination.  And the dressing was bland.  Added fish sauce and hot sauce to give it some flavor.  If I could handle raw garlic, I think it would have helped.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: Surprised at how meh everyone felt about this. I thought it was quite good and a quick meal! I crisped the chickpeas in my cast iron because I had so much eggplant. It was a welcome change to use rice noodles, something I don’t normally eat at home!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: This was really good, but like others I found the ratios a bit off. I made it as written and there was not enough sauce for the noodles. I had ran out of cashew butter so used tahini to make more sauce, which I think I prefer. I would also increase the amount of eggplant and chickpeas next time. I am not usually a huge fan of spring onions (scallions) but these are essential here, as is the hot sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: This is a favorite dish in our house. I make it as written, otherwise upping the noodles from 6 oz to 8 oz, and the sauce to noodle ratio manages the increase just fine. It’s a solid, easy weeknight recipe that has a unique and delicious flavor profile.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody:
I used this as more of guidelines:
- subbed tofu for chickpeas
- used soba noodles
- subbed tahini for cashew butter, since that's what I had on hand. But Tahini is so much thicker than cashew butter so I wouldn't recommend equal ratios next time (maybe 1/4  C).
- I was also out of sesame seeds and subbed some toasted cashews.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: It didn’t work for me. Kind of bland.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: This was a "meh" for me and my family. I think what was really missing was something acidic like vinegar or lemon.  I may play around with it as I think the sauce has potential, but as is, I would not make it again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
commentBody: No cashew butter so used crunchy deep-roasted PB instead. Very good! Will be making again. Not quite filling enough for us though, so gonna add crispy tofu or Samin Nosrat’s coconut tofu (recipe is on here) next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Skillet Pork Cutlets With Fresh Peach Butter  Recipe
commentBody: This was good but could be better. This seems like the first draft of a solid recipe. Next time I will add the peaches to the butter and let the juices cook down to get the peach butter a little more jammy. I threw some lime juice and that helps bring everything together a bit.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Skillet Pork Cutlets With Fresh Peach Butter  Recipe
commentBody:
Oh man, you guys??  I tried the just the peaches with brown butter and it wasn’t so great. Decided to follow the suggestion of cooking it down a bit in the butter, then adding lime. It was excellent with the pork, had it with roast potatoes and green beans. 

But I had some extra sauce, tasted it, and thought “vanilla icecream!!”. Drove to the grocery store, bought the most expensive vanilla icecream they had, got it home and and slathered it with the sauce. It was FANTASTIC. Skip the pork, have it for dessert. I bet a sprinkle of crushed ginger snaps toasted in butter would take it out of orbit.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Skillet Pork Cutlets With Fresh Peach Butter  Recipe
commentBody: What a sumptuous, luscious way to bring pork to life. Yum. "Medium-ripe" threw me off for a second. A medium-sized ripe peach? A peach that's medium-ripe? It doesn't matter in the end. Also, I didn't bother using room-temp butter. I just cut up cold butter so it melted quickly and uniformly.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Skillet Pork Cutlets With Fresh Peach Butter  Recipe
commentBody: "Medium-ripe peach": the hyphen is between medium and ripe, so the peach should be medium-ripe. If medium sized peach were called for, it would be "medium ripe peach" without the hyphen. The recipe calls for 1/2 cup of pulp and juices; that quantity should help selecting the size of he peach.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Skillet Pork Cutlets With Fresh Peach Butter  Recipe
commentBody: The peach butter was pretty good as written, but a bit tart since my peach wasn't super sweet. I added a bit of honey and that made a huge difference. Excellent. Will by trying the butter on grilled chicken and corn later this week.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Skillet Pork Cutlets With Fresh Peach Butter  Recipe
commentBody: Too much butter.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Skillet Pork Cutlets With Fresh Peach Butter  Recipe
commentBody:
This is not "peach butter." It may be buttery peaches, but it is not peach butter. That would require long, slow cooking of a puree to reduce it to a very thick consistency. And then usually canned for preservation.

This recipe describes more of a quick jam. But it's really just pan-cooked grated peaches. They end up jammy just as would tomatoes onions or anything else cooked with this method. But peach butter, it's not.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Skillet Pork Cutlets With Fresh Peach Butter  Recipe
commentBody: Has potential but needs somethings… shallot? Wine? More like a pan sauce?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Skillet Pork Cutlets With Fresh Peach Butter  Recipe
commentBody: Followed the instructions to a T and it was perfect for a quick summer meal.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: The steps or list of ingredients should be revised to include taking the corn off the cob and scraping the cobs, slicing the leeks, mincing the garlic, cutting the potatoes into whatever size pieces are optimal, and cutting the cod into whatever size pieces are best to cook at same time as the shrimp.  I know to do these things, but one would expect these details to be included in the recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: FYI for others - prep instructions for ingredients are on the website and not the iOS app (idk why)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: @Hugo The ingredients list has ingredients listed in their prepped form.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody:
Excellent soup. Boiled an extra potato (separately) mashed with extra coconut milk, thickened the soup nicely. Did add chili flakes. 
Insecure cooks could be stumped with no chopping instructions.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Made pretty much according to the recipe. Delicious. I would add a pinch of red pepper flakes next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: For us less experienced cooks:  how is leek prepped?  If using frozen corn, how many cups? Are potatoes whole? Thanks. Looking forward to trying

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: I put the corn kernels in with the spinach, they don’t need to be cooked that long.  I, too, added red pepper flakes. Next time I’ll add chopped cilantro and change from lemon juice to lime juice. I think that would be very interesting with this. It was delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and a keeper! Yes to adding red pepper flakes. I added the ginger earlier and don’t know if this would make a difference. Also opened a few cans of chopped clams which gave plenty of clam juice plus some chopped clams couldn’t hurt!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: My Celiac partner is gluten-free and lactose-free, and I've struggled to find a comforting chowder recipe that meets his dietary needs. This soup is so flavorful and filling--it transports you to summer on the New England coast. I'm always hesitant to use coconut milk as a dairy replacement as it sometimes overpowers with too much coconut flavor, but it works perfectly in this recipe with the sweetness of the corn and fresh seafood.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Chopping instructions are there!  In the ingredients list.....

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: I used cilantro instead of ginger.  I also substituted 2 cans of minced clams w/ juice instead of the bottled clam juice.  I didn't have the shrimp and it wasn't missed.  Instead of cutting up the cod, break it up as it cooks in the broth (much easier to know when it's done too).  It's a very adaptable and delicious summer recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: This was a big hit with my family!  I will make again. But I don't understand people's comments about the lack of instructions regarding sizes to chop the cod and the potatoes. Those instructions are clearly included.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: This was amazing! For such a relatively hands-off recipe, it had surprisingly deep flavors and a really nice consistency to the broth. I took the suggestion of one reviewer and used lime and cilantro instead of lemon and chives… also added bay scallops. Satisfied even my teen sons, who kept saying, “it’s so fresh!” Truly delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Made this as listed and it was awesome. I used a mix of white and black pepper to pipe in a little heat. The next day I reheated the leftovers over low in a saucepan and it was just as nice the second day.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: I added carrots for color …diced….used barramundi not cod (none available) added chicken stock for water,doubled the clam juice and used the whole can of coconut. It was great. Delicate lightly sweet flavor

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious! The only thing I will do differently next time is chop the cod and potatoes into larger pieces.  I thought they cooked down too small, but this is just my personal taste.  I also added a bit of chopped parsley.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: I always slice and rinse leeks - they tend to carry grit.  Leeks also dehydrate nicely so I always put up a few jars when they’re in season.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Half & Half makes a nice substitute for coconut milk. I'll echo the suggestion for adding red chili flakes or Aleppo chili powder for a nice kick.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: How much is a “bottle of clam juice”? It comes in different sizes

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: @Christine bottled clam juice usually comes in 8 oz. bottles and costs a few dollars. It can usually be found near the canned fish in the supermarket.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Adapted slightly for dietary needs, but super easy and really delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Added a tablespoon of green curry paste, a few dashes of fish sauce plus soy to amp the umami, mami. Made it for the fam and they loved it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody:
This is delicious, even made it
successfully in our rental house, without ideal tools. That said, it would be great if NYTCooking would fix the app/website recipe disconnect. In terms of the recipe, my only changes were to add some fresh chopped clams, since they were available and decreased the cod. Also used small potatoes, so did not peel. Added red pepper flakes and a dash of smoked paprika (because it makes most things better). Really delicious and easy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Also meant to say, used stock rather than water, since I don't use water when I have stock. Will try this with all fish stock next time. And would be interested in hearing from those who try this recipe with frozen corn, since it would be lovely to eat when corn season is over.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Mediocre!  The cod fell apart; the potatoes were tasteless. I used fresh corn and left the cobs  in but the fish stock and water made everything tasteless. Swordfish might have been better (and sturdier). It tasted like WATERY POTATO SOUP WITH A HINT OF CORN. BAD!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: I added the corn at the end with the spinach, and used the full 5oz of baby spinach that comes when you buy it.  Also added a couple of teaspoons of fish sauce when stirring in the coconut milk to bump up the flavor a bit.  It was a huge hit with my guests.  I served it with ATK's peach/cucumber salad (which also has a vaguely Asian flavor profile and just right for late summer).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody:
What a wonderful soup. I am allergic to milk. Coconut milk was my answer.
I tripled the recipe. I am not fond of fish in soup.  I put in the shrimp, added 2 lbs of small scallops and 2 cans of small clams with the juice in the cans. I added 2 cans of coconut mild and 2 small cans of coconut cream.  I added 5 ears of corn and a can of creamed corn, the rest was as recipe said. It was one of the best soups I ever made. Thank you for this wonderful recipe

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Add a generous amount of lime to lift the flavor, and add red pepper flakes to taste.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: It was good but a bit bland, I feel like it needs a bit more of everything, more corn, more time simmering with the corn cobs in, more potatoes, maybe some fish sauce, a bit of heat…… I like the suggestion of lime juice as well…..

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: I made this yesterday and it was delicious. I used only the cod, but the whole can of coconut milk. Also, a stick of celery, because what's chowder without celery?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Added green curry. Ab Fab!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody:
WOW!!! SO GOOD! I used chicken broth instead of water, half and half instead of coconut milk, only shrimp (dislike cod), and lots of lemon juice to finish. 

It’s 90 degrees in the house today, so we are eating this cold, and I think I like it even better like this! Adding to rotation!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Substitute 2 inches of fresh or frozen galangal (kaa) root for the ginger, fish sauce for the salt, lime juice to taste and some Thai chillies, lemongrass, and magrood leaves for the spinach and you have Tom Ka Pla

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: The best summer soup so far this year! I made with Lobster broth, shells left over from another meal. I too thickened by adding extra potato. Perfect for leftover corn and during corn season it is the best! I did cut my shrimp as prefer smaller pieces and used salmon once.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight as instructed with the exception of adding a stock cube into the water and it was brilliant. Came together quick, very little fuss, and went down a treat with everyone.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: what is a good substitute for the coconet milk?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Ok, this is seriously good.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: This is absolutely delicious. Used it as a base for small adjustments (e.g., no ginger, regular whipping cream rather than coconut milk, seafood broth rather than bottled clam juice).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: So very good! Made exactly as written. I’ll add that for us, it’s more like 5-6 servings.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody:
Thanks for that cheery restatement of the obvious. Must be a blast at parties.

Very good recipe, agree with adding a few pepper flakes. If only our CSA did cod and shrimp, too. ;-)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Can this be frozen?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: I just made this for four friends and everyone loved it. I found that the ratio of broth to fish was too small, so I added more liquid. Instead of one C of clam juice I used two 8-ounce packets of Brodo ginger and turmeric bone broth. Excellent!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Tried it as written and it was tasty! I augmented after tasting and added a splash of fish sauce because it had Tom Yum vibes. Yum!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody:
Has anyone  substituted salmon for
The cod? It seems like it would work but don’t want to serve it to book club without some input

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Superb!  As suggested by other commenters, I added the corn with the fish and used a sprinkle of red pepper flakes.  Rave reviews from my guests.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody:
Followed directions but this was the worst! Smelled delicious but tasted like poor potato soup.
I make seafood chowder and corn chowder frequently but this did not work. Disappointment.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe. I subbed about a dozen fresh basil leaves in place of the spinach, which I avoid due to the high oxalate level.  I used lime in place of lemon juice, and micro-planed some lime peel into the soup. Also chopped a fresh hot cherry pepper into the soup.  Served on rice noodles in bowls.  Probably more of an Asian taste, but a really good meal.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: The recipe is missing something—umami.  I added a few dashes of Tabasco in the bowel and it helped it a lot.  Next time I may add some anchovies or fish sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Some great ideas here.  Thanks!  I riced a 2nd boiled  potato into the pot and added 1/2 cup chopped celery sautéed in butter to just beginning to soften.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: I don’t know what I did wrong, followed the recipe,  but this was boring. So sad I wasted all that shrimp on it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Substituted finely chopped cauliflower for the potatoes as I do not eat nightshades. Delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: I made this with Chilean red shrimp and added some lemon zest and extra salt and it was great.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: This was fantastic. Grocery store didn't have leeks so I subbed shallots and green onion. Used the whole can of coconut milk. Added some red pepper flakes. I'll be making this again using lime and cilantro next time. This is a keeper.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: So I took the liberty of replacing the spinach with cilantro, the fish with Italian sausage, and of course added garlic. Yum.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: My husband and I love this chowder! I added a can of clams in juice the last time I made it (I didn't have clam juice alone) -- it made it even better.  I seasoned my fish and shrimp with the salt & pepper recommended, but also added Old Bay seasoning -- good addition.  And lastly, when I use lemon juice in a dish, I always zest the lemon, too -- also a good addition.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: I doubled the recipe (big family). Added red pepper flakes, lime & cilantro instead of lemon & chives as suggested by others. Delicious! My teenage grandsons loved it. Will definitely add to our rotation.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Excellent recipe. Chowder attested rich without being coconut-y. I used clams instead of shrimp, cooking them separately and using the broth instead of the clam juice. I also threw in a few halved cherry tomatoes that needed to be eaten. I had planned on adding basil cilantro or parsley, but there was absolutely no need for extra flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody:
2 cups of water is too much for me. One cup of water along with a can of coconut milk work for me I was looking for something a little less soupy
I would simmer and reduce the liquid, but the fish will flake Oh well next time flavor wise very good. I added just a touch of red pepper. Flake. I did not put potatoes in.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: Made this for bookgroup after reading Dark Stormy Shore in order to emphasize bounties from the sea.  It was a hit.  I added oyster mushrooms, used southern style hashbrowns to save time and imitation crab because I can't resist.  Adding a tin of smoked scallops and smoked oysters made it a lot like Cullen Skink, which I love.  Easy and wow producing.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
commentBody: I add 1 or 2 cans of minced clam meat with juice instead of the shrimp.  Delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: DO NOT be tempted to use a stand mixer fitted with a whisk attachment instead of a food processor. A mixer does not achieve the silky texture that a food processor does - it was still grainy even after 5-6 minutes of whisking. I've made it both ways now, and using a food processor produces a far superior product and is definitely the way to go. Serving suggestion: For breakfast, spread on toast, drizzle with honey, then top with avocado slices. Divine.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: @Deb W I enjoy something similar- just got back from a local farmers market and topped ricotta on toast  with a drizzle of local honey, slices of fresh strawberries and peaches (both deliciously in season!) and sprinkled toasted sliced almonds on top. Sometimes for dessert I’ll skip the toast and have a bowl of fresh berries, peaches, or nectarines, topped with ricotta, a drizzle of honey and toasted sliced almonds.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: I just made this yesterday, added lemon zest & juice and also a few cloves of garlic.  Then I added the blended ricotta to a made from scratch tomato sauce, served over pasta.  The lemon and garlic appear in the final dish as very subtle.  Everyone loved it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: This was so easy & the texture is heavenly! Me being me, I added salt, pepper, lemon zest, & a sprinkling of garlic powder & Aleppo pepper - I know, I know, not the same but it’s really the texture that this recipe reveals & creates a lovely base for imagination.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: Whisk it by hand with a little heavy cream. Easy and comes out light and very creamy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: Transformative! I used a good local ricotta, and spread it on fresh sourdough bread, topped with thinly slide peach and drizzled with good honey. So simple, but so delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: Add honey and lemon juice to whipped ricotta. Finish with fresh thyme leaves.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: Try it with some pine nuts sprinkled on top. Delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: Looks like it could substitute for cream cheese, which I don't tolerate because it's cultured like most cheeses. That would be fantastic!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: Made today following directions using mini-food processor.  Excellent results.  Tonight will use with Serious Eats' "beet and citrus salad with ricotta."  Thanks for all the suggestions for other seasonings and uses -- will try them.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: Much better flavor made with dry cottage cheese curds. And yes to the food processor!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: Never, never use ricotta other than Polly-O. There is no brand that compares. I have tasted them all.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: @Eric C. In the rural area where I live there are three different local cheese makers (including a sheep farm) that make and sell fresh ricotta. I know some of them are carried further afield, even down to NYC. I bet other folks in different areas might have access to the same. I’d guess they might be worth trying, and possibly better than an industrial sized brand. Of course, homemade ricotta is a cinch as well. I know cost can be prohibitive if you’re trying to get things far from their source, but I encourage everyone to search out milk and cheese from happy cows, and support small scale farmers where you’re able!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with a blender?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: Yes, I use a high-power blender (Vita-Mix) and it comes out perfectly smooth.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: I have whipped ricotta cheese for another purpose:  making an Alfredo sauce without the heavy cream.  Just add milk til you get the same consistency as cream.  Delicious and more protein than fat.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: With which crackers?  My favorite brand is no longer available.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: Delicious but I found 3/4 tsp of salt to be way too much and I love salty!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: This was not a great dish. The ricotta blended smoothly but was too salty and goopy. I served it with a spicy tomato chutney and a flatbread cracker and people liked it. But the texture just seemed off putting.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: Try with cottage cheese and some of the additions in other’s comments.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!  I used Hot Honey for the drizzle.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: @Mary me too! Very good.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: I found this way to salty as written (and that’s something I almost never say!). I suspect it has a lot to do with the ricotta you are using. Would recommend starting with no salt and adding to taste as you are in process of whipping the ricotta. The saltiness became more pronounced once whipped. I added some lemon zest and lemon juice to help balance it out. The smooth texture was very nice. Used as a base for roasted cherry tomatoes (Ottolenghi recipe)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: Used honey to make a sweeter version, for use on a Dutch Baby with sliced, sautéed apples. Hit with the hubby!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: This was a tremendous hit for our Thanksgiving dinner. After reading the comments, I abandoned my plan to use a hand mixer and used a food processor. I added lemon zest and a touch of lemon juice, finishing it up with olive oil and cracked black pepper. I doubled the recipe, thankfully, since it was gone in a flash.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: WAY too salty (and I like salt)! I made per the directions (but tripled the recipe) and found it inedible. Had to throw out the whole batch.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody:
@Melissa Teller
Please note that kosher salt is not nearly as salty as table salt. If you used regular table salt in this recipe then that is the reason it was way too salty. 3/4 teaspoon of kosher salt is equivalent to 1/2 teaspoon of table salt.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: would an immersion blender work instead of a traditional food processor? also, how long would this store in an airtight container in the fridge? thanks!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: We had this at a restaurant last week and I was thrilled to make it as an Easter appetizer today. I added honey and then drizzled with pumpkin seeds & more honey. Love how flexible this recipe can be.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
commentBody: Seasoned ala my N. Italian grandmother's manicotti filling: parmesan, nutmeg, lemon zest, salt/pepper. Dolloped onto pasta in red sauce. Delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
@glork grating a tomato is easier than you think. Place your grater over a bowl. Cut the tomato in half and cup the skin side of the round part in the palm of your hand. Press the cut side into the grater and grate away. The skin will save your hand from injury until you use up all the flesh.

It's a fun technique that you can also use to make a fresh summer tomato sauce by grating directly into some gently cooking garlic and olive oil. Hope this helps!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: @Sunny Good one! We used that technique in a few restaurants I worked in when I was young.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
@Sunny
That’s the move for fresh tomatoes! The box grater is my best friend for fresh tomatoes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I enjoyed the simplicity of this meal and found it to be a nourishing and satisfying one pot meal. I would definitely not skip the jalapeños and cilantro, though. They add a needed heat and complexity to the dish that rounds it out well. I tried hot sauce on it but enjoyed it better just with the crunch of the jalapeños. To avoid undone rice, I simmered the pot for 22 minutes (a little more than suggested) and then pulled the pot off the heat and let it steam for 7 minutes further. The result was perfectly cooked basmati and thoroughly cooked chicken. Nice dinner.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: This recipe has potential but was a bit bland. Next time I will swap the grated tomato for a small can of Rotel, add a small can of mild diced green chiles, and double the amount of cumin.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: @Lindsay @Lindsay I make a similar dish and use a T of Knorr chicken and tomato buillon and a can of El Pato tomato sauce (both found in Hispanic aisle).  The El Pato is spicy and would certainly liven it up!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: @Lindsay agreed - it was very meh without some added heat. I was hoping for more flavor complexity from the tomato.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is super close to what my Mom in México use to make for us, it is easy and delicious.  I don't want to be a pain changing this recipe,  but if you feel like having fun try frying the rice along the poblano, garlic,  etc...add your tomatoes step later, just get that rice golden color. And for the people with not cooked all way rice just add a little chicken broth or water...I added a cup of beer, like a corona, nothing heavy,it comes out delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Great idea! I had to keep adding broth. Wish I’d had some beer!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly. This is basically a burrito without the highly caloric wrapper. Serve with classic toppings of cheddar, salsa and sour cream. Delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, I doubled the amount of broth per some other comments and it seemed to be the right amount for the rice. I also added kale for some greens, just tossed it on top of the chicken before letting the whole thing simmer.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Hi, I do liquid 2x the amount of the dry rice. (except 3x for wild rice, which is not the same as "rice")@matt

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Substitute brown rice? What difference in cooking technique and time?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Definitely not enough liquid for the rice. Just made it, and the rice is not cooked. Added more liquid and cooked for an additional 15 minutes, still not enough. Used a basmati rice. I recommend following the instructions for the rice you are using.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Is 1 3/4 cups liqid adequate for 1 1/2 cups of rice?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Yes, b/c there's also liquid from the tomatoes. My personal suggestion is: after step 3 move the cooking into an instant pot and set it to 5 minutes, with 2 minutes natural release. release the pressure, open, stir and let sit for a few minutes. I make arroz con pollo that way and it's perfect every time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: @matt. I looked at several arroz rojo recipes online and yes, the amount of water (or broth) is standard.  The pulverized tomato adds the extra liquid.  Several of the recipes did, however, call for putting the tomatoes and cooked onion with the water in a blender to make a slurry.  I don’t know whether or not that makes a huge difference, as I have not yet tried this recipe,

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Just did it and yes, with all the other water (tomatos, beans, corn)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Sadly, no poblanos in New Zealand in winter (or most other times), so I used a red bell pepper. Also I substituted pinto beans for black beans. Delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Used Mexican Oregano since that felt appropriate. I will say that the rice came out ever so slightly crunchy. Might add a little extra liquid next time but overall a nice recipe!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: True Mexican rice (arroz con pollo or arroz rojo) should use long grain rice fried gently to a golden brown.  When I make either dish, I cook whole skin-on chicken thighs (and breasts if you have white meat lovers) in water with onion, salt, and plenty of cumin in a separate pot.  I brown the rice in olive oil in a second large pot, add the chiles, finely chopped onion, and spices and cook while stirring to make the flavors pop.  Add cut up cooked chicken and use the broth you just made.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: @John Viescas do you fry the dry grains until golden brown?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
I thought this was great. I made it as written with the following minor modifications:
- used Mexican oregano and generally doubled the spices
- added ancho chili powder
- used boneless chicken breasts
- used 'spicy' black beans
- melted a little colby jack on top prior to serving
corn was fresh from the farm stand.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Once again, thighs. IMHO, chicken breasts are better, and is my protein choice. I really don’t like thighs or any dark meat, even though I’m “supposed” to

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: For me, thighs tend to be much more flavorful and less prone to overcooking.  I'm sure you could use breasts, just be careful with the timing to not overcook.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I've always wanted to try adding chicken to my mexican rice and this gives me courage (and steps).  One thing I'll do differently is at some point, saute the rice till it's lightly brown like one does with mexican rice, to get that fluff. Not sure when I'd do this though.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I was drawn to this recipe for it's simplicity and freshness. Mindful of notes that it's bland, I bumped up all the flavors (whole onion, more garlic, big chili, doubled cumin and oregano) and deglazed with wine before adding the tomato. There is NO good reason to grate tomato, unless you want to. Sub canned tomatoes. even tomato sauce, and cut back on paste.  Squeeze over some lemon and serve with tortilla chips! Needs acid and texture for sure!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: The grated tomato is also the technique used to make pan con tomate, my all-time favorite breakfast in Spain. Utter simplicity and pure flavor: fresh grated tomatoes on toasted local baguette-style bread with a pinch of salt and olive oil. Heaven on the Costa de Sol!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Discovering the technique of grating tomatoes in recipes was an AHA! moment for me when we moved to Spain- it's a game changer for rice dishes!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I can’t have corn. Is it possible to leave it out? Also, I prefer chicken breast to chicken thighs. Has anyone tried substituting breast instead of thighs?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Added some smoked paprika and ground chipotle powder to the chicken seasoning for some extra smoke, and this dish was amazing. Also if you're using fresh corn (like I did), you probably need some extra water to take into account the ice in the frozen corn bag, because my rice turned out a little firm

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Nice recipe. The flavors aren't going to blow you away, but it's wholesome and comforting home cooking. The key to making the flavor pop a little more is going to be salt, and the ambiguous instruction "season with salt and pepper" isn't much help. My rule of thumb, which I learned from Melissa Clark, is to use approximately 3/4 teaspoon of Diamond Crystal kosher salt for each pound of chicken. I used 2 teaspoons here, and that was about right.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: This recipe grossly underestimates the quantity of liquid required to cook the rice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: @Sturgis Riobinson. Absolutely!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: After step 3, move the cooking into an instant pot and set it to 5 minutes, with 2 minutes natural release. release the pressure, open, stir and let sit for a few minutes. I make arroz con pollo that way and it's perfect every time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
This was tasteless. 
But I liked the combination of ingredients. 
Next time, I will double the oregano and cumin, the corn, and definitely the garlic. I will also add a hot pepper to the poblano. 
I will also reduce the amount of rice, and therefore the chicken stock. 
1 cup of raw rice is more than sufficient, especially because this mixture contains beans.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Recipe is annoying.  Followed it to a T, and rice was not cooked.  I looked back to ensure it said to drain the black beans.  Yep.  I looked back to see if I was supposed to use instant rice (yuck).  Nope.  I used extra long grain rice.  1-3/4 cups of broth is not enough for this amount of rice especially when a step says to cook down the liquid for 5 mins after adding the grated tomato.  I’m on 40 mins now and had to add about two cups more liquid so far.  And this is with a heavy Dutch oven lid and no peeking other than when timers went off.  Please test and correct.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Dish is fairly easy to make but I added some sazon for extra seasoning and also substituted the beans for pigeon peas which I think work better in this dish. Everyone loved it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: the rice takes forEVER to cook :(

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I found this to be very disappointing. There was way too much rice, which overwhelmed everything else.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Did it all in the InstantPot. Used the sauté function, then switched to high pressure when everything was combined. Five minutes on high, two minutes natural release, then quick release. No problem with the rice not being cooked sufficiently . Subbed fresh corn for the frozen and some leftover San Marzano canned whole tomatoes for the fresh, grated tomato. Cilantro for the win!  I would definitely do this again, maybe with a squeeze of lime at the end. I thought it was tasty, and easy enough to tweak to personal preferences if so desired.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Echo that the liquid isn’t a sufficient amount.  Spicing is boring.  This was a bland gummy mess.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Made this exactly per the recipe and was impressed - easy to make on a week night, and tasty. It's super 'tweakable' to your own taste. I might make it a little more spicy next time around, but otherwise, great.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Recipe reads as if it will be mouth-watering- I'm just curious about grating a tomato, Anyone have any suggestions ?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Just made it tonight. It’s delicious. You’re overthinking the grating of the tomato. The thick skin of the tomato will save your palms I promise. I fire roasted the jalepeno because I didn’t want the intense heat. Nice Smokey flavor

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I can't wait to try this recipe. It seems ideally suited for an InstantPot / pressure cooker. Curious if anyone has tried it using an IP and what cooking times were used.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: @John B See Edith’s comment about using the IP. Melissa Clark even endorsed her comment in the NYT Cooking email today.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: No beans for this cook! But it should be wonderful anyway!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
If I use some additional vegetables and more black beans to replace the chicken to make it vegetarian does anyone know if that would work?
I imagine vegetables such as green, red, orange peppers and maybe chopped onion along with a two cans black beans would work. 
Just curious if anyone has tried that.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: What kind of tomato is most flavorful?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: @kristina could you use maybe a jalapeño or some spicy pepper but remove the seeds and blanch them for 2 minutes, this would remove the excess heat (in case little children or grown-up not liking the spiciness)? can you get some kirean green Chiles that are not spicy ? poblano is mostly not spicy. sometimes my Mom would mix a bell pepper and a jalapeño  and broiled them , that gets you closer to a poblano.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Can one use  boneless chicken breasts?  How will this affect the cooking time?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Kind of meh for the amount of work involved. Needed almost 2x as much broth & simmered for close to 40 mins before rice was softened.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: One pot recipes always feel like bait for failure. Disappointed in this recipe because as suspected, the liquid ratio wasn’t enough and it’s too late to take my trust back from the recipe. Gloppy failure. Thanks for nothing NYT! Cook rice separately if you want a chance at an edible dinner.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: After reading this recipe online I searched my pantry for the required ingredients and had enough of them to proceed. It was easy enough and turned out great. This recipe is close enough to Arroz con Pollo so I added Sliced olives and served it with mayo on the side. It made a lot for the two of us and there was enough left over for another meal

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I had slightly less than 1 1/2 pounds of chicken thighs, but next time I will make the full 1 1\2  pounds. I didn’t have a fresh chili, so I used a scant teaspoon of Kashmiri chili powder. 1/2 cup of corn would get lost in this dish, so I used a generous one cup of roasted frozen corn. I increased the spices considerably, double and almost triple, because black beans would definitely soak up much of the spices. In light of the other comments I used 2 1/2 cups of chicken stock and checked to make sure the rice was fully cooked. I ended up adding a splash more stock about 15 minutes into the covered simmering time. All in all, this was a very tasty one pot dinner. It makes a ton, so I froze some for a night I didn’t feel like cooking.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: This was really bland…followed the recipe exactly but added 1/2 c more broth at the end because the rice was not cooked through. very ordinary and not worth the effort.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I had to add another splash of liquid and cook for 10 more minutes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I used overripe heirloom tomatoes because I didn't want to waste them. They were too soft to grate, so I chopped them. They produced a lot of liquid, so I used double-concentrate tomato paste. The tomatoes dissolved and I didn't mind little pieces of skin. I always taste chiles so I can adjust the amount. The one I bought was really hot, so I used half, no seeds. Just enough heat for me. I used long-grain brown rice and parboiled it so it would be done in 20 minutes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: This was so flavorful and easy to tweak! I used skin-on bone-in chicken thighs, shisito pepper, and no beans. I cheated and just used grape tomatoes cut in half, sautéed a bit with onion and pepper. I didn't really stick to the measurement and I tend to use spices and herbs quite liberally.. and it came out so so good. Didn't even need hot sauce, and I am a big fan of spicy food. Go for it, don't be afraid of spices and herbs and have fun!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Going in I thought there wouldn’t be enough liquid. Three cups of liquid needed for a cup and a half of rice. It was crunchy at 20 minutes. I added the extra water and it was enough after extra time. The cumin and oregano make the dish. Cilantro and some hot sauce heat help. I freeze dollops of tomato paste. That was easy. There is no way I’m going to grate a tomato. I do cheat with my summer garden and cook whole stemmed tomatoes in a large pot, all summer. Cook until they can be reduced and mashed some. Then blender portions twenty seconds and into the freezer bag. Even with the skin on it makes a fine sauce when thawed and reduced on the stove for spaghetti day. Tangy fresh. Anyway that’s what I used, 3/4 cups. Did this recipe work for anyone as written?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
