commentBody: @TDaddyTodd I had a similar experience with some of the rice still crunchy at 20. Seems that the liquid in the grated tomato is very important to overall rice cooking, but the amount is difficult to pinpoint with grating, so it didn’t work for me.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I loved how this came together, but needed more liquid. A cursory glance at the liquid-to-rice ratio would suggest it needs more liquid. It is delicious, and will make again

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: It’s summer, why not use fresh corn for more crunch. Cut back on the rice by a third so it’s not such a carbo load, add medium dice red bell pepper for even more color. Someone wrote more cumin, I agree.  Other than that, a tasty meal!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Great dish but bland. Don’t be shy on seasoning. Next time we make it we’ll add some chipotle peppers from the can for more heat and seasoning.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Made as written but added 2 tbsp of tajin, and a bouillon cube. Delish!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I added 2c of broth, not 1.5, which  worked beautifully. Addresses the issues others had w crunchy rice!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Not impressed with this recipe, though I usually like what I find on this app.   Very bland, rice was under-cooked using the time suggested.  In addition to being bland, the time spent preparing was long compared to the result.   Definitely do not recommend.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Needed more liquid as rice cooked unevenly and not fully. I guess it depends how juicy the tomato is…Added habanero while cooking and some lime to top.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I liked this and would likely make it again. I agree with it needing more spice, even when I used 1/2 cup of mole sauce instead of the tomato paste. Used fresh corn which has so much more flavor, sauteed the rice with the onion and chilies before adding everything else.  Also I would increase the beans and decrease the rice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Really good. Simple to make, but complex flavors nonetheless. This time of year, with fresh tomatoes and fresh corn--excellent. I followed the suggestion to use Instant Pot after step 3 and  was very happy with the result.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly and it was great. Not always the case though. The jalapenos and cilantro were necessary. The second day when we ate the left overs, we added sour cream and sriracha and it was also great.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Followed advice of others- doubled spices, added paprika. For extra liquid grated 2 tomatoes. Cut grape tomatoes, avocado, cilantro, limes, grated cheese, sour cream for toppings. Took more like 25 minutes. Delicious! Would have used Instantpot but didn’t have it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Love this recipe. And here's the obligatory, "but I changed this" ... I hate grating tomatoes so I used a small can of Rotel (or any diced tomato will do), doubled the poblanos and seasonings and I unfortunately have the soap thing for cilantro so left that out. Everything else to a T except I needed an extra 5 minutes cook time. 20 min was still too soupy. Definitely a new addition to my meal prep list.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
Very bland, needs lots more seasonings and a big squeeze of lime. 
Too hot to cook here so I adapted it to the instant pot.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
Made this tonight for supper. The only changes I made was using a small can of mild Rotel for the grated tomato. I seasoned a little more aggressively with the cumin. 
Very tasty. I can see making it without the chicken and using as a side with grilled meats or a taco bar.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Tried this - made according to directions except I added a little extra chicken broth (noticed in the comments that people said the rice was dry.) It was okay, but not a lot of flavor. I’m glad I added cilantro and jalapeño to spice it up a little. I probably won’t make it again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I agreed that this recipe is surprisingly bland.  It really does need the jalapeños or hot sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
Quite bland. The amount of spices could easily be doubled. Also, for 1.5 cups of rice, you need 3 cups of broth, maybe a little less if your grated tomato is very juicy.

If I try this again I think I'll add about 1/4 cup Lizano Sauce, which is used in Costa Rican Chicken and Rice. That'll help.

This recipe is flawed as it's presented here, but it has potential to make a tasty and healthy one-bowl meal with a few changes. (e.g., use brown rice and adjust liquid and simmer time accordingly.)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
Loved this recipe, made a few adjustments to simplify:
1. Substitute a 14.5 oz can of diced tomatoes instead of grating a tomato
2. Use 1 cup of uncooked pearl barley instead of rice - boost the fiber content and introduce a chewier texture
3. I didn't have poblano peppers or cilantro in the house, so I skipped those and used jarred jalapenos and it worked beautifully
4.  Throw it all in an instant pot, all at once. Pressure cook for 15 minutes. Done & easy!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
A little bland.
Next time I'd use a full onion, more tomato, double the corn (I used fresh)and double the spices.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
I made this and it was a success with caveats. This is one of those recipes you really need to use only as a guide and adjust for your own tastes. What I did:
* used the prescribed amount of rice and broth but,
* grated 2 fresh tomatoes. When cooked with the tomato paste they didn’t end up too liquidy cos you cook it down a bit. 
* used 1kg of chicken instead of 680gm
* something like tripled the spice - used garam masala cos I was missing cumin and loads of oregano plus 4 garlic cloves and about 1.5 cups of onions. Sorry I don’t measure this I just eyeball it. 
* added about half a jar of chipotle in adobo during the tomato paste stage. This gave tons of flavor 
* add a teaspoon of msg cos why not
* used madame Jeanette and jalapeño instead of poblano cos I live in the Netherlands and that’s all I could find in terms of chili
* used a whole can of corn - about 285gm instead of frozen 
*my rice was jasmine rice and unwashed. 

Cook time once I added it all together was about 20mins. Rice didn’t need extra liquid and wasn’t gummy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Read all the comments about needing more liquid for the rice, added more liquid and ended up with mushy rice. C’mon y’all!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Ummmm that prep is not 20 minutes… more like 40 for me and I cook every night!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: It was a bit on the bland side.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Meh. Agree with the comment that it tastes exactly like the inside of a burrito - and I'll add a mediocre burrito. Added cheddar and lots of Cholula and wrapped it in a tortilla because might as well. Tasted fine.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Definitely a problem with rice to liquid ratio! I added water several times and cooked it at least twice as long and it still had crunchy rice (and a scorched pan). Very disappointing; wish I had read the reviews first.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I’m looking for some warmer weather dishes  to make ahead and freeze. Any reason this wouldn’t be a good candidate.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: @Jocelyn I freeze arroz con pollo all the time in individual portions for lunch. Go for it!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly and could not get the rice cooked. After 40 minutes it’s still crunchy. I don’t know how people are getting tender rice in this recipe. Waste of time and ingredients.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Try par-boiling the rice in salted water until it's "al dente", rinse and drain, then add to the pot per the recipe. After 20 minutes of cooking, remove from heat and the rice will continue to absorb liquid when left covered for an additional 20-ish minutes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: When would I add fresh corn to this recipe? TIA

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: IDK where I went wrong, but I needed lots more liquid and lots more time.  Likely due to "operator error," but finished product was wonderful.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: @Patty same. Poor recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I do not know what a Dutch oven is 😭

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: @Victor, it’s a big heavy pot with a kit. Usually cast iron. Sometimes coated with enamel. Mine is my favorite kitchen item. They are fantastic to cook with

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: After the chicken is browned & removed from the pot, there is no need to add more oil. There was plenty of rendered fat from the chicken thighs to sauté the onions, peppers, garlic, etc.  I used brown rice & used 2x the broth & twice the time and it was a great meal!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
This was really delicious! makes for fabulous leftovers as the tastes permeate more (if you like leftovers)
I usually just look at the ingredients and make it to suit our tastes. Loved it!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: if you cook it low enough the amount of broth listed works well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Very simple, easy pantry meal. Aside from the poblano everything was already in the kitchen. That said I found the result to be a bit flat and on dimensional. I might try adding some additional spice to the kitchen next time and perhaps fire roasting some of the produce. Definitely more complicated but will likely address some of the lacking flavor in the original recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I had high hopes but should have read comments before preparing. I think it can be tweaked, but as is, it just lacks flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I made this entirely in my InstantPot. Even steps 1-3, using the Saute function. But after step 3, when I switched it to Pressure Cook, after a few minutes it would display a "food burn" message. I added more chicken broth, stirred it and scraped up the burned bits, and tried again. Same food burn message. So I just shut it off and let it sit in the pot covered for a couple hours. The rice eventually softened up, but it's still very dry. I also used smoked paprika when sauteing the chicken.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: This was really tasty. I added some smoked paprika and aleppo chili powder. My main criticism is that I needed to add way more liquid than what was called for in order for the rice to cook properly.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I make a version of this in my instant pot using canned tomatoes with chilis and leftover rotisserie chicken. It seriously comes together in moments, cooks in minutes and makes a big batch that freezes well. Great served with shredded cheese and sour cream.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I saw this recipe and thought it sounded delicious.  As was mentioned by someone, I too found it bland.  1/2 teaspoon of ground cumin?  I was trying to follow the recipe but had to use more cumin!  The rice to liquid ratio worked fine.  I love the idea of Rotel tomatoes, thanks!  I thought it could have used a squeeze of lime juice, but didn't have one on hand.  Certainly more onion.  Overall, okay.  I would have baked it in the oven but it's nearly 90 here and I don't have central air.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: This was okay. Made recipe exactly (first time). It was bland. I would add more cumin, more onion, more garlic. I did not have any issues with the rice, my rice came out perfect.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Definitely make sure to add more chicken broth so the rice cooks properly. I also added some smoked paprika and more cumin than the recipe called for.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Definitely agree! My rice was a little crunchy after 35 minutes cooking. Otherwise pretty tasty.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I would advise adding at least double the cumin and using native field tomato in season,  otherwise canned puree. The jalapeño and cilantro topping are a must. Chopped green olives with pimento also enhance flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I just made this. In was better in charred tortillas with a little cheese otherwise it was meh.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Can this be done in a rice cooker, after initial prep?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I felt like this needed more flavor so I added 3 squeezed lemons and 1tsp smoked paprika as well as adding half as much more of all the spices.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Hi Friends, I made this twice and it’s a hit! The only issue is that my rice becomes too wet and mushy. I’ve tried reducing the water by 1/4c but it’s still too wet. Can I get your expertise what I should do? I’m not sure if it is because my grated tomato adds too much liquid.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: A tasty dish! Don't omit rinsing the rice, or simply use more broth. We failed to rinse, and it was too dry. But when we reheated the leftovers, adding a bit more liquid, it was delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Made with long grain basmati brown rice.  Pre-cooked in the broth (amount according to brown rice recipe).  Did this concurrent with Step 3.  Came out VERY nice!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
I can't understand how these ingredients came out so, so bland. Made it exactly as written, but now realize I could have likely doubled the cumin and perhaps the oregano. As it was cooking and I was sampling it and realized how weak it was, I found the comment above about adding ancho chile, and tried that. It didn't save it.

Also, if you are using brown rice, double the cooking time (and maybe drop the chicken in later as a result).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I would at least double the cumin and the oregano on the chicken.  Probably sub in a large beefsteak tomato (or two) or perhaps a couple cans of Rotel

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I did everything Greg suggested in other reply and it still did not save it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: The family loved this dish and asked that it be included in the regular rotation.  I skipped the cilantro (because I can't stand it) and the jalapenos (because I have a daughter with Crohn's) and served with sriracha on the side for those of us that wanted a kick.  A winner.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: This is a really nice base recipe. Neither me or my heartburn were interested in adding jalapenos or hot sauce, but a spritz of lime right before eating brightened it up nicely.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is super close to what my Mom in México use to make for us, it is easy and delicious. I don't want to be a pain changing this recipe, but if you feel like having fun try frying the rice along the poblano, garlic, etc...add your tomatoes step later, just get that rice golden color. And for the people with not cooked all way rice just add a little chicken broth or water...I added a cup of beer, like a corona, nothing heavy,it comes out delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Substituted green hatch chili (one can) and canned diced tomatoes (15 oz can) for fresh. Excellent.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: This was OK but agree with comments on lack of noteworthy flavor. Personally prefer the NYT Arroz con Pollo over this.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
Great recipe. I made it with brown rice that I parboiled in plenty water for about 20 minutes, otherwise followed the recipe closely. Only change for next time is to be less timid with seasoning the chicken - and yes, I’ll try the smoked paprika as one commenter suggested.
Re. the liquid to rice ratio, some lids might not be tight fitting/heavy enough and/or the heat is too high, so too much steam escapes after adding the chicken.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is too good! Typically when my friends gets together I cook for everyone and I always decide the menu. When I first cooked it my bandmates straight up told me that this has to stay in the rotation permanently and I have no say. Haha

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: So I bumped the spice level WAY up and it still was pretty bland. Also, not enough liquid (or something). Seems like all of the "one pot" rice dishes end up with rice that is mushy with a crunchy center.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Not my favorite recipe. The recipe came underwhelmingly lacking flavors.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
too much rice
bland
poblano pepper didn't add much flavor

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
Slight mods to recipe
1 3/4 lbs chicken
1 C onion
No poblano
1 tsp cumin 
1 1/2 tsp oregano
2 C beef stock 

Easy and very tasty. Served with cilantro & hot sauce and yogurt. Comfort food!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I doubled the garlic (I pretty much double the garlic in every recipe.) I also like more tomato so I will use more next time. But it is very good as written.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Excellent hearty dinner.  The family loved it.  I followed the recipe as described and served with chopped cilantro and pickled jalapeno slices as suggested, but also served with Mexican crema.  We found the crema added a nice, creamy touch.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: As is this is too bland to eat, even after adding a ton of other spices and some salsa it was still just meh, would never make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I love this recipe! I increased the amount of cumin and oregano. Since I can’t get poblanos where I live, I know I’m missing an additional layer of flavor. Even without them, this recipe is a keeper my family loves.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: This was just OK. Edible, but not great. Followed recipe exactly and everyone agreed it was too bland.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: My rice was way undercooked following the recipe as is, though I didn’t rinse the rice first and that may have contributed to that issue a little bit. I was actually quite shocked that this recipe was not as fool proof as everything else I’ve made using this app. I won’t be using this recipe again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: If you don’t have a tomato but you do have salsa (writing from the American Southwest here)—use a bit of salsa instead!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I needed more liquid to get the rice to cook (and it took 30 min) and I added a lot of red pepper flakes. Other than that I loved it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody:
A rare miss here. First off, the broth to rice ratio is just wrong. My rice didn’t cook. Based on packaging instructions for the rice I used and factoring the extra stuff in the pot, it should have 2.25-2.5 cups of broth. Or, maybe just bump it down to 1 cup of rice. (I think that would come closer to the pictured dish.)

Also, some have said, this is pretty bland as written. Definitely could use some additional spicing and/or a helping of salsa/guac/cheese.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Need to double the broth

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I cheated by shredding a rotisserie chicken and cooking the rice in a rice cooker with chicken stock. Cooked the onion, poblano, garlic, added the oregano and cumin to the tomato/tomato paste, cooked that down, added the cooked rice, beans, corn, and chicken with as much stock as necessary to maintain desired moistness. Was a little bland so added more cumin and some cinnamon to make it more interesting. Delicious and saved time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Very bland—made to recipe. Next time I would add more spice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: The recommended condiments (jalapeño, cilantro, hot sauce) take this to the next level. We also added a squeeze of fresh lime juice on top and Greek yogurt on the side.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I added some smoked paprika for depth. And way more human than called for. Next time I will add lots more salt and/or serve with a wedge of lime

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Followed recipe exactly. Rice was completely undercooked after the 20 min cook time. It required an additional 30 minutes of cooking and additional liquid for the rice to be cooked properly.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Very dry.weak flavor. It needed too much tweaking.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I made half the recipe. Skipped the beans and doubled the corn. Small can of diced peppers instead of fresh. Excellent.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t have a tomato, so used a small can of diced tomatoes. After 20 minutes, the rice was nowhere near done. I added broth and simmered on for 10 minutes. Still not done. Took more broth and 20 more minutes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: A tasty dish that my family loves, but I can’t seem to cook the rice fully without burning the bottom layer. I keep trying to tweak it with little success. Also, I found the whole recipe takes much longer than indicated. The prep alone is fairly extensive.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: Would triple the cilantro and poblano peppers. Ratio seemed off. Added 2 Jalopeno peppers to give more kick. Too much rice- 1 cup would be better.  (Used basmati rice and heard this from others but didn’t modify first time cooking) Otherwise great recipe!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly to the recipe. The good is that it can be on the table in one hour. That said, I found it pretty one note in flavor. The addition of the fresh jalapenos helped as well as hot sauce and a dollop of sour cream. But overall, just a bit flat lined. I think if I were to make it again, I would omit the chicken and make it as a side.  Also, my wife forbids a box grater in my home. :) So I seeded and chopped up the tomato. I think next time I would sub in a can of diced tomato.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I used salsa instead of a tomato. All I had was Tatemada tomatillo salsa and the sour taste really enhanced the carnelized tomato paste!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
commentBody: I feel like the rice to stock ratio is a fair bit off in this recipe, I ended up having to add almost another cup of stock and cook it a fair bit longer than recommended.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Tofu Salad With Honey Chile Dressing Recipe
commentBody:
Tasty and easy to make! I ended up subbing the birds eye chili with regular red pepper flakes, because I couldn't find the former at the first two stores I hit up and didn't feel like running to too many stores. I also ended up baking the veggies and tofu instead of grilling. Just extends the cooking time a bit if you like things on the crispy side.

The vinaigrette is really good, though I do think you could sub in soy sauce for a fully plant-based, if slightly different vibe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Tofu Salad With Honey Chile Dressing Recipe
commentBody: Chile is a country. The peppers are chilis. C’mon NYT.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Tofu Salad With Honey Chile Dressing Recipe
commentBody: You're right that Chile is a country. But...in this context, chile refers to Capsicum peppers, while chili refers to chiles mixed with other spices or a spicy stew made with chiles.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Tofu Salad With Honey Chile Dressing Recipe
commentBody: But now we get to call it Honey CHILE (rhymes with 'style') Dressing :)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Tofu Salad With Honey Chile Dressing Recipe
commentBody:
@Madeleine Sann
Here in the southwest we call the spicy peppers “chile.” Heaven forbid anyone call our green chile “chili.” How gauche!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Tofu Salad With Honey Chile Dressing Recipe
commentBody: My wife is a vegetarian and also allergic to fish. Can I sub soy sauce 1for 1or in some other proportion?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Tofu Salad With Honey Chile Dressing Recipe
commentBody: Your kitchen, your rules, go for it -- but I'd use Tamari instead if you can find it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Tofu Salad With Honey Chile Dressing Recipe
commentBody: I found vegetarian fish sauce at my local asian grocery store. I think you could reasonably sub in soy sauce for the dressing. I made this salad and the dressing was my family's favorite part. The sweet combined with the chilis pleasing.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Tofu Salad With Honey Chile Dressing Recipe
commentBody: the sauce makes it.  added corn from cob to the uncooked components.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Tofu Salad With Honey Chile Dressing Recipe
commentBody: Broiled the  green beans with oil, salt, and pepper for 5 min since I didn’t want to them to fall through my grill grates.  Love the dressing.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Tofu Salad With Honey Chile Dressing Recipe
commentBody: For vegetarians, what can we replace fish sauce with?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Tofu Salad With Honey Chile Dressing Recipe
commentBody: Added dry rub marinade for tofu 1/2 tsp each salt, pepper, smoked paprika, onion powder, coriander, 1/4 tsp each garlic powder, cayenne

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Tofu Salad With Honey Chile Dressing Recipe
commentBody: Used chicken, because that’s what I had on hand- delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Grilled Tofu Salad With Honey Chile Dressing Recipe
commentBody: While this appears to be a vegetarian recipe, I would either eliminate the fish oil or substitute it with another oil such as avocado, sesame or olive oil.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Calamari Salad With Potatoes and Olives Recipe
commentBody: I used TJ tinned calamari and it turned out well

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Calamari Salad With Potatoes and Olives Recipe
commentBody: I made it pretty much exactly as written. It was easy and delicious. Next time I'm going to make it with shrimp.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Calamari Salad With Potatoes and Olives Recipe
commentBody: Quick sautée or roast of the protein provides more flavor than boiling. I used a mixture of calamari, shrimp and bay scallops. Delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Calamari Salad With Potatoes and Olives Recipe
commentBody: Excellent. An easy, tasty, bright and light meal for a hot summer night. Next time I will ramp up the garlic, pepperoncini, and capers, and garnish with parsley

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Calamari Salad With Potatoes and Olives Recipe
commentBody: Made the recipe, no alterations.  Terrific flavor, great summer dish.  Wife requests more potatoes next time.  That’s just per your preference.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Calamari Salad With Potatoes and Olives Recipe
commentBody: Excellent. Quick and delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Calamari Salad With Potatoes and Olives Recipe
commentBody: Didn’t have potatoes or pepperoncini. It was still great as a calamari salad. Used Kalamata olives instead of green olives and a tbsp of capers. Used less of the vinaigrette since there were no potatoes - reserved the rest to use for salad dressing. Light and tasty, great for summer. Packed individual small bowls for a picnic - Kept them in the cooler with the drinks.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Anchovies are the unsung,quiet, heroes of the kitchen. Yum!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Would anchovy paste work in place of the anchovies? Opening a tin of anchovies that might not be used for a while would be a waste.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: @Michael Napa Of course the paste works. But you could always use more of those little fishes. We would use  the whole can in this household.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: The cod will not swim away without whole anchovies in the mix. Anchovy paste is fine.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: @Michael Napa. Another option:  open that jar or tin of anchovy paste.  If a tin, put remainder in a glass container.  make  sure the little fish are covered with olive oil.  Refrigerate.  Last a very, very long time!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody:
This sounds delicious! And also like something I would make without a recipe.

Except for the anchovies. 😊

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Truly more than a sum of its parts! Yum! First, I’m a home cook and I’m the only one who eats fish so I halved everything. A few mods due to what was available. Fish market was out of cod so I used haddock. Subbed anchovy paste, lacking tinned anchovies, and subbed dried oregano since the market didn’t have fresh, Haddock took about fifteen minutes to cook to what I thought was perfect. Easy, quick and so very delicious! Will definitely make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Cooked small white beans (Alubia Blanca from Rancho Gordo).Used about 3 cups plus some of the bean broth, no additional water needed. 2 hake fillets (1 lb. total). Didn't need to add the butter. Complex flavors, really excellent.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful flavors abound. Loved this recipe. A new way to serve cod, which always needs a little boost. Will make again for sure!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Made this for dinner tonight. Only change was using pinto beans instead of cannelli, as that's what I had on hand.  Served with salad and crusty bread as suggested.  Yum and healthy, too.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody:
My husband and I both work from home. In these dog days of summer (fall cannot come soon enough!), I’ve been finding it harder and harder to figure out what to make us for weekday lunch. I decided to open the NYT Cooking app, filter on lunch, and make the first recipe for which I had the ingredients on hand. This was the winner…and I have to say, it’s a winner! I know modifications are annoying (and I really do try not to make any the first time I try a dish), but I do recommend a squeeze of lemon over each serving. 

Also, my cod was frozen (Costco!) and I just threw it on the stew and let it poach for about 20 minutes until it was thawed and perfectly cooked. 

And the best part—my husband says he likes it too!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Made this for dinner tonight.  Only change was using pinto beans instead of cannelli, as that's what I had on hand.  Served with crusty bread, as suggested.  Very tasty and healthy, too.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Made per the isructions, a very nice and easy meal.  The flavors are subtle and lovely.  Could add small amount of paprika, smoked or not, to boost the flavor profile if you'd like

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Why is the fish in the photo lightly browned when it’s supposed to steam with the beans?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: It's sauce

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Very forgiving and flexible recipe! Had cod and some but not all of the ingredients, so mixed and matched - chickpeas instead of white beans, a few shallots for the onion, no anchovies but added capers and olives. Subbed half white wine for water, and used dried oregano and a liberal sprinkle of red pepper. Even with all the modifications, delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Outstanding on a cold damp night!  Mades subs based on what was in hand: olives=anchovies, sun dried tomatoes = paste, and blended canned tomatoes = fresh.  Added baby spinach to fulfill green veggie requirement :-) Had a ball of pizza dough and made foccacia on the side.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe, made greater, if I may, by subbing the water with white wine or fish bouillon. A serious pinch of crushed peppers also heightens the dish adding at step 4. Just me.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Very bland--more anchovies, more oregano!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night. No notes! Fantastic!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: This was good, and easy. The anchovies added umami rather than flavor, making it richer. I mostly followed the recipe, using the tilapia I had on hand instead of cod. I will add more herbs and hope to use  good summer tomatoes next time for more flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Nice. I'm using gigante beans instead of cannellini for more of a Mediterranean vibe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: We enjoyed this fine, but agreed that the "bean-forward" stew overpowered the beautifully poached fish, which should have been the star. Would not repeat this one.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone frozen this to serve later (adding fish at time of defrost)?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!  I didn’t have a carrot and used anchovy paste.  Will be making often!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: The flavor was pretty bland to me—I even added extra tomato paste. My tomatoes were on the unripe side, but still. I wouldn’t make this again as-is.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: @Emily did u add enough salt?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: This was tasty and easy, but I think it needed more herbs for a little bit of punch.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I added two extra anchovies, but I think adding the whole tin would boost flavor even more.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: This did not taste of much, unfortunately.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious and simple. One question: is there a technique I could be deploying to soften carrots in 5 minutes as the recipe suggests? I find it takes considerably longer to soften those guys.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Make sure to pick ripe tomatoes! Folded in spinach with the butter and parsley at the end for some extra greens; gently to avoid mushing the beans.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
commentBody: Just heavenly. The NYT is so reliable when it comes to beans and fish. I used a bit of bouillon (1 tsp) as is my habit when adding water to any stew and likely at least doubled the garlic (which is also my habit) with beautiful and delicate results. I used cod, true to the instructions, dry oregano as I have never come across a fresh oregano sprig, and added some dried basil. Other details: an extra anchovy made it into the pot and I think I had the freshest and sweetest cherry tomatoes of the season (in Arizona). Sorry to brag. This was perfect.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: Simplified this by 1) candying and frying nuts in the same pan: added walnuts to a dry pan, toasted for a bit, then added 2Tbs brown sugar mixed with 2Tbs water until the water boiled off, then added a swirl of avocado oil to the pan to fry the sugar-coated nuts. This worked great. 2) shallow-fried the shrimp in about 1/2” of oil in a cast iron pan. Dipped a shrimp in cornstarch, added it to the pan, flipped the previous shrimp, and removed the second previous shrimp (that had been flipped when the previous shrimp had been added.) 3) I made the sauce after the first fry, before the second fry, so nothing was hot on the pan while mixing it up. It all came out great!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: I love honey walnut shrimp.  But 8 - 10 cups of oil to fry 20 shrimp seems excessive, particularly if done in batches (I will note that I've been cooking for 50 years, and am not a novice).  I will watch comments to see what others have to say.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: @LGL I had the same reaction to all that oil. The other observation I had was this much frying and so many steps to put this together creates a lot of clean up in the kitchen for a weeknight meal. When I think of weeknight meals, clean up after is a factor for me.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: @LGL totally agree! 8 cups of oil is spendy and a big hassle to clean up! This can be pan fried in far less oil.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: If you want to use less oil, use a small saucepan. Try to find one with higher sides. The saucepan part of my 2-quart Farberware double-boiler is what I use. Yes, you will have to fry in multiple batches but deep frying only takes a few minutes, so it’s not a big deal. I only needed about 3 cups of oil for this recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: The final dish here sounds tasty, but I’ll look for comments here about simplifying the process so it’s not so head-scratchingly inefficient. As written it seems unnecessarily time-consuming.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody:
For Sharon Bakes

Look for condensed milk in a resealable tube or squeeze bottle.  Very handy when you want a Vietnamese coffee!

You can also find one ounce packets

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: Aside from rice, broccoli side is a must for honey walnut chicken. Cut to florets, a quick stir-fry with neutral oil and gentle portions of ginger, garlic, cooking wine and salt and an optional light sauce of corn starch slurry, salt, a whisper of sugar and and oyster sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: I once had this dish at a long-since-gone restaurant in NYC and the thing that put it over the top was that it had chunks of honeydew melon mixed in. It may sound odd but it was absolutely delicious! If I find the time to make this rather involved recipe, I’m definitely going to add the melon.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: My local restaurant includes cantaloupe.  It's delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: I am more than happy to use all the oil, dirty all the pans, and follow all the steps if this gets me close to the magical walnut shrimp I ate many times a large round tables in Hong Kong restaurants with other ex-pat friends pre-1997. Those were fun and delicious times!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: This is a dish we order every time we go to Chang Chang or Ni hao, two of our favorite restaurants in DC. While tempting to try it at home, it sure looks like a lot of work. And oil. Looking forward to reading comments from braver folks who try it at home.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: i substituted greek yogurt for mayo and didn’t deep fry the shrimp but instead used a few tbsp of oil to cook both onion (had no scallions on hand) and the shrimp. was delicious. Restaurant quality with those substitutions. 5 stars.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe the other night in our outdoor kitchen due to the residual odor frying leaves in the house. I found the recipe disappointing. In step 2, the walnuts turned out drenched in a sugary syrup that quickly thickened. For frying, I did use a small Dutch oven. Eyeballed the oil, maybe used 1 to 1.5 cup of oil. But when I added the walnuts, the walnut skins shedded leaving behind burnt walnut skins in the oil. When the shrimp was fried the burnt skins attached to the shrimp adding a very bitter flavor to the end result. The sauce flavor was just okay, not very flavorful. The sauce could have been doubled. But overall, I felt the recipe lacked that wonderful flavor enjoyed from a restaurant preparation. I would not make this recipe again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: I let out a slight gasp when I saw the 2T of sweetened condensed milk.  What are everyone’s thoughts on using the rest of the can?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: @Sharon Bakes check out the recipe for Brazilian lemonade on New York Times Cooking. I am happy your post reminded me of it!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: @Sharon Bakes You can use it up making Magic Bars or 7 Layer Bars. They both call for pouring a can of sweetened condensed milk over before putting in the oven.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight as an early Father’s Day dinner - my dad loves honey walnut shrimp. It is not an everyday dinner, and I made quite a mess while putting this together with sides and dessert. That said, it was worth it. Delicious, perfectly crisp shrimp, and the walnuts were so much better than previous versions I have attempted. Our family loved it, and I would definitely make it again. I don’t mind using a good amount of oil for frying in that I cool, strain, and reuse frying oil a few times. I could see that being a deterrent to some though.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: This is a very popular dish in 'fusion' style restaurants. There's no way a kitchen would have that many steps for a dish like this. Got to be a simpler method.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: My initial impression of this recipe is that it must not be authentic - I have not heard of Chinese people cooking with Dutch ovens.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody:
@My name

Probably bc many people who read this app don’t have woks

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: An Asian/Spanish restaurant near me had this on their menu. They changed hands a few years back, the dish is gone. I had it a few times falling madly in love with it, so I tried making it, it came out just ok. I figured it was not the healthiest, but 8cups of oil??? Yikes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: So, at the last minute I found out one of my guests doesn't eat seafood! So, I cut up some chicken thighs I had in the fridge, doubled the sauce, and we had honey walnut shrimp AND chicken. It was GREAT and everyone loved it. I did NOT use that much oil, and shallow fried everything in a cast iron skillet. Otherwise, everything was the same. This was a really special treat of a dish, and not too difficult.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: Suggestions regarding simplification are worth heeding. Another simplification step: get smaller, peeled shrimp (~30ct), and skip butterflying. Smaller shrimp also have more surface area and will keep their crispness well (similar to butterflied large shrimp). They’re also cheaper and skip a fiddly, annoying step.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody:
There are basically two recipes going on here: frying candied walnuts and frying shrimp with a sauce. Scroll around to see Katy M’s shortcut suggestions for the walnuts. 

I’m intrigued to try the double frying technique for the shrimp, and I’m so glad people have reminded me about using the burner outside on my grill to avoid the smell. I’m also always looking for another way to make nuts interesting as a bar-type snack. 

All that said, I actually truly dislike this particular dish, whether it’s from Hong Kong, it’s Chinese-American, or whatever, it’s just really not my thing. 

I’m happy to check out the recipe though, because I could see using the methods with different seasonings and sauces. There is just no way that I’ll ever like sweetened condensed milk mixed with mayo.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody:
@LGL
Cooking mostly for one, I have always struggled with the amount of oil you use each time deep frying. What works for me is a Japanese style enameled pitcher with built in charcoal filter for storing fry oil. That paired with a stovetop Japanese style deep fry pot (oil volume matches pitcher) minimizes waste and keeps the oil well for longer than I anticipated. Sourcing the charcoal filter replacements reasonably is a bit of a challenge.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody:
This has been one of my dad’s favorite dishes for decades. It’s wonderful to be able to make it for him now that it’s harder for him to get out. He reports that this recipe rivals any he’s had in a restaurant. It’s simple enough to make with our little Fry Daddy, and I served it with bro
Additionally, I loathe mayo. Loathe. It. So, I set aside some sauce before adding the mayo and used that for my portion of shrimp. Perfect!!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody:
Made this for the first time as my mother in law was craving Chinese food for Christmas Eve and our favorite Chinese restaurant had closed! Thoughts:
- used Kewpie mayo 
- subbed potato starch for corn starch. ChatGPT tells me this is more ideal and authentic to Asian cuisine. I was impressed with how the crisp held up to the glaze. Perfect crunch.
- my walnut glaze was pretty liquidy. When drying the walnuts I had a lot of sugary debris in the oil which was pretty annoying. I strained it as best I could into a mesh strainer into a narrow and tall pot, did a quick wipe down of the wok with tongs and wet paper towel, then carefully poured the hot oil to the wok leaving the debris that made it through the strainer into the bottom of the pot. Probably a sub 5 min detour quickly handled in the kitchen. The walnuts were fantastic… there are easier ways to candy but the outcome was ideal even though the process was a pain
- butterflying was a pro tip. The shrimp were gorgeous!
- family that didn’t “like” shrimp were joking with my husband about who would finish the last two shrimp
- my husband was skeptical about the idea of making it at home and ate his words ;)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestion about a substitute nut that would work well instead of the walnuts (allergy)?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: You do not need all that oil. Just two tablespoons.  Cook it outside and you will be happy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made this with tofu? Curious if you'd need to adjust seasoning to account for its inherent blandness.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: What a disappointment. So much effort for a bland meal. I followed the recipe as written. I thought the sauce would save the twice-fried shrimp but even the sauce wasn’t great.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody: followed this recipe exactly and it was absolutely delish, worth the clean up which is really not too bad if you have a dishwasher

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
commentBody:
Don't be put off by the amount of oil or the steps. I worked with 1/3 of the volume of oil in a medium sauce pan and it came out well.
Deep frying the walnuts as written did not work well for me. I ended up with burnt sugar bubbles in my oil and a very smokey kitchen. I will follow Katy M's recommendation for the walnuts next time.
We added the cornstarch directly to the shrimp and egg mixture to create the batter which worked very well.
The sauce was the best part. I added salt and lime juice to taste rather than to the recipe, and it was delicious. It helped tremendously that I was using a beautiful homemade version of Kewpie that was already very flavorful.
I'm looking forward to making it again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Seriously so easy and tasty. It feels hearty but not too heavy. I left the potato skins on because I’m lazy and fiber never hurts. Will be adding this to my rotation.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: So delicious! I used sweet potato and added carrots. I only had 300 grams chicken thighs so I upped the mushrooms too. Lastly, used maple syrup instead of sugar. Versatile dish, will make again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Another deeply flavored, delicious dish that the kids inhaled, and that was *VERY* easy to make. The dutch oven braise takes a little extra time, but it's a passive process, making it weeknight-friendly. Definitely plan to make again soon.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Another fantastic recipe from Kay—absolutely delicious! I added about 1 tablespoon of hot red pepper since we like it spicy, and also added carrots and daikon over the potatoes, which brought even more flavor to the dish. It was so easy to make and turned out amazing!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Maybe just a tiny bit sweet to my tastes and will try with half the sugar next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody:
Made a few modifications:
- added potatoes earlier
- closer to a tablespoon of ginger
- more water to begin, longer coverless simmer on the back end

Overall very tasty, definitely needed some more heat. Next time I would cut the sugar by maybe half of a third and add some kind of heat—we ended up finishing with chili oil and chili crisp, which both helped greatly with balance. Would suggest adding maybe a 2 teaspoons or more of red pepper flakes while it’s cooking.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: @Gabe - your suggestions are spot on!  I was going to write the same 😀

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: The mushrooms!!! It soaked up all of the amazing sauce. Next time, I will add more.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: When I sauté mushrooms, I almost always start them all alone for a minute or two before adding anything else. If you keep stirring them, they don't stick, but they do start to brown nicely. I suspect that this approach would prevent them from soaking up all the sauce? (That might be a good thing, depending on how much sugar, salt, and flavor you want specifically in the mushrooms.)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious! Did not have mirin on hand so subbed rice wine vinegar and also just used white mushrooms: worked fine. 

Potatoes needed longer to simmer but I probably did not cut them small enough, but due to the longer simmer it was even more tasty.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: The coq au vin of Korea! Rich, with lots of flavor and texture. Super easy to make. I half-peeled the potatoes and used honey instead of brown sugar. 5 stars!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Next time I make this. I’m going to double the garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and mirin. The flavor was subtle and tasty, however I feel increased volumes of the braise ingredients will amplify the flavor perfectly.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: I added a tbsp of fish sauce for extra flavor and feel like it really pulled it all together

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: delish and easy! I used sweet potato instead of the regular, half the sugar, and added cilantro + a bit of a cornstarch slurry at the end.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: What can I use in place of mushrooms?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Would this work with tofu instead of chicken?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: There's a recipe for Tofu Jorim as well on here. It's on constant rotation in my kitchen!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Why is the sugar added? As someone who doesn’t have any sugar or other sweeteners in my house I am curious why so many recipes call for sugar, honey or syrup to be used in meat dishes.  Besides turning the food into candy what does it add?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: @Aaron Of Gladstone I agree. I avoid sugar as much as possible, and find that often, the dish is just as good.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: @Aaron Of Gladstone sometimes sugar is used to balance the flavor especially when there is soy sauce in a dish. Leave the sugar out if it bothers you but you might be surprised at how much it adds to the dish.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Easy and delicious. Added more soy sauce and Serrano pepper. Jalapeño would also work nicely. Won’t recommend pepper flakes or gochugaru or gochugang or hot sauce. If I had added some carrots, then it would be just like from my Korean childhood.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody:
Easy, great dish that I've made 2x this month. Very good to prep the veggies and sauce components in the AM and finish when you are ready.

I modified by adding more potatoes and mushrooms (like 8oz of mushrooms and 1.5 lb of potatoes) so I could have more leftovers.
doubled the ingredients for the sauce (and doubled the water as well) because sauce is delicious. I did not peel the potatoes.

I browned the chicken and onion a bit before the ginger and garlic so I could render some of the chicken fat.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Followed other’s advice and added carrots, which balanced beautifully. Second time we made it we added a heaping tablespoon of Dijon mustard at the end, off the heat, which was fantastic.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious but no visual appeal.  Either make with more potatoes or skip them & have it over rice.  I thickened the gravy with cornstarch & added spinach at the end.  Baby bok choy would work, too.  Next time, I’ll add some red peppers with the onions.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Very simple and tasty- I used carrots instead of mushrooms and served over rice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Would recommend bone in/skin on chicken thighs and removing the skin to render the fat to cook with (bonus crispy chicken skin to sprinkle on at the end.) Can also cook the bones and then add them into the water and sauces and let it stew to make the water more brothy

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: We enjoyed this. Had no kim chi so should have added a little red pepper for a kick. Next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Excellent recipe, easy and tasty! I made it just as written and think next time I will add a bit of kimchi to the braised chicken just before serving and have additional kimchi on the table. The addition of kimchi takes the dish to a whole new level.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: The potatoes in the photograph are much larger than what’s called for in the recipe so they would take longer to cook.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Less sugar, possibly add spice during cooking or to garnish when plated. Some suggest adding carrots or even daikon.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: not sure if it was just me but i needed like 3x the water this recipe calls for. also added extra soy sauce to match. otherwise turned out well!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Used daikon instead of potatoes and cut sugar in half.  Worked fine.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Double, triple, or quadruple the mushrooms. Otherwise this dish is seriously great, even my 2 year old inhales it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and super easy. I also love having a quick Asian dish on rotation that isn't a stir-fry. With a bowl of hot rice, this is a saucy, comforting alternative and I'm here for it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Made it without mushrooms or potatoes and it was kind of plain. Not enough sauce to full braise the chicken

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: The comments suggesting the addition of carrots are right on. I needed less water than provided by the recipe and added some hot peppers to spice it up. will definitely make it again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: I forgot to cut up the chicken beforehand but I don't think it actually mattered.  It was pretty tender by the end of cooking so we didn't even need knives to eat it!  I doubled the shiitakes and next time I might add some kind of vegetable at the end to make it more of a one-pot-meal.  I made spicy cucumbers as a side dish and that was a nice complement.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
commentBody: Coconut milk in place of water….

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Squid Ink Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Why bother using bottle clam juice when you just use fresh clams. No fresh clam? How about shrimp, scallops or mussels?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Squid Ink Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Yes, just toss in debearded and scrubbed mussels in lieu of the bottled clam juice, cover and steam just until open and they'll release their juice. Fish them out so they don't overcook and reserve to add when you combine the pasta at the end.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Squid Ink Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
You don't need the reserved cup of pasta water. The clam juice and tomato mixture was enough. 
I also doubled the lemon for some extra zing. Delicious dinner!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Squid Ink Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Made this as written and it was lovely! Did it once with the squid ink pasta and once with regular pasta and both were delicious. The second time around we used scallops instead of calamari but we treated the scallops like the shrimp (seared at the start) - they were perfect. Highly recommend.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Squid Ink Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Squid innards can add 30-50% to the weight of the squid.  I’m assuming the 1/2 pond specified here is for squid AFTER cleaning, yes?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Squid Ink Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight and it is without a doubt the best dish I’ve ever made. I’m actually depressed no one else was home to witness (rather, taste). I have never cooked mussels before, and felt slightly sorry for them as I was scrubbing the shells, but I added them in at the step for clam juice. WOW they added that magic flavor! I will never need to go to a restaurant again!!! This dish overall is absolutely divine!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Squid Ink Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I have my doubts about the shrimp browning at all in just a minute, in which case why not just start with bite-sized shrimp, only cooked at the end? Less work and less expensive.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Squid Ink Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Maybe I am missing something but, if you don’t use squid ink pasta and use plain pasta (given as an option), how would this then be “squid ink pasta”?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Squid Ink Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
@Lucie it would
Be just regular pasta cooked - prepared like one would ink pasta.  ;)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Squid Ink Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Yes, just toss in debearded and scrubbed mussels in lieu of the bottled clam juice, cover and steam just until open and they'll release their juice. Fish them out so they don't overcook and reserve to add when you combine the pasta at the end.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Squid Ink Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Loved this! Subbed fresh clams for shrimp, and added fresh mint and julienned sundried tomatoes to enhance depth. Even better the next day.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Squid Ink Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Where I live, there isn’t easy access to clam juice, so I made an anchovy broth (dried anchovies, kombu, green onions, water) as a substitute and it added an amazing umami! Also zested the skin of the lemon I used for juice to add some freshness. Definitely recommend it!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Squid Ink Pasta Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious!  Made this tonight for guests who loved it.  I substituted red onion for the shallot since that’s what I had on hand.  Definitely more than 4 servings! 8 easily if paired with a salad and a piece of crusty bread.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Squid Ink Pasta Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t have a shallot so I used a small onion. I don’t have clam juice but I did have a small can of whole clams in juice so I used that. I didnt have any squid, either, and it turned out great! My family loved it. It’s an easy recipe and relatively quick to make. It took me about 35-40 mins total.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Squid Ink Pasta Recipe
commentBody:
This came out wonderfully made mostly as written. Couple changes: 
- I had fresh littleneck clams so I added those in lieu of the clam juice (2lbs). Took them out as they opened, removed the clams from their shells, and added the clam meat back in at the end with the pasta. They released enough liquid that I did not need to add any pasta water either. 
- I cooked the sauce in a skillet large enough to add everything else to at the end, I don't see the point in transferring the "tomato mixture" from one pan to another and bringing back to a boil when it was already happily simmering where it was. 
Other notes: 
- Throughout the entire recipe, there seemed to be more liquid in my pan than described. No browned bits to scrape up etc. Still delicious. 
- Also don't understand par-cooking the shrimp at the beginning; shrimp cook so quickly that I think I'll just add chopped raw shrimp at the end with the calamari next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Tanghulu (Candied Fresh Fruit Skewers) Recipe
commentBody: My teen loved this sugar on sugar treat but it wasn’t my thing. Tasted like sugar, flat, prefer fresh fruit alone. Took longer to reach ideal cracking level temperature, despite 300 degrees. Had to wait longer. Fun one-time project.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Tanghulu (Candied Fresh Fruit Skewers) Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is really fun and easy. It took a couple of tries (the first one burned) they are all so delicious! 😋😋

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Tanghulu (Candied Fresh Fruit Skewers) Recipe
