author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Tanghulu (Candied Fresh Fruit Skewers) Recipe
commentBody: This was ridiculously easy to do, very clear instructions, first time was a great success and it was all so quick! We did green table grapes (always try one at the store to figure out which grape is best), strawberries, and mandarin oranges. We did it with the neighborhood kids and had a blast right after the bus stop. I was over here thinking this would take a couple tries, and I would have mess ups. Nope! Perfection. Tastes exactly like what we got in NYC last week.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Added a thinly sliced fennel bulb along with the leek, also pumped up the garlic a bit. It was delicious! We had it with noodles and loved it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: @Mark Amen. I used potatoes and half and half, then threw in a half cup or so of white wine. It was delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: An amazing alternative to beef stew. So much lighter and more refined. Wonderful, simple, and delicious. Crusty bread to sop it up is highly recommended!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! My only regret was adding the cream at the end. It turned the beautiful brown stew an unappetizing white/beige color. Some kitchen bouquet helped correct it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: @Tom I think the cream important from a flavor/texture perspective. Close your eyes if you don’t like the color, lol.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: @Tom add a teaspoon of Kitchen Bouquet after adding the cream. You’ll have a nice golden brown gravy. And the flavor will be delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: THis is a riff on a blanquette de veau and the light colour - and the refined taste - is what distinguishes it from a more fundamental stew.  You certainly don't have to add the cream but your antipathy to the traditional white colour of this meal and preference for colouring dark is puzzling

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: If you don’t want to make 2 lbs of meat I believe boneless “country ribs” are actually pork shoulder. Sometimes hard to find but Safeway seems to always have them.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Boneless 'country ribs' are thick cuts from a boneless loin of pork; ie. the the loin was separated from the ribs before cutting into thick boneless chops. Then the meat is broken down further by cutting the 'chop' into 2 pieces, each somewhat like a rib shape. There is more fat on country ribs than on tenderloin, but pork shoulder fat is better distributed and cooks down to a more 'fall-apart' texture. Tenderloin would be a distant third best option for braising. I speak from experience.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: “Country Ribs” are from the end of the loin near the shoulder and cut in strips to look “rib-like”. They make an excellent substitute for shoulder when you don’t feel like buying a big shoulder roast.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: What would be a good dairy free sub for the heavy cream for those with an allergy? I know coconut milk is pretty common but I don't think that flavor would do well here.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: @Janis I think you could easily use a dairy free half-and-half here, dairy- free butter alternative, or even just an extra glug of olive oil.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: @Janis  Perhaps some cashew cream? It’s easy to whip up and pretty tasty and thickening in most soups. It’s the kind of thing you might want to try to see how it works.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: @Janis could sub cashew cream.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Did this in an instant pot to save time. Browned the pork in batches on Sauté setting. Removed the pork cubes, sautéed and seasoned the leeks, garlic, mushrooms and carrots to heat them up. Stirred in the broth, rosemary and a couple of thyme sprigs. Didn’t have a Parmesan rind on hand. Covered and set to manually Pressure Cook for 18 minutes. Let sit for 10 minutes and then used the handle of a wooden spoon to depressurize fast. I fished out the rosemary and thyme stems—the thyme leaves fell off in the cooking process. I turned on Sauté and stirred in the buerre manie, let it cook for a minute, then turned off the power and finished with half and half — to cut the calories. Delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody:
Ah there’s the comment I was looking for!

I’ll give this a try, thanks!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Could you use pork chops for this recipe?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Lots of people asking if you can use pork tenderloin or other leaner cuts of meat. In my experience the pork shoulder when slow cooked is a lovely tender pull apart meat. I would think a pork tenderloin would not "fall a part" in quite the same way but I'm willing to try it and find out if it's still delicious. Will report back.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody:
@Carol
I made it with a pork tenderloin the first time cause that’s what I had in the fridge. Since 1.5 hours in the pot would kill the tenderloin I gave the pieces a hard sear on one side and put it aside. I cooked everything else as the recipe calls for then added the pork at the end and cooked it for another 15 minutes. I made it again  with shoulder and liked the tenderloin version better. Not as porky but lighter. The tenderloin was closer in texture to veal.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Can this be prepared the day before serving?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: It would probably taste even better as most stews taste better the next day.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: @Ronni  Others may weigh in…I would prepare a day ahead but add the cream once slowly reheated and top with fresh herbs at time of serving.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Could pork loin be substituted for pork shoulder? Or would it be too lean?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Would anyone know how to adapt this recipe to a crock pot?  It's summer and too hot to turn my stove on for two hours!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Five Stars!! Used cubed pork shoulder because its the tastiest cut these days and I added 1/4 t. Red pepper flakes. Otherwise followed the recipe but put the covered pot in a 300° oven for an hour, adding carrots for an additional 30 minutes. Good enough for guests!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I was worried about the high fat in pork shoulder, so trimmed a lot off. I made it exactly as written except white culinary chicken broth instead of plain chicken. It was tasty but if I make again, will reduce salt, add more fresh herbs to serve.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Very good. Used country ribs. Used 2 leeks and 8 oz of mushrms. Deglaze with white wine after garlic. 3 cups broth is plenty. Add Tbsp vinegar before beurre manie. Served over noodles, topped with parsley and chives. With salad and bread, worthy of special meal.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Is there a slow cooker version of this recipe? Would love to try it!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: This is a good basic recipe.  I included a thinly sliced fennel bulb with the leek (as sugggested by Mark) and the sauce was delicious.  However the meat still tasted bland.  Blanquette de veau calls for more herbs, including thyme, onions, and white wine, or dry vermouth.  Would the dish be more flavorful  with those additions?  Looking for opinions.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: All tips aside, it’s perfect as is. I’m embarrassed to say I was maybe 60 (a decade ago) when I learned the value of a Parmesan rind, now one is never discarded here! Really a super recipe. Sure, it takes time, but much of it is time you can do other things. I’ve done laundry, fed horses, wasted time on Netflix, etc etc.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: When cooked as written, this is a super delicious way to introduce some variation in your stew menus.  When the weather is cold, nothing beats a stew - beef stew, veal stew, pork stew...having variation (with tomatoes or not, with red wine or not, chicken stock or not, with celery or not) makes each one seem like a delicious new discovery.  I keep them all in my rotation.  Not include the cream?  Have you lost your minds??  And be sure to chop some herbs for the finish.  It elevates.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody:
Hmmm. Recipe geeking here.

Why buerre manié versus butter paste? Does it make it taste better? 

I understand a term like roux--it's much shorter than its description, which is a whole recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: @Blue cowboy - a roux by any other name is still a roux. I don’t get it either!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: @Blue cowboy Roux and Beurre Manie are very different things. Both are fat and flour, but BM requires butter, where a roux can be made with another form of fat such as oil. The main difference is that BM is added raw to your dish, where a roux is cooked for several minutes, then the sauce or soup base slowly whisked into it. You would never dump a cooked roux into a finished stew as you do with the Beurre Manie in this recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone adapted this recipe for an instapot? Thank you.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody:
I haven’t found it in a comment but I’m going to try it. I’m thinking start it in a pan on the stove, then when you get to the part where you cover it, dump it in the instapot, cook for 20 minutes with pressure? 

I’ll ask my roommate, he’s the master of the InstaPot. But since all of these ingredients are readily accessible I’m definitely going to give it a try.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Made this in an instant pot for 20 minutes with quick release. Thanks to the first post for the suggestion. I used 3 cups of broth and it was too much. Next time, I’ll use two. Delicious recipe! Despite the 2 pounds of meat, after trimming, it really is just four portions, and we are not big eaters.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody:
This was simply wonderful! I didn’t need to cook it for 90 minutes. By 60 minutes, my pork shoulder was sooo tender. The added cream makes the whole dish so rich and comforting to eat. I had a rich homemade chicken broth which I used.

I can not get real leeks where I live, but I could get a large Naga Negi/long Japanese onions similar to leeks but with a different taste. I added more button mushrooms as I like lots of them in a dish like this. It was perfect. And the Rosemary and Parmesan rind are a must! The resulting sauce is ambrosia! We ate it with a baguette but I will try fettuccine or a short frilly pasta that will pick up lots of sauce next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: We loved this recipe: I added small potatoes to the stew and did not serve it on noodles. I only had 1.3 lbs of pork stew meat. Due to a dairy intolerance, I skipped the parmesan rind and used NutPods "creamer" instead of cream, though I did use real butter for the beurre manié (for flavor, since the dairy intolerance can handle a tiny bit of dairy). After bringing it to a boil, I let it braise in the oven at 325F for 1 1/2 hours. Wonderful!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Delicious recipe as written. The only change I made was to deglaze the pan with a little white wine right before adding the garlic. I just couldn’t let all that fond go to waste!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: The pork fell apart! Simply phenomenal. A show stopper that will impress a crowd!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Followed Jonquil’s Instapot suggestions. Absolutely delicious, but next time I would reduce liquid to 3 cups. Used a large rosemary sprig and it was perfect.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: @llw I did the Instant pot too, and thought 2 cups would be more than enough!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: 5/5! I took another commenter's idea of adding a fennel bulb but otherwise followed the recipe to a T. Trimmed the fat off the pork shoulder a bit but it still had plenty of flavor. Final result looked a bit like beef stroganoff. It was delicious! Will definitely make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I learned a valuable cooking lesson… never use purple carrots in a stew or sauce.  Despite the delicate braised pork and blend of spices and herbs and hearty broth, it was purple, like squid ink purple. I did use a homemade pork broth made from pork chop dinner bones and it was excellent!  Other than purple carrots no deviation from the recipe although adding other ingredients is pretty okay.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: This was so much better the second day. Next time I’ll try the addition of fennel.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: how would you do this in the pressure cooker? Instant pot

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe

recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I've made a pork stew that calls for pork shoulder with pork tenderloin. It's good enough and won't take as long to cook.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: You can buy dairy free heavy cream at most grocery stores.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I hate mushrooms.  What can I sub? Or can this be made well just leaving them out?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Could you substitute a beef roast of some kind? I just have a lot of them in freezer and these flavors sound nice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with Turkey thighs?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody:
I would make a roux out of the butter and flour to ensure no raw flour taste.  

4 cups of stock was too much.  It ended up watery.  Start with 2 cups and see how it goes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Amped up veg by using three carrots and tossing in some sliced raw asparagus for the last 5 minutes of simmering. We also used a mix of sour cream and heavy cream to more closely ressemble creme fraiche.  We served it over gemelli pasta as that was what we had on hand.  A great, colorful braise for spring!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Sorry, I guess I’m a novice. How much of a “Parmesan rind”?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Excellent.  I followed the recipe except I used baby carrots that I added during the last 45 minutes of a 1 hour, 45 minute cook on low.  Carrots were perfect.  The only tweak I'd make is cooking the mushrooms longer so they carmelize more. Crusty bread is great for sopping the sauce.  Used fresh snipped chives from my windowsill.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Doubled the recipe but skipped the cream entirely and added uncooked pasta (I used gluten free shell) into the pot to soak up the voluminous chicken broth. Came out wonderfully and we're still eating on the dish days later. It just keeps getting better!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Country style ribs as sold in most supermarkets are typically cut from the shoulder not the loin. To aid in identifying the right cut to use here look at the price per lb. Shoulder cuts are less expensive,  delicious and loaded with fat that breaks down during the cooking process resulting in a tender, flavorful cut of meat.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I made this for the first time, and I really want to like this recipe, but it was lacking flavor and the sauce didn't thicken enough (or hardly at all, really) after I added the flour and butter mixture.  I might try thickening the leftovers and see what happens.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Yes, “country-style” ribs are sometimes cut from the loin but for this recipe you’ll want to purchase “ribs” that are cut from the shoulder or the butt. More fat, better taste! Great recipe as written a keeper for sure.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight. Followed recipe but baked it in pie plate. Phillo comes in 2 packages so that was helpful.  Delicious and would make again.  Thank you.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: It’s the best recipe on the website I’ve cooked thus far. Pork is ridiculously tender. Careful not to over salt it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: We used bone in lamb shoulder. After trimming the meat off the bones made a broth instead of chicken stock. Delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: The next time I make this, I’m going to cook it the night before I plan to eat it. On the night I made it, it was very good, but the leftovers on the next night were out-of-this-world delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody:
Made this last night. Wegman's sells 2.xlb pork shoulders that aren't labeled as roasts, but it worked perfect for this with a bit of additional trimming. I served myself a massive bowl on egg noodles and probably ate it faster than anything else I've cooked in the last few months. I'd say I have an easy 2 large meals still left.

The butter/flour didn't seem to really do much, I think I'm going to double it next time around and hope for more body.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Made this with fresh leeks from the garden - delicious, but much better the next day. Next time I serve this, I’ll make it the day before. Also, chunky carrots, not slices are an important ingredient.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I doubled the recipe (other than the stock, but added a glug of white wine) and did it in the slow cooker without browning the meat. After 6 hours on low, I strained the liquid, added some cornflour instead of the butter and flour, reduced it by a quarter, then added the cream. It was excellent. 5/5 from the family.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Horrible stew, very disappointing... I wanted a wonderful autumn dish and it sounded so delicious! I made it exactly as the recipe called for and it was still thin and sad, despite using prime ingredients. I do not recommend this in any way. I have never ever given a NYT recipe such a bad review.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Very good as (almost) written :) Based on comments, I doubled the leeks and added another large clove of garlic. We served over mashed potatoes. We will definitely make this again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Why not use a butt instead of a shoulder?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I made it last night and it was delicious. I agree that the cream isn't needed. Next time, I'll add some port wine. My pork shoulder was tender after 1.5 hours.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody:
This is delicious! The only thing I changed was gluten-free flour (no effect) and used beef broth and water b/c I grabbed the beef one by mistake. 3/4 people in my family loved it—4th one has Celiac and lots of sensory issues with foods like mushrooms. 
Do take the timing seriously. The full simmer time really makes a difference in the pork and the overall married pot of flavor. The leeks melt into the sauce and the pork is delectable so tender. As with all NYT Cooking time advisories, do your prep and then start the clock.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Oh my, this was one of the best recipes. My pork was so tender and succulent after 2 hours. The depth of flavor of the broth was amazing. I used less broth than the recipe calls for, since many commenters found the dish too thin. I think 3 cups is about right. I deglazed with some white wine. Other than that I followed the recipe exactly and am glad I did. I thought the beurre manié and touch of heavy cream provided the perfect finishing touches. Am sad for those who left off the cream!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone else found that the sauce is a bit thin? I thought I followed the recipe closely, although I was a little short on mushrooms. I also had to supplement the open chicken stock I had in the fridge with water and Better Than Boullion. I wanted to serve this over egg noodles but I’m afraid it doesn’t have the viscosity to cling. Should I add more flour and butter?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Made this exactly according to the recipe, and it was good, not great.  Although tasty, it was a bit on the bland side, and the sauce was not as thick as I would have liked.  Definitely needed some bread to sop up the sauce, which largely slid off the noodles. I may make this again, but will use more carrots (and might try turnips or rutabaga) add some white pepper, and use more flour to thicken the sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I wonder if this could be made, at least in part, in an insta-pot ? Has anyone tried it? It sounds wonderful.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Can this recipe be made using chicken breasts?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: @dianne I think chicken breasts do not take to long cooking times. It would end up very very dry, I fear.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I would love to try this and my electric pressure cooker. I could brown meat first and then does anyone have advice on what setting (high or low pressure) and for how many minutes?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I need to make this dairy free for a dairy intolerance. Instead of cream, I'll use unsweetened/unflavored NutPods as I usually do, with some flour if it needs more thickening. But does anyone have any idea what the Parmesan rind adds to the recipe and how I might replicate it without dairy?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: @BayCook, for this recipe, I think the Parmesan rind adds a good umami punch. So, you could maybe sub in some mushroom powder or browned tomato paste (~1 Tbls).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Is there a non-dairy substitute for the heavy cream?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I use cashew cream for a dairy sub. It works really nicely in most recipes. Soak raw cashews in boiling water for 30 minutes then blend them until smooth.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I’d like to make this, but unfortunately I’m a mushroom hater. When I make Coq au vin and other stews/braises that call for mushrooms, I generally just leave them out. But is there something else you’d suggest adding instead, just to keep some complexity?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Could this be place in an oven? At what temp? And for how long?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: @Patrick definitely works out well in a 300° oven for one hour. Then add the carrots for an additional 30 min. I poured the liquid into a fat separator and made a gravy with 2 T. Butter, 2 heaping Tablespoons of flour plus 2 cups of the hot defatted liquid. Added about 1/4 C. Light cream and more salt and pepper to taste. Fabulous.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I expect lamb would make a good substitute?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: made this.  It was great but very rich for a spring evening. i might try it again without the beure manie, and just put some cream on the table so people can add as they like. the flavors were terrific

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Only had a 1lb pork tenderloin, brined it for 3 hours prior to starting this recipe.. had extra mushrooms.. and extra carrot.  Passed on the heavy cream, as I didn't have any.   Came out good.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: We found this pretty bland and the sauce a bit thin. 3 stars at the most.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Excellent.  Did it in the multi/slow cooker.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I used four leeks, 24 oz shiitake and Bella mushrooms, 6 cups duck broth, and diced the carrots. Followed recipe with the above changes and served it at a party last night. It was fabulous and very easy!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: This is super satisfying. I keep planning to add some chopped kale in my leftovers, but haven't yet. I suspect that will be delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Wow, made as is in a cast iron Dutch oven except I used just a splash of 1/2 & 1/2 instead of cream. Used boneless country ribs. Served over noodles.  Very delicate flavor- recipe is def a keeper.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!  A few people asked about slow cooker - I used it because it's hot here this week. Seared pork as directed in large cast iron pan; transferred to slow cooker.  Sauteed leeks, mushrooms, and garlic in the same cast iron pan, except I deglazed the pan with white wine to make sure I got all the little bits.  Transferred to slow cooker, added broth, carrots, herbs, parmesan rind, and cooked on high 4 hours.  Completed Step 5, and served over noodles. Came out very well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Preparing 2lbs of meat for 4 servings makes the splash of cream a nutritionaly irrelevant addition. It's a good recipe, and can be done in advance - giving you time to invite a few friends over.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious, I added a few more carrots then asked for but other than that... recipe is pretty much the same.  Egg Noodle tip, salt the water so it's like sea water then cook... what a game changer that is.  Everyone had seconds... so little effort really, but planning is needed I prepped everything at noon, and then took 30 minutes to assemble it 2 hours before we planned to eat.  So read the timings on the recipe so you dent' rush a step.  Braising requires time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: This was time consuming and resulted in a dish that was bland in flavor (despite adding extra rosemary and some white wine), with pork that was rubbery and unappealing despite giving it extra time to cook.  I will never make this again.  Yuck.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I had exactly the same result and reaction. The meat drowned in all the broth, and the flavor was icky.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: can you freeze once you've stirred the cream in?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody:
Wow this was fantastic. I started with the best of intentions to follow the recipe, but alas, some detours had to be made due to disappearing (and found) ingredients. 

I used very old dehydrated mushrooms as the fresh ones got used in another dish by mistake and I was too lazy to go get more. I used a combo of rehydrated mushroom water and water from boiling potatoes for another recipe with some Better Than Bouillon chicken base for the broth. I had some leftover chickpeas that needed a home, so they went in. A forgotten bunch of fresh collard greens were found, so they went in too! I didn’t have any Parmesan rinds, but found some other rinds, so used that instead. I recently banned heavy cream because the unused portion would get whipped up and enjoyed by me, and I need to lose weight, so I skipped it entirely. I ended up buying 1.7kg of meat, so I increased the leeks and rosemary. I also trimmed quite a bit of the fat from the well marbled pork shoulder. 

Despite all that, it still tasted very rich and flavourful, and was ridiculously delicious. We had it with buttered noodles the first time. The second time, I made freekeh with some sautéed onions, garlic and Better than Bouillon chicken base. I liked it better with the freekeh. 

Really a fabulous meal, and will be on the rotation going forward.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Making this for company in a few days. Can any of this be prepared a day ahead.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Sure. Start it the day before you plan to serve. I would say you can complete everything through Step 3. Put it in the refrigerator overnight, then reheat and continue with Step 4 to completion.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: We had farmers market lamb stew meat from Truest Farms, so we used what we had on hand- and it was fabulous! We also had to sub yoghurt for cream at the end. Overall, an amazing meal we will do again! We did the veg chopping in the morning, did a long bike ride, had cocktails during the long simmer. Thanks, Times!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, tender but thin despite the beurre manie.  As often, as I was adding the broth, I was thinking "this seems like too much liquid", so next time, I will decrease by about 1/2 cup. I substituted the heavy cream (my husband can't tolerate it) with non-dairy heavy cream substitute but that seemed neutral, so I think next time, I will just omit it. I will definitely make it again.  It seems like a great meal for a crowd!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Eating with children who needed to deal with fatty bits and “boing” due to a well-marbled piece of pork shoulder.  Next time I’ll just cut into 2-3 large pieces that are cooked longer (3 hours?) and can be de-fatted and de-boinged and shredded before serving.  Would add carrots for last 1.5 hours of cooking and maybe throw in some frozen peas.  We ate it with boiled potatoes due to another egg noodle dish later in the week.  Skipped the cream, but would add it if serving over egg noodles, which I think we would do next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Silk brand makes a non-dairy heavy whipping cream, I used it and it was a great dupe!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Ugh this was so fabulous. Kay Chun has NEVER let me down but this was especially scrumptious. I may sub it in for my traditional Thanksgiving Eve beef stew (sorry Kenji). It’s just that good!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Very good, though not quite worthy of five stars. Made exactly per the recipe but used a pork tenderloin. Served with noodles, sauce was delicious but next time I might double the butter/flour to make it a bit thicker sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight with 2 variations- half and half instead of cream, and I added a can of rinsed cannellini beans. It was delish! I think it would also be great with veal stew meat

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Definitely a keeper! As @Mark suggested I added a fennel bulb with the leek. I also doubled the garlic, swapped 1/2 c brandy for part of the broth, and added peas at the end. Served with rosemary focaccia. Great.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Forgettable, unfortunately. Followed the recipe exactly.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I cooked this recipe as instructed and I loved the soupy sauce but the pork shoulder pieces came out pretty dry. Is there some modification to the recipe that I could try that would result in more moist meat?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: had the same result after the 1.5 hour simmer - so I kept simmering. Took about 4 hours to get the meat moist and tender, but the result was great!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Cooked following the recipe and served over egg noodles.  It came out great!  Just for a twist, next time I make it I may top it with drop dumplings -like the ones I use for Chicken & Dumplings.  It would make it heartier for the cold winter months!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: We really, really enjoyed this meal but I found it to be a bit thin.  I used some corn starch to thicken it up.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, but the cook time is way off. After simmering for 1.5 hours, the meat was dry and tough... To get the pork tender and juicy, a very long simmer is needed - mine took about 4 hours, but the results were worth the wait

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Can you make this in the crock pot?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was incredible but didn't necessarily need the cream. I tasted it prior to adding the cream and it was already rich and wonderful. I can't wait to make this again, thank you!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: This was an amazing dish as written!  I cooked it very slowly for at least four hours—the pork was incredibly tender.  I did add parsley for serving.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe except I subbed lean beef chuck roast for the pork since I had several roasts in my freezer from my CSA. It was absolutely delicious and pretty easy - just leave yourself plenty of time to braise the meat until tender and let the flavors meld.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t have a leek so I used two small yellow onions. It turned out great. I also did not have fresh rosemary so I used dried. I have celiac and used gluten free flour and gluten free noodles. Honestly do not use gluten free noodles to serve with just get some bread, it’s too easy to taste the noodles

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: This was maybe 3-4 stars the first time around. But leftovers were a definite 5 stars! Something about braised meat dishes… second day is always better!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Really yummy. I used non-dairy cream

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Made as written, it was delicious. I might edit to say “chicken broth to cover,” as sauce was quite soupy and I ended up doubling the beurre manie, but that was not a biggie. Definitely recommend adding the cream for both flavor and texture.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Delicious flavor and super tender pork. Was a bit brothy so next time I will decrease by a cup and see what happens. The fennel suggestions sound delicious as well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody:
We made sure to give the pork a thorough browning for crisp edges, which took more time, but the results were phenomenal. 

We also added fresh parsley to brighten up the flavors, and plenty of Parmesan. It was absolutely delicious. And even better the second day!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: The meat alone took me at least 20 minutes to prep (honestly, why bother putting a prep time in when it never reflects reality?). Cooking time was accurate though, with it taking almost the full 1.5 hours for the meat to be fall-apart tender.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Change nothing except double the recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Like others, I found the sauce to be a bit thin, even though I made a half recipe but used the full portion of beurre manié - easily remedied by allowing the stew to cook for a while at the end with the cover off. I was impressed with how tender the pork became, even though i had used loin steaks cubed, rather than pork shoulder. I'm still unclear about what constitutes "a parmesan rind" - how big???

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent with chopped spare ribs and served on wide egg noodles. I didn't have a Parmesan rind on hand, so I added a 1/2 tsp of Better Than Bouillon roast beef base, for a similar umami punch.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: I will definitely try this dish, as it sounds very appetizing.  I know I am probably in the minority by requesting this, but I wish that some of the ingredients that may be unfamiliar to readers be marked with an asterisk and/or an explanation provided as to what they are.  For example, ponzu.    I know I can Goggle these, but perhaps these recipes take a lot for granted with readers' familiarity with some ingredients.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: @Beverly I always take their use of unfamiliar ingredients as an invitation. Something new! They’re trusting their audience. I don’t choose to feel alienated by something I’m not immediately unfamiliar with.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: @Stacey I wish they would offer a tip for what can substitute for ponzu. I doubt it’s available locally and I have having an item I use only for one recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody:
Confession: I did not follow the recipe because I do not like overcooked broccoli and my salmon quality was okay but not amazing. 
Approach: I cooked the elements separately, then combined. The dressing is very good and key to the dish. It was interesting to combine these flavors with quinoa...it felt vaguely Peruvian. There is an idea here that I will return to.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: @Lynne this is the way! I followed the recipe hoping to learn something but wish I trusted my gut which is what you described here

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Directions are unclear as to what to do with the broccoli after it’s cooked in Step 1. I assume it’s removed from the pot and added back to the bowl to serve, but it’s not mentioned at all in the recipe after step 1.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: @KC It is cooked in with the quinoa.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: won't the broccoli get overcooked, if it's cooked with the quinoa?  It seems to me that one ought to remove the cooked onions/broccoli and add it back at the end.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: @Monica D just made it, the broccoli was definitely over cooked and there was plenty of water left over in the pot as well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: I didn't trust that both the broccoli and salmon wouldn't get overcooked, so I roasted the salmon in the oven and steamed the broccoli separately and slapped everything together at the end. it was fantastic. The tahini ponzu sauce is *chef's finger kiss*. I almost didn't buy the almonds, but I'm glad I did - they added a satisfying crunch. This one is going into the regular rotation.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight. We enjoyed it. Used chives as well. The almonds give it a nice flavor and crunch to what otherwise would have been rather single textured. Good balance of flavors with the tahini sauce and lemon.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: I've never used or bought Ponzu sauce.  Is there a substitute? I hate to buy something I'll rarely use.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: @Julie Ponzu substitute half and half soy sauce and lime juice, change proportions to taste.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Going to make it tonight, but definitely will not be cooking the broccoli 13-15 minutes!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight for a group and it was a huge hit! Used chives and dill for the herbs, may try cilantro next time. Didn't have any fresh lemons, but that might have added some more punch. Will definitely use this again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: This was a hit even with just fresh chives for herbs.  I used squeezable garlic instead of fresh, didn't notice any difference.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: I'm cooking this now. The brocolli is super overdone and I've not added the salmon yet!  I am waiting for the quinoa to cook but don't think there was enough water in the recipe.  And steaming it isn't making it cook as far as I can tell.  I'll stick in the salmon and cook but I don't think this will be very nice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Cooked per directions. Broccoli was way overcooked. Overall it was fine but won’t be making this recipe again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night and really liked it. The sauce is especially good. (I found a recipe for Ponzu online with ingredients I had on hand.). However next time I make it I will deconstruct it. Skinning raw salmon is no fun, so I will cook it separately. And, I will just roast the broccoli.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: @Deborah you can slowly pour near-boiling water over salmon skin and it will shrink and pull off easily.  There are YouTube videos out there for reference.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Broccoli is wildly over cooked. Separate from quinoa. Further quinoa doesn't like fats when cooking, water or stock only. Again sauce is tasty, but gummy. I'll do it again, but my way.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: I think the broccoli has to be cook separately or the water is too much. I still have soup in my pot and the broccoli is mushy. Not sure what I did wrong but it turned out very mushy for me.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Is it possible to sub the quinoa for rice?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Yes, but the quinoa cooks in half the time as white rice. I'm leaning toward cooking either the rice or the quinoa separately, then add oil to the pan, add the starch and some water to help steam the broccoli and onions, and let the fish steam under the lid.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: The author notes that quinoa is gluten-free, but she calls for Ponzu sauce, which contains soy sauce, which contains gluten. It would be wonderful if more recipe writers offered truly gluten-free substitutions/options in their recipes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Big Tree Farms sells a soy free/gluten free version made with coconut aminos and it's delicious!  I have found it at some Whole Foods when travelling. (We don't have a WF in my area).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Tasty and easy. BUT ( I can’t believe I’m saying this ) I’d cut both the almonds and Tahini down by 1/3. It made a healthy dish somewhat heavy

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody:
Everyone who said the broccoli is overcooked is right. Cook it separately. 

For those who adjusted the dressing to be less tahini-heavy, I would like to know what you did!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: I may have missed it, but I don’t see any indication of sesame used in this recipe

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Is the ponzu the pure citrus variety or the citrus/soy combo? Can anyone clarify?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe is one of those perfect examples of "use your best judgement when following a recipe". Cooking broccoli for 5 minutes on their own and THEN boiling them for another 15 minutes is obviously going to nuke them. Take em out! 

I also don't really like the way boiled/steamed salmon turns out, so I tossed it in the oven instead. Mix it all together at the end and it's an awesome recipe (provided you're able to tweak it a bit)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: I made it straight from the recipe, which was VG! As others have said, the sauce is spectacular! I made my own ponzu the day before from lemon juice and soy sauce and fish flakes and kelp and rice vinegar and mirin- easy. I could see taking the broccoli and onions off and putting them back in at the end, but I did not find it to be over cooked. I could also see adding quarter rounds of water chestnuts or some chopped bamboo shoots to give more crunch. I could also see using sesame oil instead of olive oil for a more Asian flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Yes, the broccoli becomes hopelessly overcooked. Better to saute it until crisp-tender while the salmon cooks and mix in with the herbs and almonds. Snow peas might be a better bet than broccoli. And better yet, just skip either and along with the sliced avocado, add in chopped Persian cucumbers.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody:
Based on all these comments, I cooked the quinoa separately and wished a bit that I’d cooked the broccoli separately—maybe even roasted it instead—it was pretty good but not amazing. Steaming the salmon with the broccoli already finished was already too much. Should’ve done onions first, then broccoli and salmon together, quinoa separately and then add, plus the herbs and nuts. 

The almonds are necessary. I cooked it without and it does lack a certain crunch that would be helpful. Might try with some crusty bread or something else to give some crunch.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody:
I used more broccoli and I cooked it separately after reading all the comments. I used pistachio nuts instead of almonds.
I used half salmon and half tofu. 
It’s totally delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Well/ this was underwhelming. i kept on thinking i was missing a major spice component. And trust me, I am NOT one that loves super spicy! But this so bland. Maybe fresh ginger?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: A Lebanese coworker told me that tahini and lemon is commonly used as a fish sauce. It's also used on felafel balls.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: I just cooked this last night.  Very good, but I would like the tahini/ponzu sauce to be a tiny bit sweeter, so I think I would add a little honey to the dressing next time.  I didn't have enough broccoli so I used a bunch of curly kale and that was great.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: My family hates broccoli. Any ideas for subs?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: @ELA the ratio for quinoa is 1.75 liquid to 1 quinoa. So this would likely leave the quinoa a little underdone.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: This was not a hit at our house. I won’t make it again. The salmon was overcooked. The veggies mushy. The sauce lacked flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly except I steamed the broccoli separately and added it to the dish together with the herbs.  Delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: May be the only NYT recipe I've made that I found inedible; turned into a gelatinous mess. Trying to retrieve expensive morsels of salmon out of dish.  A true disappointment. It must have just been me, but I don't know what I should have done differently

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Great ingredients, but benefit of using only one pot is

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Dig all the ingredients (thanks for the soy lime fix for having no Ponzu) but I question the ultimate value of having used only one pot. Next time I’ll cook broccoli separately so it doesn’t overcook.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody:
I did not have a problem with overcooking the broccoli, as others noted. Possibly, it's because I kept the heat to medium and low as per recipe instructions. That said, I might consider putting the pieces of broccoli stem in earlier than the bits of floret. 
This is tasty, but I am not sure I like the ratio of tahini & ponzu yet. May adjust that in future. 
Will see how this does reheated. I did not add herbs into the pot, but will add those, avocado and dressing after reheating.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Where is the sesame?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: @Kristina tahini is sesame paste for the most part

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Not very exciting flavor wise.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Made it per the recipe the first time and then for the second time I used canned salmon and added a can of chopped green chilis with the broccoli and onions.  Both times were excellent!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Kind of a dud. Hopes were high for a flavorful one pot dish with the high nutrition of quinoa, salmon, and broccoli. Did not cook or taste as expected. Quinoa took much longer than it should have. It didn't end up tender, and the salmon was overcooked as a result. The broccoli was lost in the dish. The ponzu dressing was not a real flavor punch. The whole dish was kind of bland and overly filling. Crunch of almonds might have helped a little, but I had left them out because I don't like them.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this twice. A great recipe! The second time I added fresh corn (one ear) and one red slivered bell pepper with the other veg, and a mix of cilantro and Italian parsley for the herbs. Used Eric Kim’s recipe for ponzu sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: When do you add the garlic? It’s in the ingredients, but not seeing it in the instructions.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: This look promising, I'm glad I went through the comments first and based on the feedback decided to cook everything separately. That was a good call! Nothing espectacular, I won't be making this again

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: This has become a staple in my family for an easy weeknight dinner. I do a super simple version by buying a dill sauce and skipping the tahini, almonds etc. I love that it takes less than 15 mins and is healthy and tasty!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: "Sesame"?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Tahini is like peanut butter for sesame seeds.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: This recipe should be renamed. “Sesame salmon” is misleading. You don’t think of tahini as sesame. You think of it as tahini. Sesame salmon evokes seeds.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody:
Absolutely loved this. Very few modifications. Didn’t use the almonds though I could see the allure. Could have used more broccoli as it kind of disappears. I did add some zucchini which was a nice touch. 

Adding the broccoli at the very end of step one helped it not be overcooked. Didn’t have ponzu so substituted soy sauce, rice vinegar and more lemon. Don’t be shy with the tahini, lemon or sauce in general. It really makes it pop. May try some samba olek next time. And green onions. 

Can’t wait to make this again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Tasty, but there ended up being more water than expected in the quinoa. Also added a couple Serranos, some cumin, and some paprika.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: This reminds me of a warm bowl you might get at a fast casual salad place. Next time I will throw the broccoli in to steam with the salmon in the hopes for more texture. I love the sauce, and also could see adding some crushed red pepper or additionally garnishing with a spicy sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: I took advice from others and steamed the broccoli separately. I also did a mix of quinoa and jasmine rice. I really liked the tahini sauce but my partner wasn't a fan. He tasted it before adding it to his bowl and opted for drizzling the ponzu on his serving. We had never had ponzu, but now we are fans! This is definitely a make again for us.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: @john j  tahini is sesame paste

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: I skipped the broccoli, for reason many people said. Instead, I had two fat leeks, about the same weight as the broccoli called for, sliced them thin, omitted the onion, and it all worked out. Very delicious. I think next time I won’t cut up the salmon, just a slab on top of the quinoa. Since I’m only one person, I think this will  better control how I store and use the leftovers.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: This was most disappointing.  I read comments and made adjustments; cooked broccoli separately and added lemon, lots of lemon! The sauce did not impress either.  Too bad.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: Just like most comments - I suggest just cooking each component separately so you are certain to get the textures you want. So many things went wrong for me and its ALOT of food to have ruined if you don’t get it perfect on the first try.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody:
This is a great recipe. I am lucky to have more than one Asian supermarket nearby so made a quick trip for ponzu, an ingredient I had not previously purchased. 

I had some cooked broccoli on hand so just added after the salmon was cooked. 

Made this with Trader Joe's Harvest Grains, which I use all the time. I like the texture and it was perfect in this recipe. 

Sauce is delicious! Will definitely put this recipe in rotation.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
commentBody: This recipe feels disjointed. The flavors are regionally all over the map and none of them really sing together. I feel like the same concept (one pot salmon over quinoa with veg) could have been executed way more coherently. (I smelled the sauce and the freshly chopped dill/chives I had ready and almost was nauseous. Using cilantro instead, but even so, this feels like a sketch of an idea, not a finalized beloved recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: I'd recommend cooking the egg after browning the beef and setting aside or adding it after the onion and carrot. Cooking the egg after adding rice will turn it gooey, by adding moisture and cooling your pan too much. At least that's my experience working a busy wok station.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody:
@Bored headache
Both are accepted techniques of Asian cooking.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody:
@Bored headache
It depends- if you like discrete pieces of egg, then yes, cook it separately, then chop up before adding it to the rest of the dish. If you don’t care for discrete pieces ( like my husband) then cook it as described in the recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody:
Great dish. Much enjoyed. A few modifications;
1) yes do the eggs after meat (not once rice is in wok)
2) rice; instead of adding to wok to breakup, instead place rice in bowl and spray with oil as you break the rice up by hand. This makes the rice less clumpy 
3) make often and enjoy

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: @BGE the recipe says to leave at least 2 tablespoons of oil in the pan after cooking the beef – if I’m cooking the eggs after the beef should I cook them and all that oil?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody:
Y'all. I made this with hamburger meat. That's right; I velveted the hamburger meat and while it is not a textural profile that some may enjoy (I'm honestly on the fence still) - this was an explosive favorite with the kiddos and hubby. And I'm about to make it again. Just putting it out there as another option for that package of meat you might have.
FYI - I come to the comment section exclusively for substitution recommendations.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Another option to help tenderize the beef is to add 1/4 tsp of food grade sodium carbonate to the velveting marinade. Sodium bicarbonate can also be used but it tends to leave a metallic taste vs sodium carbonate which is tasteless.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: @Scott that’s why use use common bicarbonate of soda (or whatever name your country dictates) and wash it off well afterwards. I don’t see why you should spend more or waste unnecessary carbon (which we should all worry about!) buying online when both products literally do the same thing (It’s not a matter of quality)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: For the beef, many cuts will work-- my inclination would be to go with boneless beef ribs.  However, about the marination: you'd do better to not add any salt, to add a slosh of shaoxing wine (not "cooking wine-- which has salt added) or sherry, and increase the time to a minimum of 30 minutes, an hour if it's convenient.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: @Mike in Maine yes!! Shaoxing wine!! I looked to see if this in an ingredient and sadly it is not. One of the most important ingredients in Chinese cooking - just a tablespoon or so brings out the most authentic, restaurant quality flavor. Buy a bottle and level up your Asian cooking.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Prep took longer than expected. I usually velvetize beef with aluminum-free baking powder so this beef was a bit tough for me. Too be honest I am no spring chicken and neither are my teeth. I used onion, red pepper, peas and lots of celery since I love it but otherwise followed the recipe. My husband loved it. He has better teeth!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: @Joan H. I’m with you on the celery, I need some in every stir fry as well as cabbage!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Helpful tip - par freeze the beef for 30-60 minutes so it’s just firm for easier thin slicing

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: I’m baffled at the idea that carrots, even finely chopped, could soften in three minutes. And also what is “finely chopped”? Is that different than diced?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: @rlalumiere.     Diced means cutting in cubes chopping is using a knife to cut finely

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: @rlalumiere Save time and buy yourself a bag of frozen peas and carrots.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: 12 oz of steak is 3/4 lbs. If I ask my butcher for 12 oz of steak they'll probably look at me funny.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I made two changes. I cooked the eggs as a flat omelet by themselves, then rolled the omelet up and sliced it into strips. I added the strips to the rice with the meat. Also, I used only about two and 1/2 cups of rice - that's all I can make at one time in my rice cooker!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: @Janice that’s how my Chinese cooking instructor taught me to make the egg. Fry the scrambled eggs in the wok first, roll up, julienne, then toss into the rice at the end. Also add a tiny splash of soy sauce to the scrambled eggs.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody:
You can cook rice then cool it fast in freezer for fried rice. 4 day is excessive.
Marinate meat tossed in baking soda then rinsed off will give you tender Chinese beef

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: The recipe calls for 4 cups of day-old rice, not 4 day-old rice. ; )

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: @Barbara in Alaska she said 4 cups of day-old rice, not 4 day old rice lol

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Simple. Nice. Needs some spice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Ginger, garlic, soy sauce, sesame oil-- Not sure what spices it needs.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: @quaas if by spice you mean heat, I added Old Grandma chili oil with black beans. Any excuse to eat that stuff…… also…. Had no ginger so used some sugared ginger bits and no scallions so grabbed a handful of chives from where the dogs can’t pee on it (still washed it…) and last years frozen peas and final product was great!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody:
TJs has a frozen vegetable fried rice that can shave a lot of time in preparation. I usually "kick it up a notch" with some extra ingredients, peas, corn, garlic, eggs, sesame oil, etc and use it as a side or add a protein of choice to the mix.
I've tried the other TJ fried rices but the vegetable one is the only one I'd recommend.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Wouldn’t this be better cooked at high heat in a wok, to get that wok hei char? It was fine in the nonstick skillet but…

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: @Kitfo woks work great if you have something akin to a commercial-style high BTU burner, but most people don’t have that capability on their homes ranges, especially if you have electric.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: This was yummy! Steak was so tender and delicious! Only changes were to double garlic and ginger and I added red pepper flakes to the rice mixture as it cooked. I’m betting this is even better tomorrow!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: As someone trying to manage high blood pressure, I appreciate the use of low-sodium soy sauce here, and for anyone else in the same situation, I highly recommend using coconut aminos instead. Better flavor and lower sodium.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Will this dish work if the rice isn't a day old? Want to make it tonight!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: @Nancy - I made the rice then cooled it in the fridge then popped it in the freezer to dry it up a bit like day old rice! Turned out great!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: @Nancy technically yes but you’ll get a different texture. Some folks prefer the texture with ready cooked rice, most don’t. It’s true ready cooked rice doesn’t soak up flavourings or fry well for the same reason - ready cooked steaming rice is full of moisture, but as I say some folks/family members enjoy it this way.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: @Chelsea Much easier than having to think about making this a day ahead. After steaming I spread it on a small pan and left in uncovered in the fridge for a few hours.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: I followed previous advice about the eggs- no problem there. The meat was tender but lacked flavor. I feel like I should reverse the process and cook the veg first so the meat can absorb those flavors more from the pan. Thoughts?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: @Laurie I agree.  My meat was also flavorless, even though I marinated it for the full 30 minutes.  I also cooked the eggs after the beef.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Best part was the beef. I like crispy, tasty beef with rice and vegetables. This was a nice change for dinner. Husband did the chopping which made my part go quickly. We included some other vegetables. Thank you for something other than chicken.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Pretty good. Could be more flavorful. Added extra veggies from the fridge

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Cooked almost as written; subbed snow peas for peas (added in last minute of carrots and onions). Absolutely delicious and perfect. Thank you!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is not good and surely not deserving of 5 stars. Very very very bland and basic. Would not recommend nor make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: I’d recommend using leftover steak or ro

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: This was a big hit with my family of four, despite not having ginger (I subbed kimchi paste and felt the absence). There was plenty leftover for lunches tomorrow. 100% will make this again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: It’s always been my understanding that velveting meant blanching in oil or water, not just marinating it in cornstarch and liquid. Why do you call that velveting? Why does it tenderize?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: This recipe required way, way, way more oil than is stated. Seemed like every step required more oil. I cooked the eggs separately as others suggested. I also allowed the chopped carrots to steam with both water and oil with a lid on the non-stick pan, stirring once in a while and testing the carrots for crunch before I moved onto the next step.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody:
We added Snap Peas, Green Onions, Broccoli and Asparagus.   Yum!
I also did the garlic, ginger and carrots in the small food processor.   So much earlier than chopping

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: It was really good as written. Thanks!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: I followed the instructions exactly and it was easy and delicious.  None of the flavors were overpowering. Thanks again NYT Cooking!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Prep took longer than expected. Although good, the dish needs MUCH more flavor. I had to quadruple the amount of soy sauce, and still was missing something to make it very tasty.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: I wish there were more guidance in this recipe on the amount of salt and pepper to add.  There are four places where it says to "season with salt and pepper."  As a novice cook (and this is meant to be an"Easy" recipe), I am unsure how much to use at each stage in the process.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Yum, this recipe is so tasty.  I typically make fried rice with chicken, but the beef is so good.  The velveting of the meat is so helpful to guarantee tenderness.  I wish I had been a little more patient and cooked in smaller batches so I could get a crispier exterior, but it was still delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: I followed the general structure of this recipe with a few mods based on what I had on hand. I used a frozen vegetable mix which I zapped briefly in the microwave to defrost. Such a flexible recipe that came together easily. I’ve never tenderized meat like this before—totally worth it. I’ve made fried rice hundreds of times and this just pushed me to try a different path to making it. I enjoyed it with a side of kimchi. Delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Great tasting dish and easy to make. A couple of things I would do differently: Slice the meat much thinner and try other meats. Both chicken and pork would work just fine. I would also use more eggs to make that part more visible. I did my dish in a non-stick wok and that worked well for me.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious.  Some tips:
Prepped ahead so everything was at my fingertips before I started cooking 
Marinated the meat for 45 minutes 
Sautéed all the meat at once in 12” non stick pan Made an omelette in a separate pan and sliced the eggs into strips, added them with peas
Sauteed chopped green pepper with onions and carrots for 3-4 minutes
Added extra soy sauce and crushed red pepper 
I love Asian food, my husband not so much. He gave this a thumb’s up!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: This is only okay. Needs some help to punch up the flavors. We wound up adding soy and a light Japanese BBQ sauce to help. It worked but not sure we’ll make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Quite tasty, but a lot of work and mess.  And the picture shows way more beef vs. rice than my results.  Could use more soy sauce - and why specify low sodium soy when you're going to add salt to the dish?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: The low sodium is used  exactly because  there  is salt added at every stage.  It's a common sense thing: either add low sodium soy sauce & salt or add regular soy sauce and omit the added salt .

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Great with kangaroo too!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!  The meat was so tender and juicy!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Fish sauce… but I put Red Boat in most everything

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: 4 cups of uncooked rice is an insane amount, not sure if i misinterpreted, but definitely dont use that much unless you want to eat this for a week. It was very good though and very easy

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: @JW recipe calls for COOKED rice, and 4 c was hardly enough as recipe turned out great!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: This is a great recipe that the whole family loved. I had cooked a flank steak the night before and used leftovers for this. I cut it up into small pieces and tossed it into the cornstarch/soy mix. It came out delish!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: @rlalumiere:  whenever a recipe calls for carrot to be chopped or diced very small, I put it through a coarse grater instead.  You still get the orange flecks, but are guaranteed it will always be soft.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: I don't eat meat so I am going to substitute meat with Beyond Meat Steak.  And maybe try aquafaba for eggs or use tofu.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: The prep took longer for me than 20 minutes, but this turned out so delicious! I added fried wonton strips to the top, and that gave a nice crunch. I will try the two suggestions below next time I make this, which will be soon!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: YUM!!!! Only had leftover brown rice and it worked well. Wanted a veggie side so steamed broccoli florets then gave them a quick sauté with sesame oil and topped them with some rice vinegar and put the florets on top of the finished plate -fabulous. The thick broccoli stalks were chopped up and added with the carrot and onion. So very tasty. In this recipe I found that had I not had my mise en place ready to go, it could have made for  quite the clean up. Oh, and topped the finished bowls with chili crisp. This recipe is just too good!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Can this recipe be frozen and rewarmed?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: The eggs are super delicious if you scramble them in a little sesame oil and some scallion whites!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody:
Great, easy to throw together recipe using anything from the fridge/freezer. 
Yes, definitely scramble the eggs BEFORE the rice is added.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: I was told  by a reliable source cornstarch will make the beef crunchy not act as a tenderizer .. with the cost of beef I’ll try a tried and true soya marinade not experiment with baking soda  vs cornstarch .

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Proportions are way off. 2 cups of rice was more than enough.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: I used skirt steak which I usually like but this recipe only has it marinate in the soy sauce/cornstarch mixture for 30 minutes. I found it really needs much more time to really tenderize it. Next time I'll marinate it longer or try the sirloin. We really did like the recipe even if the skirt steak was a bit chewy for our taste.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: Why are the commenters talking about a wok when the recipe calls,for a frying pan?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody:
