Aaahhhh, so that's what we use to do with beef before the Great Meat Famine of 2026!  Its like deja vu to read a recipe with beef in it, kinda like going to Costco to admire the beef case and remember the good ole days.

Jus sayin

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
commentBody: I was with you until it came time to add the egg. The egg is unnecessary, imo.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Perfect combination of tasty, quick, cheap, and relatively healthy. When I make it again (which I’m sure I will) I’ll use a wok or a Dutch oven instead of a large frying pan, as it was a little too full to comfortably stir. Great recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Very good weeknight dinner! It definitely needs a good bit of salt- 1 lb of cabbage   1 lb of pork requires a lot. I wish NYT recipes would recommend at least a base amount of salt to start with. “Season with salt” implies a sprinkling (to non-chefs), which is definitely nowhere near enough for most dishes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: @Carly Fish sauce is quite salty, and varies according to brand. This will determine thee need for additional salt.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: @Carly totally agree. I wonder if it’s so they don’t get flagged or filtered out of searches for sodium content. Unhelpful!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: @Carly agreed, needed maybe a tablespoon.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Just made this for dinner with ground chicken instead of pork and really enjoyed it. I also used gochugaru instead of red pepper. Hubby and I think we may use it as a bibimbap-like base for leftovers.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: @Shana Ditto on the gochugaru!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: @Shana Terrific substitute!!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
Yes! Fish Sauce is brand dependent. One can be so much saltier than the other. My preference is to go light on the salt and check.
Personally, peanuts will be an addition to the recipe the next time I make it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Such a tasty and easy dish using a Dutch Oven.    I added 1 diced Jalapeno with the garlic, ginger, and green onions for the second step (no crush red pepper).    I used Napa Cabbage (better flavor IMO), followed by additional salt and pepper before adding the pork mixture back.  Juiced one whole lime and more salt + pepper.  I used Squid Brand Fish Sauce (available at H-Mart).  Great one-pot recipe!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This is a killer recipe. It's inexpensive, it's fast, it uses easy-to-grab-on-the-way-home ingredients, and it's bursting with Southeast Asian flavors that elevate it well beyond the usual weeknight meal. Sensing it would make good leftovers, I doubled it, cooking the pork and aromatics in two batches but managing the do a double qty of cabbage in a single wok. Tip: don't be shy about the lime juice and fish sauce - they really sing here, and blend nicely with the buttery sauteed cabbage. 5/5

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
As others have commented, fish sauce can vary greatly in its saltiness.  Most grocery store fish sauces are heavily diluted with water and then loaded with salt to make up the difference making their flavor almost all salt.  “Good” i.e. not diluted- fish sauce adds a lot of funk and some saltiness.  I love Thai food so require good Thai fish sauce - best brand I’ve found is Red Boat-ingredients  “anchovy, sea salt.”   
Great recipe for a variety of proteins.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: @MaryBretired. I concur… we have tried several different brands of fish sauce over the years and have concluded Red Boat has the most authentic and consistent flavor.  We use it in everything.  It is easily acquired thru Amazon if one cannot find it at their local grocery store.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Splendid, exactly as written.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I used a wok and that let me build a good char on the cabbage which added another layer of flavor and prevents over-crowding.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent. I used a Taiwanese cabbage, and a leftover jalapeno instead of the chili flakes. Served over brown jasmine rice. A single tablespoon of fish sauce is very salty, but it is  not enough for the whole dish, so it is important to add a bit of salt at the other stages as indicated. Next time I might try using oil only instead of butter, whose flavour might have been overwhelmed by the jalapeno.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This was good - I added chopped red bell pepper and used already prepared coleslaw (already had a bag in the refrigerator - no dressing😊).  Made for a very easy, healthy and delicious dinner.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Try this precisely as written before making changes. It’s easy, tasty, and satisfying exactly as is—a perfect example of the right kind of fusion cooking.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have any ground pork, so I made this with ground turkey with some bacon fat in addition to the butter to give it some extra fat.  It turned out great!  Super quick and easy.  I also added some onions in addition to the scallions, used a dried bird's eye chili, and threw some fresh basil in there as well, since I had it on hand.  I'd love to try this another time with vermicelli noodles instead of rice and make it as a cold salad for lunch.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Thought this was delicious and easy to make! I subbed crumbled tofu (coated with flour and corn starch) for the pork and it was great! Also added some asparagus in with the cabbage

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: @Irene thinking of doing tofu too!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight exactly as written.  It needs way more ginger, garlic, and fish sauce than the recipe calls for.  I think it's on the right track, but using the proportions as written in the recipe it was pretty bland.  I'll make it again, but bump up the spices and fish sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This was yummy! I used 1/2 lb of pork because a full pound for four servings seemed like too much for me, and nearly 2 lbs of cabbage. It was still full of delicious porky flavor. We used a wok, which I highly recommend. Otherwise made as written.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: To paraphrase chief brody, you’re going to need a bigger pan.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
@Dana

Love that line!  Thanks for the chuckle.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: The point of seasoning with salt is becoming experienced with the level of salt that works for you. The wonderfully named Hugh Fearnley-Whittingstall (of River Cottage in England) encourages a good seasoning with Kosher salt when carmelizing meat, veg, etc. Kosher salt lets you be bolder.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I feel like adding cashews to the stir fry would be good in this too!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Despite all the ginger, garlic, scallions, jalapeno, fish sauce and lime, this was rather bland.  OK as comfort food.  Maybe more fish sauce and lime and hot pepper?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Great minimal effort stir fry. I personally adjusted it to double the fish sauce (im addicted to the stuff), used white pepper, and finished it with a little chili garlic sauce, but that’s me. Easy to customize this one anyway you see fit.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: @Susan same in my house. I used 2 T soy sauce and it was good. also included lime zest.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Any good substitute for fish sauce? My husband cannot abide it and he can taste it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: @Susan I will try miso mixed into a little water, I also dislike fish sauc

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I used a bit of soy sauce and Worcestershire sauce, since I didn't have any fish sauce on hand.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Try Braggs Liquid Aminos (or coconut liquid aminos) -- I've been using this since the 1980s and love the stuff. Also made from soy beans, I prefer it to soy sauce -- a less strident, softer, richer, warmer, more umami-esque flavor. If soy intolerant, don't use. And use carefully, though -- it's salty -- don't need additional salt if using.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Simple and delicious! Will definitely make it again, I may bump up the fish sauce a little and reduce the butter next time.  I will for sure be adding this into the rotation.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: New favorite weeknight meal! Used napa cabbage that we had on hand. Perfect recipe as written.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: 10/10... I did it with marinated pork chops (soy sauce/ fish sauce/ garlic) and added furkaki to the rice. TOO good. yuuuum!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Excellent dish! We prefer cabbage well cooked so after stir-frying it I added two or three tablespoons of water to the wok and put a lid on it to let it steam until cabbage was well cooked and tender before I added the meat mixture. I added a little extra lime juice as well and used a tablespoon less of the butter called for. I will keep this recipe on my favourites list.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: this is REALLY good. I think 3 tbs of butter would have been too rich, i just did the 2 with the cabbage. I drained the pork so it wasnt so greasy too.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
-- Dutch oven
-- peanuts 
-- cabbage really needs to be done at a blazing heat so the internal sugars can char and caramelize without it getting soggy
-- add light brown sugar when adding the cabbage or at the end to balance the flavor

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
4 out of 5 as written. Followed it closely with the exception of gochujang subbed for red pepper flakes, which I would def recommend. My two additions/alterations for next time will be

- use a wok or heavy bottom skillet as cabbage really needs to be done at a blazing heat so the internal sugars can char and caramelize without it getting soggy
- add light brown sugar (or palm sugar if you have it) when adding the cabbage or at the end to balance the flavor. It was desperately needing a sweet note. 

Overall, a very nice, straightforward, low effort, minimal cleanup dish. Perfect for a weeknight. Will report back when I try the updates. Happy cooking!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Nice - but of course you need to double the fish sauce, lime, garlic, ginger (or triple the garlic and ginger) - use the full recipe proportions for half the pork and cabbage.  Also, gochugaru is a welcome addition for heat.  Serve on rice noodles, too.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: OK, this was bonkers good. I didn't have scallions or cilantro so I added extra garlic/ginger. It was amazing, flavorful, and easy to cook on a weeknight. I used a large enameled Dutch oven to get maximum sear/flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: We used thinly sliced chicken thighs instead of ground pork. Big hit!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Does anyone think that tofu instead of pork would work in this recipe?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I'm not a big cabbage fan, BUT this recipe was absolutely delicious, easy, and quick to make! It's going on our regular rotation. I added a bit more fish sauce (Red Boat brand) than called for because it needed the extra seasoning. So yummy!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I made this with tofu and it was very good. I eyeballed the fish sauce and lime juice, erring on the generous side with both. Didn’t add spice since my children were having some, but this could be good with some gochujang.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I love this recipe, it’s on a regular rotation. One note is that 1lb pork and 1lb cabbage don’t fit in a 12 inch skillet. Use a Dutch oven to avoid spillage!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty weeknight meal. I subbed a block of crumbled tofu for pork and it worked out well w additional oil and seasoning (soy sauce and salt) to taste.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Wanted to love this but with twice the seasoning and a fair amount of salt we found this pretty bland.  It is fast and economical.  We served with Jasmine rice and garnish of cilantro, lime wedges and chopped peanuts.  Glad we tried but moving on.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Used a Wok, as nonstick pans are toxic.  Added a teaspoon of sugar, as it looked like they were going for a Thai-style sauce, and swapped high temp oil for butter. It slaps!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Swapping sesame oil for butter and using Nappa Cabbage, which cooks quickly so watch it if you want it crisp.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: My husband and I loved this dish. Thought it might work well as a lettuce wrap. Next time. This is definitely in the rotation now. I agree with previous poster that it is perfect combo of tasty, quick, and cheap. Also agree that a deeper pan works well. I used a Dutch oven. Thank you Kay Chun for yet another delicious weeknight recipe. You are making me look good!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: There has to be a better name for this.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Discovered from a prior NYTimes recipe: roasting Napa cabbage in the air fryer produces a sublime product. The pork can be added into the basket at the end (yes, it is no longer one-pot, but there is not a bowl to wash).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Good stuff. I added chopped dry roasted peanuts the end.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe, very tasty! Ended up being a bit oily for my taste between the oil/pork fat/butter that even the cabbage didn’t end up soaking up, but maybe next time I’ll just skip the last tbs of butter at the end.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is my favorite recipe on NYT cooking! It is so easy and quick, and the flavor is out of this world! it's like a flavor explosion in my mouth! Make this! you will not be sorry!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly as written with 1 addition: as I waited for the cabbage to cook, I zested the skin of my juicing lime into the pan. The freshness of the lime juice and the zest gave the overall dish a very nice aromatic. Great with Thai jasmine rice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Sheer perfection. So easy for such a big payoff. I salted moderately as I went and used Red Boat fish sauce. Ended up adding more fish sauce and lime. Basil would be fantastic if you don’t like cilantro. Will be making this again and again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! Looking forward to making it again. I can imagine subbing half the pork with ground turkey or chicken, as the pork flavor was less prominent than I expected anyway. The garlic/ginger/lime/fish sauce stole the show.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
Had leftover Vietnamese Pork Ribs (see excellent NYT Cooking recipe here) so subbed that for the ground pork and made only the cabbage part. 

Awesome! Really good use of ingredients, so much more than just the sum of its parts. Will definitely make again. 

Don't be shy about using other proteins⁰0 here, tho I think it'd be best if it were flavored well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Didn't like the combination of butter, fish sauce, lime and aromatic spices.  Also, this needs a lot of salt or fish sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This was so incredibly delicious and easy. Perfect for a weeknight meal. As someone else mentioned, I may try napa cabbage next time. I cooked mine in a dutch oven and it came out perfectly. I may use a little soy in place of some of the Kosher salt next time and maybe a smidge of sesame oil at the end for an extra bit of dimension.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This was a real simple dish to prepare.  It literally takes no time. I did find the recipe to be a little bland so I added a little soy as well as some 5 spice powder. I served with a little chili crisp on top.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty and pretty easy/quick for family meal. A few notes: Agree with those cautious about over-salting. I put extra fish sauce and a little soy sauce for more flavor, before it had time to meld, making it way too salty.  I had to add a pound of ground beef (that’s what I had ) to balance it out. I added some honey– salty, spicy, umami AND sweet. Pulled it all together.  I used the basil suggestion too.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Great dish. Made it as written sans cilantro because I didn't have it on hand, but I will use it next time for added depth. I used Red Boat fish sauce and still needed a good amount of salt, so taste as you go and add accordingly to your tastes. The recipe reminds me of a Moroccan Beef & Cabbage recipe I've been making for years that includes golden raisins. Going to try adding some next time for a nice burst of sweetness. This is definitely going in the "weeknight" rotation.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
Serviceable for a weekday meal, but a little flat. It felt like a comfort food. 

The butter in step 3 is important, but it didn’t need the bit at the end. We doubled the fish sauce and sprinkled on a little sugar. Next time we might try adding sliced water chestnuts, then sprinkling 
more scallions on the finished dish.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
A great weeknight meal that is so much more than just cabbage and pork stirfry. The combo of fish sauce and butter - wow! Will it be the next miso-butter umami pairing?? Who knows!
So add that little extra bit of butter at the end - it's worth it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I added some cooked rice noodles at the end,  doubled the sauce ingredients, and added oyster mushrooms. Delicious and easy!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I made with ground turkey and added kale. It was delicious, we will definitely be adding to the menu :)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: The browned butter is the magic ingredient in this dish.  I used ground beef (my wife hates pork).  I failed in adding enough salt, so it was too bland at the end.  But the table was impatient, so in desperation I threw in another Tbsp each of fish sauce and lime.  Bam!  That brought it all together and the freshness of the cilantro and lime popped right out.  The reviews from the table were glowing.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I caramelized the pork and did it all in a wok so got some nice charred bits of cabbage.  Outstanding!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Made this in a wok, and it was quite good in the end. I had a lot of cabbage on hand, both Napa and green cabbage, which probably needed more than the small dash of salt I put in at first. While I used two Tsp. of fish sauce at first, adding more fish sauce and a strong squeeze of lime juice gave it deep flavor. (Even better with Sri racha.) I used ground turkey, which was okay, but not as sweet as ground pork. Next time!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Loved it and my leftover averse wife was happy to dig in a second time

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Totally delicious. the recipe appeared when I had excess cabbage, both red and green. There was a small amount of leftover pork sausage in the fridge, so I tossed that in for a flavor boost, tThe result was wonderful.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I added some firm tofu after the pork was mostly cooked, cooked the cabbage with some shallots, then added some chicken stock once the pork and tofu were added back to the pot. I added a little chili crisp for some heat. An easy and tasty weeknight dinner.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Great easy recipe. I added 2tbsp of fish sauce instead of 1, and I also didn’t add any spice, instead had chili oil at the table. Fly by Jing pairs really nicely with it, while spice sensitive folks can just skip and it’s still very tasty!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Made this for family dinner and added Thai basil and mint along with cilantro as passed herbs. And pickled carrot/daikon as well for a bit of sweet to balance the fish sauce. Very easy, rave reviews.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: So good as-is, but because of this, I'll need to double the proportions for the future - this worked for 2 adults with no leftovers!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Made a second time, this time with ground lamb, and loved it even more. A sprinkle of lemon zest adds some extra brightness.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Good stuff!  I used a small chunk of leftover pork tenderloin and it got nice and crispy-edged when cooked with the garlic, etc.  Added a bit of diced serrano pepper before it met its maker, and the rice was from the day before so I scooted the goods to the other side of the pan, added a dash of oil and cooked the rice a bit. Stirred it all together and it was great!  How much do we love cabbage?  Tons!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
I made this the first time because I had some ground turkey in the freezer and half a cabbage that needed a home. It's surprisingly good, My teenager requested it for dinner tonight--sounds good!
definitely use a dutch oven.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I used ground beef (no ground pork in store).  Good recipe, easy to make.  I don't know if it's cheap - (depends on what is already available in kitchen).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Great, under 30 minute recipe to use up that last piece of cabbage and in our case spinach and onion.  very salty because of fish sauce so we had a light hand on extra salt. Definitely going to be in our rotation.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I used chicken instead of pork and a mix of Asian veg instead of cabbage and it was still perfect. This is a new favorite family recipe. Absolutely easy and soooo delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Wow this is so good… and easy. I added a finely chopped jalapeño to the ginger and garlic, which I used liberally. And added part of a red pepper with the cabbage, cooked cabbage longer than suggested, and used Thai basil and mint to finish because that’s what was in my garden.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
Ok, ok, I did NOT follow the recipe as written. Sorry, not sorry. I'm under the weather, didn't have ground pork. So not my intention to*disobey*, but had to use what was available.

Sauteed cabbage in bacon fat. For protein, defrosted, then sauteed shrimp.

YUM! Amazingly, it worked! Added zest from lime B4 juicing. Everything else, same as recipe. 

Just shows how versatile and wonderful this recipe is!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I love cabbage and am always looking for different ways to use it - this was excellent! I did add a little more fish sauce, as my grocery store brand is kind of puny, and a bit more lime because I love it. Great reward for minimal effort - it's going into the regular rotation!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Enjoyed this stir fry very much. Had 90% of the ingredients on hand which made it even better!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Love this recipe! Just as written… looking forward to my leftovers!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Delicious as written. Charred cabbage with butter is a revelation!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This needs chili crisp condiment!! I’m going to do it with Taiwanese cabbage.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I loved this, but I added little soy sauce, seseme oil and hoisin, to make it a little more interesting. They would good, easy additions.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Outstanding. I couldn’t get ground pork, so used boneless pork rib and did a coarse chop of it: would do this again. I bumped up the aromatics and spices as suggested, used a teaspoon of chili crisp for the heat. Golly it was good. Dumped the leftovers on the remaining rice and tossed that around. Reheated a portion for lunch, then had a couple poached eggs over the rest for breakfast this morning. Fast, easy, really delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: basic. added maggi sauce, lite soy sauce, extra garlic and ginger all after tasting original recipe and if making again, will add sliced onion.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely loved this dish (didn’t want it to be over lol). I didn’t have access to good cilantro so we subbed Thai basil and it was absolutely delicious. Serious 10/10!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This recipe affirms why cabbage is my favorite vegetable! (And I love them all.) Half red and half green made for a very pretty dish. I subbed ground turkey for ground pork and it was still delicious. I, too, used a Dutch oven. The cabbage was beautifully caramelized.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Yum! Used a wok on high, 1/2 lb pork which browned when up the sides w white pepper & salt. Added ginger, garlic, leeks (scallion would be better) and reserved. 1 Tbs butter until foamed, red cabbage, celery, white pepper, little bit of salt; when softened fish sauce (add more), lemon (lime would be better), some chili flake, small amt of sriracha to taste. Tossed in vermicelli noodles soaked while cooking. Cilantro/basil and more acid/zest would be good on top. Fast, easy, fun!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I’ve cooked this several times. Always easy, satisfying, and very good. I love cabbage so I usually add extra.  Adjust lime juice and fish sauce to taste. I sometimes add extra veggies like roasted broccoli.  Maybe next time I’ll add a package of cubed tofu for extra protein and saute it with the pork or ground turkey. Very riffable.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
I cook a lot of Asian cuisines and had just finished cooking up a Thai cashew chicken dish before I made this for the first time.

Instead of using salt I added soy sauce to the pork.  Many Thai dishes use both soy and fish sauce.

Coarse gochugaru is a must.  It adds a smokiness that red pepper flakes don't.

Invest in a butane torch.  It helps to char the cabbage.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Super tasty and fresh! I used closer to 1.5 lbs of pork and just slightly upped the seasonings, since the first time I made it I found 1 lbs to be a bit skimpy. I also enjoy this topped with peanuts and a drizzle of Sriracha and Kewpie mayo.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Wasn't expecting this to be a hit with the picky spouse but he really liked it. He has an aversion to fish sauce, so what I did was add soy sauce in addition to the called for ingredients. It was so delicious. Willvmake again for sure.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Third time I’ve made this. I use boneless pork loin rib, chopped bite-size (I keep a pack or two thereof in freezer). Double the garlic, ginger, fish sauce. Teaspoon of chili crisp. Easy to make and ingredients not too exotic, but truly delicious. Mix it with white rice after both are cooked and refrigerate. Good for any meal, even breakfast with a poached egg.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Wow!  We loved this, and it was an easy recipe. I swapped green beans for most of the cabbage and also added some zucchini halfway through the veggie cooking step, and it was delicious.  Excellent taste to effort ratio for a weeknight dinner.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Really good. I used crumbled tofu and vegan fish sauce to suit my diet. Otherwise made as written. Next time I may add some honey or something for a bit of sweetness. Yum!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This is so delicious. I've made it twice as written, with two minor changes: I grated the ginger and used a wok. It's easy, inexpensive, and craveable!!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I couldn't find fish sauce in the supermarket and decided to use oyster sauce instead. It was a delicious dinner and I will definitely make it again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Very easy to make, and quite delicious. I substituted pre-cooked rotisserie chicken for the pork, and it was fabulous. Would definitely make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful flavor! Light and easy. A terrific idea for a meal and one to be repeated often.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I don’t like the taste of butter. Is there an alternative that wouldn’t make the dish too oily?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: @Kim a neutral vegetable oil

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: What’s a good substitute for fish sauce?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: If you need a vegetarian alternative, I'd recommend vegan fish sauce (it's made with mushrooms and seaweed). I don't think regular soy sauce or coconut aminos would have the earthy funkiness to bring this next level the way the fish sauce does.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I enjoyed this but next time will add in a bit of sliced celery.  Just not enough veg for me but tasty.  Will do this again

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Double the cabbage, use 12 oz pork, and triple the sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This is excellent!! I did not have lime so used lemon and still good but I am sure the lime gives it that special acidic punch! Also used oyster sauce instead of fish sauce. I do not think you really need the last pat of butter or even 2 tbsp to brown the cabbage honestly. Will definitely make again!!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Tasty and easy recipe.  Used napa cabbage and mint instead of cilantro for a family member who can't stand cilantro and thought that worked well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: It was a big hit for my family; the kids (ages 2 and 8) loved it! I added gochujang sauce, carrots, and bok choy since I had them. Costco microwave sticky rice was a super quick and easy add!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe for the most part but add some crunch: celery, red pepper and shallots.  I made many stir fries and the secret is sesame oil--it adds that Chinese restaurant taste which, to me , is lacking with other cooking oils.  A couple of squirts of hot sauce and dinner was ready.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Yum! This is delicious. Used just 1/2 pound of ground pork, and 1 jalapeno rather than crushed red peppers. Otherwise, followed the recipe exactly. I make a lot of Asian food and I was skeptical about the butter, but I think it made the dish. Great mouth feel. Will definitely make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I doubled the cabbage, garlic, scallions, and ginger, while keeping the amount of pork the same. Because I ran low on fish sauce, before serving rather than adding salt per the recipe, I sprinkled each dish with a few drops of tamari.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: We made this as written, and while my choice of Napa cabbage was challenging in a stir-fry sense, we both loved the dish. Fusion cooking at its comfy best.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This dish is great. I used some pork belly I had braised and cooled, and rice vinegar as I was missing limes. Probably should have gotten limes but still 10/10. Topped with some fresh celery for crunch.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Given the amount of fish sauce I expect this to be quite salty.  Currently looking for reduced salt fish sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Just happened to have a head of white cabbage in the frig. Used it to make this last night for dinner.  Used ground turkey instead of pork, serving it over rice linguine instead of the recommended white rice.  Absolutely delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Can see putting a “Pad Thai” spin on this by adding sugar/honey/tamarind juice w/lime juice (& maybe a splash of vinegar to taste if needed) & sprinkling w/chopped roasted peanuts to serve. LOTS of possibilities for personalizing this great "template" recipe!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I added a few stalks of celery, but next time I make it I think I'll add carrots and/or peanuts (as others mentioned). I also omitted the final tbsp of butter, as it didn't look dry and I don't really need that extra fat! Definitely use a large skilled or wok, as it does get full. Excellent, fast recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This is a good, week night dish, but I feel it needs a bit more liquid such as more fish sauce or a hit of soy soy sauce or even some water.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I made this with oyster sauce instead of fish sauce since that’s what I had on hand and I think it still turned out great. Very flavorful

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: We seasoned the meat and the rice with Penzey's Revolution (a combination of flake salt, tellicherry black pepper, cane sugar, tumeric, orange peel andcoriander.) Fantastic! This recipe is a keeper!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: simple and delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Ground turkey instead of ground pork, bok choy instead of cabbage.  Just as yummy - less fatty.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Tasted way too greasy even though I didn’t use any oil with when browning the 90% lean meat, used half the butter for sautéing the cabbage and didn’t add butter afterwards. I would use a small amount of peanut oil and toasted sesame oil, maybe one tbsp total I’m to sauté the cabbage. Added the gochugara powder as suggested by another commenter. Liked the addition. Might add a tbsp instead of tsp next time. Might also sauté some Shitake mushrooms with it next time I take a crack at this.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Very good. Cooked mostly as written.  Used 12” skillet, wasn’t non-stick - no issues.  Was heavy on ginger, and included a chopped red bell pepper for color & taste.  Yum.  Added 1/2 tsp sriracha when serving over rice. Quick, easy, gratifying.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
Great recipe! Already a favorite 🤗 
I used red cabbage and added  two finely chopped shallots and two dried Facing Heaven chilies. I also added a little soy sauce and some caster sugar when frying the cabbage.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Couldn’t get pork so used ground chicken. It was great! Added a bit of extra fish sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Really a delicious recipe. Used a chopped up Thai chile instead of red pepper flakes. Would double it next time for leftovers. Definitely worth getting a nice char on the cabbage. Good back pocket quick meal, and wouldn't hesitate to sub ground chicken (or turkey, not my thing but would still work).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have scallions, so I chopped a small onion and added that after I had started the pork.  I also sliced a red bell pepper to add after I started the cabbage.  I used a little less butter, and less salt, but it still came out just a bit salty for my family, but still very popular and asked to be put into the regular rotation.  Next time I'll season to taste at the end.  And at least once at some point, I'll swap the butter for coconut oil.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: what a delicious weeknight dish with just a few simple ingredients!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Easy and delicious as written.  I'd give it 6 stars if I could.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I've made this now twice. Cabbage is an all time fave and it works really well here (the butter is what transforms this dish). Used what I had in the way of veggies, including serrano, zucchini, and baby broccoli along with cabbage and onion. Very forgiving and works well with ground chicken or turkey. Served with cauliflower rice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This was good and I’d do it again — but the cabbage generated a lot of water in the pan and I really wish I’d poured it off before adding all the other components. This should have been a flavor bomb but it was super diluted by all the water.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious, better than anything we’ve had lately in a restaurant! We followed the recipe and only change was to substitute parsley for the cilantro as didn’t have cilantro. I used 1teaspoon salt on the meat and about 1/2 teaspoon salt on the cabbage. I also used red boat fish sauce. Yummy!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Bland…added soy sauce and that helped somewhat?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Fine. Good jumping off point. Butter and cabbage are nice together. Agree that it’s a nice way to get cruciferous vegetables in at a reasonable price. Might or might not make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Very quick and easy but maybe a little simple and forgettable. Needs generous amounts of salt and lime to wake things up. Wok is the right pan for this. Could probably use a Serrano or birds eye chili to make this more interesting in the future

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
This dish was good, but not nearly as good as the description made it out to be.  I think the oil, butter, and fat from the pork were a little heavy--cabbage felt greasy.
I served with brown rice (as suggested) and pickled red onion.  Others at the table appreciated the lime juice, but said the pickled onion really added a needed tang.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Excellent, inexpensive, flavorful meal. Made as written, except that we hate cilantro. Subbed in some fresh mint. Served over long grain basmati rice and some steamed haricot verts alongside. We like spicy food, so in addition to the red pepper flakes, I added a finely chopped red thai chile pepper. TIP: buy a package of them and keep in freezer for use as needed.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed. The fish sauce and lime work their magic. I wanted to dress up this dish a bit, so made rice with half can of coconut milk.  Yum!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This is one of those recipes that is so simple that you are suspicious of how good it could possibly be, but it is worth the hype.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This is easy and delicious, especially if you buy your cabbage pre-shredded. I added more lime juice and crushed red pepper flakes, as well as roasted salted peanuts for a little bit of crunch. Easy and delicious week night dinner.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: We loved this. Did double the fish sauce for our family, but I think the recipe's 1 TB is a good place to start. You can always add after cooking if need more. We had it with jasmine rice, but you definitely don't need it if you are watching those carbs!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Two thumbs up from 5 year old twins! And quick and easy. A new staple in our house. Thank you!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: @Christine H. don't freeze chillies, they lose their spiciness. Birds eye/Thai keep super well in the fridge (mine are 1 week beyond the best before end date and haven't deteriorated)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: We have been making a super simple variation of this recipe for years which has two ingredients and a box of jiffy corn bread mix.  Cook one pound of sausage in a large pot and then add one small to medium chopped cabbage with a little water.  Cover and cook on medium low until cabbage is tender while stirring occasionally.  The sausage is all the seasoning needed.  Serve with the cornbread.  We have made this when camping by taking a frozen pound of sausage which keeps in a cooler just fine!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Tasty, quick, and satisfying. Made exactly as specified.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This worked well for us, which kind of surprised me given my ambivalence about cabbage.  Definitely needs more of the flavouring - more ginger, more fish sauce, more lime. But easy and quick to make.  Will make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious and quick to make! I halved the recipe, using only one chicken breast--cut in 1 inch cubes and pulsed in the food processor.  Added the coarsely chopped ginger and some fresh turmeric plus the garlic to the last pulses.  This saved a step in an already quick prep.  Added a sprinkle of black sesame dish to each serving.  Our fish sauce was a salty one, pointing out the value in tasting at the end!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I followed the ingredients and directions exactly. It turned out delicious and was easy to make.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Tasty and quick recipe and adaptable. We added some sambal oelek to our servings.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, easy weeknight dinner.  To save calories, made as directed using riced cauliflower and pork tenderloin ground in my food processor (vs higher fat ground pork from the meat market).  Will definitely make again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Love the dish.  Don't think I would change a thing although I used kosher salt.  I would suggest that some seasoning (salt and pepper) is good as you cook the dish, but then just adjust at the end. And you definitely want to use the fish sauce as it adds a lovely tang to the dish.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Not bad…the cabbage came out a smidge firmer than I’d like, but I’d make it again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Oh lord this was good. Easy, fast, tasty. Butter and fish sauce is even better than butter and miso. I probably used more ginger than was called for but whatever. Will try it with gojuchang or gojucharu next time as someone suggested. This goes in the “Go-To” recipe box!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Quick and easy, and pretty delicious as written.  Next time, I might add a bit more heat (maybe some thinly sliced fresh chili pepper).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I've been making something similar to this for years, only I use a mandolin for finely shredded cabbage and noodles instead of rice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Also good with rice noodles

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I made a double batch because it is ideal for a quick reheating and lunch. The flavor profile was surprisingly umami-goodness. The proportions are quite good. We used 1/2 tsp Aleppo pepper instead of red pepper. You might want to add a sprinkle of chow mein noodles for crunchiness but it is a solid quick recipe. Use a big wok.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: In my opinion, this dish needs a great deal more seasoning. I'd suggest doubling the fish sauce, and maybe adding a healthy dose of hoisin as well. And as others have said, you might need a good deal more salt, which I found to be true even with a sprinkle of MSG, but that can be remedied at dinner time with a splash of soy sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
I loved this recipe. The pork was perfect and the cabbage was delicious. There’s a lot of prep here to start cooking, but once everything is in place, it comes together quickly. The fish sauce and the lime juice add great flavor to the final dish!

One note: I used parsley in place of cilantro. I didn’t have any in the house, and the parsley worked perfectly.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: @bocaboy thanks for that comment because I don’t have any cilantro

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
The night I cooked this I thought keeping the oil from the ground pork made everything too glossy and oily, but I did eat the dish without rice that night. 

The next few nights I ate the leftovers with rice and everything was perfect. 

My only small modifications were double the pepper flakes and a little extra cabbage. When reheating, it goes well with soy sauce if you’re looking for a little more salt and liquid. Speaking of salt, I used about 3/4ths tsp for the pork and 3/4ths tsp for the cabbage and was happy with overall salt flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Made with ground chicken. It was fine, but not any better than a "regular" stir fry.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This was surprisingly good! Reminds me of Nam Sod. I had some cooked Italian sausage that I chopped up and used. I don't understand why they would add salt into the recipe, just add some extra fish sauce - otherwise it will be way too salty.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe! Certainly need a wok or bigger skillet. I cut the last tbsp of butter and bumped up the spices

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Could you use spinach in place of cabbage?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Incredible! My family of cabbage haters couldn't get enough.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Used a wok, didn't add salt, and it was stunningly good!!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Pretty good just as written. I think it could use a bit more fish sauce. Maybe some chopped peanuts on time if you are feeling decadent.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: We just loved this recipe! Made it exactly as written except I took Michael L’s advice and doubled everything so we could have leftovers.  Just loved the Southeast Asian flavors.  And yes, don’t be shy with the fish sauce and lime.  My husband loved that we’ll have it again tomorrow!  5 stars.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Loved this dish!  I used ground chicken instead of pork and it was still delicious.  Will definitely make it again. The flavors are great and easy to make.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This was easy to make and absolutely delicious!  Next time I might use more red pepper flakes.  I think it could use a bit more kick.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Made this a few times now. Latest one I used gochujang instead of red pepper flakes, added earlier to jazz up the cabbage. Yeah, it steps it away a bit from the Thai influence, but still was delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I think white cabbage is a much better fit in both texture, firmness, and taste.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: We doubled the  garlic and ginger + 1 tbs of each: oyster sauce, tamari, 1 1/2 tbs of hoisin sauce and 1/2 tbs chili crisp + a bunch of asparagus. We cooked that recipe for 4 friends tonight,  they all loved it. We almost doubled everything and there were no leftovers !

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I thought the pork needed a lot more cooking and a lot more seasoning.  The cabbage was perfect.  I ended up cooking onions in a separate pan, adding the pork to continue cooking, then putting it all into my food processor to break up the pork chunks even more.  Then I added water and cooked it a while until the pork was tender.  The first night I served the pork and cabbage separately.  The second night I mixed them and the entire dish tasted much better.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Very yummy. I put more garlic of course.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Made it tonight and we agreed it was terrific.  My only error was salting the cabbage .  Did not need it since I used salted butter and as others mentioned the fish sauce already adds a salt element.  But wow, easy and low effort/mess.  Served it on brown rice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Very easy and excellent!  Definitely add the squeeze of  lime juice and the cilantro at the end. This will be added to our rotation!!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This was so delicious and yet so simple. I don't really like pork, so I use ground turkey and added a bit more garlic and ginger to make sure it was flavorful.  I also added some rough chopped peanuts at the end with the cilantro, and it was outstanding! I did not add salt a second time, owing to the fish sauce, and it was the perfect level of salty for us.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
This is just so good. I've made it many times: sometimes with chicken, often with less meat and more cabbage, and it's always great.
Six out of five stars.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
Wow. I could eat this once a week for the rest of my life.
This time I used chicken (that's what I had.) It is a great meal, fast and simple, for autumn.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Excellent quick supper!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed except I used ground turkey and Napa, which I had on hand. Hubster thought it could use more heat; I thought it was fine.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Great weeknight dinner. I doubled the garlic and ginger and added extra red pepper. Very tasty.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely fantastic and SO simple! Only change I made was some extra garlic and to serve it on rice vermicelli tossed with sesame oil & sesame seeds (I didn’t have rice).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I used left over pork roast and sliced it very thin and short pieces. The rest was totally following the recipe. This was the best cabbage I ever have eaten. I will make this again

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: It's easier to add salt than it is to remove it. I always take recommendations for salt in recipes.with a grain of salt. I'm 85, and I got this old by using salt very sparingly - if at all. Recipes that call for soy sauce, fish sauce, and other salty ingredients usually require no additional salt, IMHO. Taste first, and then add if necessary.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I love braised cabbage. I prefer it cooked to a soft stage with loads of onion and butter. I would think with the brief cook cooking time that this cabbage would still at that ‘squeaky’ stage when you bit into it, if one is using standard green cabbage. For a tenderer result with a quicker cook time, use savoy cabbage or Chinese cabbage. One could complicate things by sautéing the thicker stem pieces with the pork and adding the leafier green sections near the end to add greater textural form.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I only used 1/2 as much pork and it was plenty.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Used ground turkey instead of pork. Step 2: Add snow peas and sliced carrot and celery. Step 4: Add a tablespoon each of soy sauce and chili crisp or sambal oelek. Step 5: Garnish with sliced green onion.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This dish is outstanding. So good and so fast.  I wouldn’t change a thing.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I always read the comments before cooking. I usually disregard the too salty comments. In this case, trust the comments. It is way too salty. Didn’t like it because of the salt and too much fish sauce, in my opinion.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Scaled this up because I wanted to use the whole bag of lentils.  I used water, but will use 1/2 stock next time.  Added a little curry powder as well.  The herbs are a must!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I made this pretty much as is. It was surprisingly delicious and very easy. Something about the butter with the fish sauce... I used a very large pan, it was not non-stick and it was fine.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I added a can of chickpeas to boost fiber.  Subbed small onion for scallions.  Otherwise made as specified. Served with bone broth rice.  Fish sauce and lime juice in combination with butter and pork really give this simple dish some wonderful complexity. Absolutely delish!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
This was the weeknight trifecta of easy/fast, "in this economy?" approved, and absolutely phenomenal. For two people I used 1/2 lb pork and half a large cabbage, kept all the aromatics etc the same. Served over white rice and we both had second helpings.

I used white pepper which really complements the pork. Thank you Kay Chun!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Quick, easy, and tasty! Love that it gave me a chance to use up the half of a cabbage that's been in my crisper, seemingly forever.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: used pork tenderloin tails cut into thin strips, otherwise followed as written, with amplified amounts of Red Boat & lime juice to great effect. yes to peanutz

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Thai pork and cabbage is delicious Vietnamese cold salads with fish sauce and the seasonings. Here is also delicious. This is a mixture of the two that doesn’t work. This is the worst recipe I’ve ever tried. I have never given anything but four and five star reviews and this one gets it too. I just want my ingredients back.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I was lazy and pushed the ground pork, garlic, ginger and scallions to the sides of the pane, and put the butter and the cabbage in the middle.  I added cremini mushrooms I wanted to use up (who doesn’t love pork and mushrooms?) and covered the pan for about five minutes.  Open it, stirred it up, and added the rest of the ingredients.  Love it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I cook a lot of NYT recipes and this might be my favorite. So easy. And so delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Easy. Tasty. A great fast meal when you’ve had a long day and just want to sit down and eat.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Made this for dinner and it was delicious! I used dew drop cabbage that I got from Trader Joe's, added shitake mushrooms, subbed red chiles flakes for a bit of gochugaru and reduced the butter from 3 to 2 tablespoons. Will absolutely make this again for a quick weeknight meal.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: PHENOMENAL! No notes. Made zero modifications and it was perfect. Extremely easy and quick. Will be adding to the rotation!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody:
Made for the second time. Half recipe, but full amount of garlic, ginger, and fish sauce. Subbed basil for cilantro (I have that gene). 

Really, really tasty, big flavors, very quick for a weeknight, becoming a family favorite. Great with a Viognier or southern Rhône blend.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Such a great recipe! Made it exactly as written—and it almost took me only 30 minutes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Good recipe, though I found it a little bland in the end despite the fish sauce funk and salt. A little drizzled of oyster sauce did the trick to round out the flavors for me.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and simple!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This was so so good for how cheap and fast it was to make. Immediately added to our dinner rotation.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Did not love this. Followed recipe exactly as I always do on the first try. I wanted to like this after reading all the reviews, but this was rather bland. Orzo also came out kind of gummy but firm on the inside. Would not bother making again, but my husband liked it enough out of 3 of us.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: @Sarah. Orzo?  There is no orzo in this recipe,,,

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Great weeknight dinner. Super fast to put together and a nice change of pace. I added in a minced jalapeno when cooking the pork to add a little extra heat. Had soy sauce on the table, as it needed a little more salt.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Loved this. It probably did not need so much butter as the ground pork is pretty fatty. Might try with ground turkey next time

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I read the top comments first and I'm kind of puzzled why so many said it didn't taste like much. I loved it as instructed... I was generous with the pepper (I used white). It was a lifesaver actually because I was pressed for time today and this recipe was quick and easy to cook.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Great weekday meal. I used green cabbage and almost doubled the ginger. It needed a little something at the end so I added sesame oil and based on another suggestion, peanuts. Excellent over brown rice

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: The butter just makes it !  Very easy and fast for a quick meal.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: My husband and I enjoyed this a lot. Loved the cabbage. My only modifications were increasing the garlic and ginger, and that the cabbage took a couple minutes more than 5 mins to cook. May have been the size of my chop though. Also followed the suggestion and made in the wok.  So Easy and delicious and will be in the rotation for sure.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: BLAND!  I even doubled the sauce and added chili garlic sauce.  It makes a lot so I am hoping to salvage the leftovers by making Nuoc Cham sauce and adding it.  What's with the 5 stars???

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: This was an eye-opener. I was searching for a recipe that was not cole slaw to use up extra cabbage and this looked interesting enough to at least use it up with. Wrong. What a fantastic mix of flavors, different than any other recipe I make. And the fact that it was quick and easy was just a bonus. This will go into some version of my regular rotation.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Butter was overwhelming but otherwise a great recipe!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Fairly easy recipe yielding great comfort food results.  Made this with one mod and a couple suggestions.  1) added one thinly sliced jalapeno (or maybe a serano?) pepper in either step 1 or 2; 2) double the amounts of both fish sauce and lime juice for a little more punch; 3) I'd use a wok if you have one, because it's a lot of cabbage (i used a whole head of cabbage).  But without mods?  Yes, a comfort meal.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: I substituted bok choy for the cabbage and added chopped radishes as a garnish. It is delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Made with sliced boneless country rib meat since that's what I had.  Came out fine.  Certainly easy to make and healthy

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Very good, and I will make it again. I agree that the brand of fish sauce makes a difference. I added salt to steps, as directed in recipe, but used only 2 tsp salt. For me, it was a bit too salty (and I like salt). The fish sauce almost over powered the other flavors. Next time I'll use a different brand and reduce the amount of additional salt. I'd also use a bit more lime juice. The cabbage (5 c chopped) cooks down considered, so I wouldn't hesitate to use 6 or 7 cups to balance the proportion of pork. overall, though, very tasty!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
commentBody: Just made this and due to the comments rather than pepper flakes I put in some gochugaru sauce..made it nice but not overwhelming..will make again

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: Too fussy. Fry the cabbage in a mix of olive oil and butter w/ s & p. When it's getting gold in spots and crisp on some edges, squeeze half a lemon over it and serve. It's delicious and far easier.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody:
@Susan

Sure! but that isn’t southern style.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: I've cooked cabbage like this for several years. I haven't used mustard, but will try it. I often finish with a splash of balsamic vinegar or a small scoop of brown sugar, or both. Everybody raves about it - even kids.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: I make this with red cabbage and add a chopped white onion and finish it with champagne vinegar.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: Thoughts on adding a can of, say, cannellini beans to the dish? For those of us seeking a main dish and wanting to minimize animal protein. I so want to try this.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: I had the same idea, and am going to try it tonight.  More news later!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: One can of cannellini beans, drained, made a main dish out of this.  Very nice meal!!  The small dash of mustard at the end helped balance the sweetness of the cooked cabbage and bacon.  A squeeze of lemon juice or a dash of your vinegar of choice would probably do so as well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody:
My mother (and now I) has been making something like since for 40 years. We use ground beef and often I add sliced bell peppers if I have them. Sometimes I throw some balsamic vinegar in at the end. 
Easy weeknight dinner when it is made with ground beef. I call it Polish Fries and my kids love it. One of my favorite dishes from childhood as well

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: Love how low cal this is. We cut the onions into bigger chunks so they didn't all fall to the bottom of the bowl and doubled the mustard and bacon to make sure there was enough for every bite.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: You can add hot sauce to this when finishing the cooking for "hot cabbage" a truly Southern treat.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: We make a similar weeknight meal by adding the uncooked bacon for the cabbage after it’s half cooked (crispy bacon doesn’t work as well here), replace mustard with red pepper flakes and serve over spaghetti.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: This was a nice change from the typical Slavic way that I sauté cabbage. I did add at least double the mustard. To make it a more complete meal, I served it with a poached egg on top. With the leftovers, I cooked up a small amount of orichietti and added it to the heated leftover cabbage. I used the pasta water to loosen everything and make it saucy. I grated a half a cup of Parmesan cheese in it and gave it a few generous grinds of black pepper.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: Adding sliced apple, reducing mustard and using a splash of apple cider vinegar gives this a sweet and sour punch. Delish!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: I've done the same thing for years with brussel sprouts, sliced into ribbons.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: I make this regularly; the kids and I love it! We will often substitute rooster (sriracha) sauce for the mustard or red pepper flakes. While some consider the bacon optional, bacon makes everything better!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: Simple, easy, delicious. (Halved the recipe and ate the whole “2 servings” portion for dinner.) I hadn’t thought of using mustard before, but it added a nice acidic note and earthy flavor to the sweet, salty, caramelized, umami notes of the rest.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: Put a fried egg on top with a splash of Tobacco for a delicious paleo breakfast!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: This is just as delicious (if not better) the next day! Reheated by spreading everything on a baking sheet and popping it into the toaster oven for a few minutes until the cabbage was slightly charred around the edges.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: Instead of bacon, I sautéed with 2 tbsp balsamic vinegar, 1/4 cup red wine and 1 tbsp brown sugar. Takes awhile for the cabbage to get tender, but worth the wait.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: I subbed tempeh for bacon, used extra garlic, and used some tamari at the end. A success.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: I used my left over potsticker filling of ground pork, minced ginger, garlic& scallion whites instead of bacon because I had to use it up. Followed your recipes going forward and it was incredibly delicious.  Trying it with bacon this weekend.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: My mother was a Southern citizen ok from Arkansas. This is just like her recipe, except she would have had only yellow mustard on hand. Thanks for the memories. I will cook now that I am reminded of its delicious and comp flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: Usually there’s nutrition information for NYT recipes. Could it be added?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: So good!  Made as written sans onion (didn't have) and was absolutely delicious.  Next time more mustard.  Didn't miss the onion.  Will try this as a filling for pierogi.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: I so wanted to love this. I followed the recipe to the letter and found it meh. Might try the variations listed in comments.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: This made for a simple, quick, and a very satisfying dinner with whole grain toast and goat cheese. Used a Savoy cabbage, whole onion and 2 garlic cloves. For bacon, I used tesa from NYT home cured pork belly recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: Question: can you freeze this?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Sweet from the cabbage and slightly vinegary. Made it for breakfast, added 3 carrots in 1" pieces, added with onions, rosemary halfway through steaming and served topped with lightly fried eggs. Yolky herby cabbagey goodness!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
commentBody: Use a cast iron skillet, properly seasoned.  Non-stick cookware is coated with Teflon, a carcinogenic, toxic chemical.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
