commentBody: Hey folks--use the bacon!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Galbitang (Short Rib and Korean Radish Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Great base recipe that you can totally make your own! I added some dried shiitake and kombu for a boost on umami. This went into the Instant Pot for 50 minutes and I seasoned to taste with cane sugar, fish sauce, and salt. Served with rice, sweet potato noodles, and kimchi. This was a very simple yet nourishing meal. I’d definitely refrigerate overnight to remove the excess fat next time I make this.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Galbitang (Short Rib and Korean Radish Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Simple, comforting and refreshing. I made the stew as written, with an extra inch of ginger. I did not defat the broth as aggressively as I could have and wish that I had - otherwise this was perfect. Served with kimchi and short grain rice on the side, it is a perfect light lunch or dinner. If short ribs were cheaper ($15/pound near me) I’d make this more often - I wonder how chuck roast or beef stew meat would fare?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Galbitang (Short Rib and Korean Radish Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Made this oxtail, which wss delicious. Added some shiitake too

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I'd make this again 'as is' except I would dirty the extra pot to caramelize and sweat the mushrooms separately (while caramelizing the onions in the main pot).  Then add them together and proceed. Even in my large Dutch oven, the large onion + 1lb of mushrooms put out a TON of water, added a lot of cooking time, and made it hard to get that sweet sweet caramelization. Otherwise, so easy and delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I've found the best way to cook mushrooms is start them first, without any butter, oil, or salt. (Pepper is ok at this point.) Stir frequently, and after a few minutes, add the butter, salt, and the onions

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
@Dan W
Great idea! Will try it out

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
@Victoria

I toss the mushrooms with oil and herbs, then roast them. They turn golden with no effort. It’s so much easier! I think 425°. Sure, you have to wash the sheet pan but you won’t burn the bottom of your pan or smoke yourself out. Then just add them into the pot and go from there.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This is almost identical to the Hungarian mushroom soup I make. To amp up the mushroom flavor, I also add about an ounce of dried porcini mushrooms that I have rehydrated in 1/4 cup hot water. After the mushrooms have softened, I chop them up and add them and the water used to hydrate them to the soup. Also, a little dill goes a long way in our house, so I often just use thyme.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: @dkrieger  Except for the last sentence, I could have written the exact comment!  However, the last sentence does strike home because I love dill and my wife hates it!  100% on the dried mushrooms (any kind) and adding the mushroom water to the stock.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: @DKrieger- fantastic idea. Porcini is the butter of vegetables - makes everything better!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
@DKrieger
Thanks for the dill comment. I’m extremely allergic to dill and was wondering if more thyme or other herbs would work.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This is almost identical to the The Moosewood Cookbook’s,  “Hungarian Mushroom Soup.”

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: @Shari Forcina can you let me know how it's different? I made the Moosewood recipe often and with great success in the '90s,  but every time I've revisited it in recent years, it hasn't been as good. I think I did something with the roux back then that I can't remember how to replicate. I'm wondering if the roux directions here would help.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
@GG
I am an iconoclast when it comes to roux.
1)  I use less butter/oil and I cook the roux until it starts to look dry or crumbly. That cooks the flour past the "raw" state. 
2) When I add the liquid, I make sure it's cold, then pour it in all at once and immediately whisk like crazy. And I *never* get lumps. My theory is that the cold liquid solidifies the fat and whisking like mad breaks up the solidified fat and flour mixture and eliminates the lumps before they can happen.

I am 77 years old and I have been making bechamel, etc., using this technique for well over 50 years, and never a lump. 

This is a challenge to the NYT test kitchen: Try it. Several of you, please. (Melissa Clark? Eric Kim? Vaughn Vreeland?) And please report back. I'm longing to hear about your results. And remember: immediately whisk like fury!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: @GG i made moosewood’s this weekend, I was surprised to see the identical soup here! yes, a roux with 1 cup of milk cooked 10 min, and then add 1.5 cups broth. all the same spices and seasonings EXCEPT no thyme, and tamari instead of worcestershire. i usually add a touch more broth :)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Hi. Could Greek yogurt replace the sour cream?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I did, full fat whole delicious Greek yogurt. Yum!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: @Margo if you want it to.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: @Margo I used nonfat greek yogurt and it was delicious

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This is the first NYT recipe I didn't have to tweak the flavors. It is delicious as written. I caramelized my mushrooms first in butter and red wine. Removed them from the pan, added a little more butter, and sauteed the onions and garlic. I added the mushrooms back in at the end. Next time, I would add another cup of mushrooms and finely dice them to ensure you get mushrooms in every bite.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: So good! Def double this!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Moosewood Cookbook was my first cookbook. This recipe, though familiar is lighter with the addition of lemon juice. I do use at least three kinds of mushrooms and mix my onions. I love this soup!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This is almost identical to the Moosewood Cookbook's Hungarian Mushroom Soup. You are remiss if you do not cite that as a reference.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Why in the world would you serve this  in  tiny cups as an appetizer? This soup begs to be lunch, in a big bowl.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: It would be helpful to have some kind of quantity indication any time you write "season with salt and pepper." A light sprinkling? 1/2 t. each? Something else? Since this comes so early in the recipe it's a while before we can taste and adjust.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: @Bike Walk Bake Barb this seems to me like it would fall under the “personal preference” category….

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: @Bike Walk Bake Barb I SO agree with you! Having a starting point would be so much more helpful than “salt to taste”!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: @Bike Walk Bake Barb Generally, when you see salt added early on to a sauté its primary purpose is to help draw the water from the veggies. You do not need a lot, and you do not want to overdo salt this early in a recipe because you can always adjust salt at the end. Personally, I save pepper for the final seasoning of a recipe. In other words, it’s just a light sprinkling this early in the recipe. 😊

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: It took me longer than the recipe called for to sweat the mushrooms. I added more lemon and a bit more garlic than the recipe called for. Very good and next time I would add some kombu flakes at the beginning.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: A/A+. Try again.  As per tips;  Cook mushrooms separately without butter until all liquid evaporates then stir in butter.  After onions , garlic, spices cooked add mushrooms.  Also dehydrate 1 oz.porcini in 1/4 c hot water after dicing, including soaking water.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Hungarian here. Never heard of dill or MILK ?? in mushroom soup. We make the roux with just butter (or actually goose fat/lard) and flour. And much more common today would be to mix a tbsp of flour in the sour cream and thicken the soup that way, omitting the roux entirely.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This was wonderful! Lots of flavor and although lots of steps, easy to prepare. I cheated and used a food processor for chopping…..

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I made this soup and followed the recipe to the T. Delicious but while I was eating it I wondered about using a T spoon of Sherry instead of the lemon. May try it the next time I make it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Question: You suggest freezing. Is it possible to freeze a soup/sauce with flour in it? My enchilada sauce breaks down if frozen, have tried both flour or corn starch, but that recipe uses a roux to begin and this soup calls for four incorporated toward the end.  So maybe that is the difference?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: @Patricia freezing cheese sauce aka macaroni cheese sauce works and that uses a roux and no acid. Enchilada has tomatoes aka acid. Acid’s job is to break down stuff, that’s why vinegar aka acid is a good cleaner. I think it’s the acid and thawing processes happening simultaneously that’s breaking down the flour after the sauce defrosts. But if you post your question on quora or reddit, you should get a more qualified answer (a food scientist hopefully). I’m just a nerd

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
I added 1/2 cup of uncooked wild rice at the end of step 2 and cooked it much longer (about 50 min to soften the rice) before moving on to step 3. Added a bit extra liquid but it would have been fine without, too. 

Highly recommend adding wild rice!

I also subbed Greek yogurt for the sour cream (it’s what I had) and it worked great.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This was incredible. I was surprised how well the flavors of dill and paprika blended—neither comes across too strong. I topped bowls with dried mushrooms rehydrated in cognac and then sautéed in butter. Highly recommend.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: To add a bit more umami (if you don’t have dried porcini) you can add a teaspoon of miso paste to the mushrooms toward the end as you caramelize them - just don’t add more than a pinch of salt! Also, be sure to use a silicone spatula to keep it from sticking to the bottom of the pan and burning.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: As others have said, this is almost identical to The Moosewood Cookbook Hungarian mushroom soup which I've been making since the 1980's, always to rapturous reviews.  You can make it more "refined" by increasing the amount of mushrooms or more "earthy" by increasing the onions.  Use good Hungarian paprika.  I also will make a big batch of the mushroom/onion/paprika/dill and freeze to pull out later and add the stock and white sauce to finish the soup.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I made this for my local elks lodge. I had never cooked anything and thought it would be a simple starter recipe. Boy was I wrong. First of all I couldn’t find any elk meat so I had to hunt my own. That took a week and almost lost my eyeball. Then I had to find a lodge but there was none at the market so I had to build one. By the time I got to the salmon course I was tuckered out. Fortunately my local elks only had one member, me, so I didn’t get a ton of negative feedback for my first attempt. But I will say, 116 degree weather is not ideal for a hot steaming bowl of soup made of elk and wood.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Yummy yummy in my tummy

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Do they mean Hot Hungarian Paprika?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: The recipe calls for regular paprika, which is often sold as "Hungarian" paprika. That said, you can use hot paprika if you want a spicier soup, or smoked paprika, or a combination. I use a blend of smoked and regular to delicious results.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: To clarify: you can use any paprika, but Hungarian is generally of higher quality than your generic grocery store paprika. The heat level of the paprika is up to you. Sweet, hot, or smoked all work, or a combination of two or more of them.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: In Gordon Ramsey’s Master Class, he says to cook mushrooms without releasing so much water, get your pan Very Hot. Then add a little olive oil then the mushrooms and cook for a few minutes. Then add butter to brown the mushrooms a golden hue. I’ve done this several times recently and No Water! I’m not sure it would work in a Dutch oven so I’d probably do as others in a separate pan first.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This is a great soup for mushroom lovers -- it made me think of a goulash but lighter and meatless. I tried to wing it on a second try but it didn't quite succeed the way the first batch did. It does take time for the onion and mushroom to get to their favored consistency.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: How critical is the 1 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce?  I’m generally not a fan of it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I LOVE this recipe.  Even though there is no meat, the flavor is rich, satisfying, deeply flavorful.  Will cook this again (already have since the first batch disappeared so quickly!)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Can you substitute non-dairy milk for whole milk?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This is basically the Moosewood Lodge recipe. I’ve been making it for years.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Made with 3 types of mushrooms for a dinner party- just stopped at Step 3 until I wanted to serve the soup. SO WONDERFUL! Everyone loved it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
Only complaint is that it’s all gone same night I made it - this should be scaled up to leave some left overs
It was great

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely fantastic. Myteenage  son who despises soups and is all about meat in his diet loved it. So tasty, so nutritious

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This is one of the best soups I have ever made. I used cremini mushrooms as the base. I followed other's suggestions: used separate pans for sweating the mushrooms and onions; coarsely shopped about a third of the mushrooms; added 0.5 ounce rehydrated dried porcini mushrooms -adding the excess water to broth. Will definitely make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Made as written for dinner over rice.  Delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Anyone tried this with low fat milk?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions on how to make this dairy free that does not include coconut?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I also boosted the broth with 1 cup water that had steeped dried mushrooms, and added a little bit of caraway seeds. The flavor is great, will definitely make again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: made a couple of substitutions based on what I had on hand -- Greek yogurt for sour cream and red wine vinegar for lemon juice.  absolutely delicious.  rich and comforting on a fall evening.  Pinot Noir the perfect compliment.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This was Really good! I used Paprika I got recently on Budapest. It was hot!  For us, it was perfectly balanced with the creamy soup. Loved it and will make again as the weather gets colder.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Did not get the some colors (red) as shown in the recipe. Mine turned out mire greyish.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Years ago, I worked in a restaurant that served homemade Hungarian mushroom soup every night. This recipe is a perfect replica. I used a combination of oyster and cremini mushrooms. The only paprika I had on hand was Spanish smoked paprika, which worked well in lieu of the Hungarian paprika. Served the soup with a big loaf of crusty bread and butter. It's quite an easy recipe to make.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Not so fabulous. Wouldn’t make again . No distinctive flavor

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: If my grandmother were alive, she'd tell you that sour cream is never optional in Hungarian food!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Great flavor!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Really delicious! Even though I made the mistake of using Better than Bouillon veggie broth and mushroom broth concentrate for the 3 cups broth, and it's way too salty. Will make again, just with different broth. Oh, and I followed Victoria's advice and cooked the mushrooms and onions separately. It would have taken forever if that were all in my soup pot.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: It took me about 20 minutes to brown the onions and mushrooms. I added some white wine. Used mixed mushrooms. Added the flour and milk as directed by the recipe. Delicious

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I love, love, love this recipe. After the first time I made it, I doubled it. It does take forever to cook the mushrooms and onion slowly, but the caramelization is worth it.. I saw a comment where they did it in two separate pans, that seems like a great idea. The only thing I do different is add a dash of Cayenne pepper.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: It felt like a lot of steps, but was ultimately worth every minute. This yielded a rich, creamy soup that was perfect for a cold winter night. Made it exactly as written; next time I would swap in a teaspoon or less of smoked paprika for some of the regular.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: "This is savory."

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: excellent flavor but missing some texture -- crunchy pepitas or breadcrumbs maybe

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Excellent soup! I tripled the recipe and split the mushroom and onions across the pot and a pan for caramelizing. Even so, it took probably 20-30 minutes to caramelize them. The rest of the soup came together quickly though so I think it was worth it. Great flavor—very earthy and thick with a little kick of heat which I didn’t expect (must be the paprika).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious soup, I used a combination of foraged oyster mushrooms and crimini. Many compliments!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed. Ever so delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I have questions. What kind of Hungarian paprika, smoked or sweet? Can you use plant milk in place of the dairy milk?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This was soooooo good! The ultimate comfort food on a cold, dreary day. I followed the recipe and wouldn't dare try to improve a thing!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This is very lovely but it is without question an appetizer. I made it (following the suggestion to cook the mushrooms first!) and enjoyed but made the mistake of serving it as more of a main course. Oops.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful! Made almost exactly as written, but I may have had a few more cremini mushrooms than called for, and also added some chanterelles I had on hand. Perfect as a satisfying main course served with a piece of toasted, good quality bread on the side. And the leftovers for lunch were just as delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
Serious Eats just had an essay about the Moosewood recipe and I was inspired. I made it using a half cup of porcini soaking liquid that I had in the freezer and 3 cups of chicken stock. Used 2x the dill, because we like it.
I skipped the roux- just cooked it down for a half hour and then puréed half of the soup. Excellent.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Wow delicious. Amazing balance of flavors. Comforting and warming. Served with bread and noodles.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This is like a delicious beef stroganoff soup! I haven't gone through all the comments so someone may have already said this. I did the recipe exactly as is and loved it. It's definitely an app and not a main, so I cooked a steak and made a steakhouse salad to go with it. Soooo good.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: My partner who actively dislikes mushrooms had two bowls. The whole family was raving. Outstanding!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
made exactly as is - but to the mushrooms, as many commenters have added more time
is necessary. also - i turned up the heat to high - since the mushrooms expelled a ton of water and it would’ve taken forever to get a full evaporation on medium heat. flavors here are amazing - love it!!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: @TCookie thanks for sharing. I made the moosewood version for years. and now am doing low carb so I was wondering about not using flour

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Worked out very well.  Next time I would make a white or blond roux instead of adding flour to milk,  I found the final product still had a bit of raw flour taste which the roux would eliminate.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Really good and super easy.  I used a packaged mix of mushrooms from the store, 1 T of Worcestershire sauce instead of 1 tsp, and 1.5 T of cornstarch instead of flour (to keep it gluten free).  Goes well with fresh baked bread.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I want to try this again with some tweaks like deglazing with a splash of dry sherry, or perhaps roasting the mushrooms in the oven to coax out a little more flavor from them. All in all it was a very silky soup and the seasonings were good. I've made a similar soup in the past with brie melted into it and perhaps that's why I feel like it's just missing something.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I made as directed and was surprised at how thick it was and felt there was a bit too much dill- after reading the comments I would replace the sour cream with full fat Greek yogurt and dice an additional cup of mushrooms to disperse more mushrooms throughout the- I agree, replacing the Worcestershire sauce with tamari would be good

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: This is so good! I followed the recommendation to roast the mushrooms instead of browning with the onions. It made them nice and chewy. I also had no idea what Hungarian paprika was, so I just used smoked paprika. Worked out great!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I made ours vegan (plant based butter, sour cream, and oat milk) and although I misread the recipe and started with 1 tsp of paprika (doh), luckily I realized before I served. Also used fresh herbs instead of dried  I’ll try again following some of the suggestions, I think this will become a household staple!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delish.  I wasn’t sure if my “Hot” Hungarian Paprika would be too much for my wife so I went half and half with “generic” paprika.  Perhaps next time I will use my smoked paprika.  Anyway, the wife was pleased!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: It was very flavorful without being spicy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Very good! I used half cremini mushrooms and half baby bellas. Two teaspoons of regular paprika and one teaspoon smoked. I made a couple of minor changes as follows: I sautéed the onions first then added the mushrooms and cooked them down, but didn’t brown them. And for the acid I added a tablespoon of sherry and a teaspoon of lemon juice. I’ll make this again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody:
I doubled this because 4 appetizer servings were not going to be enough!  Even though I usually make half recipes at this stage of my life.  I wish the Times had a scale function.  I always have to write out the revised quantities when I double or halve a recipe, because if i don’t it’s a sure thing that I’ll add the wrong amount of something.  

This came out great.  Browning 2 lb of mushrooms took forever so I was glad I heeded the commenters who said to do them separately.   I then did the onions in the same Dutch oven and added the mushrooms back to continue the prep.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: One of the tastiest things I've made from NYT Cooking.  Used crimini & shitake mushrooms.  Followed other comments and caramelized the mushrroms first, removed them and did the same with the onions which added a few minutes to the cooking time but worth it.  A bit of smoked paprika too.  Killer soup.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Great taste, will double next time for sure"big" bowls of soup.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Any thoughts on using vegan sour cream? I usually replace whole milk with Oatly oat creamer with good results. But not sure about sour cream in this soup... anyone try it?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: Really delicious soup! I didn't have sour cream but used full fat Greek yogurt and that worked well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
commentBody: I cook this soup each fall. Using chanterelles and adding some stripes of green peppers. Fresh dill is a must. I call it Chanterelles chowder here at the Oregon coast.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Mornay Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Recipe could use a tip to warm the milk in a microwave. Warm milk mixes into the roux more easily than cold.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Mornay Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I always simmer the milk with 1/4 onion stuck with a couple of whole cloves per Julia Child . It wakes up what can be a bland sauce

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Mornay Sauce Recipe
commentBody: My favorite use of Mornay sauce is the traditional French one: as an accompaniment to seafood: salmon, char, halibut, even scallops. But let each person at the table decide whether to put a spoonful on the fish, or on the potatoes, rice, or vegetables instead. Almost everyone (children included) will want this rich but mild sauce on something, right up to apple tart.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Mornay Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Oeufs Chimay: eggs, duxelles and mornay.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Mornay Sauce Recipe
commentBody: That's right, agreeing with other comments; Béchamel has a hint of onion. One good way is to stud a half onion with a couple of whole cloves and let that simmer in the milk while heating in a saucepan.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Mornay Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This is exactly the base for my Mac & cheese, & how I “build” it. I incorporate 4 different cheeses in average, including some additional shredded as assembled.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Mornay Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Milk needs to be heated and slowly whisked in warm. Won't incorporate well if cold.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Mornay Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I always warm the milk, brings the sauce together more smoothl, and faster.  And I've been using oat flour, works fine, reduces indigestion for me.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Mornay Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Add dry mustard and sub a little beer for the milk for Welsh rarebit

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Mornay Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Am I the only one not feeling keen to press plastic wrap on my hot sauce?  Do we have a 21st century alternative to plastic wrap to prevent a skin from developing?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Mornay Sauce Recipe
commentBody: @MARGARET G. Use parchment paper!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Mornay Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Was very good as a sauce with Salmon. Very beginner friendly.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Mornay Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
Reduced fat milk works just fine in this recipe; the butter has enough fat to deliver the texture you expect from such a sauce.
Wonderful for ham & asparagus crepes!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gai Lan (Chinese Broccoli)  Recipe
commentBody: Gai lan, or “green vegetable” (青菜), is a common and very simple side dish in the “water country”around Suzhou, Shanghai, Hangzhou and Shaoxing. In fact, adding a splash or two of Shaoxing wine into the pan might not hurt things a bit…

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gai Lan (Chinese Broccoli)  Recipe
commentBody: Gai lan is 芥蓝 in Chinese, not "green vegetable/青菜." The latter usually refers to bok choy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gai Lan (Chinese Broccoli)  Recipe
commentBody: Slicing leaves in half and stems into 2” to 3” large angled pieces makes the Gai Lan cook quickly.  I cook with minced garlic and ginger in a few tablespoons of olive oil.  After 2 to 3 minutes stirring on high heat the Gai Lan is a beautiful deep green and doesn’t need any further ingredients.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gai Lan (Chinese Broccoli)  Recipe
commentBody: The sesame oil can be applied when plated. You will get the full fragrance of it that way.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gai Lan (Chinese Broccoli)  Recipe
commentBody: Agreed - I would never use toasted sesame oil for the heating stage of cooking, only added at the end for flavor, or else in a marinade. I made this with a generous amount of peanut oil and even used defrosted frozen garlic and ginger cubes (from Trader Joe's) because I was hungry and tired -- and it was simple, bright green, and delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gai Lan (Chinese Broccoli)  Recipe
commentBody: This dish cries out for a couple of dollops of oyster sauce!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gai Lan (Chinese Broccoli)  Recipe
commentBody: Okay to use common broccoli?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gai Lan (Chinese Broccoli)  Recipe
commentBody: Why don't you give it a try? What is the worst that can happen?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gai Lan (Chinese Broccoli)  Recipe
commentBody: We like to top it off with oyster sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gai Lan (Chinese Broccoli)  Recipe
commentBody: We've always stir fried, usually with beef, garlic, etc.  Never boiled or steamed.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gai Lan (Chinese Broccoli)  Recipe
commentBody: I found the ginger was too overpowering. I would reduce the ginger to 1/2 teaspoon. Instead of adding additional oil. I prefer to blanch the green and then toss it in a teaspoon of garlic that has been heated in a tablespoon of olive oil.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gai Lan (Chinese Broccoli)  Recipe
commentBody: Would it be wise to use baby bok choy if gai lan isn't available near me?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Gai Lan (Chinese Broccoli)  Recipe
commentBody: I always enjoyed this dish in China. I’ve found that here in the USA, with my other menu items like roasted salmon and rice, I like to add hot sesame oil as a garnish before serving.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: I recommend making a 1:1 slurry of light soy sauce and corn starch (1 Tbsp of each), whisking it together, mixing with the sliced chicken, and letting this sit covered on the counter for 30 minutes before cooking. It much improves the texture of chicken in stir frys, even when using chicken breast, and the residual corn starch on the chicken with help thicken sauces. Also, instead of rinsing the noodles, drain them and put them on a tray in the fridge to dry. This helps keep them springy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: @Frank I agree with your very good suggestions. I sometimes make a slurry using Chinese cooking wine and arrowroot powder (1 Tbs each) which likewise marinates the chicken and helps it retain good moisture while stir frying.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Shaoxing can be replaced by sherry fairly easily. Black vinegar is harder because its flavor it very unique—kind of roasted and deep. The only thing that comes to mind is balsamic but I wouldn't go that route. I think there's enough other flavors and such a small amount of it that something like cider or malt vinegar will give you the acidity and a little flavor without getting in the way or punching up flavors that might not work with the dish.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody:
Love a recipe that doesn't over complicate by using half tablespoons this or three quarters teaspoons of that--this is a recipe that will easily go together with one trusty implement--a tablespoon and easy to measure components!
Thanks from someone who hates having twenty measuring spoons out, and wonders how much did that recipe say?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: My wonderful local Asian grocer directed me to “Chinkiang Vinegar” which is “Chinese Black Vinegar”. However, no where on the label does it say “Black Vinegar”.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: @Bob does it need to say that it is black vinegar since you already know that it is black vinegar?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: this comment helped me find the vinegar. I'm thinking it's a helpful comment for others.@Mike in Oz

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: What are the best options for replacing this vinegar and wine for something I may already have in my kitchen?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Try balsamic vinegar for the Chinese black vinegar and dry sherry for the Shaoxing wine.  These are both common subs.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: white pepper always

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: I have read that Worcestershire sauce is a good replacement for black vinegar.  Balsamic vinegar can be too sweet.  That being said, I have black vinegar, but I live near,any Asian food stores and also have ordered it online (kept in either the fridge or a cool place, it lasts a very long time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: As for the cooking wine, someone commented on another NYT Cooking recipe that manzanilla sherry is a good substitute and I concur!  Also, sake is another alternative.  Bon appétit!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: You could substitute pretty much any protein for the chicken.  I used beef because I had a small piece of flank steak in the freezer, but I think pork, shrimp, extra-firm tofu, turkey, maybe lamb, etc. would work just fine.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: @Don—or any leftover meat and/or veggie can be used, cutting length of “cooking” time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: I learned how to make almost the exact same dish watching my best friend's mom cook.  I've had many people ask how can somebody as white as you make something like this?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: I’m confused by the direction regarding thinly sliced snow peas?  I think I’ll either leave them whole or possibly cut in half after I de-string them and trim the hard little end. Much smaller and I’d think they’d be difficult to pick up with chopsticks.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: How can I get that smoky wok hei flavor without a blow torch?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody:
Why is EVERY oriental dish these days destroyed by pounds of Cabbage ? Almost everywhere you go nowadays - you order Chow Mein & it is almost totally Pan Fried Noodles (Good) & a large Bowl of Cabbage (Huh?)  with a Thimble full of tiny Frozen veggie mix from a bag. Sorry that ain't Chow Mein. 
It got so I missed decent Chow Mein SO much I searched it out - found some recipes & Advice & learned how to make it myself. A few times it turned out very good - better than take out for sure

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Because cabbage is dirt cheap!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Yes ,that makes sense ( "Because it is cheap") - but doesn't make it good ,especially if a person dislike Cabbage

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: @Horase it was $5.69 a head at Stop n Shop here in Westchester, NY this week! I’ll sub broccoli slaw.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody:
I’m someone who loves having leftover meals in the fridge but hates having leftover fresh ingredients in the fridge. So I made this with several tweaks with that in mind and it came out great:

Used a whole small Napa cabbage and just halved it and sliced it with a knife (I’m sorry but I cannot imagine grating a Napa cabbage). Cut out the bean sprouts, used a large carrot but grated it with a box grater, and used a whole package of snow peas that I just ran a knife through. Did the velveting technique on the chicken where you marinate it in a bit of cornstarch and soy sauce while you prep everything else. Doubled the sauce quantities. Used a whole 1lb package of shelf stable chow mien egg noodles prepared per package directions. 

It all barely fit in my 15” skillet and it took a while for the veggies to cook down but the result was quite tasty and made a lot of leftovers. If you prefer having two extra ready-to-eat meals in the fridge instead of half a Napa cabbage you have to use before it goes bad, give these tweaks a try.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: For those saying the ratio of noods to veg is too noodle heavy, I always at least double the amount of veg (and adjust the sauce amount) in any recipe that combines a carb with a veg

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody:
I have heard that a mix of balsamic and rice vinegars is a good sub for black vinegar, I think I read that in a Milk Street recipe.  

But black vinegar is a great thing to have in your pantry if you can find it and like cooking asian food.  It has a very interesting flavor which will immediately remind you of Chinese take out.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: I have saved this to my recipe box.  Will try to develop a version containing a reasonable amount of sodium.  I started tracking my sodium intake and it is way above healthy levels.  This recipe has so much sodium in it, maybe I would just go ultra-low sodium on the next day or two to even things out.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: I made this with Mark Bittman’s Chinese BBQ pork instead of chicken. Otherwise followed the recipe, except for subbing out snap peas for snow and left out the bean sprouts as I couldn’t find them in dead of winter. Was rather delicious IMHO

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Overall this is a straightforward lo mein recipe that was not hard to make and satisfying.  If making again I'd seek out ways to reduce the sodium, perhaps add in a bit of hoisin, and re-balance the noodles vs. vegetables.  I added extra veg and some yellow pepper as it was, but this is still quite noodle-heavy.  Would be good with bok choy, sliced mushroom, water chestnuts would add a nice crunch.  A squirt of sriracha and a squeeze of lime to brighten it up are also great options.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Used thin-sliced lean pork loin to make pork lo mein instead. Very good. At my local Asian market I had a hard time finding any noodles marked "lo mein," but found  and used Shandong noodles. Very good!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: used 1/2 tsp of basalmic and 1/2 tsp of rice vinegar to sub for the black vinegar.  Also Mirin for the Shaoxing wine, and left out the sugar.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Loved it! Made this exactly according to the recipe. I wish people would like a recipe as read instead of all this advice and changes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: @Nancy I agree with you! I always make a recipe the first time as written. Then, I’ll make modifications as necessary to accomodate my fam or my frig. It’s not the recipe, folks, if you make all these substitutions! Advice on where to source ingredients, and techniques is appreciated, but not all the subs!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: I made this a few weeks ago with substitutes for some of the sauce ingredients: sherry for the wine; regular soy sauce for the dark and light; rice vinegar for the black vinegar. We liked it so much I ordered up the correct ingredients which we are using this evening. I’ll see if we notice the difference. However you make it, this was a quick and yummy Lo mein.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Sake is a good substitute for Shaoxing wine. It can also be used to "velvet" the chicken. 2-3TBL of sake, soy sauce, corn starch. You can add a pinch of baking soda & sugar. Mix & marinate for an hour or two.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Good - and I even went out and bought dark soy sauce and black vinegar - two items I don’t usually have on hand - but not ‘wow’. I may come back to this recipe but I’m still in search of a more perfect flavoured sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: @Natalie B I bought black vinegar and dark soy as well. I was surprised by how quickly I used them! I don’t cook Chinese food often but when I do, I guess I was always subbing those ingredients

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: This was good. Next time I will make 1.5x the sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: If you cannot find Chinese black vinegar or Shaoxing wine at your grocery store (and if there isn’t an Asian market near you), both are easily sourced online (and pretty inexpensive). This made a ton, and the noodles sucked up all the sauce overnight in the fridge, so I made another batch of the sauce and added it to the leftovers. Gotta say… this beat the pants off any lo mein I’ve ever gotten for takeout. Will ne making this on the regular.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Finally bought the Shaoxing wine. Made a huge difference. Everyone loved the flavor and had seconds!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: I used fresh egg noodles from H Mart, substituted 1 T balsamic and 1 T Worchester sauce for black vinegar, skipped oyster sauce (didn't have) used low sodium soy sauce, sherry for Shaoxing wine. For veggies I used what was in my fridge, zucchini, carrots, onions, celery and broccoli. Used boneless chicken breast. Everyone loved it. Will make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody:
@MJ
In other words, you used the recipe as a jumping off place to make an entirely different dish! Interesting approach. Do you plan to try the actual recipe at some point ?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: I live in a community with NO good Asian food, so I attempt to make it from time to time. I would use Asian peanut oil for this instead of canola, is that OK? I ask this sincerely, as there are so many good comments on this dish and I’d like to know.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Peanut oil has a very high smoke point (higher than Canola, in fact), so it is good for frying and high-temperature cooking in general. The main difference you'll find is the mild nutty flavour, but there is so much flavour going on in the sauce I doubt you'll even notice. (Possibly not a great swap for baking, mind.) Enjoy!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Can't give this recipe an honest and precise review because there were some necessary adjustments as it's the middle of winter in Northern California (actual north- Humboldt County), so didn't have snow peas or mung bean sprouts. Used lots of extra carrots, some lacinato kale, napa cabbage, rice vinegar for the black vinegar, sake for the Shaoxing wine, and some Trader Joe's Thai wheat noodles I picked up last time I was in the TJ's vicinity.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Can someone please comment with a the brand name of real lo mein noodles and where I can order online?? Even the Chinese restaurants in my town use spaghetti... :-(

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: @Craig S. https://a.co/d/2mrbsTu

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: For anyone still looking for noodles - I use Twin Marquis (which are made in New York and can be purchased at my local Asian Market in NJ, and at Wegmans). They are in the frozen section of both stores. So they are 'fresh' but frozen. You can search on Instacart for Twin Marquis to see if any stores near you carry them. This brand has about a dozen or more different noodle varieties at our Asian Market (udon, pan fried, lo mein, thick, thin) - I buy a variety and keep them in my freezer.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Added Teriyaki with plant based faux chicken breasts - came out well for a veg option... my son loved it.   I used malt vinegar as a sub - I think it also came out well.   Very tasty!

commentBody: I like everything about this recipe and will make it again, but with less noodles—8 ounces rather than 12. The flaoare great, but for me, the noodles overwhelmed the protein and veggies. Otherwise delicious.

recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: I modified for what I had in stock and it still turned out great. Subbed celery for the cabbage, 1 T rice vinegar to replace both the black vinegar & wine, and used rice vermicelli.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody:
Great recipe. Definitely velvet the chicken with corn starch and the wine/soy sauce as another commenter suggested. 

I used a bag of cabbage/carrot slaw in lieu of cutting my own. Will use two bags next time. 

I also suggest finishing the chicane in a small portion of the prepared sauce and serving  on top  as it can be a difficult to get the chicken to stir through the noodles and veggies. 

Mostly writing this here so I remember foe next time!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: This turned out really well. I subbed rice wine vinegar and dry sherry for the wine and vinegar in the recipe. I used green beans and carrots...I'd say any vegetables would work. Definitely a keeper.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Chopped baby bok Choy is a good addition or substitute for some or all of the cabbage. Add later for more crunch. Can add with bean sprouts. Haven’t tried this but something like arrow root or gelatin to thicken it might be good. Also added one whole Serrano with seeds for spice with bean sprouts.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Easy.  I added broccoli and mushrooms ... used more noodles and doubled the sauce.  I followed what others have suggested and used sherry wine vinegar and didn't use the Chinese black vinegar or the shoaxing wine.  enjoyed buy all and lots of leftovers.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: Substitute ponzu sauce for black vinegar. Was great!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: One night with only a few missing ingredients: no black vinegar, dark soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, cabbage lo mein noodles nor oyster sauce. Did have thick spaghetti, cole slaw mix, fish sauce, sherry, balsam vinegar, doubled the soy sauce.  Put noodles in refrigerator per comments. Turned out pretty good for this non-Asian couple, though I'm guessing we'd enjoy the real thing more.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: This is my go to recipe for a great weeknight Chinese take out make at home meal. My family loves it.

author: Kay Chun

commentBody: Recent heart attack in family necessitates revamping of our diet. Is there such thing as a palatable low/no sodium version of this dish?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody: You aren't going to get the same punch of flavor but using nothing but the reduced sodium soy is the way to lighten up the sodium in this recipe. Unfortunately removing the soy all together would change the flavor profile so the sodium can only be reduced. I personally would add a pinch or two of MSG too to up the umami while keeping the sodium low as MSG has about a third of the sodium content as salt and 5% less than soy sauce. You can purchase it on Amazon and a little goes a long way.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
commentBody:
We subbed 1:1 Worchester for the Chinese Black Vinegar because even my local Asian grocery didn't have it. It threw the taste off a *bit* so then we added 3 tbs of water and a bit more sugar and that rescued it. Other than that, velveted the chicken for 30 minutes (velveting makes a HUGE difference) and subbed broccoli for snow peas. Parcooked both broccoli and then chicken for 1 minute each in boiling water before the stir fry.

Absolutely incredible. My friend and I ate all of it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I just made this tonight for my wife and I. The recipe was followed exactly as written. The prep and cooking was painless. The taste of the dish, we are still talking about. This is an easy to follow recipe with high reward when served. Give it a try. And, we used ground turkey. My wife Jan’s only comment was next time I should increase the size of sweet potato cubes. We will make this again. I’m already thinking about how I might substitute fresh shrimp for the ground turkey.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
@Lynsey 
I made this with pressed and crumbled extra firm tofu. Other than the swap out for chicken, I made it exactly as written. It is very good but I think the coconut milk gravy probably relies on the schmaltzy flavor of the chicken fat, especially because the author calls for thigh meat. I ended up adding a tiny amount of white miso paste to deepen it a bit.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
@Lynsey
Freeze, thaw, squeeze out the water, then grate the tofu on the large holes of a box grater. The end product amazingly resembles ground meat.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: @Lynsey Have you ever cooked with tempeh? It’s a plant-based protein that would probably be a perfect substitute. It crumbles like ground meat and nicely takes on the flavors of the dish. And it’s widely available in most supermarkets.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
Glad I read the comments here! I made some slight changes using what I have available in rural
Oregon.  Made it a solid 8.5/10 for my family. Starting with adding pre diced and frozen butternut squash and sweet potatoes to the pan with avocado oil AND butter. Swapped the ground stuff (impossible to find here) with cubed chicken thighs for the fats and seasoning with red pepper flakes, garlic, onion powder, smoked sweet Spanish paprika, and lots of fresh pepper. Can’t find spinach this time of year here so skipped that and ended up using the whole can of coconut milk. Added the potato mix after I’d stirred the chicken and milk together so they didn’t get too mushy. Used lime juice out of a bottle spritzed on top. Delish! Will add to the rotation for “non-white people” foods. Served with minute rice cause I’m a tired mom and salad.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
Quick weeknight stir fry. Is it the most delicious, life changing dish? No. But it’s quick and the flavors can be easily punched up if you like more spice/seasonings. I made the following changes and the left overs are still good. If you like sriracha and/or hot chili oil they’re def a necessity here and complement the dish well. 

I used Ground pork. Although I wouldn’t use chicken, I’d use turkey if i wanted it lighter. And I would  season the ground turkey to taste with the recipes spices or some blend of sympathetic flavors.  For the pork I fried it with just some salt and pepper.  I did drain some of the pork fat. Left a thin coating in the pan for some flavor. 

I added some chopped water chestnuts. Adds a nice texture to the dish. Fried them up alongside the scallions, garlic & ginger. 

Served with quinoa rather than rice. Seasoned the quinoa with some Goya sazon for color and flavor. 

Used the whole 15ox can of coconut milk. Upped my scallion, garlic, and ginger to taste to account for that. 

I do understand the bland comments. But if you like adding your own flair, following the recipe is a good “base” to then flavor up to your preferences.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
When I tasted this before serving I found it a bit bland, so I followed the suggestion of other commenters and added fish sauce. That—and the lime juice added at the end—helped immensely. 
Rather than using the whole can of coconut milk or freezing the rest, I used the remaining ~2/3 cup to make Tejal Rao’s coconut rice to serve this over. A winning combination!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: It took ~forever~ to dice the sweet potato, but it was worth it (and at least it did cook up quickly). I was worried the recipe might be too gently flavored, but I liked it as-is, I didn't even add any hot sauce. I'm a cilantro-tastes-like-soap person, but I chanced it and didn't omit it, and it didn't stand out in the finished meal. This is a new favorite, it'll be going into the rotation (and maybe I'll get better at swiftly dicing).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Peel the potatoes. Slice into rounds. Slice each round into strips, rotate the round and slice strips into cubes. Goes pretty fast.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: @Susan Can even skip the peeling part for certain sweet potatoes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I liked it. I made it exactly as directed. I didn’t have any issues getting the sweet potatoes to cook in the 8 minute window. I didn’t bother finding out if the ground chicken I used was dark meat or not. I find all that stuff is pretty interchangeable and don’t stress about it. I liked the addition of veg, a great way to get some in. I probably would have used a bit more coconut milk and spinach. I also will amp up the garlic, ginger and lime juice next time. And I agree with others that hot sauce is a must. But all in, I would make again. It has protein and veg and some starch. Very doable for a weeknight meal.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I actually roasted the cubed sweet potato and threw it in after I cooked the ground meat. I only have a small pan so it made it a bit easier and I liked the flavored of the roasted sweet potato.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Somehow, this recipe defied all laws of nature by being delicious while also needing "something more." The hot sauce is definitely the coup de grace that makes this dish slay (ha!), but I think it needs additional depth during the cooking process. I salted beyond my comfort level and added ample pepper, but next time, I'll up the onions, ginge, garlic and lime and  add some crushed red pepper or diced fresh pepper. Moreover, I'm gonna be roasting sweet potatoes like this a lot from now on!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: We made this with ground chicken, however I have never found dark meat ground chicken or turkey for those asking about turkey.  This has a great scent as eluded to in the title, but we found the flavor a bit bland even with hot sauce.  Would be better with dark meat as suggested or even just using chicken thighs. We won’t make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Although the prep makes this dish take at least twice the promised 20 min, it was worth it. I doubled recipe, used 1 lb of ground chicken (white) and 1 lb of pork. Two full heads of garlic, two inches of ginger. I followed others’ suggestions and after dicing the sweet potatoes I added salt, pepper and a little oil and roasted them in a 425 oven for about 30 minutes. That meant they were perfect to add in with the coconut milk. Also, that’s a lot of ginger and garlic- I started by peeling and finely chopping ginger, peeling and smashing garlic cloves, but then put both in a small prep bowl of food processor and that made them the perfect melt-away consistency (and saved time!). I used twice everything else- two potatoes, 1/2 cup scallions, and a whole can of coconut milk. We happily fed four people with rice and or mashed cauliflower and had leftovers for lunches. My 14 year old has asked that it go into regular rotation at our house!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: With thanks for all previous tips, I doubled the garlic and ginger, added just a touch of fish sauce and a generous teaspoon of chilli crisp, and also amped up the coriander and lime. I think it would have been on the bland side if I had not taken those measures, as the flavor was still not overwhelming. I also found that the whole can of coconut milk was necessary. It was pretty good and I might make it again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Added 6 cloves, 1.5 TBSP ginger, more salt and pepper, along with a seeded serrano pepper, kids and husband loved it. Easy recipe even for cooking in an RV while camping.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Really tasty. I upped the heat by adding in a couple Serranos in with the garlic and ginger, and some white pepper, coriander and cayenne as well. Obviously this takes closer to 40-45 minutes when you factor in all the time rice, chopping, etc. But it's tasty and filling and will make for a delicious round of leftovers too.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
This was simple to make and tasty. We'll make it again. Did the naysayers even try it?
Used ground turkey and cut the sweet potato into 1/2 inch cubes. The mild sweetness of the potato and the coconut milk is nicely balanced with the lime juice. Didn't add salt and pepper the third time as called for. It didn't need it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Whole family gave thumbs up, no way 20 min but certainly easy enough.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
A couple things I missed as I read & cooked:

Remove sweet potato to plate after sauteeing
Only use 1 cup of coconut milk, not full can.

Hard to critique without following the recipe but i agree, the flavors fell more flat than I was hoping.  I will have to try again with the above steps.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
this sounded like a great vegetarian dish. 
Until I got to the "Chicken" part.
Why-oh-Why can the Times food writers not include just a single sentence (for dishes that are close to V) "For a Meat-Free version, use X in place of Chicken"
I'll figure it out, but you could help.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Really enjoyed it; added more spinach, scallions, ginger and garlic and a heavy dose of pepper. Would add more spice and sweet potatoes next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Anything that needs ingredients to be peeled and diced, thinly sliced, minced and coarsely chopped will not “come together quickly.”

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
A keeper! Made this tonight, easy! It was pretty good when finished but I wanted more flavor so I stirred in a little curry paste and added a bit of mint and basil I had leftover in my fridge. 
I ate it without any rice, sort of a Thai inspired chili bowl - YUM. 
I want to make it again with crumbled tofu in place of the chicken, and a spoonful of chili crunch, I think it would be equally good. Versatile recipe that's easy, satisfying and healthy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: One of those recipes that is just a bunch of ingredients. Tastes like nothing!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Wow, made this last night and it was really good. Read reviews and punched it up with more garlic, ginger, and a couple of tablespoons of miso. Also used ground turkey (because I had some in the fridge). Swapped kale for spinach. Browned the turkey first, then the aromatics, then cubed sweet potatoes, and coconut milk (used the full can). Covered it and let it cook until sweet potatoes were tender, about 40 minutes. Added the kale, cilantro, and lime juice. Served it over white rice. Excellent.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: It might taste good, but it doesn't look very appetizing (to me, at least).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I made it with pressed tofu. Excellent!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: This is a great basic recipe. I amped up the flavor by adding a teaspoon more of both the ginger and garlic (I keep cubes in the freezer) and added Penzey's Bangkok Blend (a little more than a teaspoon). I finely chopped the sweet potato in the food processor as well as the arugula (no stringy pieces!) and cooked the sweet potato with the chicken. I used the entire can of coconut milk and added a few dashes of fish sauce, and some lime zest when I added the lime juice. Served with brown rice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Made per recipe, but it was terminally bland. The recipe has promise, though, so next time I'll dice a pound of boneless, skinless thighs. I'll also double the aromatics and add a teaspoon of turmeric and a pinch of crushed red peppers or cayenne.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: This is a delicious dish. For me, however, the prep time was more than 20 minutes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: 2x made, 2x a hit! I think next time, instead of doing the lime and cilantro in the dish, I’m gonna make cilantro lime rice to serve it over. Maybe lime zest in the dish. This one gets big compliments from family and friends.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is very similar to another NYT recipe for Thai-inspired chicken meatballs, only it’s easier to put together and has sweet potato. The one tweak I made was to add a teaspoon of fish sauce like the meatball recipe- you won’t be disappointed! Will definitely make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have a sweet potato, but I did have butternut squash, which was a very good substitute.  Easy to make, and everyone liked it--will do again

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Next time I’ll pump up the garlic and ginger. I liked it, but I wanted stronger flavors. It was easy and tasty though.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: As others have said, the recipe is a great starting point, but I think would be quite bland if made as is.  Per other suggestions, I doubled the ginger, garlic, and scallions.  I added about a teaspoon of curry powder to the sweet potatoes and also about a teaspoon to the chicken.  I added a half teaspoon of fish sauce.   For a touch of heat, I added a good squirt of Ninja Squirrel coconut sriracha sauce.  With all of that, we loved it.  Next time, I would also add more lime juice, and maybe something that would give it a bit of crunch, as the overall texture is very soft.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Can easily substitute tofu for chicken. Just as delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: No to canola or safflower oil.  Use coconut oil or avocado oil.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Care to elaborate?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Add a splash of fish sauce

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I did this as well and also added a splash of black vinegar.  I used coconut aminos as a flavor enhancer at the table.  It was very good.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Can this be made with tofu instead of with chicken?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: There was another recent recipe for Sheet Pan Coconut Shrimp and Sweet Potatoes that used very similar ingredients but roasted in the oven instead of using a skillet.  Has anyone tried both?  Which one is better? I am curious how the sauteed method of this recipe might work swapping shrimp in place of the chicken.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I had some lemongrass on hand and added that which was delicious! This was a quick and easy recipe, I would definitely recommend.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Excellent recipe! Added a bit more sweet potato as others suggested and used the whole can of coconut milk because I didn't want to freeze the rest (the recipe calls for one cup).  Turned out great!  Winner of a recipe and easy to make.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: So delicious, I didn't find it bland at all. I did add about a tablespoon of red pepper flakes when cooking the garlic etc. I used ground turkey and sub'd in frozen chopped kale for the spinach, and frozen cubed sweet potato because chopping is not it sometimes. This will be a repeat for me.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: So simple and tasty. Would be good with ground pork, tofu, shrimp, mushroom. Will add to my quick weeknight dinners list!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: This is such an easy and healthy meal for weeknights; it lacks some punch. Especially it being labeled "curry" but with zero curry paste or spice. Will try again but will add that green curry paste

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Made it exactly as written but with ground turkey instead of chicken and it was absolutely delicious. It did probably take closer to an hour from beginning to end to make with all the prep and steps.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: This wasn’t terrible but just a bit bland. I would not make it again. Definitely add more sweet potatoes, garlic, ginger and lime to improve the flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Minus one star because it looks like a fresh pet commercial. But! It’s DELICIOUS, so 4 stars. I subbed kale for the spinach, both because I like it better and because I had some on hand. Added kale after the chicken was cooked through, softened for a couple min, then added the coconut milk. Great healthy dinner option, just wish it looked as yummy as it tastes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I added 1/4 tsp of cayenne flakes and sauteed the spinach separately. I served the chicken on the spinach, which kept the spinach from getting stringy. Seemed to be good changes to the recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: This is wonderful. I accidentally bought butternut squash instead of sweet potato and it was still great. I've tried a lot of the five star recipes on this site recently and this is one of the best. Can't recommend it highly enough.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: The recipe could be improved with some curry type ingredients, cumin, coriander, cardamom, tumeric, ground cloves, and chili crisp.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Definitely did not come together for me.  Sweet potato were so mushy and I even baked them in oven before to speed up process.  No flavor and tasted like baby food.  1st fail on NYT recipes and I have made ALOT of them.  But at least it was healthy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious in my opinion; kids didn't like at all so this won't be repeated in our family dinner rotation. I think this will be great as a meal prep option (make on Sunday, have for lunch during the week). Extremely adaptable. I used what I had: 2 sweet potatoes instead of 1, lemon instead of lime, kale instead of spinach. I used the full can of coconut milk and just increased time to reduce. I would probably add red pepper flakes next time but this was a huge win in my book.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Easy to make and popular with the whole family! I’m eating the leftovers right now cold  for breakfast.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Could I use chopped leftover dark turkey meat instead of ground chicken?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: @Deb L, I freeze meals with all of these ingredients frequently. The only thing I see degrading a bit is the sweet potatoes may end up a little bit mushy in the leftovers. But I'm imaging this will still taste good.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Love!!! Eating it right now. Doesn’t need rice either.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
Made half the recipe for 2 people and used ground pork over jasmine rice. 
I thought it was pretty tasty and very easy to put together.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: This was great - didn’t have goulash do served with broccoli on the side. I felt it needed something? Maybe bit of red chile minced or dab of red chili paste , or minced jalapeño? . Or add some Aleppo pepper as desired.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Added extra ginger, lime juice and some chili pepper flakes because I used whole can of coconut cream for extra sauce.  A hit with entire family - glad to have a new dish for the rotation.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed except with the following changes: ground turkey breast, light coconut milk, and brown rice. Delicious! A new addition to our regular rotation.  Loved the 1/4 inch cubed sweet potato.  Never had them this way and they were delightful. Five stars!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight, it was really a quick and easy weeknight meal. I found it a little bland as is so I made the choice to add a ton more lime juice and a little more coconut milk to make it feel more like Thai inspired. I’d recommend it!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
Yum! Made it tonight, almost - for once - sticking to the recipe!

I used ground pork, and subbed in the dark parts of some romaine leaves, roughly chopped, for the spinach. I also used fish sauce instead of plain salt, probably about two tsp in all, and threw in part of an orange bell pepper that was kicking about in the fridge, chopping it about the same size as the sweet potato. 

My partner has far less heat tolerance than I, so I just stirred in a good glug of Chinese chili oil into my portion, which gave it some nice warmth. Like others, I might try upping the ginger/garlic/scallion mix a bit, and a bit more lime juice, but it was lovely just as made!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: One note about the chicken: you could easily make this dish vegan by using chickpeas instead of meat. I eat meat, but I’m moderate about it. I eat a lot of all-plant meals and chickpeas are one of my favorite plant proteins. The mild flavor never outshines the flavor notes of whatever dish I use them in. I typically used canned chickpeas with no salt added and just rinse them and dump them in towards the end of the cooking cycle. So good!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: We loved this dish. I added 3 chopped anchovies (could use fish sauce instead) and a tbsp of tomato paste to the garlic/ginger/scallion sauté for a layer of umami. Followed the recipe as written other than that and it was excellent—bursting with flavor. Even my mother, who dislikes coconut, thought it was delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent! Hubby asked me to make it again. We ended up using the whole can of coconut milk (why only 1 cup?) and 2 limes. Will make again! Even my toddler liked it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious, I only made minor modifications, doubled the sweet potatoes and used the whole can of coconut milk and added extra spinach, scallions, and cilantro.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Delish as is!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I added part of a serrano pepper to the ginger/scallion/garlic mix. Gave it tons of flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I used ground turkey and chopped onion instead of scallions. It was really tasty and different.

author: Kay Chun
