recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Hot sauce definitely zips it up to the next level. It’s like an amazing curry but without curry, if that makes sense!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I don't know what this dish wants to be.  I made it with all the recommendations to amp up the flavor and roasted the sweet potatoes in the oven and it was good, but I wouldn't make it again because it's too similar to other dishes that I've made that were better...and less work.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: This was so disappointing.  So little flavor. The prep was reasonable but wound not be for the not so skilled . The lack of flavor plus the prep of sweet potatoes in 1/4 dice?!?!?!  Really ? So this is a complete no for me.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I have now made this twice. It is much better with diced chicken than with ground turkey or chicken.  The spices need to be increased a bit. More garlic and ginger.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: This is delish! My only adjustment was adding 1 tsp. Turmeric for a bit more depth. I also simmered it down for about 10 minutes until almost all of the the liquid reduced. Cast iron skillet worked perfectly. Adding this one to my rotation!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I made this before and didn't follow the recipe not realizing the potatoes were cooked first but it came out fantastic.  Second time I had white sweet potatoes and followed it exact.  What a difference in timing.  The white sweet potatoes were no way done in 8 minutes and not even close even after when you add the coconut milk.  The 40 min cook and prep time is now far out of an hour now and still not done.  Only now have I realized what a difference between red and white sweet potatoes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: It's a winner. Doubled most of the seasonings and used the full coconut milk can. Freezes and reheats really well, even with the spinach, imo. A great healthy meal to add to the rotation.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: A touch of coconut aminos sends this up a notch. Made it with turkey.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
this was absolutely delicious. I made a few small tweaks to meet my personal preference. used 1/2 cup coconut milk and 1/2 cup chicken broth with a little coconut extract. also just used the tablespoon of oil for sweet potato and gave the pan a liberal amount of avocado oil cooking spray for the aromatics. I had ground chicken break, so I used that and added since chicken powder to boost the flavor a bit. we skipped the rice. 
a quick and easy dish that I'll be making on repeat. 
my husband decided to make wraps with the leftovers, and it was genius - worked great, and we loved it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I don’t understand the high rating. My husband and I thought it was just OK, but quite bland. The hot sauce can spice it up some, but still felt like it lacked flavor on its own.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I made this with grated tofu. Next time I may make it with shredded chicken breast or pre-baked, grated tofu to remove some of the moisture. I'll also add more ginger, lime juice, and garlic. Maybe some crushed red pepper too.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: good base recipe. Came together easily. I Adtercreading comments, I doubled ginger and added a Serrano pepper. I used kale instead of spinach. Next time, I'd add snow peas or snap peas or something crunchy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Making this recipe a second time, I doubled the amount of garlic and ginger, added a julienned zucchini and a tsp of fish sauce.  Much better!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I added some small-diced yellow pepper in step two. After browning the chicken, I added a can of drained chickpeas. Based on the comments, I also decided to add a splash of fish sauce. My husband and I really enjoyed this dish. Tween daughter would have preferred if I'd left out the spinach. It was a good way to use up a sweet potato and some other ingredients I had on hand.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: This dish looks unappealing--a kind of dull gray. My partner asked if it was _____ food. You can add an institution you'd never want to eat in.  I used high quality ground chicken and increased the spinach, but the recipe needed more sweet potato chunks to add flavor/color. It also needs a big boost in spices and ginger. My partner snatched the hot sauce from the fridge. One great tip in this recipe--how to quickly cook sweet potatoes in a skillet with neutral oil.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Love this recipe. Super easy, and versitle. Used a full can of coconut milk, extra spinach (5oz), more lime juice plus rice vinegar at the end. The whole fam raves about it. Hubby even asked for it again tonight, after giving last night's leftvers away.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this twice now — first as written and found it good but a bit bland. The second time, I went for ground pork instead (grocery store was strangely out of chicken but a happy accident), added a good bit more ginger and garlic, added about 2 tablespoons of fish sauce, added some red pepper flakes and it went from about a 7 to a 9 out of 10!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Made this with a few modifications. No chicken so used ground turkey. Doubled the sweet potatoes and heavy on the ginger. Great taste. Kind of a Thai taste; loved it. A keeper.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I think if you wanted to make this vegetarian chickpeas or mushrooms would be a better sub than tofu.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: A keeper recipe that once the chopping was done came together very quickly.  I took the recommendation of bigger sweet potato chunks and additional garlic and ginger.  Lots of spinach.  It was a very pretty dish and a nice weeknight meal. (I added crushed red pepper to the sauce for some zing.)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: simple and so delicious

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: A lot of flavor and punch for minimal effort and time. Very good easy weeknight meal. I wouldn't say it's in the cannon of best NYT recipes of all time. It's pretty good. It didn't keep me thinking about it for days to come. But a solid, easy to execute, flavoful one pan meal. That's about it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe exactly as written. The flavor was fantastic and it was super easy to make. Highly recommend. I will definitely make this again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: added about a TBSP of fish sauce and honey because it needed something else! Delicious. Did with turkey too which was great!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: This may be one of the best recipes I've made from NYT. I used sea salt from Normandy from a recent trip, who knows how much this influenced the final flavor. The only comments I would add are: drop the spinach in before removing from the heat. It's enough that it needs to cook down a bit. Also, with all the chopping required, this is NOT a 45 minute prep. All in all, it took me 2 hours from start to finish. But I started early, so no harm.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
This came together super quick. Loved it! Followed the recipe, didn’t have fresh cilantro used freeze dried. And added 1/4 t ground cayenne.

I am blown away with the taste of this one dish meal for such a simple recipe. 

I could experiment with different protein - white beans, tofu, or maybe even chopped chicken thighs? 

A great weeknight meal.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
Really good. Didn't bother pulling the potatoes out of the pan before adding garlic/ginger/scallion/chicken and it was still delish (and less cleanup). Used a whole can of coconut milk instead of 1c and just reduced a smidge more.

Strongly recommend adding 1-2Tbs fish sauce and a thai bird chili (if you like heat)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
We liked it. But thought it was kind of bland. 

I used two Japanese yams cubed small. We like them better than sweet potatoes as they are less sweet. Like others, I also used a whole can of coconut milk and then I added another half  can of water as the extra potato needed more liquid. 

Also added some Thai fish sauce for some umami and next time would add some chili pepper flakes. It needed the lime juice (acid) and I served it with more limes to squeeze on each portion. 

I’d also top each portion with more cilantro with the scallions next time. It made a lot so enjoyed the comments about freezing it. 

And I served it with brown rice but not sure we really needed it with the extra potato.  I think I would skip it next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
I followed this exactly, except l doubled the ginger and garlic as per previous comments.  My husband described it as "pleasant".  I thought it was "Meh".  The recipe certainly reads as though it should be delicious, but it is bland.  I tasted while cooking and at the end added more cilantro, but still "Meh".
I served it with a wedge of lime, sliced scallions, cilantro, sliced peanuts, and hot sauce on the side   hoping individual adjustments would help  . . Not really.   I won't make it again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Just made this. Only had 1/2 cup of coconut milk - tasted fine. Needs more salt. I added a little fish sauce and would plan to add a tablespoon during cooking the next time. Also needs some heat. I plan to add a jalapeno with the  ginger, garlic and scallions.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Boy Kibble. Without the lime and hot sauce, a very bland clean-the-fridge random ingredient meal.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as written. Thought it lacked pizzazz. I will make it again and add more lime, ginger, garlic, and scallions. Also some green curry paste. Also use the whole can of coconut milk.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: This is a brilliant yet simple dish. After reading some of the comments, I used twice the ginger and added red pepper flakes. So good. We all had seconds!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Fish sauce and sugar are a nice addition. And crispy onions and kimchi to serve.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Could I use boneless skinless chicken thighs and just cut it up into little pieces?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: @Elise yes.  I just chopped up the thighs in a food processor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I'd definitely up the seasonings and the spinach.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I used the full can of coconut milk, a bit more lime juice, and it worked out perfectly! We are a saucy family so having more sauce was a good thing. Such a great recipe!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: I am that guy who didn't make the recipe but loved it anyway.  Based on flavor comments, and a desire to preserve texture, I browned the potato with salt, cumin and paprika and set it aside.  I cooked the turkey, then added the garlic, scallions and the whole can of coconut milk and let it reduce for a bit.  I wilted the spinach before removing from heat and adding the potato mixture.  We found it flavorful at the table but still added a little lime juice and a few drops of MAGGI Wurze there.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Excellent!  Quick, easy, tasty, adaptable.  Used ground turkey and chard.  Per comments, nearly doubled ginger, scallions, cilantro and used nearly a whole can of coconut milk.  All good!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight and it was easy and tasty.  The sweet potato cooked to golden in about 10 mins. I used low fat coconut milk and don't feel the dish was missing any flavor. I added the zest of a lime which punched things up and added some asparagus since it's in season. Will make this again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody:
Crispy buttery and wonderful. I made the dough into logs, refrigerated then sliced. The dough slices better after being back at room temperature for a few minutes.

CAUTION! Don’t pour the vanilla into the hot butter in a measuring cup—it boils over!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Those flavours alone didn't inspire me so added a spoonful of red curry paste!   It was really good...

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Be sure to cook the chicken/turkey at a higher heat to give it some crisp edges -- otherwise it can had an odd texture. And definitely follow the (many) recommendations to increase the garlic and ginger amounts.  An easy, weeknight dinner.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: Yery good, easy. I used 1.5 lbs ground turkey, 2 sweet potatoes, full 14.5 oz can of coconut milk.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Fragrant Coconut Chicken and Sweet Potato  Recipe
commentBody: This was incredibly easy to make and super delicious. When I added the sweet potatoes back in, I also added two cut carrots. It was great.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Dumplings With Zucchini, Tofu and Chives Recipe
commentBody: I’ve only got 103 pine nuts. Will this recipe still work?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Dumplings With Zucchini, Tofu and Chives Recipe
commentBody: I have to ask if anyone else is puzzled/amused by the ingredient list item “104 pine nuts?!?”

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Dumplings With Zucchini, Tofu and Chives Recipe
commentBody: very amused. But watching the video, she puts 2 pine nuts in each dumpling. And the recipe yield is 52 dumplings. So, that is where the 104 comes from!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Dumplings With Zucchini, Tofu and Chives Recipe
commentBody: @Hillary from Durham did you count?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Dumplings With Zucchini, Tofu and Chives Recipe
commentBody: @CeeVee12 or annoyed by the 14oz beef when every package is 16?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Dumplings With Zucchini, Tofu and Chives Recipe
commentBody: Made these tonight: amazing flavor!!!! I pan fried some per the recipes directions and deep fried the rest- both were great (albeit the deep fried was less healthy). I used Nasoya brand plant based wrappers- they were on the smaller side and a little fragile- wasn't able to get quite a tablespoon in so ended up with more filling left at the end than I had wrappers for. I pan fried the leftover filling and ate it with rice! Added a side of cucumber/soy sauce/sesame oil!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Dumplings With Zucchini, Tofu and Chives Recipe
commentBody: Wrappers are made out of wheat.  They're all plant based.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Dumplings With Zucchini, Tofu and Chives Recipe
commentBody: Made these last night and they were *so* good. My young kids loved them too. Got the wrappers, Korean chives, and some fun learning chopsticks for the kids at H Mart. Wrappers were in the freezer section and I just defrosted them on the counter. Served with a cucumber, avocado and green salad. Stir fried leftover filling and ate it straight from the pan after clean up. Highly recommend!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Dumplings With Zucchini, Tofu and Chives Recipe
commentBody: Made it this evening and we loved it! I had to omit the pinenuts because they were so overpriced at the grocery store, but I don’t think we missed much not having them. If you get all the chopping and prep done before it’s actually not that difficult. However, our first few potstickers didn’t look great. But once we got the hang of the process, they were beautiful. I like to brown mine on both sides before we steamed them so they’re a little extra crunchy!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Dumplings With Zucchini, Tofu and Chives Recipe
commentBody: I’m always in search of good dumpling wrappers so I have to ask which brand of wrappers were used to make the dumplings in the video? Thank you

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Dumplings With Zucchini, Tofu and Chives Recipe
commentBody: We made these for a dinner party. We prepped the filling and the sauce ahead of time and invited kids and adults to help fill and fold the wrappers. The adults plopped a spoonful of the filling on the wrapper and kids got to get their hands dirty, wetting the sides and folding. The grown-ups did a little quality control afterwards to make sure all the dumplings were properly sealed. It was a hit! The kids loved eating the dumplings they folded - the meal was rated “third best thing I’ve ever eaten” by a picky 4 year old. We have leftovers and I can’t wait to dig in tomorrow. PS It was worth the trip to find Korean chives.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Dumplings With Zucchini, Tofu and Chives Recipe
commentBody: You can simply combine Steps 2 and 3, by just lumping all the filing in one go and mix everything all at once. My partner and I did this out of laziness and it turned out just fine. Being Japanese I am a long-time dumpling maker and for this recipe I found tofu and zucchini very new as filler ingredient: both very good and certainly healthy additions !

author: Kay Chun

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Dumplings With Zucchini, Tofu and Chives Recipe
commentBody:
I used up a 50 pack of dumpling wrappers (bought in the freezer section of Hmart) and still had 1/2 of my filling remaining - which I froze. Also at Hmart - found Chinese chives - which as far as I could tell, were Korean chives. 

One of my suggestions as an inexpert dumpling maker is to make sure your fillings are crumbled/cut small. I was a little cavalier with the directions. Oh - a second set of hands were helpful, too.

Otherwise - very tasty! And can recommend.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Beef Dumplings With Zucchini, Tofu and Chives Recipe
commentBody: This was a Zen Sunday activity with a big payoff! I couldn't find Korean chives (didn't feel like making a trip to the asian market for one item) so I upped the green onion, ginger, and garlic. A lot of steps but well worth it. I used the fry/steam method to cook about half and froze the other half. I had enough filling for 50 dumplings but I might have stuffed them a little to much. Personally, I thought the pine nuts gave it a great boost. Delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Hobakjuk (Korean Squash Porridge) Recipe
commentBody:
I just made this with the addition of grated fresh ginger and minced garlic to boost the flavor of the squash/rice puree. Along with the walnut/seed mix, I topped each serving with sliced dates that had been sautéed in olive oil until slightly caramelized. 
Festive and complex flavors and textures!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Hobakjuk (Korean Squash Porridge) Recipe
commentBody: This soup focuses mainly on the taste of the squash. While the add-ons helped with the flavor, ultimately it was too sweet for me (and I didn’t use honey!). I added some smoked paprika about halfway through my bowl and that was better. Maybe lime or another acid would help? Overall, I don’t think I would make this again. (My bulk food store had neither glutinous nor sweet rice, so I ended up using sushi rice.)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Hobakjuk (Korean Squash Porridge) Recipe
commentBody: @T Jones yes lime juice will cut through the sugar and any other protruding flavours. In Thai curry you use chilli, salt through fish sauce, sugar and acid through lime juice because it balances the flavours. Without lime juice it’s spicy and flat. I use that philosophy with all my cooking now because it makes such a difference!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Hobakjuk (Korean Squash Porridge) Recipe
commentBody: @T Jones so many names for the same thing sigh. Sushi rice is glutinous sticky rice. So is British pudding rice. Different common names, same rice! The author should have called for “short grain rice” which is the proper technical term. I’m surprised given the supposed calibre, okay superiority of a paid cooking service, you do expect better!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Hobakjuk (Korean Squash Porridge) Recipe
commentBody: @So many names The “sweet rice” or “glutinous rice” that the recipe is referring to is not the same as sushi rice. Sushi rice is also sticky and sometimes called glutinous rice, but this “sweet rice” has a much higher starch content and is even stickier than sushi rice. It is typically used in Asian cuisines for specific purposes, as opposed to everyday meals, such as for Korean rice cakes and Japanese mochi.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Hobakjuk (Korean Squash Porridge) Recipe
commentBody: @ So Many Names - I don't know about British pudding rice, but sushi rice is NOT glutinous rice. Totally different short grain - glutinous or sweet rice is a naturally sticky rice and is used to make Japanese mochi, Chinese pastries & desserts, mango sticky rice. because it is sticky and chewy. Sushi rice is a short grain rice similar to Korean short grain, but is MADE sticky because of the sugar, vinegar, and salt that is added.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Hobakjuk (Korean Squash Porridge) Recipe
commentBody: I used the same amount of sweet rice flour (chapssal garu) instead of the rice and used a mixture of soy milk and water. needs a generous portion of salt and honey to come to life, but so tasty and comforting! I imagine it would work well with any kind of pumpkin/squash. I plan to experiment a bit next time with the addition of some heavy cream and herbs to give it a more western-pumpkin soup feel.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Hobakjuk (Korean Squash Porridge) Recipe
commentBody: I started with some garlic and yellow onion and added some soy sauce and crushed red pepper during the cooking. Finished with plenty of salt and toasted nuts and skipped the dates for a more savory dish. Delicious! Complex flavors and deeply comforting.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Hobakjuk (Korean Squash Porridge) Recipe
commentBody: One of my favorite comfort meals. My mom always adds red bean and sweet rice dumplings which are very easy to make if there is time. A simple dish, with different takes, and always warming.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Hobakjuk (Korean Squash Porridge) Recipe
commentBody: Excited to try this out. Kabocha is a wonderful squash.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Hobakjuk (Korean Squash Porridge) Recipe

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Hobakjuk (Korean Squash Porridge) Recipe
commentBody: Glutinous rice has a very different consistency than regular rice. My guess is using any substitute rice will not give you the real feeling of this dish.

author: Kay Chun

commentBody: I was not able to find Korean sweet potatoes, so I made it with sweet potatoes and it was delicious. For a little extra protein and creaminess, I melted the butter in a pan, added the green onions and cooking until fragrant, added 2 cans of great northern white beans and their liquid and cooked until liquid was cooked off, added 3 heaping tbsp of the miso paste, and then stirred to break it down, and then stuffed the potatoes with and topped with fresh sliced scallions.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Baked Korean Sweet Potatoes With Scallion-Miso Butter  Recipe
commentBody:
No need for foil OR butter. You can roast any potato over a pan. Line with a silicone baking mat to make cleanup easier. 

I'd suggest a homemade tahini sauce if you want to add fat or sauce. Better for you and even more delicious! (Tahini, water, lemon juice, garlic and salt in a blender, search for recipes.)

I also suggest avoiding baking parchment which contains PFAS (Perfluoroalkyl and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances aka Forever Chemicals.) Unless you can find an uncoated version of parchment.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: This looked like a good recipe to try.  Until I checked the nutrition info and found that each serving has 1170 calories.  I am a 78 year old woman and my daily calorie requirement is 1500 calories per day!  There is such a disconnect between the NY Times Well column and the cooking section.  The Well section advises eating lots of bright colored fruits and vegetables, healthy oil like olive oil, healthy fish such as salmon and whole grains.  I wish there were more 500 calorie meals.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Hi Suzanne, Even though this recipe claims it makes 4 servings, there are "about" 12 pieces of chicken. I almost never eat more than 1 piece of chicken at a meal and feel plenty full (I'm 67 and active). It's possible that the assumed size of the servings is the culprit.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: 1500 calories per day is below what is recommend for elderly women. If a physician is requiring this of you and you are following that, you will likely need to tweak many, if not all, recipes from any source.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: There are so, so many recipes for fish, whole grains, and healthy foods - hundreds and hundreds of them - on this site. Have you tried searching under the categories given at the top of each recipe page? Under "Recipes"one category is "Healthy." There are vegetarian and vegan options. If the calorie count seems high, as Julie B. suggests, why not just do portion control? Some recipes on this site are extravagant special-occasion dishes. Some are very simple and healthful. Can't we have both?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: The nutrition information appears to be for all 4 servings. If you divide the nutrition information by 4, the calories, protein, fat, etc. all make a lot more sense. That being the case, the total calories are less than 293 and 20 grams of protein per serving.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Actually the nutritional info is per serving.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Well, that's what is SAYS, but I find that unrealistic.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Why are we roasting these two items separately?  Marinate the chicken and the broccoli together!  Roast the chicken for 20 minutes, then pull the pan out and add the broccoli, cheese etc.  Cooking separately, even though you're tossing the broc in the chicken juices, doesn't max out the benefit from cooking them together.  Plus you'll get on the table faster for the satisfaction of those guests who are salivating at the wonderful smells in your kitchen.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Made with skin on thighs and broccoli florets that I already had on hand. A big hit! Super easy and delicious. Even the youngest family member approved.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Cooked exactly as written (except omitted capers). So incredibly easy and yummy - will be adding to my weeknight dinners! The roasted broccoli cooked in chicken drippings was absolutely divine.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: The nutritional info seems wildly off.  How can a couple of pieces of chicken, a scant amount of cheese and broccoli add up to over 1100 cals?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: For those on calorie-restricted diets, why not vary the recipe? Use less cheddar or just parmesan, and toss the broccoli with olive oil and roast it on a separate pan? If you prepare another vegetable or two to serve with this, you can have your cheesy broccoli and stick to your regimen. There are thousands of recipes on this site. I'm sure you can find plenty that meet your requirements.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe
commentBody:
Hi! Has anyone tried the recipe
with chicken breasts? If so, what adjustments did you make? Boneless breasts might not work, but maybe skin on, bone-in chicken breasts would work? i’ve got some picky eaters who would love any insight you can provide. Thanks in advance for your help

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: I agree. I am 71 and my husband is 77.  One pound of chicken is plenty for us.  I usually 1/2 the amount of chicken and keep the amount of veggies in the recipe.  If I have leftover veggies I have them for lunch the next day.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Georgia, the nutrition info says the nutritional information is per serving.  An easy way to cut the calories for this menu is to use skinless, boneless thighs instead of skin-on.  Also 2 tbs. of oil, is actually quite a lot, particularly if you're cooking dark meat; you can easily use 1 tbs.  Even better and more efficient, is if you use an olive oil sprayer, you use less oil, but get better coverage.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe

author: Kay Chun

author: Kay Chun

commentBody: I combined this with another recipe for chicken thighs -- with sliced fennel-- and added half a jalapeño pepper for zest.  followed the recipe otherwise -- it was really good. yum

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Sheet-Pan Chicken and Cheesy Broccoli Recipe
commentBody: Doubled the capers, added a bit of extra cheese and bacon lardons with the chicken. Excellent!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: When I cook something that calls for cabbage, but not much, I sometimes use brussels sprouts from the freezer.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody:
If you’re buying a whole head of cabbage, keep in mind you’re gonna have a whole lotta leftover cabbage since this recipe doesn’t use much.

Baci,
Max

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody:
Add 1 tsp Ginger paste to Meatball mix and double the chopped dill
Sub 1 Tbls Miso Paste for Kombu
Add Parm rind to the soup while simmering

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: It did remind me of matzo ball soup! Very comforting. I used regular breadcrumbs and a flax egg due to allergies and the meatballs came out well. I also use pre-shredded cabbage and it still maintained some texture.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: This was good, not great. But could be… was missing something. Perhaps more garlic is needed? A touch more salt? Also used whole wheat orzo. Next time I won’t. Didn’t pair well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: EZ, and yes, comforting.  In part because I limit sodium intake a lot, I used more herbs.  And family enjoyed it.  Certainly 4-5x fresh dil, and generous za’atar to start.  Then to your taste, of course. I’m going to try making VEGAN by using finely chopped tempeh and egg substitute.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody:
Doubled broth
Used a big piece of kombu 
Added better than boullion
Used pearled couscous instead of orzo
Used ritz crackers and milk for the meatballs instead of saltines and water
Doubled the dill
Used half a small head of cabbage

No other notes. This is amazing.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: We really enjoyed this soup.  I used the whole head of a Napa cabbage and it wasn’t too much.  The texture of the braised meatballs is excellent.  I added a little white wine vinegar for an acid note.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: Not a fan of seaweed. What would you recommend to replace it?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: I'm obsessed with the poached meatballs, very satisfying texture and wonderful flavor. I want to try adding dried limes and some lemon juice to the broth to add a nice tart kick and some complexity to the broth.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: I used more herbs, squeezed fresh lemon juice to brighten the broth, and cooked the orzo separately.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: I added a squeeze of lemon at the end to brighten it up .  I also used dried dill of questionable age, but it still came out really well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Chicken soup is a favorite, but it can be less than “wow.” The meatballs are so much more flavorful and toothsome than cubed and boiled chicken breast cubes (usually overcooked). I can imagine a hundred variations on the seasoning (cooked dill—maybe not my first choice). Will definitely return to this recipe again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody:
No Kombu, subbing Tamari 2 Tbsp and mushroom powder 2 Tbsp and/or to taste later. Added lemon juice to broth. Using ground white turkey meat as that’s what I have. Add in 1 tsp tahini paste for a little fat in ground turkey. (Harder to find ground turkey with dark meat in my market). Ran out of saltines, subbed panko for the rest of the crackers.
Should have refrigerated meatballs for a while. Very sticky and falling apart in pan. Baked second batch in oven at 400 F. Smells great. I’ll see how it tastes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: I added some finely chopped ginger to the broth and used unsalted matzah instead of saltines.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: Very versatile, and very good. I had leeks and kale in the garden, so those replaced the onions and cabbage. Diced potatoes rather than a third pasta this week. Had bread crumbs so that replaced the saltines. Dayum—those meatballs were terrific, the dill essential, and the soup a smashing success all around.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: Delicious with few changes.  Added more broth, used lots of leeks with onion and celery, had no dill so used mint in chicken mix and tasted before adding to soup by cooking small amount chicken and found it needed more seasoning so added a little salt and pepper. Skipped cabbage since I did not have any. Turned out great

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody:
Absolutely delicious. 
The meatballs are a too salty for my taste. Could do with less or no salt. 
I read in one of the comments that you can substitute saltines with bread crumbs. 
I tried and it works beautifully so thank you guys for the helpful comments :)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: We loved this recipe! We used brown rice instead of orzo since we had it. The soup could benefit from a little more salt.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: Same with orzo... if you buy a whole box, you'll have a whole lotta orzo left over, because the recipe doesn't use much. Both orzo and cabbage are silly observations: the remainders can be used for other purposes if you plan ahead.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful  meat for balls, saving some poached breasts for later. Meatballs light & wholesome with a Matzo ball like texture. Only change with cooked chicken is more egg or some cream to better moisturize the crumbs.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: Not sure why this recipe doesn't include the nutritional info (like most other NYT recipes I've cooked do).  Due to some health conditions in our family, that is really helpful so please add!  Nevertheless we liked this one.  Used Whole Foods golden round crackers instead of saltines - all perfect.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. Of course I didn’t make the recipe.!  I almost did everything the same, except I pan fried the meatballs, slightly, added some red pepper and didn’t use that weird ingredient that I don’t know what it is. Also added kale at the end, but I let the meatballs sit in the broth for a bunch of hours and they got so fluffy and soft like matzo balls. It was really delicious

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: I've made this twice now. Added a few more carrots the second time and tripled the recipe to feed a large group. Only added dill to the meatballs. (Dill is not my favorite and didn't want to overdo it.) Delicious and comforting.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: I'd love to hear from other cooks who used the sardines. Did they give a strong flavor to the meatballs?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: @EByr SALTINES, not sardines.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Chicken Meatball Soup With Orzo and Dill  Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was delicious, I did double on dill and added better than bouillon to the broth (about a teaspoon) doubled on orzo and I also added two more extra cups of the chicken broth. Hubby enjoyed since he’s feeling under the weather.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I tried making pork chile verde once. No matter the recipe, there's a whole lot of hidden work behind "1 1/2 lb. pork shoulder or butt, cut into 1-inch cubes." You'll need a sweat band around your forehead and maybe a slug of tequila to get you through.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: That’s the New York Times lol.  1/2 hour cook time. But it’s not until you make it for the first time that you find that it’s 3 hours prep.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Dennis Chiapello I thought having Tequila was mandatory for any type of cooking!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Dennis Chiapello. just buy some boneless  pork “country style ribs” and they’re easy to cube.  Pick ones with some decent balling I’d fat still left.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: No shade to Denver compadres, but having first made this in the Rio Grande valley around El Paso since 1972 we always use New Mexico varietal green chiles — 6-4, Big Jim, Sandia — in lieu of poblanos. Note: Mexican oregano is what you want here rather than “regular” Italian. We correct seasoning at the end with a drop or two of white vinegar and/or pinch sugar if necessary. Think it’s prolly unique to EP area but we also add diced potato (1 or 2) halfway through cooking. Best burritos ever!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Being from the Dona Ana valley, I agree with this completely!  Thank you!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Agree re the NM chiles. I grew up in the Mesilla Valley and my family farmed there. Hatch chiles are good but so are chiles from the surrounding valley. In stores, we only cared that they were NM chiles. Anaheims are good substitutes for NM green chiles because the seeds from NM chiles were taken to Anaheim and grown there, where they have a longer growing season but the taste is similar. But poblanos have a totally different flavor profile.
And Rancho Gordo has great Mexican oregano.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
@Señor Dave  100% agree!…same in Southern California.  A Oaxacan pal taught me this pork stew years ago and must use Mexican oregano as well as Anaheim or NM chiles.  Also, can sub Serrano instead of Jalapeño if you want a bit more heat.  I add chard (leaves only), but not potatoes because we eat it with rice.  In Spring, I add slightly roasted fresh asparagus instead of chard.  

Also, the blender step can be skipped!  Do a quick, rough chop of the roasted ingredients and toss into the pot along with cilantro.  They’ll melt into a chunky sauce that’s incredibly yummy with some nice texture and little flavor bombs from the roasted chile and tomatillo pieces.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I've made a version of this for years after having it at the Blue Bonnet in Denver.  I've heard it called "Denver Green Chili."  It is absolutely killer.  My only comments are 1. I use Medium Hot Hatch green Chili's, but Poblano are excellent, too. 2.  Sometimes this Chili can be very acidic.  I add about 1/2 teaspoon of baking soda at the end and taste, adding perhaps up to 2 teaspoons total until it rounds out.  Too much and it tastes soapy.  3.  I like to add 1/2 cup Masa with 1/2 cup water.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: When do you add the Masa and what does it contribute to the dish?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Jim Ball Chicago But what do you do when the Hatch Green Chiles are not in season?  In Northeast, we only get them in a few supermarket chains, like Trader Joe’s of all places, in season - or canned and yuck to all the BPAs from the can

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Pam - I add it abut 20 minutes before serving.  It adds an earthy corn flavor, and thickens it a bid.  Try it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Using poblano instead of Hatch chile peppers could be categorized as a war crime. Poblano has its place, but not in New Mexican chile verde with pork.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @John R this is the Mexican recipe, not the New Mexico/Colorado green chile recipe those of us from the Southwest know and love. We use Pueblo chilis in Colorado, but I love Hatch, too.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @John R Very hard to find fresh hatch chilis here in the northeast.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @John R that would limit me making this to like 2 weeks out of the year here in Texas 😩

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Next level suggestion: Add a half teaspoon of ground clove to the puree. By the end of the roast the sharp aroma of clove softens and brings a remarkable substance to the flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I make a very similar recipe with cubed pork loin to cut the fat a bit. I char my onions, along with the peppers up front, flour and brown the pork chunks, otherwise pretty much the same, except...  add a can or 2 of hominy (pozole) at the end! The sweet, chewy corn flavor is such a nice contrast. Trust me!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Ant'ney Love the idea of adding nextamalli. I have tossed in potatoes before to create a more stew-like version of chile verde. However this would end up more like pozole. Thanks for sharing!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Ant'ney I totally agree with the addition of hominy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: My wife won't eat pork. Does anyone think this could be made with white meat chicken, if possible?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Mathew Price Boneless skinless chicken thighs substitute quite nicely in this as I have made a similar one.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Mathew Price Try it! I would consider using chicken thighs and legs and then shredding meat off of the bone  once cooked for more flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Here’s a recipe that has many of the same ingredients, but features chicken instead of pork. https://nl.nytimes.com/f/cooking/LwgPvULDNPVo6JwJscz8gA~~/AAAAAQA~

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I generally don’t see pork shoulders that are less than 2 lbs so I use boneless country style pork ribs, which still have the right meat/fat ratio. But I can never get this as good as Efrain’s in boulder.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Dave this is a brilliant idea and I have adopted it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Dave. So true.  My workaround for this is to use half a 3lb shoulder for chili verde and half to make a pate de campagne with chicken livers reserved from whole chicken purchases.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This is possibly the best NUYT recipe I've made, and I make a lot.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
I made this exactly as written, and it was delicious! I wouldn't change anything. I made it in the Instant Pot on high pressure, 40 minutes. 

To John R. This is Mexican Chile Verde, not New Mexican, so it's OK to use poblano chiles instead of Hatch green chile without starting a war. I think Mexico existed before New Mexico and Hatch. ;)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Catalina— was it spicy? My hubs doesn’t generally like spicy but I would love to try this recipe! Wondering if I need to drop some of the peppers for his sake?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Kelley Made this for the first time tonight and I’m a low-spice kinda gal. It was right at my threshold so for your hubs I might drop one of the chilis. Any more than that and the volume and texture of the puree might be off

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Kelley I found it very spicy but you never know with jalapeños I may have had a hot one. I added a little agave that helped a bit. It was very delicious though just spicy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I highly recommend Pierre Franey"s Chile Verde recipe (on this website) from his 60 Minute Gourmet cookbook.  We have been making it for many years.  And in a pressure cooker, it really can be done in an hour.  I substitute roasted poblanos and fresh jalapeño for the canned, and break up the pork using a potato masher after the pressure has been released.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
@MFClark

Serious Eats also has a killer Instant Pot version by Kenji Lopez-Alt. Very flavorful and quick!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Jennifer out in Cali Yes!  Kenji’s recipe is excellent!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
This was great, no edits. Not sure you need to peel the poblanos - won't next time. 

(We used extra pork because it's hard to get 1.5lb of pork shoulder)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I never peel em! lol I roast them low and slow at 425 with any other combination of peppers (jalapeno, Scotch bonnet, serrano, Shishito), along with the onion and garlic until all are lovely and slightly charred, about 30 minutes. And I definitely don't lose the flavor of the skin! I pre-cook my pork in a slow cooker with cumin, oregano, chicken broth and dried red pepper, and then freeze (freeze broth separately). Then, it's ready to go, whenevs!!! Thicken with a slurry of masa harina/water!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I've made my very similar version of this with chicken breasts or thighs, beef, and of course pork, and also add cubed butternut squash which adds a touch of sweetness to help balance the acid

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I've made this no less than 20 times over the past 6 or 7 years. There is no need to peel the poblanos and if you leave in the seeds, it adds more heat. I've made it with chicken breast and as a combo, but pork is far more flavorful, and I always use cubed shoulder or butt after trimming most of the fat. It's one of those dishes that always taste better the second day.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: After the challenge of cubing the pork butt a couple of times, I started buying "country ribs" instead-- much much easier to cube.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This is a good recipe, I deviate by using Hatch Green Chiles for the chile component, and would never use chicken broth in a pork chile. If you don't want to go to the trouble of making pork broth from bones, very good tonkotsu broth concentrate is available, including this from Amazon https://www.amazon.com/Yamasa-Artisanal-Concentrate-Certified-Tradition/dp/B08NTXNDQF

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I make this using well browned (in a stick free pan )chicken thigh meat cut up into bite size chunks or, if you can get it, turkey thigh meat

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Prep time is actually a solid 45 minutes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Made as written with a side of rice and beans. Turned out great.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful and easy— have made this twice.  For the second batch, I increased the ratio of pork (2x) for a more appropriate balance to the fine salsa, and I doubled the overall scale to freeze some.  The additional fat from 2x meat was slightly excessive, so next time I’ll trim the pork shoulder but keep the 2x meat overall.  And thawed batches have been a hit!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Confession: with all due respect to this fine recipe, I make a delicious albeit not-from-scratch pork chile verde using a pork tenderloin, cubed and browned with a generous amount of onion and garlic, then simmered for 30 minutes (or longer) with one jar of plain salsa verde (mild) and one jar of roasted salsa verde (medium), both by Herdez.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Great quick version.  I have made a similar dish for my clients and it was well received.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Reserve some of the broth to loosen the remaining tomatillo sauce from the blender after the bulk of the sauce is removed. Add to recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Having made this twice to rave reviews, I humbly offer a couple of tips: 
1.) Depending on the size of the jalapeño and your tolerance for heat, you may want to de-seed it before broiling. A larger pepper with the seeds will ramp it up. 
2.) Line the sheet pan used for broiling the vegetables with foil for easier clean up, and use a silicone or rubber spatula to scrape off every last drop of the caramelized juices into the blender or food processor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Every month I make a recipe with my mom, who lives across the country. This was our 14th recipe, and she and I both loved it. The pork is melt-in-your-mouth tender, the sauce is amazing and complex, and it all comes together fairly easily. I think this is the best recipe I’ve made from NYT cooking and I highly recommend it. It makes a bit more sauce than you might need, so I recommend buying slightly more pork (I used 2lbs instead of 1.5).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Country pork ribs are pork shoulder. I don't like to cut up raw meat. I use the bone in ribs, roast until brown then put in pot with other ingredients. After cooking the meat shreds easily. I also use frozen hatch chilies.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: The recipe says Prep time 15 minutes! In what world is this. Trimming and cutting up a pork butt, roasting and peeling the peppers, making the sauce, - all this before starting to cook! 15 minutes, really?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely fantastic! I made this dish for the first time. Made the sauce a day in advance. I had never cooked with tomatillos before, nor roasted peppers, so that was a fun adventure. I'll be trying more Mexican dishes now. Mole, anyone?!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. However, the 15 min prep time is a gross underestimate!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Made this on New Years Day, and it was fantastic. Ignore the commenters insisting that this *must* be made with hatch chilis. I live in NC, and cannot find them in my region, so I used a combination of poblano, anaheim, and jalepeno. Added extra onion and garlic. Would double the meat next time as others have suggested. Prep took much longer than 15 minutes. Served with fresh cilantro, queso fresco, avocado, yellow rice, and tortilla chips. Will definitely make again with some slight adjustments

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Chile Verde is a Mexican dish so not sure why people insist on a New Mexican chile for this. It may be a different style and it's definitely delicious but it's perfect to use Anaheim peppers (or Poblano if desired). Hatch will make it significantly hotter!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I used pork loin because I rarely buy the blade or shoulder cut.  I subbed a can of tomatillos for fresh which saved time. GASP!!   I also had some already smoked and pureed hatch chili peppers used in lieu of the jalepeño.  I added potatoes and skipped the rice.  I garnished with avocado, greek yogurt, a squeeze of lime.  It was delicious!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: We used chicken thighs for a non-pork eater. Now we prefer the chicken thighs over the pork version! Very tasty!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I made this yesterday for Halloween and it was amazing!! I doubled the pork,garlic,cumin and oregano. I used 1 large jalapeno(left seeds in) and it was not spicy at all, so finished the stew with a few splashes of pickled jalapeno juice. It took me an hour to get it into the oven, so the timeframe listed was spot on. Some guests had it as tacos, some as nachos, some ate it like soup. 100% will make again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious! My butcher gave me a shade over 1.5 lbs of pork but I feel 2 or 2.5lb would be better as there was a high sauce to meat ratio. I used a red jalapeno but would get green next time to keep the color greener rather than ‘military fatigue khaki’.
I charred the onions in the pan with the peppers ( as some readers suggested) but I might not do this next time because the pan juices were watery and the onions pale the point the peppers were blackened. Think caramelizing the onions in the pan would be better.

It was slightly bitter so i added a little sugar, kept the salt in the low side. Served with brown rice with kidney beans and a little lemon oil. 
Everyone in the family loved it. It’s a keeper.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent and fun to make. I left out the jalapeno and seeded the poblano due to spice-adverse diners, and it still had a bit of a kick and was full of flavor. I added a large can of hominy and was glad I did as it was a little sparse to feed 4 otherwise. I made it earlier in the day and then reheated for dinner which I think let the flavors develop. Highly recommend.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Outstanding. I nearly doubled everything and still felt there wasn't enough pork. Didn't peel the poblanos. A few squeezes of lime prior to serving helped. A unanimous hit in my house and will be added to the roster!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I used a two pound shoulder that I trimmed of fat, but I only cut it into six pieces (because I didn’t have time to do one inch pieces). Otherwise, I followed the recipe, but put the ingredients into a crockpot for 7.5 hours on low. The pork fell apart with a spoon. Outstanding! Will make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Works fine with pork tenderloin, which is much easier to deal with than a shoulder roast. The sweet onions is a good idea! If the tomatillos are very tart, I also add some apple juice at the end. When toasting the vegetables, use parchment or a sheet pan that's already well seasoned, because the scorching juices can make a crusty messof the pan.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Tomatillos make lots of liquid, so they're enough on their own. If you want more liquid, consider using a beer instead of broth. A pilsner or bock both are good. Skip the rice and put in hominy in last 30 mins -- a one pot meal. Above all -- it MUST be Mexican oregano. The flavor is unique, and Italian oregano doesn't give the same. Italian is more musty tasting, while Mexican is more vegetal. You want that brightness.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Absolutely delicious recipe, and maybe one of my favorite things I’ve ever cooked. I’d recommend the following tweaks:
- Add extra time in the broiler, I did 25 mins for extra color!
- Didn’t peel the tomatillos, the skins get charred and you can always fish them out later.
- Added shallot, because why not.
- REALLY BROWN the pork, like dark brown on all sides, salt the heck out of it, use lots of oil. You want lots of good brown stuff on the pan for when you cook the onions.
- Used 2 tablespoons of Better than Bullion and 2.5 cups of water instead of flavorless grocery store broth.
- Hot take… Add an extra HOUR in the oven, adding a little water an hour or so in, then a little extra at 2 hours. You’ll see such a drastic improvement from light green to dark green color and the smell will be intoxicating!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This was a big hit! Added some ground clove and smoked paprika with the other spices to give it extra backbone.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: i roasted the onions at the same time as the rest. it meant that they weren't 'finely diced', but it added a ton of flavor. i also reduced the liquid at the end over a high burner after removing and shredding the pork. in the end, super delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was a great recipe.  The one small addition I would consider making is to add some lime juice at the end.  The acidity of the tomatillos becomes blunted after cooking down for so long in the oven and lime juice adds some of that back in.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I cook this often; it’s one of my favorite meals.  I have made it with pork but lately I have preferred making it with wild game—deer and elk.  It is delicious in every variation.  I substitute the game meat one-to-one and add bacon fat—a couple tablespoons for a double batch.  I don’t bother peeling the poblanos.  I brown the meat in the slow cooker (mine has a “Brown” setting), then add the onions, then add everything else, except I omit the stock entirely.  I find there’s plenty of liquid without it.  I leave it in for ~6-8hr.  My 5-qt. slow cooker can accommodate a double batch, so that’s what I make.  I season liberally and serve over rice.  Delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: It would be lovely if we could search these comments.  We might find answers to questions quickly.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Not a pork eater.  I use chicken or turkey thighs with great success (imo).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe! I did a couple of things differently which I think made it faster because cutting up the 7 lb bone-in pork shoulder (all I could find) took longer than expected. I ended up with about 2-1/2 lbs of meat, and will use the leftovers for bone broth. I have a grill built into my stove, so I put all of the veggies on that with a little avocado oil. I used about 3 lbs of tomatillos from my garden and added a green pepper along with poblanos and the jalapeño which I grilled whole and deseeded before pureeing.  After everything was charred, I simply put ALL of the veggies in a blender with chicken stock, and puréed. No need to chop the onion and precook it that way! I started browning the pork in my Dutch oven, and then realized I didn’t want to wait another hour and a half to eat. So, I threw the browned pork, the puréed sauce, cumin, garlic powder and granulated onion in my Instant pot. I added more cumin to taste, and it seemed a little thick, so I added some dry white wine- about a cup, and also a couple of tablespoons of white vinegar to add acidity. Cooked on high for 20 minutes. Served over jasmine rice. My hubby claims it is really amazing, and says the leftovers tonight are even better! Enjoy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I made as written, except instead of cooking in the oven, I cooked in the Instant Pot for 20 min, released the pressure, then used the saute function for 10 minutes with the lid off to thicken the sauce. Worked great!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Man, I’m going to be sad when I run out of leftovers of this dish. This is definitely going to be added to our regular recipe rotation and I’d serve this to dinner guests in a heartbeat. 

I accidentally doubled the meat because my boyfriend bought too much, but honestly it felt like the perfect ratio of meat to stew using 3 lbs of pork. I normally de-seed jalapeños but left them in for this recipe, and the spice level was perfect.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Delicious made as written!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Similar to the chile verde from Simply Recipes.  I don't ever peel poblanos for this--I like the extra char and their skins are thin and not problematic.  Add a pinch of cloves for depth and to bring out the sweetness of the pork.  This is a great hunker down for winter meal

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Nice recipe. I live in Oaxaca and can assure you día de muertros, (Day of the Dead), is not tomorrow, it's on November 2nd, and a holiday in Oaxaca,

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Regarding the use of chicken broth, my former professional Cook husband turned me on to Minor bases. They have a pork one which is perfect here, 1 1/2 teaspoons of base for 2 Cups water. Many other good varieties , I can recome the shrimp, clam and mirapois bases. Lately have been using low sodium beef, chicken and vegetable, I'm bot selling this stuff but it does help streamline our cooking. Check out the Fire-RoastedPoblano flavor concentrate as well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: You don't have to cut the pork into 1 inch chunks - trim the fat off and cut the pork into large chunks. Render the pork fat on its own in the pot before adding anything - a tip I found on America's Test Kitchen. Pour off the excess fat, add the tomatillo sauce to the hot fat with cumin and oregano, then the pork in chunks. Cook until done, roughly shred the pork in the pot. No need to brown the pork. (Note: I add the onion to the tomatillo mixture and blend it all.)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I have also made this with chicken thighs and the results were delicious!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I am surprised that at least in the comments I read, few people mentioned using a pressure cooker to cut down on time. I've made this (it's a great recipe for riffing on to meet your individual tastes and there are so many great suggestions in the comments) before and It is delish. 10 out of 10.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I found it a bit acidic , and suggest balancing with some honey or sugar. We used tomatillo salsa, the only form of tomatillo available at our supermarket in Sydney

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Tomatilla salsa is made with lime juice and sometimes preservatives, which may account for the acidic taste. With regular tomatillos, you won't need sugar or honey. The dish is not supposed to be sweet.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Jeremy If you can’t find tomatillos, you can sub green tomatoes plus some lemon or lime juice.  Tomatillos are more citrusy than regular tomatoes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This was incredible and worth the effort with the broiling / blending. I used a whole bone in pork shoulder (which I seared in a Dutch oven) in a slow cooker for 8 hours. I doused it with Goya adobo in additional to salt and pepper.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: https://santafeschoolofcooking.com/green-chile-stew/ The Santa Fe School of Cooking's basic green chile stew recipe. Nobody in the Southwest uses anything other than Hatch green chiles - and this recipe asks for 3 cups of chopped Hatch chiles, not "three chiles". And no, salsa verde is not the same thing as green chiles. I've occasionally thrown in salsa verde, but only if I happened to have some. By adding that amount of salsa verde you've effectively made salsa verde soup.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @GWPDA - The Borderlands AZ native here. We never use Hatch chiles here. Too hot for me.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @GWPDA - The Borderlands Nobody? That’s highly doubtful. Lots of cooks, lots of chile preferences.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @GWPDA - The Borderlands There are many NM chile varieties, other than Hatch, that are delicious for this green chile.  Just saying…options are good.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Made this for dinner and it was delicious! We like spicy food, so added more cumin, jalapeno and some cayenne.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this with boneless skinless chicken thighs and it’s excellent.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: We smoke pork shoulder and season half for pulled pork sandwiches and keep the other half with just salt and pepper for our chili Colorado. It saves a lot of time as we only need to make the green sauce then reheat the pork in it. The green sauce can also be doubled and frozen for an even easier dinner.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: You might make the puree without the jalapeño, and taste it first. Then add it if you need a kick. Both anchos and New Mexico-style chiles can vary widely in heat. By the way, the only thing special about Hatch valley chiles is their huge marketing campaign. I've always found that the tastiest chiles are from northern NM.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Hatch county IS in northern NM.  I've read that the climate and soil are what makes the chilies special, though I find that chilies from Pueblo, Colorado, taste the same.  People in NM take great pride in their chilies.  

Also, I allow at least an hour to prep this dish.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: In no way is Hatch NM, "northern New Mexico".  It's only 37 miles north of Las Cruces which is definitely southern NM.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is great.  There is an equally good one at seriouseats. com, including a pressure cooker version and a chicken version.  All very good.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: real chile verde has NO CUMIN

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: You can greatly simplify things by using an Instant Pot (Kenji López-Alt's method). In a 6 quart pot, brown the meat, roughly chop everything else, add veggies and seasonings to browned meat. NO NEED for additional liquid. Pressure cook on high for 15 min. Natural vent for 10 min then release pressure. Remove meat. Blend w/immersion blender. Add meat back. Stir in up to 1 T fish sauce. Taste. Add salt & pepper as needed. (Next time I might char the veggies first per this recipe.) Very delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: No need to use plastic to steam poblanos. Put them in a bowl and cover w a plate.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
For those with limited access to Hatch chiles… when you can get them, buy a bunch, roast and peel them, then freeze them. We put 1 or 2 in a small (snack size) zipper bag, lay them out on a cookie sheet, then freeze them. Nice and easy to grab what you need any time. They defrost quickly, or you can even run the bag under warm water if you’re impatient like I am. 

Made this using some of my stash and it was excellent!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Too much liquid. I needed to add 1/4 cup of corn meal to thicken. Next time I’ll reduce the amount of stock to 8oz.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
I made this last recently and it was EXCELLENT. I followed the ingredients as written but adjusted the cooking method for a pressure cooker/instant pot:
Sear the pork in the pressure cooker. Remove and sauté onions with spices
Add pork back in along with pureed chili mixture and chicken broth
Bring to high pressure, cook for 30 minutes, then release
Remove pork and simmer for 10 minutes to reduce sauce

I added the juice of two limes at the end, and served with pickled red onions and sour cream.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I gave it 4 stars because I didn't follow the recipe, kind of. Like others, I realized that this needed more prep than I'd anticipated, so I let the oven do it. I doubled it, then followed instructions for the salsa verde, but didn't chop the pork into cubes - I braised a 3lb. butt in the salsa with the onion and stock and shredded it instead. It was fall apart tender, so the leftovers were served as nachos with some corn and cheese.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This recipe looks to be perfect for an Instant Pot. Everything could be prepared in the Pot and it would cut an hour off the cooking time. I'm going to try that, and report back. Can't decide if I will cut the pork shoulder into cubes (as per the recipe), or leave it in larger pieces and then shred the meat after it has cooked. I'm thinking, the latter.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Here's an Instant Pot version: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1017986-chili-verde

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Question - do you seed the poblano after roasting/peeling?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Up to you - I don't, but if you're sensitive to heat, go ahead. It'll still be great!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Would it be possible to make this with leftover pork roast or tenderloin?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Whenever we go to Las Vegas we have to go to Lindo Michoacán on Desert Inn Road for their stupendous version of this dish. Worth the Uber fare and the trip. Now will try it at home thanks,

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This is a wonderful recipe! I've made nearly the identical recipe for years, with whatever peppers I've been blessed with from the garden: Poblanos, Hatch (certified), jalapeno, Scotch bonnet, Shishito, Serrano. I typically do use a combination thereof, because my family enjoys a good deal of heat. I pre-cook pork when grocer puts it on sale. Sauce? 100% Pork? YES!!! Thicken with masa/water slurry? DEFINITELY. You won't believe the flavor! Add cilantro, cheese and sour cream to serve. Heaven!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
I have cooked this multiple times but it is much easier to use Hatch chile salsa, a dash of chili powder, and a pinch of smoked chipotle powder.
I cut up boneless pork ribs and see no need to brown the meat.
It is a very tasty dish.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe is good as is, but if you add a tiny bit of cinnamon to the spices, it is even better. I use Mexican oregano too. 

It’s a versatile dish—you can use either pork or boneless skinless chicken thighs—both are delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: A perfect recipe for this time on year, when the peppers are coming in fast.  I made a very similar batch a couple weeks ago using Green Bee tomatoes (large cherries that ripen to green and which I grow because I can eat them as tomatoes or use them in pretty much any recipe that calls for tomatillos).  I made a large batch of the sauce (steps 1-4 of this recipe) and froze it without meat (to more efficiently cram the harvest into the freezer).  Divine.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Love the sauce making process. I have found powdered Mexican oregano is an incredible flavor bomb.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I regularly make a very similar version of this recipe with one change.  I learned to cook the pork by putting the cut up the pork in a large pot, just cover with water, add orange peel, bay leaf, salt.  Bring to a boil, leave at a medium boil until all the water is gone, brown the meat in its own fat.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Matthew Price I don't eat pork either and prefer only chicken breast. I'd make the sauce first so that the flavor deepens, brown bone-in chicken breast, cook until almost done, remove and shred then return the chicken to the pan to finish. I might even add the sauce into the pan I browned the chicken, just for the additional flavor. I will try this.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This sounds delicious and I'd like to try it.  Unfortunately, I'm one of those with the cilantro=soap gene, so I need to ask those with experience making the dish:  do you think it would be better to substitute parsley for the cilantro, or would it be better to just eliminate it entirely?  Thanks.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Bill I made this last night and omitted the cilantro entirely, was pretty good! I did decide to add some lime juice at the end to add a bit of missing freshness, likely from the lack of cilantro. I get you on the soap gene, it’s terrible!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I do roast the tomatillos and jalapenos, but use TJ's Hatch Chili Salsa for the rest of the liquid. And no chicken stock! Cook everything in a covered casserole/Dutch oven for about three hours at 250; thicken with ground green pepitas (pumpkin seeds) to create a sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Delicious with turkey thighs instead of pork.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
This was insanely good. My family and I (even the 6 year old) devoured it with no leftovers. I made fresh corn tortillas to go with it, which was perfect.
 
When I was scanning the comments I saw something about gochuchang that I didn’t really pay attention to. But as I was putting ingredients back into my fridge, I spotted some gochuchang and added a last-minute tablespoon before the stew went into the oven and I think that helped to make this next-level. Also added a teaspoon or two of sugar for balance.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Reduce heat level for broiling the peppers if possible. Otherwise, start with poblanos, then add tomatillos and jalapeño, then garlic. Excellent heat without too much bite.
I made spicy black beans as a side. After mincing garlic and jalapeño by hand, I realized that all the ingredients were in a batch of El Napolito roasted salsa. In future, I’ll rinse canned black beans and mix them with the salsa, then simmer a while.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I add lime juice and zest at the end.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
