commentBody: I'm from El Paso. I just made my traditional chile verde on Saturday. No tomatillos, please, just green chiles. Add a couple jalapenos. A poblano is nice but not necessary. A couple carrots with the onions and garlic add sweetness. And BTW, what's that white stuff photographed next to your chile? Pinto beans, not rice, please. But again, these are not necessary but some people like to serve them side by side if they are making a thicker stew. Flour tortillas should be served with the stew.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Balance at the end with salt and white vinegar to taste

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was followed as written. It was delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful. Our pork shoulder was 2.5 pounds and we decided to use all of it. We also only had 2 quite small poblanos, so I added a small can of diced green chiles to supplement. Added about an extra cup of broth to ensure there would be enough sauce around the meat as it cooked. Came out wonderfully tender and with a mellow heat.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Satisfying!  I didn't peel my poblanos, and I subbed two small serranos for the jalapeno.  I felt a moment of horror when I removed the lid and there was a black slick on top, but it stirred in and tasted great!  I added 1/2 tsp. baking soda at the end as recommended, and I think a squeeze of lime when serving would make it even better! I melted cheese over tortilla chips in the microwave and topped with the chile verde for a snack... yum!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Recipe says “stemmed” Poblanos & Jalapeño.  Also, “seed” the Jalapeño!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Turkey thighs braise beautifully and would be an admirable substitute for pork.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: And the temperature of the oven is?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Recipe says 325 degrees.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Alexandria SLC I was wondering the same thing

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Thanks — it’s in the slow cooker now, going for high for 2 hours and low for 3. I read your reply about 3 minutes too late and I suspect you are right about halving the chicken stock. Time will tell!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Spectacular. Boneless country-style pork ribs, otherwise made as written. Blistered peppers went into a food storage container to steam instead of plastic bag. Gravy was thick, unctuous, and delicious. Pork chunks melted in my mouth with just enough chew -- perfect.  Very flavorful without being too spicy.  (I did take the seeds and membranes out of the jalapeno, but just took the stems off the poblanos.)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: sensationally good!! I added 2 serranos because it's really not spicy as written -- also a couple more cloves of garlic & a couple more tomatillos. Next time I'd consider adding some hominy for a kind of pozole vibe .. the pork shoulder I had was about 2 pounds (prior to trimming the fat cap) which worked well. Leftover the next day, after it had tightened up a bit in the fridge, popped into a hot cast iron, crisped a bit, and then put into a tortilla w/ some cheese for mulitas -- sensational.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: For all of those complaining about cutting up the pork, have the butcher do it....simple and doesn't cost a penny other than the price of the pork.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This was so good!!  I used a 2 lb pork loin and did not peel the poblanos. Could easily add another jalapeno for a bit more heat. Will definitely put this in the rotation, this displaced Texas girl felt like she was back in Texas eating this yummy dish!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This was good but too much work for the result. Followed pretty exact, little more garlic & jalapeño. Got tomatillos, peppers, cilantro (and tortillas that I served with) at local Mexican market, all good quality.  And had the butcher cube the pork for me. So glad I didn’t take the skin off the poblanos - one less step! Anyway, had a nice rainy afternoon to do the roasting, cutting, blending, browning, etc… just a lot of effort for good, not great meal.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Loved this. Added bacon to sauté the onion to add some smoke. Next time I would add more meat. There was a lot of sauce. Maybe tomatillos were unusually juicy. Served with some great Rancho Gordo beans and collards. Lots of cilantro.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: 2 cups of chicken broth way too much. Sauce way too watery. Will use half next time.  Great flavor. Doubled the pork amount

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This was a treat. I was able to use home-grown tomatillos and jalapenos, along with the poblanos.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. After braising the pork and making the sauce as directed, I finished it in the slow cooker, an i hour on high, three on low. Baked some rice and have a couple of great meals. Peeling the poblanos seemed unnecessary I’m not sure we ever get Hatch chiles in Minnesota but I’ll look for them in the short season. Don’t not make this if ALL you have are poblanos. Arrest me.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I would double the amount of pork in this recipe next time. It would improve the sauce to meat ratio.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious! Not a weeknight recipe, though. I spent 60 minutes chopping, broiling, blending, etc before the stew went into the oven for another 90 minutes. 

But sometimes with cooking, you get what you pay for. This is so satisfying. If you leave the seeds in the peppers, it will be quite spicy but not painful. I added lime and cilantro at the end.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. My only beef (pork?) is the amount of grease coating my stovetop and backsplash after browning the meat, even though I used a splatter guard. This is not unique to this recipe - it's a problem I have when browning or sautéing any meat. Does anyone have any tricks for reducing splatter? The cooking is fun; the cleanup not so much. Thanks!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @dm I keep a single burner butane stove in my greenhouse. Use it in there or outside if the weather is nice. No greasy mess in the house with this or bacon either.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
After reading all the comments, here are my tweaks:
-425 oven for peppers & 1/2 onion for 30 mins - skinned what I could
-Tripled the garlic (cuz that's how I roll)
-Puréed/pulsed until still slightly chunky in food processor
- Seasoned pork w/ salt, pepper & 3T flour
-Added other 1/2 onion & 4 more minced garlic cloves (see above)
-1T cumin & oregano
Still not spicy enough for me so will be adding 3-4 more jalapeños.
Delightfully savory but still not Efrain's in Boulder, CO per Dave - agreed!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Very good as written. E suggests adding beer for depth.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
My daughter doesn’t like the texture of meat, so we cook cubed tofu alongside the pork in the oven.  I actually have come to prefer the tofu.  

Charring peppers on grill is faster and more effective than the oven.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: My husband has the "hate cilantro" gene.  Is there any substitute that will work here?  Sounds otherwise fabulous!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Stephany substitute parsley for the cilantro and then garnish individual servings with fresh minced cilantro for those who can’t tolerate cilantro

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I think this could be made with deboned  turkey thighs for those who don’t eat pork. In Israel we call it shawarma and it is used instead of pork by both Muslims and Jews. Shorter cooking time, lower fat and plenty of taste from all the other wonderful ingredients.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Made this exactly as written and found it fine, but not spectacular. Kind of one-note. Might add beans or tomatoes or something if I ever make it again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: delicious.  a little sugar is good to cut the acidity in the pre-cooked sauce.  I cooked the sauce until it thickened before adding the rest of the ingredients; it helps blend the flavors better.  if you don't feel like cutting up the pork shoulder ask the person behind the meat counter in the supermarket.  it saves time but you won't have the opportunity to remove chunks of fat (which pork shoulder has a lot of) since they may not trim it out for you.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
To make in pressure cooker: cook at high pressure for 25 mins instead of bringing to boil and then slow cooking in oven. 

To make more of a chili: add white beans and/or hominy and/or frozen corn. 

To avoid hassle of cutting up pork: most stores sell pork stew meat - you can also ask your butcher counter to cut the shoulder

If tomatillos out of season: use same treatment of peppers/garlic and add canned tomatillos after blending pepper mixture 

Serve with fresh limes to garnish and crema

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: SO delicious. I only had beef broth on hand so used that. The pork is tender and the salsa was amazing. Used about 2 lb pork. Son ate seconds and he rarely eats seconds. Even though prep is slightly time intensive the actual cooking is a sit back and relax. Serve with any veg on the side and a carb that will absorb the divine salsa sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Question - do you leave tomatillos whole or slice in half ?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This looked so good. I had to try it today. I took a lot of shortcuts because I wasn't up for the tomatillos today. I skipped a bunch of the first steps and just used canned salsa verde and about two Chipotle peppers with the adobo sauce.  I used less chicken broth.   And, I could only find very large pork shoulder this week.  Again, not in the mood.  Used pork stew meat. Otherwise I did everything according to the recipe. And for an easy peasy low mess way to do it that was really good.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is AWESOME. Two tips! 1. Definitely throw a teaspoon of baking soda in the salsa Verde before you put it in the pot. Cuts down on the acid big time. 2. Use country style pork ribs instead of butt or shoulder! SO much easier. Just as tender.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I get the passion about chilies and all. But, I made this as written and it was delicious. Doubled the recipe to have leftovers because it is labor and time intensive

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I used boneless ‘country style’ ribs. Easy to cut and turned out delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Leftovers? What leftovers?  Got a pork shoulder that was closer to 2 pounds for 2 adults and one teenager, who inhaled it.  Lots of sauce left over that we’ll freeze for another round later this fall.  Forgot the cumin and oregano, but can’t say we missed it; maybe I’ll add them when we make it again with the leftover sauce.

Served it with toasted corn tortillas and Mexican red rice.  Delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Ahh the chile wars!  I  live in the San Luis valley and when I first got here, people asked "Hatch or Pueblo".  I say Pueblo :)

I use soooo much less cilantro in this because I don't like it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Big hit when we had it for dinner.  Also great as. left overs the next day.  With three poblanos and 1 jalapeno, it was pretty not (spice-wise).  my husband loved that.  I might use half a jalapeno next time.  It is very easy despite having phases, but the phases allow you to catch up with clean up and read your novel

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Kay Chun. thank you for this wonderful entree. Made with 1.3 # pork tenderloin. Adhered to the exact recipe. Going to be made for dinner guests this Fall/Winter. Cheers

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious!!  Added a can of hominy as someone suggested, otherwise followed recipe. Definitely will make again, we both loved it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Good luck finding a shoulder/butt this small. I found a four pound boneless shoulder and used it all; the amount of sauce created was perfect for that much meat. The browning process is tedious batching that much through, and the prep time closer to an hour, (as others have said), but this came out absolutely stellar. Delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I live in Colorado and am fortunate to have access to bushels of Hatch green chili every August.  I buy them already roasted and freeze them.  This is similar to my process, though I don't include cilantro in the sauce.  It is available as a garnish, but lots of people don't like cilantro.  I much prefer Hatch chilies to poblanos.  Also I add 4 to 6 jalapenos instead of one.  A quarter cup of apple cider vinegar or 6 ounces of beer are also added.  It's a great topping for burritos or eggs.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I am not familiar with tomatillos. I would be happy to try, but I  have loads of newly pulled green tomatoes from my garden as frost is coming.  Are they a substitute?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: They sure are. "Green tomatoes can take the place of tomatillos in guacamole, in a chile verde sauce for enchiladas, raw in salads, as a fresh topping for tacos, simmered in a vegetable soup or turned into a chicken posole stew." https://www.finegardening.com/article/use-green-tomatoes-instead-of-tomatillos

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Yes!  I just posted that this is what I use this time of year (along with pork loin).  You just need to add some lemon/lime juice to compensate for the tomatoes having less acid.  They work great!  I'll be making several green chilis and freezing for our chilly winters.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Can you make this in an instant pot?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I've also been making a similar version of this. It's one of the first dishes I learned at my first restaurant job after cooking school. Lately I've been adding delicata squash (no peeling needed) or butternut squash for a touch of sweetness

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This is basically our family recipe but a glaring exception - the 2lbs of Hatch chilis. The other peppers are just supporting characters. I once lived in NYC - I know how hard Hatch are to find but order them online. Better to make a trip to the NM/CO area in Sept and buy a bushel and watch (smell!) them roasting in that giant drum. I have 20lbs in my freezer right now.  This the first time I've seen our recipe (handed down from NM friends) ever online. This is legit but you need Hatch.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Gregor--Agree, 30 years in Colorado taught me Hatch chili only. Never tomatillos!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Mexican oregano is what you want here rather than “regular” Italian. We correct seasoning at the end with a drop or two of white vinegar and/or pinch sugar if necessary.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: yes i simmer mine in a dutch over for 2 1/2 hours add the chili sauce for the last 45 and reduce as needed, but you can add the sauce and pork all at once. i like the pork to be able to fall apart with a fork. @Sara

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I toss the cubed pork in masa harina, then brown.  Corn meal will not work, as you need the unique flavor from the nixtamalization.  I do like the idea of adding tomatillos instead of the chopped tomatoes from my recipe.  I'll leave the chile choice to you.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I have a lot of green tomatoes to use up.  They work great in recipes like this, in place of tomatillos; just add lemon or lime juice to compensate for the lower acidity of the tomatoes.  I'll be making a triple batch and freezing some.  Thanks for the suggestions of added potato and hominy.  I will be giving that a try.  And, absolutely, use Mexican oregano.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I'm cooking for someone who has difficulty chewing. Can I make this with ground pork?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I've been making my versions of this recipe since I had it on a field trip in college back in the 70's... Yes, pork (but thighs work), yes just about any chili works - taste preference or availability, seeds or not (how hot), charred or not (some dislike charred bits), canned in a pinch (but whole rather than diced) but not often because I grow and freeze for later.  Always good, sometimes great, never exactly the same way twice!  Always make enough for burritos the next day or for the freezer.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I make a very similar recipe with cubed pork loin to cut the fat a bit. I char my onions, along with the peppers up front, flour and brown the pork chunks, otherwise pretty much the same, except...  add a can or 2 of hominy (pozole) at the end! The sweet, chewy corn flavor is such a nice contrast. Trust me!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
I love to smoke the pork before cutting it up and braising it in the sauce.  So, so delish!

I also usually make a double batch and freeze leftovers in quart mason jars.  Makes for a quick meal when you just don't feel like cooking.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made this with canned tomatillos? Fresh ones are hard to come by in my neck of the woods! Obviously wouldn't be able to do the roasting/charring so it would be different but still yum?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Yes. works fine, but you might miss the charred taste and may find it saltier. (I have to watch my sodium so notice that the most.)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Holy grins and giggles, Batman, that’s good!!

I think I used anaheims but can’t be sure. I touched it up with a LITTLE brown sugar, vinegar, cornmeal, and a wisp of clove. Really rounded it out.

Gonna let this chill out in the fridge for the night and make killer ranch eggs tomorrow.

Make this!  It doesn’t suck.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed and loved it!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I know I risk offending many - is there an option for something other than Cilantro?  I'm one of "those people" who have the gene that makes it taste like dirty soap water or worse.  Thank you

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Excellent!   Made exactly as written except added one more poblano than called for because my husband bought an extra one and I didn't want to waste it.  This is just delicious abd so flavorful.  The spice level creeps up on you but is just perfect.  5 stars!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: If you follow exactly, it is a long process. You can get a good Chili Verde by skipping the broiling and processing and putting everything in the crock pot. Don't add any liquid. Each of the steps adds to it, brown the meat, broil the veg, peel the chilis etc. But if you don't have all afternoon, its still great. And by the time you put Cilantro, Avacado, Salsa etc on top no one notices.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe in a huge hurry, and it took me 45 min. and it was a blast! Then it cooked, and tho I went to my neighbors for tea, I could easily have been completely cleaned during the cook time. And it was fantastic!!!!! It’s going to be on my listw

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This is a slam dunk. Subbed country style ribs for shoulder/butt and put the onion under the broiler, but otherwise kept to the script. Fantastic rainy winter dinner with some nice flour tortillas.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious, but I made some changes…esp for anyone who wants to save time.  I didn’t have tomatillos, so used a jar of roasted tomatillo sauce.  I threw a jalapeño, garlic and cilantro (unroasted) into blender with some of the tomatillo sauce.  I always have frozen roasted peeled poblanos handy, so threw them in too.  Followed the recipe from then on.  Will definitely add to rotation, having cut out all that pre-prep!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Deelish!! I like New Mexico and Colorado green chile but thought this came out  a bit too acidic so I added a 28 can of Cento San Marzano tomatoes before putting it in the oven. Next time I’ll try roasted Hatch, Colorado or Anaheim peppers and perhaps brown a few tablespoons of tomato paste. Yum.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Based on other comments, used the instapot:  30 min under pressure, natural release. I was concerned about the abundant heat (poblanos were home grown/late season)  in the puree but it mellowed with all the other ingredients.  Wisely skipped the jalapeño.  Also, did 3/4C stock + 3/4C Negra Modelo instead of the 2C stock.  Added a grating of cotija cheese on top and some sliced avocado.  Was FAB.  Don’t be shy with salt.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I used copious amounts of Maldon smoked sea salt throughout the process. Added a wonderful punch up to the roasted poblanos.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: If the total prep time on this is 15 minutes, I'm a crypto billionaire.  Nice flavor, very spicy at first, but that mellows out.  I used Hatch instead of Poblano. It's way too thin and soupy to stick in a taco or burro.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Do not, I repeat, do not think you can put peeled garlic under the broiler for as long as the vegetables are under. Pull the garlic after about 2 minutes, otherwise they will be burnt and bitter and you’ll need to start them all over again. And if you do have to start over again with the garlic using the same pan, drain those drips of goodness from the veg before putting it back into the oven with the garlic or it’ll all cook off leaving you with none, and then you’ll have sad-pan(da).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I made this for the first time last night. It was amazing! Followed the advice to leave on skins (perfect) and had butcher cube the pork shoulder. Next time I would add at least another half pound of shoulder and would consider removing some of the seeds from the jalapeño because it was pretty spicy. I served it with rice, black beans and broccolini. Corn bread would be a great addition, too. Can’t wait to make this dish again. I wish there were more leftovers.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
I made this with sparerib-knobby ends instead of boneless pork shoulder chunks. 
Destemmed the poplanos when skinning thiem. Kept some seeds. Really a no brainer. Can be made on stovetop too. 
Family enjoyed it with polenta! Extra cilantro!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious!  I doubled the recipe to share with a neighbor.  I followed the advice of others and added a can of hominy and a can of white beans.  I think both were great additions and added some texture to the dish.  I served it over rice as suggested.  It was the perfect amount of spicy from the poblanos and jalepenos - spice, but not too hot to enjoy!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I can appreciate everyone's variations and suggestions, but they only "Americanize" an authentic and traditional Mexican dish. This dish has been around since the Aztecs and updated with pork once the Spanish introduced pigs to Mexico during its Colonial Period. As someone who is married to a Mexican and has lived in Mexico for a very long time, this recipe is as authentic as you'd find in any abuelita's Cocina or Fonda.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Huge hit at my house! The chili paste feels complicated but it got raves for balance, color, and taste. I plan to double the recipe next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Hands down my favorite NYTC recipe. It's a huge crowd pleaser. Tangy, rich, the perfect spice. I double the base, use mexican oregano, homemade chicken broth and more than double the meat because pork butts are big and the meat is delicious. It's great with cornbread.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Flavor of this is great, but it comes out a bit watery. I think using a bit less chicken broth works well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Doubled pork, cut into into 2-inch pieces, and braised stew for 2 hrs. Otherwise exactly as written. Sublime. Next time might try doubling tomatillo/poblano base and quadrupling amount of pork.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Outstanding! Made this exactly as written, with well-trimmed pork.  Next time I will make a double recipe.  Hubby loved it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
With my wife helping me the prep was still about an hour. I don’t know where that “15 minutes”
jazz came from.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Delicious  - so much flavor for minimal work. I used boneless pork ribs because they were on sale but it also made the cutting up the meat much easier.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
The flavors are very nice but the timing and concept are a bit off.
Pulled pork should be cooked  very slow and low if you want the full effect of the bark and falling apart pull porkedness. Otherwise you get this tough dried piece of gristley pig meat.
Sauteeing the spices is a fantastic idea! Grinding them in a mortle and pestle makes the flavors sing!
However, you really cant grind coriander, cumin, and black peppercorns at the same time in a pestle bc the peppercorns are a lot harder then the other seeds.
I opted to just grind fresh peppercorns directly into the rub rather than sautéing them.
The sauce is good. The apple cider vinegar adds a nice touch to the bbq sauce.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Rounded up to 2 lbs pork, very good as written.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: How spicy is this?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: The only spicy element in this is the jalapeno. (Tomatillos and poblanos are not spicy.) Jalapenos vary widely in spiciness. To eliminate the heat, you can completely seed the jalapeno. The more seeds that remain, the spicier it will be. Still, one jalapeno to 2c liquid and 1.5 lbs of meat is going to produce a pretty mild chile.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: A chile's heat is mainly in its veins or membranes and in the spongy little pod just below the stem. Carefully (with gloves if necessary), remove the veins/membranes & the pod to reduce the heat of any chile. The seeds do contain some heat by virtue of their location on the veins & the little pod. Poblanos are usually relatively mild chiles so the heat in this dish is from jalapenos or other spicy chile you use. Even mild ones can occasionally have a spicy one in there.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I make a version of this that has hominy in it. Great flavor and a nice texture.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I agree with the comments about the recipe calling for an awkward weight of pork shoulder. Instead, I used uncooked trimmings from some St. Louis style pork rib racks. Turned out great -- the bones added a depth of richness and then I just took them out after it was done. It did take longer in the oven to get the meat falling-off tender but well worth it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I have this cooking in the oven right now. Smells amazing.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I make a version of this by oven roasting poblanos, tomatoes, tomatillos, onion and garlic tossed with olive oil. Remove the poblanos and dice. Purée everything else and add chunks of pork loin, the poblano and green chilies, as well as chicken broth. Great over rice or a square of cornbread. No cilantro. Julia Child and Ina Garten hate it and I do too.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I cannot get tomatillos where I live. Is there any substitute?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Don't use them.  They're not included in any form of green chile stew.  If you must, add tomatoes, preferably stewed.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I'm intrigued by this recipe, but hesitant to put garlic under the broiler for 15 minutes. Doesn't that render them inedible?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Skin the garlic.  Put on a piece of aluminum foil, with a bit of olive oil.  Wrap up and then put in oven to roast for 20 minutes or so.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I cook alot and often make NY Times recipes, but I don't know, Kay, this one might be above my pay scale  I'm supposed to butcher a pork shoulder, peel the husks off of a pound of tomatillos, and char a number of peppers, oh and then peel the poblanos before I  Puree all of that...Sounds like way too much work! 2 1/2 hours estimated time to prepare this, which I'm guessing is conservative. lol!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: For a vegetarian version, I replaced the pork with seitan, carrots and corn.  No need to roast in the oven.  Delish!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I first ate this at the Tigua Indian Restaurant in El Paso in 1981.  I recreated it with help from the "Southwestern Indian Recipe Book", by Zora Hesse.  It's been a hit at workplace chili cookoffs, though of course it's never won the prize.  Hesse adds corn & potatoes.  Pork & green chilis were made for each other!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I guess there's no way to adapt this for someone who loves strong flavors but is medically hot spice/hot peppers-averse?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I've made a green chili like this all kinds of different ways. This is a good recipe but it can also be modified many ways. I like more heat, so I use serrano peppers and hatch chilis. I have also made it with pork, beef, chicken and lamb. Less gravy and it makes a delicious filling for burros or chimichangas. A very versatile dish.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Use Serrano chillis instead of jalapeño.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Easy and really tasty.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Added adobo, paprika, a pinch of garam masala, onion powder, and oregano  to pork to marinate. Added porcini powder and gochujang to the pork while cooking. Added roasted butternut squash instead of hominy. AMAZING!
DO to use parchment paper to roast - lots of smoke.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Just don't use parchment if you broil, lest the paper catch fire. :)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Rice is the wrong call. Have never seen it so served in any place in Mexico, New Mexico,  Arizona or Texas.  Try it over small chunks [not diced] of potatoes sauteed in butter...think Julia. Roasting the tomatillos is great, but not absoulutely necessary, just be sure to use fresh, not canned' pureeing detracts from the rustic feel of the dish.  If I did not have 5-6 roasted Hatch chiles I'd use 3-5 roasted serranos instead of jalapenos. At breakfast, top with a fried egg or two.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Grilling the chilies on a charcoal grill might be a nice touch.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Made this essentially as written only substituting vegetable stock since that is what I had. I bought a 10 pound bone in pork shoulder, cut off a 1.5 pound piece and cubed it. ( I cut the remaing pork into roughly 2 equal pieces, vacuum wtapped and froze them for later use.) That whole process took less than 10 minutes.
I substitute pork shoulder (or tenderloin) for pork loin because it is by far tastier and juicier than loin and as an added bonus is one third the price. It was great.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Can beef work instead of pork?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Barehanded Babar chicken thighs would be a better substitute than beef, because the beef would significantly change the flavor profile. It wouldn’t be bad, just different. If you want to make a Mexican/Tex-Mex braise with beef, chile Colorado is a better option.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This is a pretty good version of the recipe. A sweet older woman from the south side of San Antonio told me that to make the perfect chile verde, I should add a bunch of red radish greens (greens only, well washed and chopped) and a bottle of Modelo Negra beer. Boy was she right. As good, if not better, than Lito's in Santa Barbara.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Cumin makes it more tex-mex than mexican. Also, sweet onions are virtually unheard of in Mexico.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I would not recommend adding cilantro to the mixture with the peppers. This takes away significantly from the taste of the hatch chilies or poblano‘s, whichever you use. Also, I recommend not adding the chilies to the blender. Just chop it well with your garlic cloves. Don’t add them to your braised pork until the last 30 minutes.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This is a great recipe to begin with. In all my versions I will add more Chiles either Colorado, New Mexico or Mexican depending on type and local. I feel like the sauce should be closer to being equally chile and tomatillos. I also use a mix of fresh and dried chiles and most versions have some Hatch Green Chile in it. Any way you make it it recalls cooler fall days and Pinon logs on the fire. This satisfies heart and soul as well as the belly.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Added carrots and potatoes making this a well balanced dinner with the rice and a salad with avocado.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Found it to be a little thin. I think next time less chicken broth and more tomatillo and pepper puree

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I smoked the pork shoulder on a charcoal grill to avoid turning 3 lbs of meat into one inch cubes.  Added grilled onion to the tomatillo sauce. Delish!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Made this as is except I added a dried Chile Arbol to kick it up a notch. Pretty good. Could add two Chile arbols or two jalapenos next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This was absurdly good. Had to use Anaheim chiles but still turned out incredibly flavorful. Also added ground clove to the purée, per a previous comment. Will definitely make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: The next time I make this, and I will!, I will double the amount of cubed pork and keep the other ingredients the same amounts. A squeeze of lime juice at the end added a nice kick of acid.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Wow...that was good...no, Great.  Used 2.2 pounds of pork butt (hard to find smaller) and will freeze left overs.  Didn't adjust anything else.   I don't think there could be too much!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe cheating slightly.   I used a can of smoked hatch enchilada sauce since I didn’t have enough tomatillo and poblano on hand.  I roasted 6 tomatillo and two small poblanos and the garlic on the oven then liquified everything in the vitamix.  It was delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Excellent. Used less stock  so I could fit it all in the pot, cooked till falling apart 4 hours.  added stock to thin a bit after it sat in fridge 2 days. Very delicious with rice, sour cream, great tortilla chips, cilantro and a margarita.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Used canned tomatillos, plus canned green chilies.  This was great.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is….thrilling!…thanks in no small part to fat, glossy poblanos at the market right now. Chile-roasting season! I’m so glad I made this and am even gladder I decided to double it. Perfect exactly as written, with a mild, steady spice heat and flavors in ideal balance.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious.  Only change was used yellow onion instead of sweet.  Would increase the meat/sauce ratio next time. Did partially seed the poblano after roasting, figured it wouldn't hurt.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Would this work in a slow cooker? What do you think the timing would be?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I just made a double batch of this in the slow cooker and it turned out great. Followed the recipe up until the “transfer to oven” part, where I just dumped it in the slow cooker. After 3.5 hours on high it was very good, meat was perfectly tender. I imagine that low for 6-8 hours would also be fine. Mine was a little thin, so I thickened it with some crushed tortilla chips (thanks Alton Brown). If using the slow cooker again I might cut the chicken broth in half.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I made the recipe as written yet doubled in anticipation of a Penn State football evening this Saturday. I used poblano peppers, jalapeño and Fresno. Added a little honey to sweeten it up. Really a knockout recipe!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I made this with a 2 lb boneless pork loin, 2 lbs of tomatillos, and a variety of peppers.  It was easy, tasty, and perfect for a cool fall night.  Served over rice and topped with tortilla chips and a squeeze of lime.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is baking in my oven at the moment. I can’t leave a comment about how it tastes since I’m a vegetarian but one that loves making stuff like this. I just can’t eat it. I do love recipes like this one with steps and then it all comes together and I cannot wait till guests get to enjoy it. I made black beans and rice and will serve this stew with tortillas and homemade crema. My house smells delicious in a way that you know someone put effort into dinner!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Try chickpeas instead of pork to make it vegetarian.  I make a New Mexico Green Chili Stew with chickpeas, and it's scrumptious.  Even my carnivore husband liked it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This is a great recipe, I have used poblano and hatch, I think a big meaty poblano is awfully good. All my roasting is done on the BBQ (gas) quick and easy. Some great tips here on adjusting acidity, and adding masa I’ll give a try, cloves, well maybe. I buy pork loins, cut them into 1.5 lb. portions and freeze just partially thaw for easy cutting, no sweat band required. Probably not as rich as a shoulder or butt though.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Is this pointless making without the tomatillos? Would tomatoes do in a European pinch?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @David, the Holy Trinity of a Chile Verde are tomatillos, peppers, and cilantro (coriander in GB). Tomatillos may *look* like green tomatoes, but the fruit tastes  completely different - vegetal and tart and almost a little citrusy.  Could you ask your grocer to order some canned or (preferably) frozen for you?  You might end up with a case of them, which is not a bad thing at all!  We use them for soups, stews, posole, all of which freeze beautifully and taste better a day later. While you’re at it, you might ask them for hominy, too.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
@David
They're not really anything alike. Tomatillos are actually in the gooseberry family, I believe.  You might end up with something tasty if you substitute tomatoes for tomatillos, but it won't really be the same.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Yeah I don't typically see a small pork shoulder or butt, so I use pork stew meat, or the country ribs. Works well or in a pinch make the sauce then throw in pork sirloin cut up towards the end. The loin doesn't need that long slow cook.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: So easy!  Would definitely double next time and freeze any extras.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Hi.  Can I make this in a crockpot?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Excellent. Made as directed and salt and peppered liberally. Delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: We used Hatch Green chiles instead of pablano. Very tasty!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Step 4 certainly means to peel the tomatillos, yeah? Looking forward to making this one.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This reads like the our favorite pork dish at Linda Michoacán in Las Vegas, except theirs is pretty mild. Mexican not southwestern. Planning to try it tomorrow, thanks for the suggestions to use ribs instead since I don’t want to buy a 5 lb shoulder.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Made this today for family dinner. It’s so good! I followed directions exactly with one change..no fresh tomatillos at store. I used canned whole tomatillos. Cut in half well drained..they worked very well. Serving tonight with a Fall citrus salad topped with pomegranate vinaigrette , pumpkin seeds and pomegranate seeds . Rice and cornbread !

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I cooked this recipe exactly as per the instructions with the exception of adding a second small jalapeño because my family likes heat.  The stew was wonderful. Next time I will add another half pound of pork, as the meat to sauce ratio was a bit off. Otherwise, this recipe will find a place in our regular rotation. So delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Added some coriander to the spice mix, as well as a small can of hatch chilies.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Loved this! Think I will use the leftover sauce with some chick thighs.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Delicious- and a fun Sunday afternoon project. I don’t usually cook with pork butt/shoulder. My butcher gave me a 1.8 lb bone in picnic cut - once I trimmed the fat cap and removed the bone, we were definitely working with less than 1.5 lbs of meat. Added a cubed chicken breast prepared the same way to even out the meat/sauce ratio. Some of the chicken bites were more tender than the pork, so worked out. Would definitely make again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This is phenomenal. Made as directed but using chicken thighs and a slow cooker (6 hours on low) after searing the meat. Served w corn tortillas & queso fresco, perfection.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: If using canned tomatillo ( all we could find) How many cuos equal 1 lb AND do you roast the canned tomatillo with the peppers?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: 1.5 hrs of cook time is no way near enough where I am.  At 4,200 ft in Salt Lake City, 3 hours is about right. I am highly skeptical that 1.5 hours would yield tender pork even in Death Valley.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This is a truly delicious recipe, with my only criticism being that the amount of pork could easily be doubled. I took the suggestion of adding 2 cans of golden hominy for a pozole, which filled in nicely for the missing protein. A really lovely balance of flavors overall.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. Peeling poblanos was annoying and time-consuming, so I’m not sure the cost benefit calculation works out. Also, it came out much more like soup than stew, even after I added a little cornstarch at the end to try to thicken it up a bit. As it turns out, I wound up saving some of the “broth“ and throwing in some diced chicken sausage the next day to make a healthy lunch.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Living in New Mexico, we use local chile when possible. I love the Hatch, Pecos, and Mimbres Valley products, but must say that the Española Valley and Chimayo green (when it is available) will knock your socks off. Still, use what you can find, and this is a great recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Good recipe. Give the pork a light dusting of flour before browning, skip the rice and add diced potatoes the last 10-15 minutes or so

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: My partner does not eat pork. After Thanksgiving, when turkey parts go on sale, I think I'll make this with thigh meat. The texture will probably be pretty spot on.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I detest my broiler, so I charred the chiles, tomatillos, and unpeeled garlic on the stove top using a cast iron griddle. Blended (without the chile stems) with the cilantro, then ran it thru a food mill to remove seeds and skins. Might not be authentic for NM but it's a technique I learned in Oaxaca. The rest of the recipe as is. Cutting up the pork butt was the majority of the work... smallest I could get was 9 lbs! But I have a lot ready for the next 2-3 times.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This is amazing, but using poblanos may be an arrest-worthy suggestion. Subbed 8 ounces of frozen roasted hot Hatch (drained) for the jalapeno+poblanos, added with the cilantro before blending.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This was outstanding. Followed the recipe exactly, weights and measures. Now, the prep/cook timing of 15 min and 2.5 hrs is a bit tight. For me - and I cook a lot - prep including roasting, sautéing, and browning took about 2 hrs, so cook time at 1.5 hrs, which was perfect, puts total time at 3.5 hrs. I would give yourself at least that and maybe 4 to be safe if planning a dinner. Or draft a family member sous chef :).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Finally got round to making this and oh, was it good. I was looking for something to use up green tomatoes from a slow season's gardening, and several blogs pointed me this way. So, no tomatillos in my version. Also no poblano or jalapenos, but instead another homegrown slight miss - padron peppers that turned out to be far too hot to just eat. I froze all the produce at the end of summer and it has been waiting for my chance to cook on my parents' Aga. So, so good. I'll make it every year.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Also a Denver girl, this was absolutely excellent! However, unless you're a professional chef, prep time is a LOT more than 15 minutes!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Can I substitute Pork loin? What does that do to the cooking time?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Very good. I roasted peppers day before to save time

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Love the flavor of this recipe, cheated and used the leftover Momofuku Bo Ssam pork in step 6 and it was a hit.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Made as directed, except with boneless pork ribs and a can of hominy...delicious...very spicy, but delicious!

Next time maybe scoop out more of the pepper seeds.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Made this exactly as called for and it was delicious. Definitely spicy which I loved but would maybe deseeed the jalapeño if you don’t like a lot of spice but all in all an amazing and easy dish!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I used Pork Tenderloin, 2 of them to total 2lbs and threw in some quartered red potatoes.  I let the stew bake for 60 minutes at 325 degrees and it came out very tender and not dry.  Everyone raved over the flavors--thanks for the recipe and big hit!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Very delicious! Needed to make this vegetarian so I swapped pork for rinsed hominy & white beans.
Made exactly the same (with vegan chicken bullion) but added hominy and simmered for 15 to help soften a little, and then added white beans and simmered for another 15.

I split the recipe in half and was able to get 4 portions served with rice, pickled onion, avocado.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! Can’t wait to make it again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: My 15 year old said that we would have paid 100.00 for this meal and talked about when we would go back on the drive home. Followed this to the letter and everyone loved it.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I doubled this recipe because why go through all that work for only 4 servings. Also, who can find a 1 1/2 pound pork shoulder? Not me. I also tripled the spices. It was amazing. Served  it with Cilantro rice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely exceptional! Buy a large bone in pork shoulder and you will usually get enough to make this dish (or others) about 3 other times! Kroger had them for $16 for 8 pounds!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I enjoyed this one a lot! It's a keeper. Kept to the recipe but swapped a Anaheim for a poblano.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Grocery didn’t have fresh poblanos but they did have a medium sized jar of Hatch chilis. It was so delicious and only gets better the next day.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Made this using leftover smoked pork rib meat.  Turned out great and a quick meal since pork was already cooked

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Dennis Chiapello I used to think that too. get a really sharp boning knife. it takes apart the pork shoulder effortlessy.  mine isn't fancy- I think it might be Dexter or WinCo  brand- but it's sharp and has a thin blade that works around the bone real easy.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: What can I sub for the cilantro? I can't stand it. (Genetics!)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
I work at a small general store in Maine making their take-away meals in the fridge. I made this and it was a huge hit! It was also very easy for me, I cooked the pork in the crock pot and served it over polenta with sauteed greens on the side.

Just yum!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Made this with 2 1/2 lbs of boneless beef short rib vs. pork.  Everything else as per the recipe.  We are fortunate to live in an area with abundant tomatillos and chiles in the summer (and containers of same, roasted and chopped in the freezer for the off seasons).

Anyway, easy recipe, and excellent flavor.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This was unctuous and delicious. I grew tomatillos, poblanos and jalapeños in my garden this year and his recipe was perfect for using some of my bumper crop up. Thoroughly enjoyable!!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Followed recipe as is - it takes about an hour to prep the sauce not 15 minutes, but it's worth it. I used the slow cooker not a Dutch oven; the family loved it. Layers of flavor!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Definitely a bit of effort, but the result is delicious and leaves my family asking for double the receipe next time.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: It was delicious. I added a couple of different peppers from the garden as well as the poblanos. I served it over rice and sprinkled with cojito cheese and thinly sliced red onions.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: For those saying make it with hatch chilis, how many would you use? I’ve never worked with hatches but I’ve made chile verde with poblanos a ton of times, and want to try the variation

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: My recipe uses 2lbs. on top of the peppers mentioned. It is green chili after all.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Why does one need to cook it in the oven, instead of on the top of the stove?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: The stove is better but slower. You want direct flame on the peppers and to get them almost fully black then let them steam. There's a real flavor difference and the oven (unless you have a salamander) is too slow. Watch how hatch are roasted online.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: can I make this on sn instant pot and for how long?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I live in a place where it's almost impossible to find fresh or even canned tomatillos but it's not too hard to find salsa verde (of various quality). An acceptable substitute?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I have used canned or jarred salsa verde in a pinch.  Be careful if you are sensitive to spice as some brands are VERY spicy!  (taste and adjust)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I think so.  The sauce portion of this recipe is pretty much the backbone of my recipe for salsa verde.  Adjust for your desired level of heat from peppers.  You might want to "freshen it up" by adding in the onions cooked with cumin and oregano, as described in Step 7.  Fresh cilantro, if you can get it, covers a lot of deficiencies!  Enjoy!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: do you have any mexican corner stores or markets? they almost always have them @David M

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried freezing this?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Best with Hatch chilis. BTW, Anaheim chilis = Hatch chilis but without the heat. Good sub for those of us not able to get Hatch chilis. (Or order online during the season and freeze.) Pro tip: toast then grind your own cumin seeds. Second pro tip: toss the browned pork chunks in masa before returning them to the pot. Lookup ways to roast those peppers to easily remove the skins.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: How would this work with lamb or beef cubes?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe sounds delish! I will definitely try this with chicken. 

One question...in step 8, can this be put in the instant pot on slow cook or does it need to roast in the oven?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: You could do the pork in a pressure cooker and keep the cubes a little larger.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Try it with 1 cup broth and 1 cup Mexican beer like Modelo.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I've been making green chile stew with boneless chicken thighs, almost as good as pork, although I may go bone-in next time. Ingredients incl. chopped cactus leaf and tomatillos; Ro-Tel chopped tomatoes, onion, red potatoes and a lot of garlic; red, yellow and orange roasted bell peppers; a ton of roasted and skinned NM/CO green chiles. Chicken is dowsed and pounded in flour spiced w/cumin, paprika and salt; fried crispy. I make a roux out of the leavings in the fry pan to thicken the stew.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: You do you, but this is a totally different recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: @Mathew Price Boneless skinless chicken thighs substitute quite nicely in this as I have made a similar one.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: What a delicious recipe. Ended up using more cumin and oregano by accident but it put the dish over the top. Also used country style rib (bone in), deboned and cubed the meat then let the bones cook in the oven with the rest. Mmmmm

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Would love to try this but sourcing tomatillos in Perth, WA is tricky. Might be able to find canned ones or should I not bother?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: If you can find Heredez salsa verde, or any salsa verde, try that. Taste it first to avoid double-doses of heat or spices.  (in other words, some salsas verdes are spiced up, some not.)

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Great stuff!  I adjusted it just a skoosch and doubled the Oregano and 1.5x the Cumin. I also added a very healthy pinch of Allspice and a pinch of sugar.  What really brought the whole shebang home though was serving with some lime zest tossed across the pork. This made me think that next time I might go ahead and serve it with Lime Rice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Including chopped cilantro main stems imparts a surprising depth of flavor

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: As I suspected they might, the garlic cloves burned under the broiler, and that was just at the halfway point.  What a waste!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: the poblanos could use a zip or nip of flavor from a Serrano or two, and an ancho or two.  dont get me wrong, poblanos are great, but they are one-dimensional. Also, if you can't find good tomatillos, in a pinch you can use Herdez salsa verde.  I know, I know, it's not the same, but....

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Could this be adapted for an Insta-pot?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: 15 minutes prep??? Ha. After about 30 minutes of trying to skin the poblanos I gave up and just blended the skin with the rest. I really like this dish at restaurants, and always order it a new restaurant as a test.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Smoked pork butt also works nicely too. Fabulous recipe!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
This was exactly what I wanted.  I did all the things, except for leaving bigger chunks of pork 
Added an extra poplano pepper, and used about 3 lbs. of pork. Oh, and regular onions, as I had forgotten to pick up sweet onions.  I didn't find the prep onerous.  Quite a bit spicier than I expected,but that happens with peppers, esp. as I did not de-seed them. Ate with rice tonight, and will freeze to use for soft tortillas in the future. Will definitely make again!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: The only issue with this recipe is that it is not nearly large enough!  :)  We doubled the spices as we wanted more pop, squeezed lime for tang and buttered tortillas lightly before warming in the oven.  (vs rice). Two rolled, soft and buttery tortillas stuck in the bowl was perfect.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I just made this following the recipe precisely and it was solid but I wouldn’t say great. It’s tasty but doesn’t have a ton of flavor, and i left the seeds in my jalapeño so it would have a kick. Any suggestions to give it a little extra flavor?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: What is a good meat substitute for pork shoulder/butt for the many of us who do not eat pig

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Not sure how this can be called green chili without green chilis - Hatch or Pueblo, perhaps others, but Poblanos do not count.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Green refers to the overall color vs red chile. Besides, poblanos are green, as are the jalapeños and the cilantro contributions.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: You are kidding about a prep time of 15 minutes, right.  It took me quite a bit longer than that, but it was the first time I have ever tried this style of cooking.  However, It was EXCELLENT.  Will make again and hopefully I can cut my prep time down, way down.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Great in pressure cooker for 50 minutes with 20 minute natural release!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I'm not a chef but I woke up reading this recipe and had a craving for the meal. Just to let everyone know, this came out unbelievably delicious. Even unsophisticated cooks can pull this off simply by following the recipe.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Making this for my family but someone is averse to cilantro. What to do? Substitute parsley? Simply omit?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I'd omit; maybe bolster the oregano slightly? That being said, it is hard to detect the blended cilantro...

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Green tomatoes in lieu of tomatillos, turkey "osso buco" (slices of leg?), padron and Thai chilis, not jalapeño or poblano--all concessions to what's available in this corner of France. But I swear this bastard version is just as good as when I've cooked it according to the NYT ingredients, and I can't recommend it highly enough. Just don't feel you've got to stick strictly to the recipe as written.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: We really enjoyed this. Used county pork ribs. Added a cubed russet potato because I only had a 1lb package of ribs. Could only find canned whole tomatillos which worked great after draining and drying them with paper towels. Leftovers made great burritos! We mixed a can of white beans since there was a good amount of sauce left over.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I already had made some verde sauce and froze it.  All ingredients and process very similar to this recipe. I did add blistered poblanos/jalepenos to my mixture.  I added in the sauteed onion, cumin and mexican oregano as stated in recipe. My pork was larger chunks & I used 2lbs., cut more like 2".  I omitted the chicken broth, as I had 2 cups of the premade verde sauce.  It was more than enough to cover the pork. This was amazing served with homemade flour tortillas and yellow rice.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Added one serrano for a bit extra heat -- it was just right for us and eliminated need to add hot sauce (though we might still use that).  Next time I'll just peel poblano when they're right out of the oven, steaming didn't help much.  I'll also up the meat to 2 pounds and then more or less double the sauce ingredients.  This is a delicious dish, takes time but is not difficult.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I found it too tomatillo forward causing it to be sharp and almost bitter.  I added a lot more peppers to take that down a notch resulting in the roasted pepper flavor I remember from many trips to New Mexico.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This is wonderful, scales up nicely and freezes well.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: My family loved this.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I make this with a regular pork roast and it comes out delicious and is much easier to cut up

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This was fabulous as written.  Served with rice and beans the first night, tacos the next night, licking the dish the final night.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: To reinforce what others have said, living in Colorado and learning how to make this from a nice abuela, fire roasted Hatch Chile peppers are key, and Mexican oregano is also helpful. I buy 2 bushels of fresh fire roasted chiles, one hot and one medium, and freeze them in 1 lb packages so I can have them all year long.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight because my husband always orders this when we go to Mexican restaurants.  And he's a bit particular about how this tastes when we order it.  The good news is HE LOVES IT!  And really, it's quite a reasonably priced dish.  The only thing that was at all costly was the pork and even that, not that much!  I cut up the pork and kept it in the fridge for a couple of days so easy to cook once I got the time.  I did up the garlic and jalpeno a tiny bit but everything else as written

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Yum!! Doubled it, because who sells pork shoulder in 1.5lbs portions? Followed the recipe until I broke up the pork pieces by smashing against the pot, and added a 25oz can of hominy, because hominy is delicious and totally makes sense here. It’s so good right  outta the oven, but I’m gonna do best by a stew, and serve it tomorrow, for an even more developed result.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I made half the recipe for the two of us.  20 minutes before it was done I added a 25-ounce can of posole (hominy), rinsing before adding.  A much better fit than rice, I think.  We had leftovers for lunch the next day, so it makes plenty (we have good but not huge appetites). Also I cooked it on top of the stove.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous! A ton of work- so after all the slicing, chopping, roasting, browning, sauteing, pureeing, I combined everything and refrigerated overnight. The next afternoon I poured it into my crockpot for 1.5 hours and had a calm, relaxed dinner in a clean kitchen!  Followed commenters idea to use country style ribs. A fun project but 1+ hours prep. There are many different ways to make SV but this recipe is complex and delicious.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: So this was delicious but maybe I made the classic error of not tasting my peppers - I didn't deseed anything and it was HOT!!  Will definitely make again but next time I will check the heat on my peppers.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
but it tastes so good!
@Dennis Chiapello

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Loved this recipe! I agree with others that the prep time was more than 15 minutes, but worth the effort. I de-seeded my peppers, but I only put in two, which makes me think the required 3 are necessary for a punchier flavor. I would also recommend upping the cumin and oregano amounts if they have been sitting in your pantry for a while, I couldn’t detect either in the final dish. Makes amazing leftovers!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Re comments about the work of cutting up the butt. Got tired of it and braised it whole, dissecting it when it was cool enough to handle.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Anyone try this in a slow cooker?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: this was fantastic.  we eat a lot of pork here in north cackalacky - second biggest pork producer in the US after Iowa.  Glad to add another recipe to my many options.  Like one of the commentators, I couldn't get a pork shoulder less than 2.5 lbs at my grocery store, so we just cooked it with that but didn't really dial up any of the other ingredients.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Cooked as listed except replaced poblanos with 4 roasted Hatch green chile (I live in Albuquerque). Delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
This is so good that everyone wanted seconds. I'm doubling it next time.

I also thought that 2 1/2 hours is a great estimate for cook time, depending on how quickly your broiler heats up.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Really outstanding and surprisingly easy - just a lot of chopping.  But the flavor is fantastic.  I made it per the recipe and wouldn't change a thing.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This dish is great, but there's enough sauce to easily double the amount of pork. Served it with rice and some chopped avocado to temper the heat. Rave reviews!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: My version is similar minus the cilantro and add a bit of bitter orange.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Used shredded pork left over from a pork shoulder roast. Followed steps 1-4, and skipped steps 5 & 6. Picked up and completed step 7, cooking the onion in some pork fat pulled off of the chilled meat. Then added 4 cups (about 1 lb.) of the shredded meat, 6 + cups of low-sodium chicken broth, and 2 - 25 oz cans of hominy, drained. Brought to a boil, & simmered for about 15 minutes to meld the flavors. Served with the cilantro, lime wedges, and sliced radishes. Delicious!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Laughing at myself now (while coming back to make a second batch two days later) as I realize that what I did was turn this Pork Chile Verde recipe into a sort of Pozole. Ha! Anyway, we loved it!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: A lot of work but very good.  I've added corn and potatoes and I often serve it topped with avocado.  Doubling the recipe works well.  I roast the peppers separately from the tomatillos.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Fantastic. Among the best recipes I’ve found here. 

The only change I made was to add a habanero for extra heat. Having made this sort of meal many times before I finally got smart and had the pork shoulder cut into 1-2 inch pieces by the butcher. It saved me a lot of work and made preparation so much more enjoyable.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely divine!  AND oh how yummy the whole house smelled.  We like spicy, so I added an extra jalapeno.  Mine came out with lots of sauce - I think the next time I will add a bit more pork.  Delicious leftover, too.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: OMG!  This is so good!  Made it pretty much exactly according to the instructions.  It is as near to perfection as any recipe we've tried in the past year.  Absolutely delicious.  My wife commented that she just wanted to put her face in the pot.  I think that says it all.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I looked at all the comments - am I the only one who doesn’t like tomatillos?  It is early spring in Wisconsin and there won’t be any green tomatoes for months.  Is there any substitute besides green tomatoes for the tomatillos?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Timing on NYT Cooking recipes - it's rarely correct. 15 minutes of prep does not cover cutting & browning the pork! I look at a recipe, add in my head all the things I will need to chop/brown/blend to decide how long it will take. For this, I started about 3.5 hours before I wanted dinner. I roasted t in the oven at 300 for 1.5 hours, then I turned the temp up to 325 for 30 minutes. The pork was TENDER - I used a pork shoulder roast. Wish I had read the notes first & added a potato!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: When broiling in the first step, the oil and salt is unnecessary and not typical when making a salsa verde base. The garlic can also stay in its skin and will be ready to escape the oven more quickly than the tomatillos and peppers.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This was bomb! My changes were 2.5 lbs of pork, doubled the cumin and oregano, and added Mexican oregano.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Simplified Version:
1. Delete steps 1-4 and use Prepared Tomatillo/cilantro salsa such as Frontera Grill Tomatillo Salsa - 16 oz. with 8 ounces of chicken broth.
2. In a large Dutch oven, heat  2 tablespoons oil over medium and sauté 2 jalapeño and 5 cloves of garlic for 2 minutes.  Remove from oil and blend in mini food processor and add to the Tomatillo salsa. 
3. Follow recipe steps 5-9 and add the above to the pot in place of the broth and Tomatillo Puree (step 7).

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: I cannot figure out where that insane sodium amount comes from in this recipe! I would like to lower it, but how?

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody:
Made as written, but omitted the jalapeno, which would have been too spicy for my family. After reading the comments, I agree that there is no need to peel the poblano here since they get pureed. 
Flavors were great and my husband who grew up on Mexican food enjoyed the dish very much. Will do it again.

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This might be the most delicious thing I've ever made. The sauce is extremely flavorful and the pork came out tender. Excellent recipe!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: This is one of my favorite stew recipes to make. It always comes out delicious, with tender pork. There is never enough pork in there, though, so this time I did two pounds and the proportions were still ideal!

author: Kay Chun
recipeTitle: Pork Chile Verde Recipe
commentBody: Made as is for 4yrs now. Always a 10/10 🤤😋🤤
